era.value: 1962 mid-century pulp paperback — lurid cover stock, hand-lettered cover blurbs, dot-screen halftones, the kind of paperback you find for a quarter in a gas-station spinner rack

format.value: a full pulp paperback front cover rendered in HTML — title treatment, byline, tagline blurb, publisher logo bottom-corner, price-and-catalog-number top-corner, a 'first printing' starburst, and a back-cover blurb that flips into view when you click the spine; the cover IS the page, edge to edge, with the cover art described only in alt text inside an empty figure

subject.value: GRAVEL: a hard-boiled novel about a man who sells driveway gravel door-to-door in a small Ohio town in 1962 and gets tangled up with the woman who runs the rival quarry

tone.value: breathless pulp-cover-copy huckster — second-person 'YOU'LL GASP!' barker energy, italicized swoons, exclamation points used like punctuation salt, every adjective dialed to eleven, the voice of a paperback house trying to move 200,000 units off drugstore racks

palette.value: lurid tangerine (#ff6a1f) on butter-yellow newsprint (#f6e7a3) — pulp cover ink soaked into cheap pulp paper, slightly bleeding at the edges

mandatory_element.value: a 'pick your own pulp title' generator: three dropdowns (a verb, a noun, a place) and a button that rewrites the cover title, byline tagline, and back-cover blurb in place — the cover itself visibly re-prints

era.value: 1953 Caribbean cruise-line travel brochure — saturated tropical lithography, scalloped photo corners, palm-frond borders, the kind of trifold pamphlet a travel agent slid across a desk with a paper-clipped itinerary

format.value: a working tide chart for a fictional harbor — a 24-hour horizontal strip with a draggable sun/moon disk that, as you drag it across the day, visibly raises and lowers a CSS-drawn water line over a tiny silhouetted dock, exposing or submerging labeled critters (a starfish at low, a jellyfish at high) and updating a 'right now at the wharf' caption underneath in real time

subject.value: the daily tide schedule and harbor goings-on at Punta Calamar, a fictional sleepy port on the imaginary island of San Bártolo del Mar, where the postmaster is also the harbormaster and keeps a running tally of which fishing boats are in

tone.value: regional travel-show host — warm, enunciated, gesturing at the scenery, 'and folks, if you look just over here you'll see…', generous with local color, never sarcastic, secretly proud of every small detail

palette.value: coral pink (#ff7e6b) on seafoam aqua (#7fd4c1) with a butter-cream (#fff3d6) paper base — saturated 1950s travel-brochure lithograph, slight ink misregistration on the borders

mandatory_element.value: a real working slider input (styled as a sun/moon disk on a horizon track) that, as it's dragged, visibly raises and lowers the harbor's water line across the page and reveals different labeled sea creatures at each tide level

era.value: 1959 summer drive-in intermission reel — Eastmancolor concession-stand cards, dancing snack mascots, lobby-card typography, that eight-minute pause between the cartoon and the feature when the projection booth ran the same animation loop and every kid in a station wagon sprinted for the bathroom

format.value: a 'meet the troupe' stage roster for four dancing concession-stand mascots arranged in a kick-line across the screen — a popcorn box that hops, a soda cup that shimmies, a hot dog that bows, a candy box that twirls, each one a CSS-keyframe animation; pressing 1, 2, 3, or 4 on the keyboard makes that single mascot break formation for a solo while the other three freeze mid-pose, and the spacebar freezes the whole chorus dead; underneath them a sing-along marquee scrolls the 'let's all go to the lobby' lyric one phrase at a time, and a flip-clock countdown to FEATURE PRESENTATION sits in the upper corner ticking down the intermission

subject.value: the Snack Bar Players — the four-headed in-house dance revue of the (fictional) Skyview Drive-In of Cottage Grove, Oregon, written up by the concessions manager as if introducing a touring vaudeville act, with each mascot's stage name, hometown, dance specialty, on-screen run length, signature step, and one ongoing personal feud

tone.value: small-time concessions-manager impresario who genuinely believes his four animated cardboard mascots are a touring vaudeville revue — warm, name-dropping, proud, brags about Pippa Popcorn's lineage like she's a Rockette, drops hometown asides about Cottage Grove and the rival drive-in over in Creswell, never breaks character, would absolutely sign autographs

palette.value: cherry-red (#e6352b) display type on Technicolor cyan (#37c6d4) panels over a buttered-popcorn cream (#fff1c1) base — saturated Eastmancolor concession card, the colors slightly sun-faded and ink-bloomed at the edges like a lobby print that sat in the projection booth heat all summer

mandatory_element.value: a popcorn-kernel cursor trail — every mouse move leaves a tiny CSS-drawn unpopped kernel behind, and after about half a second each kernel blossoms into a popped-popcorn shape and then slowly fades; if the cursor sits still the kernels pile up in a little hill right under the pointer

wildcard.value: every named character must be introduced with a fictional middle name in parentheses on first mention, and that middle name must be a kitchen-utensil word (e.g., Pippa (Colander) Popcorn, Harvey (Ladle) Frank)

era.value: 1969 hand-painted sign trade — pre-airbrush peak, mahl-stick-and-quill craft, union-hall mimeographed bulletins, the last good decade before the airbrush rigs and the vinyl plotters started chewing the work out from under the brush hands; trade-card stock, fountain-pen signatures, ink stamps in red and purple

format.value: a tradesman's pocket membership card rendered edge-to-edge as the entire page — front face shows the member's name, three-digit card number, a portrait window (described in alt text inside an empty figure, no ), an embossed union seal, a 12-cell dues-paid grid (one cell per month), and a tiny 'see reverse' notice in the corner; clicking anywhere on the card body 3D-flips it (CSS transform, 0.8s ease) to show the BACK, which holds the full Code of Practice in small print — jurisdictional rules, fair-pricing tables for one-color vs. shaded vs. gilded lettering, the brotherhood oath, and a signature line. The 12 month-cells each toggle blank↔ink-stamped on click, and a live counter at the lower edge reads 'PAID UP: X / 12 MONTHS' in real time. The card has a faint diagonal crease running through it (a CSS gradient seam) like it's been folded in a back overall pocket for a year

subject.value: the pocket membership card and code of practice for Local 47 of the United Brotherhood of Hand Letterers & Pictorial Sign Painters, chartered 1923 in (fictional) Tinsmith, Pennsylvania — issued in the name of brother Walter Engle, second-generation shop man at Engle & Sons Letterworks on Foundry Street, with the back-of-card jurisdictional language laying out exactly which jobs belong to the brush trade and which (regrettably, under the 1967 accord) now belong to the airbrush local across the river in Slagville

tone.value: a soft-spoken fifty-year union steward writing the card copy himself at the hall's kitchen table after the Tuesday meeting — patient, proud of the brush, faintly defensive about the airbrush kids creeping into storefront work, warm enough to explain mahl-stick technique to a stranger at the coffee urn without ever sounding sour; he believes in the trade the way a parish priest believes in his parish

palette.value: deep union-maroon ink (#7d1f2f) printed on bone card stock (#f0e6cf), with a single accent of gold-leaf yellow (#c9a23e) reserved for the embossed seal and the dues-stamp impressions — the look of a tradesman's pocket card slightly creased from a back overall pocket, the ink slightly raised, the bone stock showing a faint linen tooth

mandatory_element.value: a real CSS 3D flip card — clicking the card body rotates it 180° on the Y axis to reveal the Code of Practice on the back, click again to flip back to the front; PLUS a 12-cell dues-paid grid (Jan–Dec) where each cell toggles between blank and a red ink-stamp impression on click, and a live 'PAID UP: X / 12 MONTHS' counter under the grid updates with every toggle

wildcard.value: every named brother of the Local must be introduced, on first mention only, with their three-digit union card number in square brackets immediately after the name AND their home shop in italics — e.g., 'Brother Walter Engle [#214], Engle & Sons Letterworks' — and the rule must be quietly observed without ever being explained on the page

era.value: 1987 cable-access public-television fundraiser pledge-drive break — the chunky lower-third bug, the donor name crawl, the studio-camera flare, the host in a too-big blazer behind a card table piled with tote bags and coffee mugs, that specific PBS-affiliate yellow-gel lighting and the ringing of off-screen pledge-line phones

format.value: a live on-screen pledge tote board that fills in the entire page like the broadcast frame itself — a giant central thermometer-style fundraising gauge rendered in CSS climbs from $0 toward a $11,000 goal as a fake donor-name crawl scrolls left across the bottom of the screen (Mrs. Helen Brubaker of Apt 4B, sustaining at $5/month; the Westbrook family in memory of Patches; an anonymous viewer in Ottumwa), while a 'PLEDGE NOW' phone-number bug pulses in the upper corner; clicking the giant red PLEDGE button in the middle bumps the thermometer up by a randomized donation amount, adds a freshly-generated donor name (with kitchen-utensil middle name) to the crawl in real time, and triggers the host's chyron lower-third to swap to a new piece of breathless pitch copy thanking the new donor BY NAME; the gauge visibly fills, the crawl never stops scrolling, and a small premium-gifts ladder along the side lights up tier by tier as the total crosses each threshold (TOTE BAG at $50, COFFEE MUG at $100, the BOXED VHS SET at $250, the WEEKEND-IN-BRANSON GETAWAY at $1,000)

subject.value: the spring pledge break for KOTC-TV Channel 38, a fictional UHF public-television affiliate broadcasting out of a converted feed-and-grain warehouse in Cottonseed, Missouri, hosted by station manager Marvin (Whisk) Halloran with telephone-bank help from the Cottonseed Friends of Public Broadcasting auxiliary, raising money to repair the transmitter tower struck by lightning during the March storms and to keep the Saturday-morning farm-report block on the air

tone.value: warm, slightly sweaty, over-caffeinated small-market public-tv host — folksy and sincere, the kind of guy who keeps thanking 'the folks at home' and dabs his forehead between pitches, drops first names of every volunteer answering phones in the background, brags about the new transmitter, gets a little choked up about Channel 38's twenty-three years on the air, never once condescends to the viewer, genuinely believes every dollar matters and will tell about how Mrs. Brubaker's pledge means another week of children's programming

palette.value: PBS-affiliate fundraiser yellow-gel studio light: a saturated marigold-yellow studio backdrop (#f4c430) with the pledge thermometer filled in a brick-red broadcast graphic red (#c8392d), all set on a beige-newsprint base (#f3ead0), with a single accent of off-air-blue chyron strip (#1f3a78) reserved exclusively for the lower-third graphics and the donor-name crawl — the look of a 1987 NTSC studio camera with the white-balance pushed slightly warm, faint scan-line texture in the background

mandatory_element.value: every named donor, volunteer, and on-air personality must be introduced on first mention with a kitchen-utensil middle name in parentheses (e.g., Marvin (Whisk) Halloran, Helen (Spatula) Brubaker, the late Patches (Colander) Westbrook), and the rule must be quietly observed throughout without ever being explained on the page; PLUS the giant red PLEDGE button must be a real working click counter that visibly raises the CSS thermometer toward $11,000, appends a new procedurally-generated donor entry (with kitchen-utensil middle name) to the scrolling crawl at the bottom of the screen, swaps the host's lower-third chyron to a new piece of pitch copy thanking that donor by name, and lights up the next premium-tier graphic when each dollar threshold is crossed — all client-side, no reload

wildcard.value: must include exactly 11 of something, and the page must point this out at least once

era.value: 1981 home-organ instruction manual — the era of the Lowrey Magic Genie and Hammond Cougar, when middle-class living rooms across America bought a console organ on installment plan and the manual that came with it was a spiral-bound, three-color-printed teaching workbook with chord charts, rhythm-button diagrams, and grinning photographs of a middle-aged couple at the bench; the manual smelled like fresh vinyl seat and walnut-veneer particleboard, and every chapter ended with a 'now try playing along!' sing-along page

format.value: a full two-page spread of a home-organ instruction manual rendered as a single horizontally-scrolling page (the spine runs vertically down the center of the viewport, the left page is LESSON SEVEN: THE BOSSA NOVA BUTTON and the right page is the sing-along sheet music for 'Moonlight Over Tarpon Springs'); pinned to the right page is a working organ-stop console — 14 rocker-switch toggles labeled FLUTE 8', REED 4', VIBRATO, SUSTAIN, AUTO-CHORD, BOSSA, BOLERO, FOXTROT, MARCH, LESLIE FAST, LESLIE SLOW, GLIDE, PERCUSSION, and ENSEMBLE — and clicking any rocker physically flips it (CSS, the rocker tilts the other way with a tiny shadow shift), updates a live 'NOW PLAYING' readout in the margin that describes in earnest prose what the organ would sound like with the current combination of stops engaged (e.g., 'a warm, breathy flute tone with a slow churning Leslie — suitable for hymns and slow ballads'), and toggles a small 'recommended for' caption underneath; the sing-along sheet music on the right page has highlighted lyrics that pulse one syllable at a time on a slow loop as if a bouncing ball were tracking through them, and a margin sidebar called THIS WEEK AT THE BENCH lists 11 student-recital tips from the manual's author

subject.value: Lesson Seven of the OFFICIAL OWNER'S COMPANION & PROGRESSIVE COURSE for the Wendell-Brockmeier Sovereign II home console organ (a fictional 1981 model manufactured in Elkhart, Indiana by the Wendell-Brockmeier Organ Works of Indiana, retail $2,495 with bench, available in Pecan, Walnut, or Mediterranean Oak finish), written by staff instructor Mrs. Yvonne (Skillet) Brockmeier-Hatch, who teaches the manual's progressive course from a showroom-floor studio above the Elkhart dealership and signs every chapter 'warmly, Yvonne'; this lesson covers the BOSSA NOVA rhythm button, the proper left-hand chord-fingering for the iv-V7-I cadence, and includes the full sheet music to the lesson's required sing-along piece, 'Moonlight Over Tarpon Springs' (words and music by house composer Vernon (Spatula) Plait, 1979, ASCAP)

tone.value: warm, encouraging suburban organ instructor in her fifties writing to a brand-new owner she imagines is sitting at the bench in their den on a Saturday afternoon with the manual open in their lap — patient, complimentary ('don't worry if your left hand feels clumsy at first, mine did too in 1962!'), proud of the instrument's features in a slightly salesman-y way, drops first names of past students who mastered this lesson ('Mrs. Doris (Whisk) Anwar of South Bend played this very piece at her granddaughter's confirmation last June'), uses exclamation points generously but never sarcastically, occasionally interrupts the lesson with a tiny aside about her cat or the weather in Elkhart, signs off every section with a warm tag line

palette.value: spiral-bound organ-manual three-color print: a warm cream paper stock (#f5ead0) carrying the body text in deep walnut-brown ink (#4a2c1a), with all callout boxes, highlighted lyrics, and the organ-stop rockers in a muted instrument-panel teal (#3d7a82) — the look of a 1981 spiral-bound instruction book printed at the Elkhart job shop, the cream paper showing faint show-through from the next page, the brown ink slightly warm and the teal ink slightly chalky like it sat in the press tray too long

mandatory_element.value: a working CSS organ-stop console of exactly 14 rocker switches that each physically tilt on click (the rocker visually flips to its other tilt position with a subtle drop-shadow shift); the current combination of engaged stops drives a live 'NOW PLAYING' description box in the right margin that updates in real time with an earnest plain-language sentence about how the resulting sound would feel (warmth, attack, suitability for hymn vs. dance vs. ballad), AND a 'RECOMMENDED FOR' line underneath that picks from a small set of occasions ('a funeral home prelude', 'a polka social', 'practicing scales at low volume so as not to wake the dog'); the highlighted lyric syllables on the sing-along sheet pulse in sequence via CSS keyframes as if a bouncing ball were tracking them; everything client-side, no reload

wildcard.value: THIS WEEK AT THE BENCH sidebar must contain exactly 11 numbered student-recital tips, and the page must point out the number 11 at least once (Mrs. Brockmeier-Hatch can mention it warmly — 'eleven tips, one for each finger plus a thumb of encouragement!')

era.value: 1958 mid-century hi-fi enthusiast magazine — peak build-your-own audiophile era, when middle-class men in finished basements bolted tweeters into walnut cabinets they cut themselves on weekends, subscribed to thick quarterly magazines printed on uncoated pulp stock, and treated stereo placement as a household scientific discipline; specifically the look and feel of GOLDEN EAR QUARTERLY, a fictional Chicago-published audio review with a Room Doctor column, a tube-tester service department, and a back-page personals section for surplus McIntosh chassis

format.value: a two-page magazine spread of the Room Doctor consultation column: the left page is the typeset letter from the reader and the Doctor's discursive reply set in two narrow columns with a small portrait cut of the Doctor at the top; the right page is a top-down architectural floorplan of the reader's living room rendered entirely in CSS — two speaker cabinets in the upper corners, a fireplace with brick hearth, a bookcase wall, a bay window, a settee, a hooked rug, a credenza with a goldfish bowl on it, and a single draggable LISTENING CHAIR icon the visitor moves around the floor with the mouse; as the chair is dragged, a live STEREO IMAGE REPORT panel beside the plan updates a plain-prose verdict on what the soundstage is doing from that exact seat, and a small CSS-drawn frequency-response curve at the bottom of the floorplan flexes in real time to reflect the room acoustics from that listening position

subject.value: a Room Doctor consultation in the pages of GOLDEN EAR QUARTERLY, issue forty-seven, Spring 1958, addressing a letter from Mr. and Mrs. Tibald Hapgood of 414 Linnet Lane in Toledo, Ohio, who wrote in last March seeking guidance on where to place their two new Brimsworth-Polk Continental III floor-standing loudspeakers in the den of their newly remodeled split-level, and whose letter (printed in full at the top of the left page) included a hand-drawn floorplan, an inventory of every piece of furniture in the room down to the goldfish bowl on the credenza, an apologetic note about the wall-to-wall carpeting Mrs. Hapgood had just had installed, and an anxious aside asking whether the family parakeet's cage might interfere with high-frequency response

tone.value: the Room Doctor himself — a fastidious, faintly pompous mid-century audiophile authority who treats two-channel stereo placement as a sacred branch of applied geometry; he addresses the Hapgoods as 'the discerning auditors' and never by their first names, drops the occasional Latin phrase ('the sine qua non of proper imaging is symmetry, and symmetry is the discipline of the patient man'), is mildly disapproving of wall-to-wall carpeting on the grounds that it 'devours the upper octaves alive,' refers to room corners as 'the bass-bloom hazard zones,' has firm opinions about drapery, recommends a diagonal hooked rug as a remedy for nearly every acoustic ill, is courteous but unyielding, occasionally cites his own forty-year career measuring rooms for symphony halls and funeral parlors alike, and signs every column 'in fidelity, the Doctor'; never sarcastic, never cool, never ironic — he means every word, and he means it warmly

palette.value: warm uncoated-magazine-paper beige (#e8dcc0) carrying body copy and rules in deep audio-cabinet oxblood ink (#6b1f2a), with a single brass-amplifier-knob accent (#b89048) reserved exclusively for the two speaker cabinets on the floorplan, the listening-chair icon, the frequency-response curve, and the Doctor's signature flourish — the look of a 1958 quarterly printed at a Chicago job shop on cheap pulp stock with one spot color of metallic gold ink for the equipment illustrations, the oxblood slightly absorbing into the paper and the gold catching the light at a slant

mandatory_element.value: a real draggable LISTENING CHAIR icon the visitor moves around the CSS floorplan with the mouse (pointer/touch drag handlers, no library); as the chair moves, a live STEREO IMAGE REPORT box updates in real time with an earnest plain-prose verdict on the soundstage from that exact seat (e.g. 'centered, with a faint pull toward the bay window — acceptable for chamber works but unsuitable for full orchestra' / 'severe bass bloom from the southwest corner; the Doctor cannot in good conscience approve' / 'midrange honk from the brick hearth — observe the diagonal-rug remedy outlined at left' / 'smothered: the auditor is sitting inside the velvet drape and should not be'), AND a small CSS-drawn frequency-response curve at the bottom of the plan flexes its shape in real time to reflect that exact position's room acoustics (corner = bass hump, near-window = treble dip, dead-center inside the listening triangle = textbook flat); the chair snaps to a 12-unit grid; everything client-side, no reload, no third-party JavaScript

wildcard.value: every distance, dimension, and measurement that appears anywhere on the page — every wall length on the floorplan, the recommended speaker spread, the listening-triangle base, the distance from rear wall, the height of the bookcase, even the diameter of the goldfish bowl and the wingspan of the parakeet's cage — must be given in CUBITS, never in feet, inches, meters, or centimeters; the cubit must never be defined, explained, converted, footnoted, or apologized for anywhere on the page; the Room Doctor uses it as if it were the universal currency of acoustic measurement and expects the discerning auditor to keep up

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1962 — ham radio and early space-program era; the moment when amateur radio operators worldwide tracked Mercury spacecraft and confirmed distant contacts via printed QSL (signal-confirmation) cards

format.value: An interactive collection of authentic QSL cards from a fictional ham radio operator's personal log. Each card is a clickable, flippable tile — click to toggle between front (call sign, frequency band, date/time logged UTC, signal report in RST scale) and back (hand-written-style note, operator's location, congratulations on two-way contact). The page presents them as a spread from Roy's station notebook, arranged in a grid.

subject.value: Roy Patterson, a ham radio enthusiast in Terre Haute, Indiana, documents his reception of space-program transmissions and QSL card exchanges with operators worldwide. The page displays seven confirmed contacts from early 1962 — operators from the USA, Japan, West Germany, Argentina, Egypt, Australia, and Canada — each logging a two-way contact via AM radio and confirming it with a physical card in the mail.

tone.value: Earnest, meticulous hobbyist. Roy treats radio operating as a precision craft and QSL exchanges as meaningful proof of global connection across borders. Proud, technical, genuinely excited about the space program and the miracle of long-distance radio contact. Every claim about the hobby is sincere and unironic.

palette.value: Warm bakelite radio-casing brown (#8b7355) + cream QSL-card stock (#f5e6d3), with brass-radio-dial gold (#d4af37) accenting call signs and frequency bands. The specific tactile aesthetic of a 1960s valve-radio exterior and printed QSL cardstock on a radio operator's desk.

mandatory_element.value: Exactly 7 working, clickable QSL cards. Each front displays call sign, frequency band (e.g., '40 meters'), date/time in UTC, and RST signal report (readability 1–9, strength 1–9, tone 1–9). Clicking flips to the back with a hand-written-style note and the operator's location. Cards return to front on a second click or after 4 seconds. Flip state persists via localStorage so returning visitors see the same card states.

wildcard.value: Every ham radio operator mentioned must be introduced with their amateur radio call sign in square brackets on first mention only — e.g., 'Thomas Murphy [W1AZ]' or 'Akira Tanaka [JA2PC]' — and this convention must be observed throughout without ever being explained or footnoted on the page. Additionally, the page must naturally reference at least one real Mercury or Gemini spacecraft by name in Roy's log notes or a QSL-card message.

forbidden_register.value: No archival / museum / nostalgic detachment. This is an active, living log — Roy is documenting these contacts in real time in 1962, not preserving them from the distant past. Everything is present-tense, immediate, urgent belonging to this specific moment. No soft melancholy or 'remember when' framing.

era.value: 1890s American small-town postal culture; the era when personal letters were primary intimate communication, and a single letter in the mail carried weight, secrecy, and ceremony

format.value: A clickable nested-letter experience. The initial screen shows an envelope addressed in period handwriting. Clicking opens it to reveal the first letter, dated 1890, written by Grandmother Eleanor to her daughter. Within each letter, certain phrases are clickable (subtle underline on hover). Clicking a phrase opens the next envelope tucking inside, with a reply letter (1895). This nesting repeats three more times (letters from 1900, 1905, 1910). A 'back' button returns to the previous letter. Header shows progress ('Letter 2 of 5'). All state persists in localStorage.

subject.value: A family inheritance and identity mystery across thirty years of correspondence (1890–1920). Each nested letter introduces a new voice—grandmother, daughter, grandson, a stranger's letter, a final mysterious relative—gradually building a revelation about obligations kept, sacrifices made, and love expressed through Victorian formality. The unfolding is emotional and tender, not dark.

tone.value: Earnest Victorian formality with genuine warmth and emotion breaking through. Each writer uses careful period language—'my dear', 'I trust this finds you'—but each also carries real struggle: grief, obligation, joy, uncertainty. No irony. These are real people communicating as honestly as their era permits, treating formal language as a genuine expression of intimacy.

palette.value: Aged cream envelope stock (#f5e6d3) + deep sepia brown ink (#3e2723) + copper/brass accents on envelope seals and penmanship flourishes (#b87333). The visual impression is precious, faded, tactile—like actual aged paper handled with care.

mandatory_element.value: Fully functional nested-letter system: clicking the envelope opens the first letter; within each letter, at least one phrase is clickable and opens the next envelope (five letters total). Each letter has a 'back' button to return to the previous one. Page header displays progress ('Letter X of 5'). All state (current letter) persists in localStorage so returning visitors resume where they left off.

wildcard.value: One of the five letters must encode a haiku about family obligation, inheritance, or enduring love disguised naturally within the period-appropriate Victorian prose—readable as normal prose but containing exactly 5-7-5 syllable structure when read carefully or aloud.

forbidden_register.value: No archival, museum, or nostalgic detachment. These letters are urgent, living, present-tense emotional communications from real people with real stakes. Avoid cool irony, literary condescension, or soft melancholy about 'the past.' Treat Victorian formality as a genuine language of intimacy and truth, not as material for gentle mockery.

A separate 'Operator's Route Map' sidebar shows the geographic territory. A working 'Music Category' filter (Ballads, Jump Blues, Country, Novelty, Standards, Rhythm & Blues, Polka) dynamically shows/hides records across all diners in real-time, with filter state persisting in localStorage.

subject.value: Chester 'Doc' Mallory, an independent jukebox operator servicing nine diners across fictitious Holbrook, Iowa (1952–1955), maintaining detailed selection logs documenting: which songs generate play revenue at which locations, seasonal music rotation, customer handwritten request cards left on tables, diner-owner feedback, mechanical jukebox maintenance notes, and Doc's personal observations about how music shapes each establishment's social personality.

tone.value: Earnest, warm jukebox curator voice—genuine pride in understanding customers' musical tastes and matching songs to each diner's specific personality and clientele. Deep respect for music as social glue and operator expertise as real hospitality work. Present-tense immediacy: these nine diners are vibrant community gathering spaces RIGHT NOW, and Doc's music selections genuinely matter to how people spend their evenings. Peer-to-peer knowledge about which records drive plays, customer relationship warmth, no irony or museum distance from the genuine stakes of good jukebox curation for small-town social life.

palette.value: Brushed chrome metallic (#c0c0c0) + glowing jukebox amber (#ff9500) + warm wood-panel diner brown (#8b6f47) + vinyl record deep red (#8b0000) + cream diner menu card stock (#f5e6d3), evoking the tactile feel of a chrome-and-wood 1950s diner interior, warm incandescent jukebox glow, spinning vinyl records, operator ledger pages, and the polished beauty of small-town hospitality infrastructure.

mandatory_element.value: Working diner selector tabs (9 fictional diners: The Sunrise, Ruby's Corner, Mel's Auto-Stop, The Copper Kettle, Twin Pines Lodge, Downtown Grill, Traveler's Rest, The Frontier, Hollister's Family Diner) that expand to reveal that diner's record selections; plus working Music Category filter buttons (Ballads, Jump Blues, Country, Novelty, Standards, Rhythm & Blues, Polka, Easy Listening) that dynamically show/hide records across all diner tabs in real-time with filter state persisting in localStorage.

wildcard.value: Exactly one song record card, when read aloud or studied carefully, encodes a haiku about community, music, or diner gathering—disguised naturally within Doc's operator notes (5-7-5 syllable structure, readable as normal log but containing haiku form when parsed). Additionally, one customer's handwritten request card must contain a subtle factual anachronism (mention of a song not released until 1959 or later, or an artist not yet famous in 1954), mimicking real jukebox operator documentation errors where customer song requests occasionally outpaced actual record availability. Typing keyboard shortcut 'JUKEBOX' reveals a hidden modal displaying a period-authentic vintage jukebox manufacturer's advertisement (described in alt-text only—no actual elements, just styled placeholder with description of gleaming chrome facade, illuminated record carousel, coin-slot mechanism, and 1950s product photography) and Doc's personal letter reflecting on how music transforms each diner's evening atmosphere.

era.value: 1880s peak magic lantern slide collecting and projection culture—hand-painted glass slides as revolutionary educational and entertainment technology before motion pictures; the golden age when amateur optical societies documented their collections, performed projection shows, and witnessed light-based image projection as genuinely transformative educational infrastructure

format.value: Interactive magic lantern projection hall and slide archive browser—visitor enters a wooden gaslit projection hall with brass apparatus visible. Left sidebar displays a scrollable archive of thumbnails (hand-painted glass slides). Clicking a slide triggers an animation simulating the slide being inserted into the projection apparatus; the slide's chromatic description appears on a central 'projection screen' alongside metadata (artist, date, subject, society origin). Below, expandable viewer reaction cards (from society members who witnessed this exact slide projected). Parallel tabs: 'Lecture Schedule' lists upcoming evening projection performances with lecturer names and program themes; 'Archive by Subject' allows filtering slides (Historical Scenes / Scientific Diagrams / Literary Tableaux / Exotic Travels / Astronomical Charts / Biblical Narratives / Natural History). Working localStorage-backed 'My Favorites' sidebar tracks personally selected slides. Fully functional form submits new reactions to slides (fields: viewer name, society affiliation, reaction to witnessing this slide projected, emotional/educational impact) persisted and displayed alongside pre-seeded testimonials.

subject.value: The Westcote Optical Society—a fictional 1887 New England amateur optical club actively producing and performing hand-painted magic lantern slides for educational evening lectures, traveling shows, and scientific demonstrations. Members include amateur painters who hand-paint glass slides, projection technicians who operate the optical apparatus, lecturers who narrate shows, collectors who acquire rare slides, and educators who design programming. The society documents the technical achievement of projection apparatus, the artistry of hand-painted chromatic slides, and the genuine joy of bringing scientific knowledge and visual wonder to audiences through light-based projection.

era.value: 1922-1928 — peak amateur crystal radio detector builder and distant-station logger culture, before vacuum-tube superheterodyne radio becomes standard. The moment when inexpensive crystal sets democratize long-distance radio reception, hobbyists document their builds, fine-tune cat's-whisker detectors with meticulous precision, construct increasingly sophisticated antenna arrays, maintain detailed logbooks of distant stations received across North America at night, and connect across regions via correspondence about component sourcing, tuning technique refinement, and the genuine thrill of pulling voices and music from thin air across continental distances.

format.value: Interactive crystal radio builder and reception-logbook browser. Visitor selects a builder from a roster of 12+ amateur radio hobbyists to expand their equipment profile (name, location, specialty focus: coil winding / antenna design / detector optimization / long-distance reception technique). Each builder reveals their current radio build specifications (inductor design, capacitor values, crystal type, cat's-whisker positioning diagrams), workshop documentation photos (alt-text only), and minimum 8 reception logbook entries showing distant stations heard (callsign, frequency, location, reception date/time, signal quality 1-5 rating, antenna configuration, interference notes, frequency identification). Interactive 'Reception Ledger' displays 20+ documented station catches with clickable expansion revealing full logged conditions. All builder selections, expanded specifications, and visitor-submitted receptions persist in localStorage across page reloads.

format.value: A working hand-twist pretzel dough toy. Visitor sees a fat rope of dough on a floured wooden board, viewed top-down. When the knot geometry matches a valid pretzel topology (two loops, one crossing, ends tucked), the dough briefly glows golden and a chime sounds; a button labeled BAKE IT slides the finished pretzel into a small oven panel where it browns over 8 seconds, gets brushed with butter, and joins a growing tray of pretzels you've made this session, each one slightly differently shaped because each twist was your hand. Tray persists in localStorage. A counter ticks up: PRETZELS TWISTED TODAY.

subject.value: The specific tactile pleasure and small craft achievement of folding a single rope of dough into the ancient pretzel knot — the geometry your hands learn before your brain explains it, the satisfaction of the crossing-and-tuck, the way each pretzel comes out a little different from the last even when you're trying to make them identical

era.value: 1968 — peak Soviet matchbox-label manufacturing era, when state graphic-design studios produced millions of tiny lithographed matchbox labels on themes ranging from cosmonauts and tractors to folk fairy tales and ice hockey, each label a miniature commission for working designers and a genuine pocket-sized art form distributed by the billion through corner kiosks across the USSR

format.value: A genuine playable in-browser cryptogram puzzle: a single tiny phrase has been substitution-encrypted (each letter consistently swapped to another letter), printed across the front of an oversized matchbox label illustration as if it were the label's slogan. Visitor solves by clicking any encrypted letter to highlight every occurrence of it on the label, then typing what they think it decodes to — letters fill in across the whole phrase live as guesses propagate. A small frequency-count panel beside the label shows how often each cipher letter appears. A REVEAL SLOGAN button (initially dimmed) brightens only after the visitor has correctly placed at least four letters; pressing it slides the label open like a real matchbox drawer, exposing the plain decoded slogan printed on the inner tray along with the designer's signature and the print-run number. Wrong guesses do not punish — letters can be retyped freely. The cipher and the slogan are baked into the page (no server, no API).

subject.value: State Matchbox Label Studio No. 4, Leningrad, autumn 1968 — specifically a junior designer's commission for a label commemorating amateur ice hockey on frozen river-bend rinks in small northern towns, the slogan she lobbied for and finally got past the studio editor printed in two-color offset across the front of a 37mm by 53mm cardboard surface, the tiny art form of pocket-sized graphic design treated as legitimate skilled commercial craft.

era.value: 1962 — peak American roadside motel neon-sign fabrication era, when small independent sign shops hand-bent glass tubes filled with inert gas to create custom illuminated signage for highway motor courts, diners, and tourist cabins along the new interstate system; a regional craft where each sign was hand-drawn, hand-bent, and hand-pumped by skilled glass technicians who worked at small benches with rubber tubing, mercury manometers, and gas cylinders, producing one-of-a-kind illuminated identities for family-owned roadside businesses across the desert Southwest

format.value: A working hand-illustrated salesman's sample book for a fictional regional neon sign company, Vega Glassworks of Tucumcari, New Mexico — the kind of bound vinyl-covered portfolio a traveling salesman would carry into a motel owner's office and flip open across the desk to show available sign styles, mounting hardware, color combinations, and pricing tiers. Visitor turns pages by clicking the right or left edge of the open spread (smooth page-flip animation with paper-curl shadow), each two-page spread showing one fully-illustrated proposed sign design pasted onto cream cardstock with the shop's typewritten specification card stapled below: the visitor can click any sign illustration to flip it to its 'lit at night' state (the line drawing transitions to glowing neon against a black desert backdrop with subtle electrical hum via Web Audio API), and can click the typed specification card to expand a fold-out sheet showing the hand-drawn glass-bending diagram, gas-fill notes (neon for red-orange, argon-mercury for blue, helium for gold), transformer wattage, mounting bracket schematics, and total quoted price in 1962 dollars. A working order-form bookmark tucked into the back of the portfolio lets the visitor 'specify' their own custom sign by typing a business name and selecting a style, which then renders as a new illustrated sign added to the back of the book and persisted in localStorage as part of the visitor's growing proposed-jobs section.

subject.value: The skilled regional craft of small-shop neon sign fabrication along Route 66 in 1962 — Vega Glassworks of Tucumcari, a three-man operation (one designer-illustrator, one master glass-bender named Eulalio, one electrical wiring tech) producing custom illuminated signs for motor courts, drive-in diners, gas stations, and trading posts across eastern New Mexico, west Texas, and the Oklahoma panhandle; the salesman's sample portfolio as a working sales instrument that motel owners actually browsed across desks to choose the sign that would identify their roadside business at night to families driving the new interstate; the specific intelligence of matching gas to color (neon for warm reds, argon-mercury for cool blues, helium for soft gold), of bending tube around hand-drawn pattern paper, of designing letterforms that read at 65 miles per hour at midnight from a quarter mile out; the entire infrastructure of regional commercial identity rendered glowing across a vast dark desert

Each typewritten specification card is clickable to expand a fold-out sheet revealing: Eulalio's hand-drawn glass-bending diagram with measurement notations in inches, gas-fill specification (Neon / Argon-Mercury / Helium / Krypton blend), transformer specifications in volts and milliamperes, mounting bracket schematic, total quote in 1962 dollars itemized by tubing length, transformer cost, installation labor, and Vega Glassworks shop overhead. Working order-form bookmark at portfolio's back: visitor types a business name (up to 40 characters), selects style category (Motor Court Marquee / Diner Roof Sign / Trading Post Pylon / Gas Station Pole-Mount), selects primary color (Neon Red / Argon Blue / Helium Gold / Two-Color Combination), and submits — a new portfolio page generates with their custom sign illustrated in line-art day state, clickable to its lit-state, with auto-generated specification card showing computed quote, and is appended permanently to the back of the portfolio with all custom signs persisted in localStorage across page reloads, allowing the visitor's proposed-jobs section to grow over multiple visits

wildcard.value: After the visitor commissions their fifth custom sign across visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden final spread permanently appears at the very back of the portfolio labeled 'Eulalio's Personal Bench — Not For Sale' — opening this spread reveals a hand-drawn sketch of a single small neon sign Eulalio bent for his own kitchen window at home, reading his wife's name in soft helium gold, with a typewritten note from Hal explaining that Eulalio bends one sign per year for someone he loves and that this one took him three evenings to get the curve of the L exactly right. When read aloud or parsed carefully line-by-line, Hal's note encodes an exact haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about glass, breath, and light in the desert, disguised naturally within earnest salesman prose. The lit-state of Eulalio's personal sign glows warmer than any other sign in the portfolio and produces no transformer hum — only silence.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when roadside neon was everywhere' nostalgic detachment, NO cool ironic distance from commercial signage as kitsch or quaint Americana, NO soft melancholy about 'the death of the small motor court' or wistful framing of Route 66 as already-lost. NO condescension toward sign-fabrication as unskilled commercial labor or skepticism about hand-bent glass-tube work as legitimate craft. NO retrospective elegy framing. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled regional craft happening RIGHT NOW in 1962 — Vega Glassworks is a working shop, Eulalio is at his bench bending glass tonight, motel owners ARE buying signs that will glow over the parking lots of family businesses serving real travelers, and Hal Reyes is genuinely proud of every commission. Treat hand-bent neon fabrication as serious skilled labor requiring real motor learning and gas-handling intelligence, treat commercial sign design as legitimate commercial art, treat regional sign-shop infrastructure as genuinely important for the working economy of small roadside businesses, and treat the salesman's portfolio as a genuine sales instrument carrying real proposals for real jobs. Present-tense warmth, full emotional weight, and earnest respect for the craft and the people doing it required.

length.description: something to explore, multiple sections, interactive elements

era.value: 1953 — peak American midcentury color-printed road map era, the specific moment when oil companies (Esso, Sinclair, Phillips 66) gave away beautifully lithographed folding road maps at every filling station, and the precise typography, color separations, and topographic shading techniques used by Rand McNally and General Drafting Co. cartographers represented the height of accessible printed cartography as both navigational instrument and quiet domestic art object kept in the glovebox

format.value: A full-viewport silent generative weather map — a fictional unfolded midcentury road map that fills the entire browser viewport, on which a slow procedural weather system is continuously moving across the printed landscape: cold fronts drift southeast as soft pale-blue ink washes that bleed into the paper grain, isolated thunderstorm cells bloom and dissipate as small darkening stippled patches, a high-pressure ridge inches westward as a faint amber glow over the dry country, and tiny SVG raindrops fall only inside the wet zones while the highways, county lines, town dots, and elevation hachures of the underlying road map remain perfectly still beneath the moving weather. No interface, no text overlay, no instructions, no controls, no buttons, no clicks required — the map IS the piece, the weather IS the only motion, and the visitor watches an entire meteorological day pass across a 1953 road-atlas surface at roughly 90 seconds per simulated 24 hours, looping forever with new randomized weather each cycle

subject.value: A single fictional 1953 oil-company road map of the central Appalachian foothills (a stretch of imagined country covering parts of southern Ohio, eastern Kentucky, and western West Virginia, with hand-lettered towns named Pawpaw Junction, Sweetwater, Coulter's Crossing, Mt. Holly Gap, and Iron Lick) experiencing one full unobserved meteorological day from pre-dawn calm through late-morning fronts, midafternoon convective storms, an evening clearing, and overnight ground fog settling into the river valleys — the quiet truth that weather happens across maps whether anyone is reading them or not, that cartography is a stage where atmospheric pressure systems perform their continuous work over printed paper, that this folded map sitting on a kitchen table in 1953 IS at this very moment being rained on inside its own depicted geography

tone.value: No tone — there is no narrator, no voice, no text visible to the visitor at any point. The piece speaks only through cartographic and meteorological visual language. The closest analogue to a voice is the silent steady patience of the map itself: a printed surface holding still while weather passes over it, indifferent to being watched, present-tense and continuous, the visual equivalent of overhearing rain on someone else's roof in another county

palette.value: Aged road-map cream paper (#f1e4c4) as the base sheet + Esso-style warm brick-red highway ink (#b53a2a) for U.S. and state routes + deep forest-green ink (#2d5a2e) for state-forest shading and river symbols + warm sepia-brown (#6b4a2a) for contour hachures and town typography + cool pale weather-front blue (#6a9cc9) for cold-front washes + soft thunderhead gray-violet (#7d7896) for storm-cell stippling + warm late-afternoon amber (#e8a850) for high-pressure ridge glow + cool fog-silver (#c4cdd0) for overnight valley fog, evoking the specific lithographic registration of midcentury folded road maps and the soft transparency of watercolor weather symbology painted across printed cartography

mandatory_element.value: A complete, fully-rendered fictional road map filling the viewport: minimum 5 named towns with hand-lettered serif labels and small filled circles for town markers, minimum 4 numbered highway routes drawn as warm brick-red ink lines with route shield badges, minimum 2 winding rivers in forest-green ink with thin tributary lines and italic river names, contour hachure shading across at least three ridge regions to indicate elevation, county boundary dashed lines, and a small inset compass rose in the lower-right corner — all rendered in pure SVG and remaining absolutely static throughout the entire animation. Over this fixed map, a continuously animated weather system runs on a 90-second loop representing one full 24-hour day: (1) a procedurally-generated cold front sweeps diagonally across the map as a soft pale-blue ink wash with feathered edges and small triangle frontal symbols, taking roughly 35 seconds to cross fully; (2) two to four convective storm cells bloom independently as darkening gray-violet stippled patches that grow, peak, and dissipate over 15-20 seconds each, with tiny SVG raindrops falling only inside their active footprints; (3) a high-pressure ridge slides westward as a faint amber glow with subtle radiating isobar curves; (4) at the evening phase, an amber-orange sunset wash briefly tints the western third of the map; (5) at the overnight phase, cool silver ground fog gradually accumulates inside the river valleys and low elevations, then thins out as the dawn cycle begins again. The animation runs autonomously via CSS keyframes and requestAnimationFrame with no user input required, no controls visible, no UI elements present

wildcard.value: Exactly one tiny detail somewhere on the map — a single town's name, a specific highway shield number, or a small italicized river label — corresponds to a real obscure midcentury cartographic Easter egg: a 'paper town' style fictional placeholder that mapmakers historically inserted to catch copyright infringement, and during the overnight fog phase of the animation cycle, the ground fog deliberately never quite covers this one specific feature — it remains visible above the silver fog as a quiet acknowledgment to the anonymous cartographers at General Drafting Co. who inserted such phantoms into their maps as small private signatures. The visitor is given no indication this is happening and no reward for noticing

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO text, captions, labels-explaining-the-piece, title overlays, instructional UI, tooltip hints, loading messages, footer credits, or any narrating voice anywhere on the page visible to the visitor — the piece must be entirely silent and entirely visual. NO museum-archival 'remember when gas stations gave away free maps' nostalgic framing, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of folded paper cartography,' NO cool ironic distance from midcentury commercial printing as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy for analog navigation. NO weather-app interface conventions (no temperature readouts, no radar legends, no time stamps, no compass UI, no precipitation percentages). NO voice, NO narrator, NO tone of any kind made textual. The map exists, the weather passes over it, and the visitor watches in silence — that is the entire piece

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

wildcard.value: must include exactly 7 of something

forbidden_register.value: lowercase sentence-fragments / hyperminimal

era.value: 1979 — peak amateur ventriloquist convention culture, when small regional gatherings of working ventriloquists, dummy-makers, and figure-carvers met annually in hotel ballrooms to share lip-control techniques, swap voice-throwing exercises, and consult on figure restoration; a thriving hobbyist subculture with its own published quarterly journals, mail-order parts catalogs for replacement glass eyes and articulated jaw mechanisms, and an internal craft language treating figures as legitimate apprentice-stage performing partners requiring serious technique and material care

format.value: A page that is a single bound spiral-flipbook of the LEFT side of a ventriloquist's act transcript only — the human partner's side of a 14-page dialogue, with the dummy's side of every exchange completely missing (no blank lines, no ellipses, no '[dummy responds]' stage directions, just the human's words running continuously as if no one else were ever speaking). Visitor flips the spiral pages by clicking the metal binding rings at the top, and at the bottom of each page a small mechanical jaw-clack sound plays at the exact moments where the dummy's missing line would have landed — the only evidence that anyone is replying

subject.value: A working ventriloquist named Doris Vellner performing her closing set at the 1979 Mid-Atlantic Figure-Maker's Convention in a Holiday Inn ballroom in Hagerstown, Maryland — her partner is a 40-year-old maple-headed figure named Mr. Tatum that her father carved in 1939, and the transcript captures the entire 14-page closing routine they have performed together for thirty-one years; the strangeness IS that you can only ever read Doris's half, because Mr. Tatum's voice belongs to Doris too and exists only in the small wooden silences between her lines, audible as jaw-clacks but never as words

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working performer voice — Doris speaking on stage to a room full of fellow ventriloquists who understand the craft, conversational mid-routine warmth with her wooden partner, genuine affection for Mr. Tatum visible in her phrasing and her pauses, present-tense engagement with a figure she has known longer than most of her marriages, no irony about ventriloquism as legitimate skilled performance art, peer-level respect for the audience as colleagues who will catch every micro-technique decision she makes, gentle laughter audible between lines, the unmistakable rhythm of someone who has timed these exact silences for three decades

palette.value: Hotel-ballroom carpet maroon (#7a2c2c) + warm spotlight amber on cream transcript paper (#f5e6c8) + deep maple-wood figure brown (#5c3a1e) + soft brass spiral-binding gold (#c4a657) + cool stage-shadow charcoal (#2a2a2a) for jaw-clack moment markers, evoking the specific atmosphere of a 1979 hotel ballroom convention room with overhead spotlights, the warm grain of a 40-year-old maple ventriloquist figure, and the tactile feel of a stenographer's spiral notebook holding a working performer's act

mandatory_element.value: Working spiral-binding click-to-flip mechanism (clicking either binding ring at top of the flipbook advances or retreats one page with a CSS page-curl animation showing the spiral rings holding the paper in place). Every page contains only Doris's half of the dialogue rendered in a typewritten monospace serif as if transcribed from tape, with consistent left-margin indent and no markers indicating missing dummy lines. At precisely the rhythmic moments where Mr. Tatum's reply would land (timed via embedded data attributes on Doris's lines), a Web Audio API hardwood jaw-clack plays automatically as the visitor reads — short, dry, two-stage attack-decay envelope evoking a hinged wooden jaw closing. Hovering any of Doris's lines reveals the embedded timing data as a faint amber dot count in the right margin (one dot per silent beat where Mr. Tatum's missing reply occupied). Exactly 7 pages of the 14 include a small handwritten margin note from Doris to herself in soft graphite pencil (technique reminders: 'soften the M here, his jaw sticks on M's,' 'wait three full beats — let him land it,' 'don't laugh at his own joke, you ALWAYS laugh') that fade in only after the visitor has lingered 6+ seconds on that page. Working 'LISTEN STRAIGHT THROUGH' button at the back cover plays the entire 14-page act's jaw-clack rhythm as a continuous 90-second percussion track with no Doris voice, no human voice, only the wooden cadence of Mr. Tatum's missing replies — pure unaccompanied dummy rhythm, the act as performed by him alone in a room with no partner. All visitor progress, lingered-page notes discovered, and final-track plays persist in localStorage

wildcard.value: Exactly 7 of the 14 pages contain Doris's hidden marginal pencil notes (as described above). Additionally, one specific marginal note, when read aloud or parsed carefully line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about wood, breath, and the silence between two voices that have known each other for thirty-one years, disguised naturally within an earnest technique reminder. After the visitor plays the 'LISTEN STRAIGHT THROUGH' jaw-clack track to completion three separate times across visits, a hidden final blank page appears at the very back of the flipbook with only a single graphite line in Doris's hand: 'he carved him with his eyes open so he could watch me work'

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO horror-genre framing of ventriloquist dummies as creepy/uncanny/possessed/sinister — this is the single most overused cultural treatment of figures and is hard-banned. NO 'the dummy is actually alive' twist, NO suggestion that Mr. Tatum is anything other than a beloved 40-year-old piece of carved maple that Doris's father made for her. NO museum-archival 'remember when ventriloquists toured the borscht belt' nostalgic detachment, NO cool ironic distance from vaudeville-era performance traditions as quaint or embarrassing, NO soft melancholy about 'the dying art of figure work,' NO retrospective elegy framing. NO SCP-foundation cryptic 'do not look at the figure' register. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose. This is LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled performance craft happening RIGHT NOW in a Holiday Inn ballroom in 1979 — Doris is on stage, the spotlights are warm, the room is full of working ventriloquists watching her timing with professional respect, and Mr. Tatum is a wooden partner she has loved and worked with longer than most relationships last. Treat ventriloquism as serious skilled labor, treat the convention as genuine craft community, treat the missing dummy lines as a structural fact of the medium and not as horror

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1978 — peak American supermarket private-label canned-goods era, when regional grocery chains contracted with small Midwestern canneries to produce their store-brand vegetables, soups, and fruits, and the specific four-color offset lithography used on tin-can labels represented a genuine commercial-art discipline; small studios in Cincinnati and St. Louis employed working illustrators who hand-painted gouache renderings of green beans, peach halves, and tomato soup steaming in ceramic bowls, with the painted originals photographed, color-separated, and printed onto thin tinplated steel sheets that were rolled and seamed into the cans stacked on grocery shelves across the country

format.value: A single oversized wraparound paper label peeled from a 1978 store-brand can of cream-style sweet corn (Vernon's Thrift Mart house brand, packed by Holcomb Canning Co. of Defiance, Ohio), unrolled flat and pinned at the corners so the visitor sees the entire 360-degree label as one continuous horizontal strip — front panel with hand-painted gouache illustration of steaming corn in a yellow ceramic bowl on a blue gingham cloth, side panel with the brand wordmark and net-weight statement, back panel with the printed recipe ('Holcomb's Skillet Corn Chowder — Serves Six'), nutrition information panel, ingredients list, the manufacturer's address and zip code, the recycled-tin-plate union bug, and along the very bottom edge a thin band of fine print containing the can-coding date stamp legend that explains how to read the embossed lot code

subject.value: Vernon's Thrift Mart Cream-Style Sweet Corn, 15.25 oz, packed for Vernon's by Holcomb Canning Co. of Defiance, Ohio in late August 1978 from the second-pick harvest of three contracted family farms in Paulding and Putnam counties — the specific commercial intelligence of a regional house-brand can label as a working commercial document combining illustration, copywriting, nutritional disclosure, recipe development, regulatory compliance, and union-shop identification on a single sheet of lithographed paper roughly 3.5 by 11 inches; the gouache painter's name (Mae Coronado, freelance, paid eighty-five dollars for the bowl-and-gingham original), the copywriter's recipe (developed in the Holcomb test kitchen by Edna Kohler, who insisted on a half-teaspoon of celery seed), and the specific dignity of an everyday object designed by named people to be held in someone's hand at a grocery shelf and chosen over the national brand because it cost eleven cents less and tasted just as good

tone.value: Warm, focused house-brand product-manager voice — Lloyd Vernon Jr., second-generation owner of a twelve-store regional grocery chain headquartered in Lima, Ohio, walking the visitor along the unrolled label panel by panel the way he'd walk a new shelf-stocker through what every part of the label means and why it's there; genuine pride in the regional canning relationship with Holcomb, peer-level respect for Mae's gouache illustration as real commercial art, earnest enthusiasm for Edna's chowder recipe, matter-of-fact appreciation for the printers' union bug as a working labor mark; present-tense, no irony about house-brand groceries as legitimate commercial infrastructure, no condescension toward shoppers who choose the store brand to save money, full warmth for the entire chain of named human work that produced this can

palette.value: Warm cream paper-label ground (#f3e6c4) + Vernon's signature deep tomato red wordmark and banner (#b8312a) + steaming-corn gouache yellow (#e8b347) + soft kitchen gingham blue (#5a7fa8) + Holcomb cannery forest-green seal accent (#2d5a2e) + faint ballpoint-pen annotation ink (#3a4a5a) for Lloyd's handwritten margin notes pointing out details

mandatory_element.value: Working horizontal unrolled-label layout filling the viewport width with the visitor scrolling left-to-right (mouse wheel, trackpad, or arrow keys) through six clearly delineated label panels in order: (1) Front Panel with the gouache corn-bowl illustration rendered in CSS/SVG, (2) Side Panel with Vernon's wordmark and net-weight statement, (3) Back Panel with the printed Skillet Corn Chowder recipe, (4) Nutrition Information Panel formatted in the period-correct 1978 FDA layout, (5) Ingredients & Manufacturer Panel with the Defiance, Ohio address and union bug, (6) Coding-Stamp Legend strip along the bottom. Hovering any of the six panels reveals a soft amber spotlight and a handwritten ballpoint-pen margin annotation from Lloyd Vernon Jr. fading in (e.g., 'Mae repainted the steam three times to get the rise right'; 'Edna fought us on the celery seed for two weeks and won'; 'the embossed code reads PLANT-DAY-SHIFT — ours says 04-241-B, second shift, August 29'). A working CAN-CODE DECODER input at the far right of the strip lets the visitor type any five-character lot code (format DD-NNN-S, e.g., 04-241-B) and decode it into plant number, Julian day, and shift letter — outputs the decoded date in plain English and adds a visitor-decoded code to a persistent localStorage list displayed below the strip. A working RECIPE PRINTOUT button on the Back Panel prints the Skillet Corn Chowder recipe formatted as a 3x5 index card (CSS print stylesheet) with Edna Kohler's name and the Holcomb Canning Co. test-kitchen credit line. Visitor scroll position, decoded codes, and which margin annotations have been discovered all persist in localStorage across visits.

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Lloyd's six margin annotations, when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5) about late-summer corn, named hands, and a tin can sealed in August, disguised naturally inside earnest grocer's pride. Additionally, after the visitor has decoded their seventh distinct can-code in the CAN-CODE DECODER (tracked across visits in localStorage), a hidden seventh panel quietly appears at the very end of the unrolled strip — a small square paper sticker that Lloyd has affixed to the back of the label with a single handwritten line in his ballpoint hand: 'Mae passed in '83 and we kept her bowl on every can for nineteen more years.'

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when grocery stores had house brands' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of hand-painted can labels,' NO cool ironic distance from 1970s supermarket culture as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing of regional canneries as already-vanished. NO condescension toward store-brand groceries as inferior or toward thrift-conscious shoppers as pitiable. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE regional grocery and commercial-art infrastructure happening RIGHT NOW in 1978 — Vernon's Thrift Mart is open for business, Holcomb Canning is running second-shift in Defiance tonight, Mae Coronado is at her drafting table painting next year's green-bean illustration, and Edna Kohler is testing a new lima-bean casserole recipe in the Holcomb test kitchen. Treat house-brand can-label production as serious skilled labor, treat the named workers as legitimate working artists and food professionals, treat the eleven-cent price difference as genuinely meaningful to real shoppers, and treat the entire object — paper label, painted illustration, recipe, regulatory text, union bug — as a working commercial document that deserves earnest attention.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1962 — peak American mechanical pinball golden age, when electromechanical pinball machines manufactured by Gottlieb, Williams, and Bally in Chicago factories represented the apex of pre-electronic arcade engineering, with relay-driven score reels, chime-bar music, hand-painted backglass artwork commissioned from working commercial illustrators, and stenciled playfield insert lighting all built on solid plywood cabinets that weighed three hundred pounds and were shipped by rail to corner taverns, bowling alleys, and bus station waiting rooms across the country, where they cost five cents per play and generated genuine craft fascination among players who learned to nudge the cabinet just hard enough to influence ball trajectory without triggering the tilt mechanism.

format.value: A working playable single-flipper miniature pinball table rendered in 2D top-down view filling the viewport: real ball physics (gravity, momentum, restitution, friction) simulated via Verlet integration in canvas, one spring-loaded plunger on the right side that the visitor pulls back by clicking-and-holding (visible spring compression and release power gauge) to launch the steel ball up the lane, one large flipper at the bottom controlled by holding the spacebar (flipper rotates with realistic angular momentum), three pop-bumpers that knock the ball back with chime-bar sounds and increment the score, two rollover lanes at the top that light up stencil-letter inserts spelling the cabinet's name, a captive-ball saucer that holds the ball for two seconds and ejects it with a bonus, and a drain at the bottom that costs the visitor one of three balls. The hand-painted backglass artwork is rendered as a parallax decorative element above the playfield, and a working three-digit relay-driven score reel sits in the upper-right corner that physically rolls digit-by-digit with mechanical click sounds as points accumulate. Real game, real physics, real flipper timing — playable until the visitor drains all three balls, then a GAME OVER plaque slides down with the final score and a NICKEL IN button to play again.

subject.value: a single specific 1962 Gottlieb four-player pinball machine called 'Skylark' — manufactured in their Chicago plant on West Kostner Avenue, designed by playfield artist Roy Parker and game-engineer Wayne Neyens, with a hand-painted backglass depicting a young woman in a blue dress standing at the edge of a cornfield at dusk releasing a single white meadowlark into a sky streaked with violet sunset clouds, the title 'SKYLARK' rendered in hand-stenciled gothic display type across the top, the playfield laid out with three pop-bumpers named LARK / NEST / FIELD, two rollover lanes spelling S-K-Y, and a captive-ball saucer in the upper-left corner labeled DAWN; installed in March 1962 at Verlin's Tap on Route 6 outside Princeton, Illinois, where it lived for nineteen years next to the cigarette machine and was played by farmers waiting out rain, traveling salesmen killing forty minutes, and one teenage girl named Linda Verlin who learned every nudge angle in the cabinet and could routinely run the score reel past 999 (which only displayed three digits, so the high score wrapped, which Linda found genuinely funny)

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working game-engineer voice — Wayne Neyens describing the table to the visitor the way he'd describe a new playfield design to a fellow engineer on the Gottlieb factory floor in 1962: present-tense respect for the relay logic and chime-bar music as legitimate skilled craft, earnest pride in Roy Parker's backglass painting as real commercial art, matter-of-fact warmth about pinball as serious recreational infrastructure for working people, peer-level enthusiasm for the visitor learning the flipper timing, no irony about coin-operated amusement as legitimate engineering. Subtle delight when the visitor lands the captive-ball saucer or runs the score reel past 999.

palette.value: Warm Gottlieb-cabinet wood-grain mahogany (#5c3a1e) for the cabinet frame + cream-yellow stenciled playfield base (#f3e6c4) + Roy Parker backglass sunset violet (#7a5a9c) + corn-field gold (#e8b347) for the meadowlark wing and stencil inserts + Linda Verlin's blue dress cobalt (#3a5a8c) for the central backglass figure + chime-bar polished brass (#c4a657) for the pop-bumper caps and score-reel digits + relay-contact charcoal (#2a2a2a) for the cabinet rails and tilt-bob shadow

mandatory_element.value: Fully working playable pinball game with: (1) real Verlet-integration ball physics with gravity, momentum, restitution, and friction, (2) click-and-hold plunger with visible spring compression and a power-gauge that determines launch velocity, (3) spacebar-controlled single flipper with realistic angular rotation and momentum-transfer to the ball, (4) three pop-bumpers (LARK/NEST/FIELD) that knock the ball back with Web Audio API chime-bar tones (warm bell-like sine tones at three distinct pitches — E5, G5, B5) and add 10 points each, (5) two rollover lanes at the top of the playfield that light up sequential S-K-Y stencil-letter inserts (each completed SKY adds 100 points and re-lights), (6) DAWN captive-ball saucer that holds the ball for exactly 2 seconds with a soft saucer-hum and ejects with a 50-point bonus, (7) drain at the bottom that costs a ball (visitor starts with 3 balls), (8) working three-digit relay-driven score reel in the upper-right that physically rolls each digit with click sounds via CSS transforms (wraps at 999 — past 999 the reel resets to 0 and a small 'WRAP' indicator lights, honoring Linda Verlin's discovery), (9) tilt mechanism: holding arrow keys nudges the cabinet (visible cabinet shake + ball trajectory influence) but excessive nudging within 2 seconds triggers a TILT relay that disables the flipper for the rest of the current ball, (10) GAME OVER plaque slides down with final score and NICKEL IN button to restart, persisting the visitor's high score in localStorage as 'HIGH SCORE' displayed on the backglass apron alongside the initials 'LV 999' (Linda Verlin's permanent factory-record high) which the visitor can only displace by wrapping the reel themselves.

wildcard.value: After the visitor wraps the score reel past 999 for the first time across visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden moment occurs: the backglass figure of Linda's young woman in the blue dress turns her head exactly one frame (a single subtle SVG transform) to look directly out at the visitor, the meadowlark in her cupped hands briefly flutters its painted wings once, and the high-score apron updates to read the visitor's initials above 'LV 999 — bested at last' in Wayne Neyens's handwritten ballpoint. Additionally, exactly one of Wayne's three engineer's-margin notes that appear when the visitor hovers the cabinet rails, when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5) about steel, relay-clicks, and a girl who learned the nudge in a tavern on Route 6 — disguised naturally within earnest engineering pride.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when bars had pinball machines' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of electromechanical pinball,' NO cool ironic distance from 1962 tavern culture as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy for coin-op amusement as already-vanished. NO condescension toward pinball as unserious recreation or toward Linda Verlin as a wistful small-town anecdote. NO horror-genre framing of the backglass figure turning her head as creepy/uncanny — it's a warm wink, not a jump-scare. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled coin-op engineering and tavern recreational infrastructure happening RIGHT NOW in 1962 — Gottlieb's Chicago plant is running second shift tonight, Roy Parker is at his easel painting next month's backglass, Wayne Neyens is troubleshooting a relay bank on the workbench, and Verlin's Tap is open with the Skylark machine glowing in the corner waiting for a nickel. Treat pinball engineering as serious skilled labor, treat the named workers as legitimate working professionals, and treat the visitor's flipper-timing learning curve as a real craft worth respecting.

length.description: something to explore, multiple sections, interactive elements

era.value: 1958 — peak American newspaper Sunday-comics-section puzzle-page era, when broadsheet metro dailies (the Cleveland Plain Dealer, the Pittsburgh Press, the Buffalo Courier-Express) ran a dedicated full-page Sunday puzzle spread in the back of the comics section, hand-set in lead type by overnight composing-room printers, illustrated by staff cartoonists pulling double duty on the Dick Tracy and Brenda Starr panels, and inked with the same four-color Sunday rotogravure process that gave the funnies their soft saturated reds, mustards, and dot-screen flesh tones; the puzzle page was a genuine craft assignment trusted to senior puzzle editors who were expected to construct logic problems that would survive a coffee-stained kitchen-table workout from a steelworker reading on Sunday morning, with the solution printed upside-down at the bottom of next week's page so families would have to wait seven full days to confirm whether they'd cracked it

format.value: A genuine solvable in-browser logic-grid puzzle rendered as a single Sunday-comics-section puzzle-page tear-sheet from the November 16, 1958 edition of the Lakeshore Sentinel-Dispatch — the visitor sees a 5×5 logic grid (five fishermen, five fly-pattern names, five lake-trout weights in pounds, with the names and weights along the top row and the fishermen names down the left column, two of the three categories present as a single nested-axis grid in the classic Sunday-paper layout), six numbered clue sentences printed beneath the grid in justified two-column setting under the headline 'WHO LANDED THE LUNKER? — A Five-Way Tangle for the Patient Solver' (e.g., 'Mr. Hollenbeck never uses any fly with feathers from a rooster.' 'The angler who tied on the Royal Coachman pulled in three pounds heavier than the one who fished the Adams.' etc.), each grid cell is a real clickable toggle that cycles through three states (blank → green checkmark → red X → blank) with each cell click playing a soft pencil-scratch sound, and the visitor solves by deduction in the grid — when the deduction is complete and consistent, the grid auto-detects the unique solution and slides aside to reveal the inked staff-cartoonist illustration printed underneath of all five fishermen standing on the pier with their catches, plus a small COMPOSING-ROOM REVEAL button (initially dimmed, brightens only after solution is correct) that flips the page upside-down to show next Sunday's printed answer key set in lead type. A SHOW ME ONE CLUE I'VE ALREADY SATISFIED hint button is available but never gives away unsolved logic — it only confirms work already done

subject.value: The fictional fifth annual Lake Sandusky Pier-and-Fly Anglers' Invitational of October 1958, in which five rod-and-reel men (Mr. Hollenbeck of the Sandusky Plumbers' Local 235, Mr. Beauchamp who ran the Vermilion Avenue barbershop, Mr. Czerwinski who taught seventh-grade arithmetic at St. Stanislaus, Mr. Pike who genuinely was named Pike and worked the freight desk at the B&O rail yard, and Mr. Quill the funeral-home organist) fished from the municipal pier at sunrise on a cold gray Saturday, each tying on exactly one hand-tied fly pattern (Royal Coachman, Quill Gordon, Adams, Hendrickson, Light Cahill) and landing exactly one lake trout of distinct weight (3, 5, 7, 9, or 11 pounds) before the noon weigh-in — a real workable logic puzzle constructed by Mrs. Eunice Tarrant, the Sentinel-Dispatch's puzzle editor for the past nine years, who handcrafted six interlocking clue sentences over the course of three evenings at her kitchen table in Sandusky's east end and personally test-solved it twice before sending it down to composing on Friday afternoon
