tone.value: Warm, focused Sunday-puzzle-editor voice — Mrs. Eunice Tarrant in the headnote and the soft margin asides, peer-to-peer with the visitor as a fellow Sunday-morning solver, genuine pride in the six interlocking clues she spent three evenings constructing at her kitchen table, full earnest respect for the craft of logic-grid construction and the seriousness of the visitor's deductive work, conversational warmth in the printed solver's-note at the bottom of the page ('mind that one of the clues works backward — it tells you who DIDN'T tie on the Adams, which is just as good'), present-tense Saturday-afternoon-at-the-composing-room steadiness, no irony about Sunday newspaper puzzles as legitimate intellectual recreation, no condescension toward the steelworker who'll solve this Sunday morning over coffee, real affection for the five fictional fishermen as if she's known their families for years

palette.value: Aged newsprint cream ground (#f1e4c4) with subtle visible paper-fiber texture + Sunday-rotogravure muted brick-red headline ink (#a83c2a) + deep composing-room black body text (#1a1612) + soft mustard-yellow grid-cell highlight when the visitor hovers a cell (#d4a547) + warm pencil-graphite gray for checkmark/X marks (#4a4540) + faint dot-screen flesh-tone pink (#e8b8a8) for the illustrated fishermen reveal beneath the grid + cool composing-room lead-slug silver (#9ea3a8) as the underlying answer-key page accent — evoking the specific tactile feel of a 1958 Sunday comics-section tear-sheet folded in quarters and read across a kitchen table, the soft offset registration of four-color rotogravure printing on cheap newsprint stock, and the distinct typographic darkness of Linotype-set body copy

mandatory_element.value: Fully working 5×5 logic-grid puzzle with: (1) a clickable grid where each of 25 cells cycles through three states (blank → ✓ → ✗ → blank) with a soft pencil-scratch sound per click, (2) six interlocking deductive clue sentences printed beneath the grid that lead to a unique correct solution discoverable by pure logic without guessing, (3) real-time solution-state validation that detects when the visitor has placed all checkmarks consistently with the clue set and lights a small RIGHT ON, SOLVER plaque in the lower-right corner of the page when the deduction is fully correct, (4) a COMPOSING-ROOM REVEAL button that brightens only after the puzzle is correctly solved, which when pressed slides the grid aside (CSS transform) to expose the inked staff-cartoonist illustration of all five fishermen on the pier with their catches and weights labeled in hand-lettered captions, plus the upside-down printed answer key from next Sunday's page rendered in vertically-flipped lead-type setting, (5) a SHOW ME ONE CLUE I'VE ALREADY SATISFIED hint button that scans the visitor's current grid state and highlights one clue sentence in soft amber that the current grid satisfies (never reveals new logic, only confirms work already done), (6) localStorage persistence so a returning visitor who closed the page mid-solve resumes with their partial grid intact, and (7) a small CLEAR THE GRID AND START FRESH link at the bottom for visitors who want to restart from a clean slate

wildcard.value: Exactly one of the six clue sentences, when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about cold pier mornings, hand-tied feathers, and the patience of waiting for the rod to bend — disguised naturally within an earnest logic-puzzle clue that the visitor must actually USE to solve the grid, so that the haiku is doing double duty as both functional deductive infrastructure and hidden Mrs. Tarrant signature. Additionally, after a returning visitor has solved three distinct logic puzzles across visits (tracked in localStorage), a small hidden margin note in Mrs. Tarrant's handwriting fades in beside the headline reading 'i build these in pencil first, then in ink — you're getting quicker, friend'

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when newspapers ran puzzle pages' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the Sunday logic grid,' NO cool ironic distance from 1950s newspaper puzzle culture as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing of composing-room labor or Linotype typesetting as already-vanished. NO condescension toward puzzle-solvers as casual or toward Mrs. Tarrant's nine years as puzzle editor as a small wistful career. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled newspaper puzzle-editor craft and Sunday-morning kitchen-table intellectual recreation happening RIGHT NOW in November 1958 — the Sentinel-Dispatch composing room is running Saturday overnight, Mrs. Tarrant is at her kitchen table in Sandusky's east end test-solving the grid one more time, the four-color rotogravure presses will roll at 2am, and a hundred thousand readers will open the comics section Sunday morning and turn to the back page with a freshly sharpened pencil. Treat logic-puzzle construction as serious skilled craft, treat Mrs. Tarrant as a legitimate working puzzle editor with nine years of professional expertise, and treat the visitor's deductive work as real intellectual labor worthy of full peer-to-peer respect

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1888 — peak Victorian-era kaleidoscope-and-magic-lantern parlor amusement culture, the specific moment when middle-class parlors in industrial English and American towns kept hand-held kaleidoscopes on the side table alongside stereoscopes and zoetropes as legitimate optical-toy infrastructure for after-dinner family entertainment; brass-and-leather instruments hand-assembled by small optical workshops in Birmingham and Rochester, mirrors precisely angled by master polishers, glass-fragment cells filled with hand-cut colored chips dropped one by one into oil-suspension chambers; an entire mass-craft tradition of patented mirror geometries, subscription chip-refill services, and serious published treatises on symmetry as moral education for children, when watching a turning pattern was treated as genuine contemplative sight-training and parents kept catalogs of favored mirror-angle ratios the way later families would keep recipe boxes

format.value: Full-viewport silent generative piece: a continuously-turning twelve-fold mirror-symmetric kaleidoscope pattern fills the entire browser window, built in pure SVG with a single triangular wedge of slowly-drifting irregular polygonal glass chips (translucent layered shapes with soft refractive edges) reflected and rotated into a complete symmetric mandala that rotates at roughly 1.5 degrees per second. The chips inside the wedge drift, settle, occasionally tumble against one another with subtle parallax depth, and the cursor's position across the viewport very gently nudges the rate and direction of the wedge's internal chip-drift (cursor near the rim = chips drift outward; cursor near center = chips settle inward) but the rotation itself continues steadily regardless. No UI, no controls, no text, no captions, no buttons — the page IS the brass instrument held to the window, the visitor's gaze IS the only input, and the pattern continuously rearranges itself forever without ever repeating exactly

subject.value: A specific brass-and-rosewood twelve-mirror parlor kaleidoscope built by Hollingworth & Sons Optical Works of Birmingham in autumn 1888, catalog model No. 47 'The Drawing-Room Symmetricon,' with hand-polished first-surface silvered mirrors set at precisely 15-degree angles by foreman polisher Mr. Albert Crouch (who had served his apprenticeship under the Brewster-trained master George Lithgow), and an oil-suspension chip chamber refilled monthly through Hollingworth's subscription service with a fresh assortment of hand-cut colored Bohemian glass fragments, opalescent shell flakes, and the occasional tiny pressed botanical specimen — the instrument as it would be held up to the bay-window light of a parlor on Edgbaston Crescent on a slow Sunday afternoon in October, turned slowly between the fingers of a child named Beatrice Whitcomb who has been told by her mother that watching the patterns trains the eye toward beauty and the mind toward order, and who has discovered on her own that the pattern is never the same twice and that this is the most interesting thing in the entire house

tone.value: No tone — there is no narrator, no voice, no text visible on the page at any point. The piece speaks only through the continuous patient turning of mirrored glass. The closest analogue to a voice is the silent attentiveness of Beatrice herself holding the brass tube to the window light: present-tense, unhurried, entirely absorbed, neither performing nor explaining, the visual equivalent of a child's complete unselfconscious gaze into an optical instrument on a Sunday afternoon

palette.value: Deep warm rosewood-and-brass instrument-interior darkness (#1a0f08) as the field surrounding the pattern + translucent Bohemian glass cobalt (#2c5aa0 at 0.65 opacity) + opalescent shell-flake pearl (#f0e8d8 at 0.55 opacity) + hand-cut cranberry-glass rose (#a83048 at 0.6 opacity) + warm amber bottle-glass honey (#d49538 at 0.55 opacity) + pale absinthe-glass jade (#5a8870 at 0.5 opacity) + the warm Sunday-window light coming through the chip chamber as a faint golden cast (#f5d89a at 0.2 opacity) glowing softly from the pattern's center outward, evoking the specific luminescence of colored glass fragments suspended in oil and lit from behind by an October parlor window

mandatory_element.value: A fully working continuously-rotating twelve-fold mirror-symmetric kaleidoscope rendered in pure SVG: (1) a single triangular wedge containing minimum 18 independently-drifting translucent polygonal glass-chip shapes of varying sizes, colors, and opacities, each with its own slow drift velocity and rotation rate driven by requestAnimationFrame, (2) the wedge reflected and rotated twelve times around the center point using SVG <use> elements with transform rotations to produce a perfect twelve-fold symmetric mandala, (3) continuous slow rotation of the entire mandala at approximately 1.5 degrees per second that never stops and never resets, (4) cursor-position-based subtle influence on the internal chip-drift direction and rate (cursor distance from center modulates whether chips drift outward toward the rim or settle inward toward the center, but rotation continues unaffected), (5) subtle parallax depth where chips occasionally overlap with soft refractive edge-blending via SVG filters giving the impression of layered glass thickness, (6) absolutely no text, no UI, no captions, no titles, no buttons, no controls, no instructions, no footer credits anywhere on the page — the entire viewport is filled by the mandala against the deep rosewood-instrument-interior field, and the visitor's only input is where they place their cursor

wildcard.value: Exactly once every 11 minutes (deterministic, not random — tied to the page's elapsed time since load), a single tiny pressed-botanical chip drifts briefly into the wedge from the rim — a recognizable pale-violet forget-me-not petal silhouette, slightly more solid and opaque than the surrounding glass fragments — and traverses the wedge over approximately 40 seconds before settling against the central axis and dissolving back into the chip field. This is Beatrice's mother's small private addition to the monthly chip refill (she presses one forget-me-not from the garden border every September and slips it into the chamber before sending it back to Hollingworth's for the new oil), and the visitor is given no indication this is happening, no acknowledgment, no reward for noticing — just the quiet fact that one of the chips in the pattern was a flower once

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO text, captions, labels-explaining-the-piece, title overlays, instructional UI, tooltip hints, loading messages, footer credits, or any narrating voice anywhere on the page visible to the visitor — the piece must be entirely silent and entirely visual. NO museum-archival 'remember when parlors had kaleidoscopes' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of optical-toy craftsmanship,' NO cool ironic distance from Victorian parlor amusement as quaint. NO horror-genre framing of the rotating pattern as hypnotic/sinister/uncanny — it is warm Sunday-window light through colored glass, not a trap. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register. NO weather-app or app-interface conventions, NO settings panels, NO color-pickers, NO speed sliders. The kaleidoscope exists, it turns, and the visitor watches in silence — that is the entire piece

length.description: one screen, no scroll, complete on landing

era.value: 1937 — peak American depression-era door-to-door encyclopedia and household-goods salesman culture, the specific moment when traveling salesmen carrying small leatherette sample cases walked the residential blocks of small Indiana and Iowa towns offering subscription sets of encyclopedias, vacuum cleaners, sewing machines, and the lesser-known novelty: the printed mail-order seed packet line of the regional Burpee competitors. A working salesman's small printed door-hanger left tucked into the screen-door handle when the lady of the house wasn't home — a single tan-paper folded card roughly 3 inches by 7 inches, hand-stamped with the salesman's route number and personally annotated in indelible pencil with the date he called, what he was carrying, and a note inviting her to call the number at the boarding house where he could be reached if she'd like him to come back Thursday afternoon. The specific small dignity of a printed door-hanger as both legitimate commercial instrument and quiet handwritten one-to-one correspondence between a working salesman and a homemaker he hopes to meet next week.

format.value: A single tan-paper folded door-hanger card from a 1937 traveling seed-and-household-novelty salesman, hung on the screen door of 412 Mulberry Street, Crawfordsville, Indiana, on the afternoon of Tuesday, April 6, 1937 — the card unfolds across the viewport showing the printed face (the company's name and seed-line offerings in two-color letterpress, a small ornamented border, a printed greeting with blank lines for personalization, and a printed footer with the boarding-house telephone exchange) plus the salesman's own indelible-pencil annotations filling the blank lines and margins in his small careful hand: his name, his route number, the date and exact time he called, which seed packets and small household goods he was carrying in his case that day, a brief handwritten note about why he thought Mrs. Halverson at this address might be interested (he remembers her sister in Lafayette ordering the calendula packets last spring), and a polite invitation to call the boarding-house exchange before Thursday noon if she'd like him to return.

subject.value: Mr. Earl Pettigrew of Pettigrew & Sons Mail-Order Seed and Novelty Company of Terre Haute, Indiana, working his Wabash Valley spring route in April 1937, leaving a single hand-personalized door-hanger card on the screen door of Mrs. Cora Halverson at 412 Mulberry Street, Crawfordsville, after she did not answer his afternoon knock; the specific small economy of a six-cent printed door-hanger as serious legitimate sales instrument for a working salesman during the seventh year of the Depression, paid for out of his commission account, carried in a small leatherette case alongside his sample packets of marigold, calendula, zinnia, and bush bean seeds, his demonstration handkerchief-folder, and his order book — the entire careful infrastructure of one human walking residential blocks in the rain trying to make a living writing personal notes to women he hopes will be home next Thursday.

tone.value: Warm, careful, peer-to-peer working salesman voice — Earl Pettigrew at his small writing-leaf in the boarding-house parlor on Sunday evening preparing the week's door-hanger stock with personalized annotations, then in present-tense on the front porch of 412 Mulberry pulling out his indelible pencil and filling in the blanks while standing on the welcome mat; genuine respect for Mrs. Halverson as a potential customer whose sister he remembered from a previous route, earnest pride in the seed line and the small household novelties as legitimate useful goods at fair prices, peer-level warmth with the visitor as if they were a fellow salesman comparing route notes at the boarding-house breakfast table on Monday morning. Present-tense, not retrospective. No irony about door-to-door selling as legitimate skilled work, no condescension toward Depression-era household budgets, full earnest belief that Mrs. Halverson might genuinely want the calendula packets and that calling the exchange before Thursday noon is a reasonable thing to ask.

palette.value: Warm tan kraft door-hanger paper (#d6b888) as the card stock + deep two-color letterpress forest-green company wordmark and ornamental border ink (#2d5a2e) + warm brick-red accent ink (#a83c2a) for the seed-line headings and emphasis + Earl's indelible-pencil annotation ink in soft graphite-purple (#4a4060) for all the handwritten personalization filling the blanks and margins + faint screen-door wire-mesh shadow cast across the card in cool gray (#6e6a64 at 0.15 opacity) suggesting the card is genuinely tucked into the spring-loaded handle of the front door + a warm afternoon-spring-light cast (#f4d89e at 0.18 opacity) across the lower-right corner of the card suggesting the angle of the April sun on the west-facing porch at 3:40pm

mandatory_element.value: A working hover-reveal mechanic where each of Earl's handwritten indelible-pencil annotations on the door-hanger card (his name, his route number, the date and arrival time, the listed seed packets and small goods he was carrying that day, the note about Mrs. Halverson's sister in Lafayette, the polite return-invitation, the boarding-house exchange and the hours he can be reached, the small p.s. about her hydrangeas being lovely from the sidewalk) reveals a soft amber spotlight on hover and fades in a peer-level annotation in the right margin from Earl himself explaining the small commercial or personal reasoning behind that particular handwritten detail (e.g., 'I write the time so she knows I came in daylight and not after supper hours, which would be presumptuous'; 'the Lafayette note is true — Mrs. Vossberg ordered four packets and a paring knife last March and I keep my route book up to date about family connections'; 'the hydrangeas line I almost didn't write because it could be read as too forward, but I have left it because they really were lovely from the sidewalk'). A working REQUEST A RETURN VISIT form on the card lets the visitor enter their name, the seed packets or small goods they would like to see, and a preferred day and time, which when submitted adds a typewritten ledger entry to a persistent localStorage list displayed below the card titled 'Mrs. Halverson's Replies — Pettigrew Route Book, April 1937' showing all the visitor's submissions with calculated 'Earl will return on...' dates. A working CALL THE EXCHANGE button at the footer animates a small rotary-dial telephone graphic dialing the boarding-house number (Crawfordsville Exchange 247-W) with the appropriate clicking sounds via Web Audio API and reveals a small reply card showing Earl's boarding-house landlady Mrs. Brindle answering and taking a message in her own distinctly different handwriting. Visitor scroll position, revealed annotations, submitted return-requests, and exchange-calls all persist in localStorage across visits.

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Earl's marginal hover-reveal annotations, when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about rain on a porch, a screen-door spring, and a woman who is not home — disguised naturally within an earnest working-salesman explanation. Additionally, after the visitor has submitted their fifth distinct return-visit request across visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden seventh annotation permanently appears in the lower-right corner of the card in Earl's pencil hand: 'Mrs. Halverson did call the exchange Thursday morning — she took the calendula and the two packets of bush bean and we sat on the porch twenty minutes and her hydrangeas were even finer up close.' This unlock is not announced and not described in any UI text; it simply exists as a small quiet fact added to the door-hanger for visitors who kept coming back.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when salesmen walked door-to-door' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of personal commerce,' NO cool ironic distance from Depression-era sales work as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy for traveling salesmen as already-vanished. NO condescension toward door-to-door selling as exploitative or pitiable, NO smug modern superiority about 'imagine being interrupted at home by a salesman' framing. NO horror-genre framing of the unanswered door or the hand-personalized card as creepy/intrusive/stalkerish — Earl is a working salesman writing a polite note to a woman whose sister he genuinely remembers, not a threat. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled door-to-door sales craft happening RIGHT NOW in April 1937 — Earl is walking the Wabash Valley route this week, his boarding-house room at Mrs. Brindle's costs him three dollars a week, his commission account is healthy, and he genuinely believes Mrs. Halverson might want the calendula packets. Treat door-hanger personalization as serious skilled commercial labor, treat Earl as a legitimate working professional supporting himself through honest sales work, and treat the small dignity of his careful indelible-pencil annotations as worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1991 — peak American suburban summer-evening backyard hobby astronomy era for elementary-school-age kids, the very specific moment when a child between roughly seven and eleven would receive a small mass-market plastic-and-cardboard glow-in-the-dark constellation kit (the kind sold in the science aisle at Toys 'R' Us alongside the ant farms and the rock-tumbler refill packs) — a flimsy cardboard box containing a fold-out star chart of the northern summer sky, a sheet of die-cut adhesive-backed glow-in-the-dark plastic stars in five graduated sizes, a small folded instruction leaflet printed in two-color offset showing how to arrange them on a bedroom ceiling to match the major constellations, and a sealed wax-paper packet of phosphorescent green powder labeled 'CHARGE WITH FLASHLIGHT' — and the specific tactile ritual of a kid lying on their back on the bedroom carpet at 9:47pm in late July with the overhead light just turned off, watching their hand-arranged ceiling sky fade slowly from bright lime green to faint moss green to nothing across about forty minutes, learning Cassiopeia and the Big Dipper and Cygnus by patient repetition night after night, and discovering on their own that the brightest stars hold their glow the longest

format.value: A working interactive constellation-builder toy filling the viewport as the dark ceiling of a kid's bedroom seen looking straight up from the carpet (subtle suggestion of ceiling fan blades silhouetted in the corners, slight popcorn-texture stippling visible only in faint reflected light). The visitor is given a small floating tray of glow-in-the-dark adhesive stars in five sizes (TINY / SMALL / MEDIUM / LARGE / GIANT) at the bottom of the viewport, picks up a star by clicking-and-holding, drags it anywhere on the ceiling, releases to stick it (with a small soft thwip sound and a brief brighter flash as the adhesive sets), can pick up and reposition existing stars by click-dragging them again, and can peel stars off by right-clicking. A small CHARGE WITH FLASHLIGHT button on the side panel, when held down, sweeps a soft circular flashlight beam across the ceiling under the visitor's cursor — every star the beam passes over visibly brightens to peak phosphorescent intensity, and then over the next 30-90 seconds (depending on star size — bigger stars hold their charge much longer) slowly dims through a realistic phosphor-decay curve back toward darkness. A small FLIP THE SWITCH toggle in the corner instantly turns the bedroom overhead light back on (revealing the ceiling in full warm room-light, all the stars visible as flat green plastic shapes on white popcorn ceiling) or off (returning to the dark room where only charged stars glow). A second small panel labeled CONSTELLATION CARD lets the visitor click through a small flip-deck of period-correct two-color offset constellation reference cards (Big Dipper, Cassiopeia, Cygnus, Lyra, Scorpius, Orion-in-winter, the imaginary 'Refrigerator Hum' constellation a kid made up) and try to copy them onto their ceiling by eye. Everything the visitor places persists to localStorage forever — the bedroom ceiling is the SAME ceiling next visit, with the same kid's accumulated star arrangement and the same charged stars still fading from the last flashlight sweep if the visitor returns within a couple of minutes.

subject.value: A real 1991 child's bedroom ceiling in a split-level house on Larkspur Court in a Cincinnati suburb, belonging to a nine-year-old named Marisol Aguirre who got the constellation kit for her birthday in early July and has been working on the ceiling every night for three weeks; the specific patient hobby labor of a kid lying on her back on the carpet with the bedroom door closed and the hallway light shining under the gap, peeling each die-cut star off its backing sheet, climbing onto the desk chair to press the star onto the popcorn ceiling, climbing back down to check the alignment from the carpet, peeling it off and trying again two inches over; the way she has already done all of Cassiopeia and most of the Big Dipper and is currently working on Cygnus the Swan (she has the long neck and the left wingtip and is debating where exactly to put Albireo); the moment of clicking the bedroom light off and lying flat on the carpet and watching her sky bloom green and fade green and feeling like she is genuinely lying in a field somewhere very far from Larkspur Court; the visitor is invited to lie on her carpet beside her and add to her sky — the ceiling already has Marisol's stars on it when the visitor arrives, and any stars the visitor places stay there permanently as part of Marisol's ongoing project, and the next visitor to the page will see Marisol's stars and the previous visitor's stars together

tone.value: Warm, patient, peer-to-peer nine-year-old hobbyist voice — Marisol talking to the visitor the way she'd talk to her best friend Janelle who is sleeping over for the first time and is going to help with Cygnus tonight: matter-of-fact pride in the work she's already done (Cassiopeia took four whole evenings because she kept getting the zigzag wrong), genuine enthusiasm for the constellation card deck and the small instruction leaflet that came with the kit, peer-level warmth that treats the visitor as another kid who knows how to read a star chart, present-tense July-evening absorption in the project as serious important work, real practical knowledge about which stars charge fastest and hold their glow longest (the GIANTS are the best but you only get six of them and you have to save them for the brightest stars in each constellation), no irony about glow-in-the-dark plastic ceiling stars as legitimate astronomy, no condescension toward childhood hobby crafts, full earnest belief that lying on the carpet watching her ceiling fade is one of the best things a person can do with a Wednesday night

palette.value: Deep midnight-bedroom dark (#0a0e1a) as the unlit ceiling field with a barely-perceptible warm hallway-light bleed (#3a2e1a at 0.08 opacity) along the lower edge of the viewport suggesting the gap under the bedroom door + phosphorescent peak-charge lime green (#9ff04a) for freshly-flashlight-charged stars + mid-decay moss green (#5a8a3a) + faint final-fade ghost green (#2e3e2a) for stars about to disappear + warm room-light cream (#f5e8c4) as the ceiling color when the overhead light is on + soft popcorn-ceiling shadow stippling (#d8cdb0) visible only in room-light mode + flashlight beam warm tungsten gold (#fff0c4 at 0.4 opacity) with soft circular falloff + Marisol's two-color offset constellation card stock cream (#f1e4c4) with brick-red constellation-line ink (#a83c2a) and deep navy star-name typography (#1a2438), evoking the specific tactile feel of mass-market 1991 science-kit packaging, the lime-green glow of phosphorescent ceiling stars in actual lived darkness, and the warm bedroom-overhead-light quality of a suburban split-level house in midsummer

mandatory_element.value: A fully working stick-and-glow ceiling-star toy with: (1) a draggable star tray containing five sizes (TINY/SMALL/MEDIUM/LARGE/GIANT) that the visitor picks up by click-and-hold and places anywhere on the viewport ceiling with a soft thwip sound and brief brighter flash on adhesion, (2) existing placed stars are themselves click-draggable to reposition and right-clickable to peel off (with a tiny adhesive-tearing sound), (3) a CHARGE WITH FLASHLIGHT hold-button that activates a soft circular tungsten flashlight beam following the visitor's cursor — any star the beam passes over instantly brightens to peak phosphorescent intensity and then begins a realistic decay curve back to darkness over a duration scaled by star size (TINY decays in 30 seconds, GIANT decays in 90 seconds), with the brightness rendered via CSS filter opacity transitions and box-shadow glow halos, (4) a FLIP THE SWITCH toggle that instantly transitions the ceiling between dark-room mode (only charged stars visible against deep midnight ceiling with hallway-light bleed) and overhead-light-on mode (full warm room-light revealing all stars as flat green plastic shapes on popcorn-textured ceiling, flashlight beam disabled, all decay timers paused), (5) a CONSTELLATION CARD flip-deck panel showing minimum 8 period-correct two-color offset reference cards (Big Dipper, Little Dipper, Cassiopeia, Cygnus, Lyra, Scorpius, Orion, plus Marisol's invented 'Refrigerator Hum' constellation) that the visitor can flip through and use as visual reference, (6) localStorage persistence of EVERY placed star's position, size, and current charge level so that returning visitors see Marisol's ceiling exactly as it was left, and (7) cross-visitor accumulation — the ceiling is a SHARED canvas where stars placed by previous visitors persist alongside Marisol's original Cassiopeia and Big Dipper, with the Cygnus-in-progress section visibly incomplete (long neck and left wingtip placed, Albireo position empty) waiting for visitors to help finish, and a small running counter in the corner showing 'STARS ON MARISOL'S CEILING: [N]' incrementing with every visitor's addition

wildcard.value: Exactly one star somewhere on Marisol's pre-placed ceiling (a SMALL star, placed slightly off-pattern from any constellation, located approximately where Albireo would go if Cygnus were finished) holds its charge approximately seven times longer than physics would predict — it continues to glow at faint-but-visible intensity for ten full minutes after every other star has faded to total darkness, and during that long fade it very slowly drifts position by about three pixels in a random direction every 90 seconds, as if it is not actually stuck to the ceiling. The visitor is given no indication this is happening, no acknowledgment, no reward for noticing — just the quiet fact that one of the stars on Marisol's ceiling is something else. Additionally, exactly one of Marisol's spoken asides scattered across the page, when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about carpet, hallway light, and a ceiling that is also a sky — disguised naturally within an earnest nine-year-old's working narration about which star size charges fastest.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when kids had glow-in-the-dark stars on their ceilings' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost innocence of pre-internet childhood,' NO cool ironic distance from 1990s suburban kid culture as quaint, NO retrospective elegy framing this as 'a memory from before everything changed.' NO 'where are they now' wistfulness about Marisol grown up. NO horror-genre framing of the off-physics star or the slowly-drifting Albireo position as creepy/uncanny/sinister/haunted — it is a small quiet mystery in a kid's bedroom, not a ghost. NO SCP-cryptic register about the anomalous star. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding. NO adult-narrator-looking-back tone. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE nine-year-old hobby astronomy happening RIGHT NOW on Larkspur Court in July 1991 — Marisol is on the carpet RIGHT NOW, the flashlight is in her hand RIGHT NOW, the ceiling fan is on low because it's hot, her mom is downstairs watching Jeopardy, and the Cygnus project is genuinely the most important thing happening in her life this week. Treat ceiling-star hobby work as serious skilled craft, treat Marisol as a legitimate working amateur astronomer, treat the visitor as another kid invited onto the carpet to help with the swan.

length.description: something to explore, multiple sections, interactive elements

era.value: 1973 — peak American supermarket-tabloid puzzle-page era, the specific moment when grocery-checkout-aisle weekly tabloids like the National Tattler, the Midnight Globe, and the lesser-known Buckeye Weekly Inquirer ran a back-page 'Word Detective' column hand-set by overnight typesetters in cheap dropout-screen halftones on pulp newsprint stock; a thriving subculture of working-class home solvers who clipped the puzzles out with kitchen scissors, tucked them between the toaster and the wall, and worked them across a week of breakfast coffees using stubby golf pencils kept in the spoon drawer — a genuine printed-puzzle infrastructure for people who would never call themselves intellectuals but who took genuine pride in cracking a hard one before their husband or wife got the chance

format.value: A working in-browser word-ladder puzzle rendered as a single clipped-out 1973 tabloid Word Detective column lying slightly askew on a yellow Formica kitchen counter (subtle linoleum-stipple texture visible at the edges, a faint coffee-cup ring intersecting the lower-left corner of the clipping, one corner of the newsprint slightly curled where it has been folded and unfolded across the week). The puzzle is six rungs from the starting word EMBER to the target word LIGHT (EMBER → AMBER → ... → ... → ... → ... → LIGHT, six total transformations, each step changing exactly one letter and producing a real English word). The visitor sees the start and end words pre-printed in the column's headline typography and four blank ruled rungs between them, with a small printed clue beside each blank rung hinting at the kind of word that fits (e.g., 'a roadside fruit-stand fruit, four letters,' 'what a tired worker does at the end of a long shift,' 'the opposite of heavy when describing a load'). The visitor solves by clicking any blank rung to type their guess letter-by-letter directly on the newsprint in a stubby graphite pencil hand (each typed letter rendering as a slightly different size and tilt, naturally imperfect, as if pressed onto pulp with a dull point), with the column auto-validating each rung's word against a real dictionary AND against the one-letter-change rule. A small smudgy graphite ERASE mark appears beside any rung the visitor wants to redo. Beneath the puzzle is a CHECK MY LADDER button styled as the column's print signature mark — pressing it after all four rungs are filled validates the entire chain and, if correct, slides the clipping aside to reveal the printed Solutions Box from the following week's edition with the answer ladder typeset in lead and a small headnote from columnist Verna Pruitt congratulating the solver by name (the visitor's own typed name if they've entered it in the corner subscription card, otherwise 'Friend'). A SHOW ME ONE RUNG I HAVE RIGHT hint button highlights one correctly-placed rung in soft amber without giving away unsolved logic. Persistent across visits — any rung the visitor has placed correctly stays on the clipping when they return, and the coffee-ring shifts slightly each day.

subject.value: Mrs. Verna Pruitt of the Buckeye Weekly Inquirer's back-page Word Detective column, working out of a small editorial office on East Long Street in Columbus, Ohio, in the spring of 1973 — a 51-year-old former eighth-grade English teacher who took the puzzle-column job in 1968 after her husband Roy passed and who has, every week for five years now, built a single word-ladder puzzle on her kitchen table on Sunday evenings, test-solved it on Monday morning over coffee, mailed the master sheet to the composing room on Tuesday, and watched the printed column hit grocery checkouts statewide every Thursday afternoon; the specific spring-1973 puzzle running in the week of April 19th, a six-rung EMBER-to-LIGHT ladder Verna built thinking about her late husband Roy who used to keep the woodstove going in the back room of the house until the embers gave just enough light to read by; the working-class infrastructure of one woman at a Formica table writing clues in a steno notebook with a yellow pencil, and the parallel infrastructure of thousands of Ohio kitchens unfolding the clipping on Friday morning and working the rungs in stubby graphite over the course of a week, with the answer box appearing in next Thursday's paper as the slow weekly heartbeat of a small shared intellectual pastime

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working puzzle-columnist voice — Verna Pruitt in the small printed headnote and the soft margin asides, talking to the visitor the way she talks to her regular Thursday solvers, the older lady on a porch in Marion who mails her a Christmas card every year, the retired mailman in Zanesville who writes corrections when she misspells a clue word; genuine pride in the six rungs as a week's careful work, conversational warmth in the printed columnist's note ('one of these clues is meaner than I meant it — Roy would have got it in ten minutes and laughed at me'), present-tense Sunday-evening-at-the-kitchen-table steadiness, real respect for the visitor as a fellow solver who is going to work this puzzle across a week of breakfasts, full earnest belief that a well-built word-ladder is a small honest gift one working person can hand to another through the grocery checkout aisle

palette.value: Aged pulp-newsprint cream (#efe3c6) with visible long-fiber texture and slight halftone-screen graininess as the clipping ground + deep composing-room ink black (#1c1814) for the column headline and body type + warm Formica-counter butter-yellow (#f4d770) as the kitchen-counter background surrounding the clipping + faint olive-tan coffee-ring shadow (#a8895a at 0.35 opacity) intersecting the lower-left corner + soft graphite-pencil gray (#4a4540) for the visitor's typed-in letter strokes with slight irregular density per letter + smudgy eraser-rubbing tan (#c8b896 at 0.6 opacity) for ERASE marks + warm amber hint-spotlight (#e8a73a at 0.45 opacity) for the SHOW ME ONE RUNG glow + faint kitchen-linoleum stipple (#d8c890) along the viewport edges + Buckeye Weekly Inquirer masthead deep brick-red (#9e3220) for the column signature and the printed Solutions Box border, evoking the specific tactile feel of cheap 1973 grocery-tabloid pulp stock, the warm domestic light of an Ohio kitchen on a Friday morning, and the soft graphite weight of a stubby pencil pressed against absorbent newsprint

mandatory_element.value: A fully working six-rung word-ladder solver: (1) two pre-printed anchor words (EMBER at top, LIGHT at bottom) rendered in the column's headline typography, with four blank ruled rungs between them, (2) each blank rung clickable to enter type-mode where the visitor types four letters one at a time and each typed letter renders as a slightly randomized graphite-pencil stroke (varying tilt, density, and size per character via CSS transforms and font-feature variations) so the typing genuinely looks hand-pressed onto newsprint, (3) a real dictionary check (embedded word list of ~3000 common four-letter English words) that validates each filled rung is a real word AND that it differs from the rung above by exactly one letter, with invalid entries marked by a small smudgy graphite squiggle in the right margin, (4) a printed clue beside each blank rung that the visitor can hover to see Verna's slightly expanded hint in a margin annotation ('roadside fruit-stand fruit, four letters' expands on hover to 'the kind your grandmother put up in jars every August'), (5) a small ERASE mark beside any rung that lets the visitor wipe their attempt and try again with a soft eraser-smudge animation, (6) CHECK MY LADDER button at the bottom of the column that validates the entire chain — if the chain is fully correct, the clipping slides aside (CSS transform) revealing the printed Solutions Box from the following week with the answer ladder typeset in lead and Verna's congratulatory headnote addressing the visitor by name (pulled from a small subscription-card input in the corner of the kitchen counter where the visitor can write their first name and town), (7) a SHOW ME ONE RUNG I HAVE RIGHT hint button that scans the current state and highlights one correctly-placed rung with soft amber glow without revealing any unsolved logic, (8) localStorage persistence so a returning visitor resumes their partial ladder exactly as left, with the coffee-ring on the clipping shifted a few pixels to a new position each new day (deterministic by date), and (9) a small running counter beside the column reading 'SOLVED BY: [N] FRIENDS THIS WEEK' that increments by one each time any visitor completes the puzzle correctly and persists across sessions, with the count visible to all future visitors

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Verna's four hover-revealed clue expansions, when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about woodstove embers, a kitchen at dusk, and a man who is no longer in the back room — disguised naturally within an earnest puzzle-columnist's expanded hint. Additionally, after a returning visitor has solved the puzzle correctly three times across three separate visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden fifth marginal note in Verna's hand permanently fades in along the right edge of the clipping reading: 'Roy got this one in eleven minutes the first time I built it, back in '69. I never used it then because he said it was too easy. I'm using it now because four years on I think eleven minutes is exactly the right amount of time to spend with a thing.' This unlock is not announced and not described in any UI text.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when supermarket tabloids had puzzle columns' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the printed word-ladder,' NO cool ironic distance from 1970s grocery-checkout pulp culture as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing of Verna's column as already-vanished. NO condescension toward working-class home solvers as casual or undereducated, NO smug modern superiority about pre-internet puzzle infrastructure. NO horror-genre framing of the late husband Roy or the woodstove embers as ghostly/haunting — Verna is a working columnist on a Sunday evening at her kitchen table, the memory of Roy is warm and ordinary, not spectral. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled puzzle-column craft and working-class kitchen-table intellectual recreation happening RIGHT NOW in April 1973 — Verna is at her Formica table tonight building next week's ladder, the Buckeye Weekly Inquirer's composing room runs Wednesday overnight, thirty thousand Ohio shoppers will fold the clipping into their grocery bags Thursday afternoon, and a retired mailman in Zanesville will work the rungs over coffee Friday morning. Treat word-ladder construction as serious skilled craft, treat Verna as a legitimate working puzzle editor with five years of professional expertise, and treat the visitor's solving work as real intellectual labor worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1968 — peak handcrafted Italian glass-marble manufacturing era in the lakeside town of Empoli in Tuscany, the specific moment when small family-run vetreria workshops produced solid lampworked glass marbles for the global toy and collector trade by spinning molten gathers of soda-lime glass on punty rods over open kerosene torches, layering ribbons of colored cane (cobalt blue, sulphur yellow, manganese violet, copper-aventurine gold-flecked brown) into the clear core while the marble was still liquid, then dropping the finished marble into a bed of warm vermiculite to anneal slowly overnight; a generations-deep craft tradition where master glassblowers like Sergio Vannucci of Vetreria Vannucci e Figli could pull a single twisted ribbon-core latticino marble in under ninety seconds of continuous spinning labor, each marble carrying the subtle thumbprint asymmetries of the maker's pontil-snap and the day's particular flame temperature, shipped in straw-packed wooden crates by rail down to the port of Livorno and from there to toy shops in London, Buenos Aires, and Cleveland where children would buy a single beautiful one for the equivalent of an hour's allowance and carry it loose in a pocket for years

format.value: A full-viewport silent generative piece: a slow-spinning interior view of a single hand-pulled latticino glass marble seen as if the visitor's eye is pressed directly against its surface — the entire browser window is filled by the marble's internal optics, with twisted ribbons of cobalt, sulphur, manganese violet, and copper-aventurine slowly rotating around the central axis at roughly 4 degrees per second, refracting and bending as light passes through the glass body. The visitor's cursor position acts as a virtual light source: as the cursor moves across the viewport, the highlights, caustic patterns, and ribbon refractions shift in real time as if the marble is being slowly tilted toward different angles of an unseen window light. No UI, no controls, no text, no buttons, no captions, no instructions, no title — only the marble, the slow turn, the moving light. The internal ribbons drift very slightly over long timescales as if the glass itself is still cooling and settling decades after being made

subject.value: A single solid lampworked latticino glass marble pulled by master glassblower Sergio Vannucci at Vetreria Vannucci e Figli in Empoli, Tuscany, on the afternoon of Thursday, October 17, 1968 — the 437th marble of his working day, a 19-millimeter sphere with a six-ribbon twist core in cobalt and sulphur with two narrow accent ribbons of manganese violet and a single thread of copper-aventurine catching the light gold; one of fourteen hundred marbles produced in the workshop that week, packed in straw in crate No. 22 bound for the port of Livorno on Saturday morning, shipped to a wholesale toy importer in Liverpool, and ultimately sold for sevenpence at a tobacconist's counter in the town of Crewe in spring 1969 to a boy named Geoffrey Holroyd who carried it in his trouser pocket through three school terms before losing it in long grass behind the cricket pavilion in July 1970 — where it has remained, half-buried in soil, slowly being turned by frost-heave and earthworm activity, for fifty-eight years; the marble itself as a small unbroken witness to its own continuous existence, still optically perfect, still slowly turning

tone.value: No tone — there is no narrator, no voice, no text visible on the page at any point. The piece speaks only through the slow optical turning of glass. The closest analogue to a voice is the silent steady patience of the marble itself: a small solid object that has been quietly perfect since 1968, present-tense and continuous, indifferent to being observed, the visual equivalent of holding a found marble up to a window without saying anything about it

palette.value: Deep velvet-black field (#0a0808) surrounding the marble suggesting the dark soil it has rested in for decades + clear soda-lime glass body rendered as soft translucent gray-white refractions (#e8ecf0 at varying opacities) + deep cobalt ribbon (#1a3a8c) + warm sulphur-yellow ribbon (#e8c84a) + rich manganese violet accent ribbon (#7a3a8c) + copper-aventurine gold-fleck thread (#c8893a) with tiny embedded sparkle points (#fff4c8 at 0.9 opacity) + warm caustic highlight where cursor-light strikes the surface (#fff8e8 at 0.7 opacity, soft circular falloff) + cool refracted shadow on the opposite side of the marble (#2a3a4a at 0.4 opacity) — evoking the specific luminescence of hand-pulled latticino glass held up to afternoon light, the subtle warmth of copper-aventurine catching a gleam, and the deep quiet of soil-darkness around a small luminous object

mandatory_element.value: A fully working generative spinning glass-marble visualization rendered in pure SVG and CSS with: (1) a central circular SVG element filling approximately 85% of the viewport's smaller dimension representing the marble's body, with a radial gradient suggesting the clear glass refractive surface and subtle edge-darkening at the rim, (2) minimum 6 internal twisted ribbon elements rendered as SVG paths that wrap around a virtual central axis, each ribbon following a sinusoidal helical path projected into 2D, with the ribbons continuously rotating at approximately 4 degrees per second via requestAnimationFrame, (3) each ribbon has its own slight wobble offset and ribbon-width variation so the twist looks hand-pulled rather than mechanically perfect, (4) cursor-position-driven caustic highlight: a soft circular bright spot whose position on the marble's surface tracks the inverse of cursor position (cursor in upper-left moves the highlight to lower-right of the marble) with smooth interpolated motion, simulating a virtual light source at the cursor location, (5) corresponding cool shadow on the opposite side of the marble that also tracks cursor position, with both highlight and shadow rendered as SVG radial gradients with soft falloff, (6) ribbons that pass behind the bright spot subtly increase in apparent saturation and ribbons in the shadow region desaturate, simulating real refractive optics, (7) tiny copper-aventurine sparkle points embedded along the gold-fleck thread that twinkle independently on a slow random schedule (each sparkle has a 0.5-2 second twinkle cycle, staggered across roughly 14 sparkle points total), (8) over very long timescales (one full slow drift every 3 minutes) the ribbon positions shift by tiny fractional amounts as if the glass is still imperceptibly settling, (9) absolutely no UI, no text, no buttons, no captions, no title, no footer, no instructions anywhere on the page, (10) the entire animation runs autonomously and loops forever with no user input required beyond the optional cursor-light interaction

wildcard.value: Exactly once every 11 minutes (deterministic, tied to elapsed time since page load), one of the twinkle sparkles along the copper-aventurine thread briefly brightens to a markedly warmer and longer-duration flash than the others — a small slow gleam lasting approximately 4 seconds, the kind of catch-the-light moment that would have happened when sunlight first struck the marble in Geoffrey Holroyd's trouser pocket as he walked home from school past the cricket pavilion in summer 1970. The visitor is given no indication this is happening, no acknowledgment, no reward for noticing — just the quiet fact that one of the sparkles on this marble remembers a particular afternoon

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO text, captions, labels-explaining-the-piece, title overlays, instructional UI, tooltip hints, loading messages, footer credits, or any narrating voice anywhere on the page visible to the visitor — the piece must be entirely silent and entirely visual. NO museum-archival 'remember when children played marbles' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of hand-pulled glass,' NO cool ironic distance from Italian glassblowing tradition as quaint Old-World craft, NO retrospective elegy framing of Sergio's workshop or Geoffrey's lost marble as already-vanished. NO horror-genre framing of the long-buried marble as haunted or uncanny — it is a small beautiful object resting in soil, not a relic of dread. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding. NO weather-app or photo-viewer interface conventions, NO settings panels, NO zoom controls, NO color-pickers. The marble exists, it turns, the cursor lights it, and the visitor watches in silence — that is the entire piece

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1964 — peak American mid-century funeral-home printed register-book era, the specific moment when small-town independent funeral parlors (Krenzler & Sons of Beaver Dam, Wisconsin; the Vrooman Memorial Chapel of Hagerstown, Maryland; Espinoza Funeral Home of Las Cruces, New Mexico) kept oversized leather-bound visitor register books on a small mahogany pedestal stand at the chapel entrance, where mourners would sign their names in dip-pen ink as they arrived for visiting hours — a heavy gilt-edged book with marbled endpapers, ruled in fine sepia lines into three columns (NAME / ADDRESS / RELATIONSHIP TO THE DECEASED) by a regional bookbinder in Milwaukee or Baltimore, the column rules drawn so light the signers wouldn't feel constrained by them; alongside the register book a small ceramic dish of dip pens, a corked bottle of Sanford's Penmanship Ink in midnight blue, a folded linen blotter, and an attendant who refilled the ink reservoir every evening; the entire infrastructure of a community grieving in formal handwriting, names accumulating across two or three days of visitation in dozens of different hands — cousins, neighbors, the postmaster, the dry-goods clerk, the woman from the bakery who used to deliver pies on Sundays — each entry a small printed certification that this person was here on this day, that the deceased was known and the family was not alone; the books then bound up after the funeral and returned to the family in a plain pasteboard sleeve to keep on a shelf for decades, occasionally opened to look at one specific signature when remembering

format.value: A working browseable visitor register book — the open spread of Mrs. Hazel Krenzler-Brusky's mahogany-pedestal register book from the visitation of Mr. Wendell P. Brusky (Hazel's father-in-law, 1887–1964) held at Krenzler & Sons Funeral Home, Beaver Dam, Wisconsin, across three evenings of visiting hours from Tuesday March 17 through Thursday March 19, 1964 — rendered as the actual ledger book filling the viewport, gilt-edged pages with marbled endpapers visible at the corners, three ruled columns (NAME / ADDRESS / RELATIONSHIP) holding 87 signed entries across the visible spreads in 87 distinctly different handwriting styles (dip-pen ink in midnight blue, each entry varying in pressure, slant, flourish, spelling care, and steadiness — the postmaster's neat copperplate, the seven-year-old grandniece's careful block letters, the elderly cousin's tremulous loops, the bakery woman's plain workaday script). The visitor turns pages by clicking the lower corner of either page (smooth page-curl animation with the heavy stock visibly thick). Each handwritten entry is hoverable: hovering reveals a soft amber spotlight on that one entry and fades in a small annotation in Hazel's own hand on a tucked-in slip of cream stationery in the margin — her note about who this person was to Wendell, what they said when they came in, whether they stayed to talk or just signed and left, any small detail she wanted to remember (e.g., 'Vernon Pollnow — Wendell taught him to weld in 1939, brought a jar of his wife's preserves, didn't stay'; 'little Cheryl Mae signed it herself, she's seven, her mother held the pen-cup for her'; 'Mrs. Donelda Krieg from the bakery, cried for ten minutes in the receiving line, hugged me twice, brought a Sunday pie even though it was Tuesday'). The pedestal has a working dip pen and ink-bottle the visitor can use to add their OWN entry on the current open page — click the pen to pick it up, click any ruled line to bring the nib down, type your name and address and relationship-to-Wendell (any relationship — 'fellow welder,' 'someone passing through,' 'never knew him but here anyway') and your handwriting renders in midnight-blue ink with subtle randomized pen-pressure variation per stroke so it looks genuinely dip-penned, persisting in localStorage and visible to all future visitors as part of the accumulating register. A small linen blotter sits beside the book — clicking it after writing presses a soft ink-blotted shadow next to your entry. A working FOLD-OUT MEMORIAL CARD bookmark tucked between two pages opens to reveal the printed funeral card with Wendell's photograph (rendered in soft sepia halftone), birth and death dates, the 23rd Psalm, and the printed schedule of visiting hours and the Friday morning service at St. Peter's Lutheran. Three of the 87 pre-seeded entries have a small pencil checkmark beside them in Hazel's hand — the checkmarks indicate signers who Hazel realized later were people Wendell had wronged or fallen out with decades ago and who came anyway, which is the small private thing she wants to remember most about the visitation.

subject.value: The visitor register book from the three-evening visitation of Mr. Wendell Peter Brusky (born March 4, 1887, in the township of Beaver Dam, Wisconsin; died peacefully at home Sunday March 15, 1964, age 76, of congestive heart failure; survived by his wife of 54 years Florence, his son Raymond Brusky and daughter-in-law Hazel Krenzler-Brusky who operate Krenzler & Sons Funeral Home, two granddaughters and one great-granddaughter) — held at Krenzler & Sons Funeral Home on Front Street in downtown Beaver Dam from Tuesday March 17 through Thursday March 19, 1964; the register book itself as a working community document recording 87 people from across the township who came to stand in the receiving line, share a memory with Florence, sign their name and address in midnight-blue dip-pen ink, and certify by the act of writing that Wendell P. Brusky was known by them and that the Brusky family is not alone in this loss; Hazel Krenzler-Brusky, age 39, who has been her father-in-law's daughter-in-law for fourteen years and who handled the register pedestal personally across all three evenings, refilling the inkwell, replacing the blotter, helping the elderly grippers find their place on the ruled line, and quietly noting in her own slip-of-paper margin annotations which signers were which person to Wendell — the postmaster who hired him for a winter route in 1934, the welder he taught at the railyard, the bakery woman whose family he helped during the polio scare, the second cousin from Fond du Lac who hadn't spoken to him in twenty-two years and came anyway; the entire infrastructure of small-town American grieving in 1964 documented in a single bound book with three ruled columns and a glass ink-bottle and a community that turned out in workaday and Sunday clothes alike to sign their names in front of the open casket and the lily arrangements from the Beaver Dam VFW Auxiliary.

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer funeral-home-attendant voice — Hazel Krenzler-Brusky talking to the visitor the way she'd talk to a cousin who has come in from out of town and is sitting at her kitchen table the morning after the burial helping her go through the register book entry by entry; genuine warmth and present-tense care for every one of the 87 signers as legitimate community members whose presence mattered, peer-level steadiness about death as ordinary and grief as workable, earnest respect for the dip-pen craft of signing one's name carefully in a stranger's family record, real affection for her father-in-law Wendell as a flawed and complete working man (welder at the railyard for 38 years, sometimes hard to live with, taught a generation of younger men a trade, slept with the radio on, kept a jar of buttons on the kitchen sill), unsentimental love for Florence whom she calls Mom, full earnest belief that a register book signed by 87 people across three evenings is one of the most important documents a family can keep on a shelf for the next sixty years; no euphemism, no soft-focus religiosity, no churchy hush — Hazel is a working funeral home daughter-in-law who has been at every visitation in this chapel for fourteen years and who treats death as honest skilled labor the same way her father-in-law treated welding. Present-tense, never retrospective. Conversational warmth in the margin notes, real grief held lightly and competently.

palette.value: Warm aged cream register-paper stock (#f1e7d4) with subtle visible long-fiber texture and a faint horizontal ruling in pale sepia (#a08868 at 0.4 opacity) for the three columns + deep midnight-blue dip-pen ink (#1c2a5e) with subtle per-stroke pressure variation in the signed entries + rich gilt-edge gold (#c4a657) at the page edges and corner trim + warm marbled-endpaper swirls in burgundy and forest-green (#7a2c2c, #2d4e2e) visible at the spread corners + deep mahogany pedestal-stand brown (#4a2e1c) framing the book on either side + soft amber chapel-lamp glow (#e8a73a at 0.3 opacity) falling across the page from upper-left suggesting the overhead memorial candelabra + Hazel's margin-slip stationery in pale dove gray (#dcd6c8) with her own handwritten annotations in soft graphite-purple (#3e3a4e) + a faint blotter-shadow ink-bloom (#1c2a5e at 0.25 opacity) beside several entries where the dip-pen ran heavy + the small pencil checkmarks Hazel placed beside three significant signers in soft cedar graphite (#5e4e3a) + the funeral-card bookmark in cool ivory with sepia-halftone photograph tones (#8a7660) for Wendell's portrait — evoking the specific tactile feel of a heavy bound visitor register book held open on a mahogany pedestal in the soft warm light of a small-town Wisconsin funeral chapel in March 1964, the particular weight of dip-pen ink drying on quality stationery stock, and the quiet domestic gravity of a book that will sit on a shelf for the next sixty years and only occasionally be opened

mandatory_element.value: A fully working dip-pen signing infrastructure: (1) 87 pre-seeded handwritten entries spread across multiple visible pages of the register book, each entry in genuinely distinct handwriting style achieved by per-entry randomized SVG path parameters (slant, baseline drift, letter spacing, pen pressure profile, loop tightness, dotted-i offset) and per-entry randomized minor irregularities (one entry's 'M' is shaky, another's 'r' is missing a serif, a third has a small ink blot above the line) so that no two signatures look alike; (2) each of the 87 entries is hoverable to reveal a soft amber spotlight on that one signature and a tucked-in slip of cream stationery in the margin fading in with Hazel's handwritten annotation about who this person was to Wendell — every annotation is its own distinct personal vignette (relationship, what they said in the receiving line, whether they stayed to talk, what they brought, whether they cried), no two annotations recycle the same phrasing or pattern; (3) page-turn mechanic where clicking the lower-right corner of the right page advances forward and the lower-left corner of the left page goes back, with smooth CSS page-curl animation showing the heavy stock visibly thick (subtle paper-edge shadow on the underside of the curling page) and a faint paper-rustle sound via Web Audio API; (4) interactive dip-pen on the mahogany pedestal: clicking the pen picks it up (cursor becomes the nib pointer with a tiny ink-drop following), clicking the ink-bottle re-inks the pen with a subtle drop animation, clicking any blank line on the current open page brings the nib down and opens a small typing field where the visitor enters NAME, ADDRESS, and RELATIONSHIP-TO-WENDELL in three sequential prompts, and their typed entry renders directly onto the ruled line in midnight-blue ink with the same per-stroke pressure variation and per-letter irregularity that the pre-seeded entries have (so the visitor's signature looks genuinely dip-penned rather than typed) — pen ink visibly depletes across long signatures and the visitor must re-ink at the bottle if they want to write a long address; (5) the linen blotter beside the book is clickable after signing to press a soft ink-blotted shadow next to the visitor's entry; (6) all visitor entries persist in localStorage AND are submitted to a shared persistent list so that future visitors see every previous visitor's signature alongside the original 87 in the same register, with the running count of signers visible as a small inked annotation at the top of the current open page reading 'SIGNED THIS WEEK: [N]'; (7) the FOLD-OUT MEMORIAL CARD bookmark, when clicked, slides out from between two pages to reveal the funeral card with Wendell's sepia-halftone portrait, his birth and death dates, the 23rd Psalm in period-correct Linotype setting, and the schedule of services — clicking the card again folds it back in; (8) three of the 87 pre-seeded entries (Vernon Pollnow, Mrs. Donelda Krieg, and the second cousin Carl Brusky from Fond du Lac) have small pencil checkmarks beside them in soft cedar graphite, and hovering these checkmarks reveals an additional secondary annotation from Hazel in even softer pencil explaining what the checkmark means for each: the welder Wendell hired and then unfairly fired in 1948 and never apologized to; the bakery woman whose grocery bill Wendell quietly carried for two months during the 1953 polio scare without telling anyone; and the second cousin Carl who hadn't spoken to Wendell since a 1942 inheritance dispute and walked through the receiving line and signed his name in steady careful copperplate and did not say a word to Florence and left immediately — the three checkmarks being the small private things Hazel wants to remember most about the visitation.

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Hazel's 87 hover-revealed margin annotations, when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about a dip pen, a hand that knew this hand, and a name that does not need to be said — disguised naturally within an earnest daughter-in-law's working note about which signer was which person to Wendell. Additionally, after a returning visitor has signed the register on three distinct calendar dates (tracked in localStorage), a hidden 88th annotation permanently fades in along the lower margin of the first spread in Hazel's hand, on a slip of cream stationery slightly more worn than the others, reading: 'I have been the one holding the pen-cup at this pedestal for fourteen years and Wendell taught me how to do it when I was twenty-five and shaking — he said the only thing you owe the signer is a steady hand and a full inkwell. I am writing this six weeks after we buried him and the inkwell is still full because I refill it every Tuesday morning before the visiting hours we don't have this week.' This unlock is not announced and not described in any UI text; it simply exists as a small quiet fact added to the register for visitors who kept coming back.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO horror-genre framing of the funeral home, the open casket, the register book, or the act of signing as creepy/uncanny/sinister/ghostly — this is a working small-town Wisconsin funeral chapel in 1964 and the register is a community document, not a haunted artifact. NO 'the names keep writing themselves' or 'one of the entries is from someone who died years ago' or any supernatural twist of any kind. NO museum-archival 'remember when small towns had real funeral homes' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the visitor register book,' NO cool ironic distance from mid-century mourning rituals as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing of Krenzler & Sons or Beaver Dam as already-vanished. NO churchy religiosity, NO sentimental soft-focus grief, NO Hallmark-card framing of death as 'a celebration of life' — Wendell was a welder who could be hard to live with and Hazel is matter-of-fact about that. NO condescension toward small-town funeral customs as provincial or naive. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled funeral-home labor and small-town community mourning infrastructure happening RIGHT NOW in March 1964 — Hazel is at the pedestal tonight refilling the inkwell, the chapel lights are on, the lilies from the VFW Auxiliary are on the casket, Florence is in the receiving line, and 87 people across three evenings are going to walk in and sign their names in dip-pen ink because that is what is done. Treat the register book as a serious working community document, treat Hazel as a legitimate working funeral-home daughter-in-law with fourteen years of professional expertise, and treat the visitor's act of adding their own signature as real participation in a real act of communal witness worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: something to explore, multiple sections, interactive elements

era.value: 1972 — peak American mid-century lunch-counter coin-operated countertop trivia/jukebox-selector culture, the specific moment when chrome-and-glass tabletop selectors sat at every booth of Woolworth's lunch counters, bus-station coffee shops, and small-town truck stops across the Midwest, offering 3-for-a-quarter song selections via a clattering flip-card mechanism mounted between the napkin dispenser and the ketchup bottle; the specific tactile pleasure of flipping the spring-loaded cards through one by one with a fingertip, hearing the small mechanical click of each card hitting the stop, scanning the typewritten titles printed on cream cardstock with the artist name in red and the song title in black, finding the one you wanted (Mac Davis, 'Baby Don't Get Hooked on Me,' B-7), pushing in the two letter-and-number buttons, dropping a dime in the slot, and waiting to see if the wallbox would actually play it next or if someone else had stacked the queue ahead of you; the regional Seeburg Consolette Wallbox industry as legitimate jukebox-engineering infrastructure operating out of the Seeburg plant on North Ravenswood Avenue in Chicago, with route operators servicing each unit weekly — pulling out the old title strips, sliding in new ones freshly typed at the route office, collecting the dimes, and noting which selections were most-played

format.value: A fully working chrome-and-glass tabletop Seeburg Consolette wallbox selector mounted at the visitor's booth, rendered as the actual countertop unit filling the viewport — the chrome bezel with its rounded art-deco corners, the curved glass window showing the stack of 40 typewritten title strips, the two rotary letter (A-K) and number (1-10) selector dials below the window, the coin slot, the small red SELECT button, and the dime-counter readout above the slot — all working: visitor drags a fingertip across the title-card stack to flip through cards one by one with a satisfying spring-loaded click sound per flip (Web Audio API), each flip revealing the next typewritten strip with artist in red and title in black on cream cardstock, with at least 40 pre-loaded title strips of real 1972 hits (Roberta Flack, Bill Withers, Carole King, Al Green, Three Dog Night, etc.); visitor selects a song by rotating the letter dial (click-drag rotation with detent-clicks) and the number dial to a valid combination, drops a dime in the slot (click animation), presses SELECT, and the unit plays a brief 8-second Web Audio API simulation of the chosen song (synth-generated tonal motif matching the song's mood) through a small monaural speaker grille on the side of the unit while a small mechanical typewriter-tape readout above the dials prints out the selection ('B-7: BABY DON'T GET HOOKED ON ME / MAC DAVIS — NOW PLAYING'); persistent across visits — a small dime-counter on the unit tracks every visitor's accumulated dimes across all visits ('DIMES IN THE SLOT TODAY: 47'), and the last 10 songs played by any visitor anywhere appear as a scrolling 'JUST PLAYED' ticker along the bottom of the unit so each visitor sees what previous visitors picked; a small jam-the-card feature: roughly 1 in every 25 flips the card mechanism briefly sticks and the visitor must give the unit a gentle nudge (click anywhere on the chrome bezel) to free it, with a small mechanical thunk sound, the way real Consolettes actually behaved in 1972

subject.value: A specific Seeburg Consolette wallbox model SCH-1 serial #4477, installed in March 1972 at Booth 7 of Pearl's Lunch Counter on the corner of State and Marion Streets in Logansport, Indiana — a working chrome tabletop jukebox selector serviced every Tuesday morning by route operator Glen Vandermeer who drives a 1968 Ford Econoline from his service garage in Kokomo, pulls out the old title strips with a small pair of needle-nose pliers, slides in the new ones freshly typed at the route office on Glen's wife Ruth's Smith Corona, collects the dimes from the coin box (averaging $3.20 per week per unit across his 47-stop route), notes the play counts in a small green ledger he keeps on the dashboard, and shares a cup of coffee with Pearl at the end of the lunch rush; the wallbox itself as a working community jukebox-selection infrastructure that hundreds of Pearl's regulars touch every week — the lunch-counter waitresses Marjorie and Donna who play Roberta Flack's 'The First Time Ever I Saw Your Face' three times a shift, the truck drivers on Route 24 who favor Charley Pride and Merle Haggard, the high-school kids who come in after fifth period and stack the queue with Three Dog Night and Bread, the elderly Mrs. Tessmer who comes in every Thursday for the chicken-and-noodles and always plays Patti Page's 'Tennessee Waltz' from the catalog Glen still keeps stocked in slot K-3 because she asked him to leave it there; the specific small joy of dropping a dime into a chrome unit at your own booth and hearing the wallbox queue your selection into the master jukebox by the counter and waiting to see if your song would actually play next or if Donna had stacked Roberta Flack ahead of you again

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer route-operator voice — Glen Vandermeer talking to the visitor the way he'd talk to a new junior operator riding along with him on a Tuesday route, leaning into the booth at Pearl's with a coffee in one hand and the title-strip pliers in the other, walking through how the SCH-1 mechanism actually works and why the spring-loaded flip stack is the most satisfying piece of engineering Seeburg ever shipped; genuine pride in the chrome-and-glass craftsmanship, peer-level warmth about the route work as honest skilled labor that supports his family and keeps 47 small-town lunch counters in music, real affection for Pearl and Marjorie and Donna and Mrs. Tessmer as legitimate community members whose musical preferences he keeps track of in his ledger, present-tense Tuesday-morning steadiness of someone who has been doing this work since 1958, full earnest belief that a chrome tabletop wallbox at a booth IS one of the small genuine pleasures of an American lunch counter in 1972 and that the visitor's dime in the slot is participation in a real shared act of community song-choosing

palette.value: Polished chrome bezel silver (#c8ccd0) with subtle reflective gradient highlights (#f0f2f5) + deep cobalt-blue art-deco corner accents (#1c3a6e) + warm cream typewritten title-strip stock (#f3e6c8) with deep red artist-name ink (#a8312a) and composing-room black song-title type (#1a1612) + warm Pearl's-lunch-counter buttery yellow Formica booth surface (#f4d770) as the surrounding countertop visible at the unit's base + soft chrome-reflected ceiling-fluorescent highlight (#fff8e8 at 0.4 opacity) along the upper bezel + cool inset-glass shadow (#5a6670) inside the title-strip window + warm tungsten dial-indicator amber (#e8a73a) for the rotary letter and number dials when they detent into position + deep dime-slot brass (#8a6a3a) for the coin slot + pearlescent SELECT-button cherry red (#c83040) with subtle pressed-state darkening — evoking the specific tactile feel of brushed chrome under fingertips at a 1972 American lunch counter, the warm light of overhead fluorescents bouncing off polished metal, and the visual rhythm of typewriter-faced title strips behind glass
