mandatory_element.value: A fully working tabletop wallbox: (1) at least 40 pre-loaded title strips in the visible card stack, each a real 1972 song with artist name in red typewriter face and title in black, indexed by letter (A-K) and number (1-10), (2) fingertip-drag-to-flip mechanic with spring-loaded click sound per card and smooth CSS card-flip animation revealing the next strip, (3) two working rotary selector dials (letter A-K and number 1-10) that the visitor click-drags to rotate with detent-click sounds at each position and visible indicator-arrow alignment, (4) clickable coin slot that animates a dime dropping with appropriate metallic clink sound and increments a persistent dime-counter readout above the slot, (5) cherry-red SELECT button that activates only when a dime is in the slot AND a valid letter+number combination is selected, which when pressed plays an 8-second Web Audio API tonal simulation of the chosen song's mood (synth pad + bass line + simple melody matching the song's key/tempo) AND prints out the selection on a small mechanical typewriter-tape readout above the dials, (6) persistent localStorage state tracking total dimes deposited across all sessions displayed as 'DIMES IN THE SLOT TODAY: [N]' on the chrome face, (7) a scrolling 'JUST PLAYED' ticker along the bottom of the unit showing the last 10 songs queued by any visitor anywhere (shared persistent list), (8) the jam-the-card stuck-mechanism feature: roughly 1 in 25 flips the card sticks and a small 'NUDGE IT' indicator lights, requiring the visitor to click anywhere on the chrome bezel to free it with a satisfying mechanical thunk sound, (9) all selector states, dime counts, and played songs persist in localStorage so returning visitors resume exactly where they left off, with their own personal play history visible as a small fold-out 'YOUR SELECTIONS THIS MONTH' panel that slides out from the side of the unit

wildcard.value: Exactly one of the 40 typewritten title strips is Mrs. Tessmer's permanent K-3 slot holding Patti Page's 'Tennessee Waltz' — a 1950 record that has no business being in a 1972 catalog, and the title strip for this one is slightly more worn than the others (subtle yellowing, a faint coffee-stain shadow in the corner, the typewriter ink slightly faded). If the visitor selects K-3, the 8-second tonal simulation plays in a noticeably warmer and slightly slower rendering than the other songs, and a small handwritten margin note from Glen fades in along the right side of the unit reading: 'I keep this one stocked because she asked me to in 1968 and she still comes in every Thursday for the noodles. Glen.' Additionally, exactly one of Glen's hover-revealed margin notes about the mechanism (visible when the visitor hovers any part of the chrome bezel), when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about a dime, a slot, and a song someone is waiting to hear — disguised naturally within an earnest route-operator's explanation of how the spring-loaded flip stack works.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when lunch counters had jukeboxes' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the chrome wallbox,' NO cool ironic distance from 1970s small-town lunch-counter culture as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Pearl's or the Seeburg Consolette as already-vanished. NO condescension toward route-operator work as unskilled labor or toward dime-jukebox entertainment as trivial recreation. NO horror-genre framing of the stuck-card mechanism or the warmer-sounding K-3 slot as creepy/haunted — the K-3 slot is a working route-operator's quiet kindness toward a regular customer, not a ghost. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding. NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher framing of the jukebox network — Glen is a route operator with a green ledger and a Ford Econoline, not a signal-transmitter. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled jukebox-route labor and small-town lunch-counter community music infrastructure happening RIGHT NOW in March 1972 — Glen is at Booth 7 this Tuesday morning, Pearl is pouring his coffee, the chrome wallbox is mounted to the booth wall and Marjorie is about to stack Roberta Flack again. Treat the wallbox as a serious working community instrument, treat Glen as a legitimate working route operator with fourteen years of professional expertise, and treat the visitor's dime in the slot as real participation in a real act of communal song-choosing worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1955 — peak American post-war drive-in movie theater concession stand era, the specific moment when family-owned outdoor cinemas dotted the rural highways outside small towns from Pennsylvania to Oregon, and the concession stand at intermission was the actual economic engine of the operation; the carhop infrastructure of teenage girls in pleated uniforms walking a tray with paper-wrapped hamburgers, waxed-paper cones of buttered popcorn, glass bottles of orange Nehi and grape Crush sweating in the dewy summer night air, and small cardboard trays of french fries with little ramekins of ketchup, delivered to the driver's window of a 1949 Pontiac parked in space C-14 of the Starlite Drive-In outside Wexford, Pennsylvania; the specific printed paper-bag menu folded into the dashboard speaker-pole holder at the start of each evening's first feature, listing the night's offerings in two-color hand-set type with prices in cents, the projectionist's name printed at the bottom alongside the names of tonight's carhops, the listing of the double feature, the intermission countdown schedule, and a small printed coupon good for a free Coca-Cola on your birthday if you brought the bag back to the booth

format.value: A single printed paper concession-stand menu from one specific Saturday evening at the Starlite Drive-In, designed to be unfolded across the visitor's lap as if just pulled out of the dashboard speaker-pole holder — front panel with the theater's hand-lettered logo and tonight's double feature, interior bifold spread with the full concession listing in three columned categories (SANDWICHES & HOT EATS / POPCORN COLD DRINKS & TREATS / FROM THE GRIDDLE — LATE BITES), back panel with the intermission countdown schedule, the carhop roster for tonight's shift, the projectionist's signature line, the birthday-Coke coupon, and a small handwritten margin note from the concession manager about the weather and what's selling fast tonight

subject.value: The Saturday-evening concession-stand printed menu for July 16, 1955, at the Starlite Drive-In on Route 19 outside Wexford, Pennsylvania, owned by Hilbert and Margaret Stankiewicz who took over the operation from Margaret's uncle in 1951 — tonight's double feature is 'Strategic Air Command' followed by 'The Cobweb,' first feature begins at 8:47pm at full dusk, intermission countdown reels begin rolling at 10:38pm, and the concession stand is staffed by manager Margaret herself behind the griddle plus four carhops (Eleanor 'Ellie' Pacholski age 17, Doris Jean Stankiewicz age 16 who is Margaret's niece working her second summer, Beverly Wojcik age 18 who is leaving for nursing school in Pittsburgh in three weeks and tonight is her second-to-last shift, and the new girl Carolyn Markowicz age 15 who started Wednesday and is still learning the tray balance); the menu itself printed Thursday morning at Kowalski Printing in nearby Sewickley on cream paper stock in two-color letterpress (deep maroon and forest green), 400 copies, folded by hand by Doris Jean and Eleanor at the concession counter Friday afternoon, slipped into the dashboard speaker-pole holders one per car as families pull into their spaces

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer concession-manager voice — Margaret Stankiewicz in the printed headnote and the handwritten margin annotations, talking to the visitor (the carload of customers in space C-14) the way she'd talk to a regular family who has been coming to the Starlite every other Saturday since 1952 and whose kids she watched grow from car-seat babies to teenagers: matter-of-fact pride in tonight's griddle work (the burgers come from Heimerl's butcher shop in Wexford, the rolls are baked Thursdays at Pisko's bakery), genuine warmth about her four carhops as legitimate working professionals (Beverly is going to make an excellent nurse, Carolyn has finally got the tray-balance down), peer-level steadiness about the work of running the concession stand as serious skilled small-business labor that feeds her family and pays Doris Jean's tuition next year at the community college in Butler, real affection for the regular customers whose orders she remembers (the Bachowski family in the green Buick always splits two large popcorns and four orange Nehis), present-tense Saturday-evening readiness, full earnest belief that a hand-printed paper menu in the dashboard speaker-pole holder is a small genuine gift from her family's business to yours, and the warm summer night and the screen lighting up at full dusk and the carhops walking the gravel between cars with trays is one of the better things that happens in this part of Pennsylvania on a Saturday evening

palette.value: Warm cream letterpress paper stock (#f3e7cf) with subtle visible long-fiber texture as the menu ground + deep maroon two-color letterpress ink (#7a2c3a) for the theater logo, category headers, and decorative ornaments + forest-green secondary ink (#2d5a3a) for prices, the carhop roster, and the bifold ornamental rules + warm Margaret-handwritten ballpoint blue (#2a3a6e) for the margin annotations and the concession-manager's signature + subtle dashboard vinyl-grain shadow (#3a2e1a at 0.15 opacity) along the menu's lower edge suggesting the speaker-pole holder it just came out of + soft warm summer-dusk amber (#e8a73a at 0.18 opacity) cast across the upper-right corner of the menu suggesting the projection-booth light bleeding through the windshield at 8:35pm + faint griddle-grease translucent finger-smudge (#a08868 at 0.3 opacity) at the lower-left corner from Margaret's thumb when she folded the bags + deep concession-counter chocolate-brown (#3a2418) framing the menu on either side suggesting the dashboard the visitor's lap rests on + a single small forest-green letterpress birthday-coupon perforation line (#2d5a3a, dashed) along the right edge of the back panel

mandatory_element.value: A fully working bifold paper menu interactive experience: (1) the menu opens flat in the viewport showing front panel + interior bifold spread + back panel as a continuous unfolded surface, with subtle paper-fold creases visible as faint shadows where the menu was folded into thirds for the speaker-pole holder, (2) minimum 32 concession items listed across the three interior columns in two-color letterpress typography, each item with its 1955 price in cents and a brief one-line description (HOT BUTTERED POPCORN — 15¢ — popped fresh every twenty minutes in coconut oil; HAMBURGER ON A BUN — 25¢ — Heimerl's beef ground this morning, lettuce and Margaret's pickle relish; ORANGE NEHI BOTTLE — 10¢ — bottle deposit 2¢ refunded to your carhop; etc.), (3) each item is hoverable to reveal a soft amber spotlight on the listing and fades in a handwritten ballpoint margin annotation from Margaret in her own hand explaining something specific about that item (where the ingredients come from, which carhop just restocked it, whether they're running low tonight, any small story attached to it — e.g., 'the pickle relish is my mother's recipe, I make a gallon every Tuesday morning, the secret is the celery seed'; 'Beverly will be sad to see the last of these grape Crushes go — they're her favorite and the distributor is discontinuing them in October'), (4) a working ORDER FROM YOUR CARHOP form panel at the bottom of the menu where the visitor selects items by clicking them (selected items get a small checked-off pencil mark and accumulate in a running order tray with subtotal in cents), then enters their car space number (defaulted to C-14 but editable, valid spaces A-1 through F-22) and presses the small horn-button graphic labeled FLASH YOUR HEADLIGHTS TWICE to summon a carhop — pressing it triggers a soft summer-night ambience (Web Audio API: distant crickets, faint movie dialogue bleeding from neighboring car speakers, the gravel-crunch of carhop footsteps approaching) for 8-12 seconds, then one of the four carhops 'arrives' at the visitor's window with the order itemized on a small handwritten paper ticket signed by that carhop in her own distinct handwriting style (Eleanor's neat cursive, Doris Jean's careful round print, Beverly's quick efficient slant, Carolyn's still-learning slightly-wobbly handwriting), with the total in cents and a printed THANK YOU FOR COMING TONIGHT — Margaret Stankiewicz line at the bottom, (5) which carhop arrives is determined by a small rotation algorithm tracking each carhop's current tray-load so the work distributes fairly across the four (visible to the visitor as a small concession-stand-window status panel showing each carhop's current location on the lot — 'Ellie: returning from row B'; 'Doris Jean: at the counter restocking popcorn'; 'Beverly: delivering to E-7'; 'Carolyn: walking to D-11'), (6) the visitor can place multiple orders across the evening (intermission countdown clock in the upper corner of the menu shows current time advancing from 8:47pm through the double feature and intermission to 12:24am end-of-show), and all orders persist in localStorage as a running tab visible on the back panel labeled YOUR TAB AT THE STARLITE TONIGHT with running total and the carhop signatures collected, (7) the birthday-Coke coupon along the right edge of the back panel has a small TODAY IS MY BIRTHDAY checkbox — checking it (and entering a name and a birth-date month/day that matches the system's current date) tears the coupon along its perforation with a small paper-tear sound and adds a complimentary 6oz Coca-Cola to the visitor's tab marked FREE — HAPPY BIRTHDAY FROM HILBERT AND MARGARET, (8) at the bottom of the menu's interior spread Margaret's handwritten WHAT'S SELLING FAST TONIGHT note updates dynamically based on what items visitors across all sessions have ordered most this hour (shared persistent count), with Margaret's voice commenting on the leaders ('the cheeseburgers are moving — we're down to twelve patties on the griddle, Hilbert is bringing more from the walk-in'), and (9) at the end-of-show 12:24am the menu's back panel reveals a small handwritten end-of-night tally from Margaret thanking the visitor by name (if entered) and noting which carhop served them most often tonight

wildcard.value: Exactly one of the 32 concession items — the GRILLED CHEESE ON RYE, listed in the FROM THE GRIDDLE — LATE BITES column at 20¢ — is Margaret's quiet personal favorite that she only makes for customers who order it after intermission has already started, and the menu's printed description for this item is slightly longer and more carefully worded than the others (Margaret hand-corrected the printer's proof on Thursday afternoon to get the wording exactly right). If the visitor orders the grilled cheese before 10:38pm intermission, the carhop's ticket comes back with a small handwritten note from Margaret reading 'the griddle isn't hot enough yet for a proper one — try again after the countdown reels start, the rye toasts better at full heat.' If the visitor orders it after 10:38pm, the ticket comes back with the grilled cheese listed AND a small additional handwritten line from Margaret reading 'made you mine — the way my mother showed me, butter on both sides and a little salt on the top crust. Margaret.' Additionally, exactly one of Margaret's hover-revealed margin annotations, when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about coconut oil, a paper bag, and a summer night that smells like popcorn and cut grass — disguised naturally within an earnest concession-manager's working note about the popper machine.

NO sock-hop Happy-Days pastiche, NO 'gee-whiz Mister' aw-shucks fake-1950s dialogue, NO Norman Rockwell sentimentality, NO 'simpler times' framing. NO condescension toward concession-stand work as unskilled labor, toward carhop work as demeaning, or toward family-owned drive-ins as small-business pitiable struggle. NO horror-genre framing of the empty drive-in, the late carhop walking the dark gravel, the projection-booth shadows, or the after-midnight last-show emptiness as creepy/haunted/uncanny — this is a working family business with the lights on and the popcorn popping and four girls in pleated uniforms walking trays. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding. NO radio/CB/broadcast/transmission framing of the speaker-pole audio — the speaker is a dashboard accessory, not a transmitter. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled concession-stand labor and small-business drive-in infrastructure happening RIGHT NOW on the evening of Saturday, July 16, 1955 — Margaret is at the griddle, Hilbert is in the projection booth threading the first reel of 'Strategic Air Command,' Ellie and Doris Jean and Beverly and Carolyn are at the concession counter folding paper bags and counting change, the gravel lot is filling with families, and tonight the orange Nehis are properly cold because Hilbert filled the ice chest at 4pm. Treat the printed paper menu as a serious working commercial document, treat Margaret as a legitimate working concession manager and small-business partner with four years of professional expertise, treat the four carhops as legitimate working teenage professionals doing skilled service labor, and treat the visitor's order in space C-14 as real participation in a real Saturday-night act of family-business commerce worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1994 — peak American suburban big-box hardware-store paint-chip sample card era, the very specific moment when Sherwin-Williams, Behr, and Pratt & Lambert competitor brands all stocked free fan-deck-pull paint sample cards in towering wall displays at the entrance of every Home Depot and Builders Square, each card a small printed object roughly 2.25 inches by 4 inches with three or six stacked color swatches in graduated tints sharing a single poetic color-name family (DESERT MORNING, ATLANTIC FOG, MEADOWLARK HEIGHTS), and the small American hobby of going to the paint aisle to take a fistful of them home not to paint anything but just to have them, fan them out on the kitchen table, and read the color names aloud as if they were tiny printed poems

format.value: A single full-viewport printed paint-chip sample card, scaled up enormously so it fills the entire browser window — one tall vertical card with six stacked tint swatches running top to bottom from palest blush at the top to deepest saturation at the bottom, each swatch labeled with its individual poetic color name in tiny capitalized serif type centered on the swatch (six names in total, each from the same naming family), and at the very bottom of the card a small printed paint-brand logo, a product code (e.g., '4471-C / SEMI-GLOSS / INTERIOR'), and the tiny printed line 'PEEL THIS SAMPLE FROM THE DISPLAY — TAKE IT HOME — IT IS FREE.' The page IS the card. No header, no nav, no footer outside the card itself, no buttons, no instructions — the visitor's entire browser window is the printed surface of the chip card, edge to edge, with subtle paper-stock long-fiber texture visible across the whole expanse and the very faint shadow of a slight bend at the upper-left corner where someone once folded it accidentally in a coat pocket. The only thing that 'happens' on the page is that hovering any of the six swatches with the cursor causes that swatch to silently expand its name across the full width of the card in large serif type fading in beneath the swatch, plus a small handwritten-in-pencil margin note from someone (an unidentified person who has previously held this chip) about why THAT particular color reminded them of something specific from their actual life — and clicking any swatch peels that swatch off the card with a tiny SVG paper-tear and tucks it into a small accumulating pile at the lower-right corner of the viewport that persists in localStorage forever, so a returning visitor sees their own slowly-growing pile of peeled-off swatches from previous visits. The strangeness is that the card is enormous, the visitor is small, the colors don't do anything practical, and the page is just a printed object you can stand inside.

subject.value: A single paint-chip sample card from the Pratt & Lambert competitor brand 'Coronet Color' (a fictional but plausible mid-tier 1994 paint line manufactured in Akron, Ohio, by Coronet Manufacturing Co. and distributed regionally through Builders Square and independent paint stores across the Great Lakes states), specifically the card from the 'KITCHENS THAT REMEMBER' color family — a six-swatch graduated tint card developed by Coronet's in-house color designer Marlys Vanderpoel in the spring of 1993, where each of the six tints carries a poetic name evoking a specific small remembered kitchen moment (PIE COOLING ON THE SILL / FLOUR ON THE COUNTER / BUTTER LEFT OUT / KETTLE WAITING / RHUBARB IN A COLANDER / LINOLEUM AT TWO PM) and the entire card is meant to be one of fourteen families in Coronet's 1994 'AMERICAN INTERIORS' deck — a card a fictional woman named Donna Halverson of Toledo took home from her local Builders Square on a Saturday afternoon in October 1994 along with eight other chips, intending eventually to repaint the kitchen of the small ranch house she and her husband Larry had moved into in 1987, but then she never did repaint it because she liked the wallpaper Larry's mother had hung in 1982 better than she had admitted to herself, and the chip ended up in the kitchen junk drawer behind the rubber bands and the takeout menus for the next eleven years where she would occasionally find it while looking for a twist tie and pause for a moment to read the names again before putting it back.

tone.value: Warm, attentive, peer-to-peer working color-designer voice — Marlys Vanderpoel at her drafting table in Coronet's small color-design office on East Market Street in Akron in spring 1993, talking to the visitor the way she'd talk to a fellow color designer visiting from a competitor brand who has dropped by to see what she's working on this season: genuine pride in the six tints as a season's careful work, conversational warmth in the hand-penciled margin annotations on each swatch about what specifically she was thinking of when she named that one (the kitchen of the house she grew up in on Princeton Avenue in Cleveland Heights, her grandmother's farmhouse outside Wooster, the kitchen of the rental apartment she and her husband had their first year), peer-level steadiness about commercial paint design as serious skilled craft requiring real color theory and real attention to how light falls in real domestic rooms at real times of day, present-tense Tuesday-morning-at-the-color-bench focus, full earnest belief that a free printed paint chip card is a small genuine gift one working color designer can hand to a homemaker in the paint aisle of a Builders Square in Toledo, and that the names matter at least as much as the pigments. Real warmth for the swatches as small printed poems on the way to being practical commercial documents.

palette.value: Warm cream printed cardstock (#f3e7cf) with subtle long-fiber paper texture as the card ground + the six graduated tints running top to bottom from a palest blush-pink (#f7e0d4) through warm peachy-cream (#f0c8a8) and soft butter-yellow (#e8c98c) and warm honey-amber (#d4a36e) into a rich terracotta (#b87850) and finally a deep mossy umber (#7a5a3e) at the bottom + tiny composing-room black serif type (#1c1812) for the color names centered on each swatch + soft graphite-pencil annotation gray (#4a4540) for the hand-penciled margin notes + faint brick-red Coronet Color brand wordmark (#a83c2a) at the bottom of the card for the logo and product code + a barely-visible warm afternoon-window-light wash (#f5d89a at 0.12 opacity) falling diagonally across the upper-right corner of the card suggesting the kitchen-table light Donna read it under + subtle dust-gray shadow (#9e9890 at 0.2 opacity) at the lower-left corner of the card suggesting it has been sitting in a junk drawer for eleven years + a faint creased-paper fold-shadow (#6a6058 at 0.25 opacity) tracing a diagonal line across the upper-left corner where the chip was once accidentally folded — evoking the specific tactile feel of mid-1990s printed paint-chip cardstock held in afternoon kitchen light, the warmth of small printed poetry intended for domestic walls, and the soft quiet patience of a card that has been forgotten and rediscovered many times in a junk drawer.

mandatory_element.value: A fully working hover-and-peel chip-card interaction: (1) the card fills the entire viewport with six clearly delineated horizontal tint swatches stacked top to bottom, each labeled in tiny composing-room serif type with its individual color name centered on the swatch (PIE COOLING ON THE SILL / FLOUR ON THE COUNTER / BUTTER LEFT OUT / KETTLE WAITING / RHUBARB IN A COLANDER / LINOLEUM AT TWO PM), (2) hovering any swatch causes that swatch's name to silently re-render in large elegant serif type across the full width of the card just beneath the swatch (smooth CSS opacity fade-in over 600ms) AND fades in a handwritten graphite-pencil margin annotation from Marlys in the right margin of the card explaining the specific kitchen moment she was thinking of when she named that tint (e.g., 'my grandmother's pies cooled on the sill of the house on County Road 8 outside Wooster every Sunday from August through October and the windowsill paint had aged to exactly this color by 1971'), each annotation a distinct personal vignette that does not recycle phrasing from the others, (3) clicking any swatch triggers a small SVG paper-tear animation along the swatch's perforated edge with a soft tearing sound via Web Audio API, after which the swatch detaches from the card and migrates with a gentle CSS transform to a small accumulating pile at the lower-right corner of the viewport (visible as a stack of slightly-rotated peeled chips with subtle drop shadows), (4) the pile persists in localStorage forever — a returning visitor sees every chip they have peeled across every previous visit still in the pile, with a small running count visible as faint pencil writing beside the pile reading 'CHIPS YOU HAVE TAKEN HOME: [N]', (5) the original card always regenerates the next time the visitor returns so all six swatches are available to peel again (the chip-pile accumulates but the card itself is always whole), (6) clicking any peeled chip in the corner pile expands it briefly to full-card size in the viewport so the visitor can read it again, then it returns to the pile on a second click, (7) at the very bottom of the card a small printed line in tiny serif type reads 'PEEL THIS SAMPLE FROM THE DISPLAY — TAKE IT HOME — IT IS FREE.' and clicking this line slides up a small fold-out flap revealing Marlys's name as the color designer, her drafting-bench address on East Market Street in Akron, and a single handwritten line: 'every color in this family was a real kitchen first.', (8) a small subtle interaction along the upper-left corner of the card where the accidental fold-shadow appears: hovering exactly that corner shows a faint additional pencil mark in someone else's hand (not Marlys's) reading 'found behind the rubber bands again — September 2003' — Donna Halverson's own annotation, never explained, persisting on the card forever.

wildcard.value: Exactly one of the six margin annotations from Marlys, when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about flour on a counter, a hand that taught this hand, and a kitchen that is no longer there — disguised naturally within an earnest color-designer's working note about the specific tint she was matching. Additionally, after a returning visitor has peeled all six swatches across at least two separate visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden seventh tint quietly appears at the very bottom of the card below LINOLEUM AT TWO PM — a deep nighttime blue-black swatch labeled THE KITCHEN AT 11 P.M. WITH ONLY THE STOVE LIGHT ON, with Marlys's handwritten note beside it: 'this one was never approved for the deck — the marketing department said no one paints a kitchen this dark. I left it on the master sheet anyway because it is the most honest color I made that year.' This unlock is not announced and not described in any UI text; it simply exists for visitors who kept coming back.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when hardware stores had paint-chip walls' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the printed color name,' NO cool ironic distance from 1990s big-box home-improvement culture as quaint suburban Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Coronet Color or Builders Square as already-vanished. NO condescension toward commercial paint design as unskilled marketing labor, toward homemakers who collect paint chips without painting anything as wistful or sad, or toward Donna Halverson's eleven-year delay as small-life pathos. NO horror-genre framing of the junk drawer, the never-repainted kitchen, the hidden seventh swatch, or the accidental Donna annotation as creepy/uncanny/haunted — this is a working color designer's careful craft document and a homemaker's quiet domestic gesture, not a ghost story. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the physical card itself (the card is a printed commercial object, not a vault relic). NO radio/CB/broadcast/transmission framing of any kind. NO observatory/society/club scaffolding. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled commercial color-design craft happening RIGHT NOW in the spring of 1993 in Akron — Marlys is at her drafting bench this Tuesday morning matching the tint of her grandmother's windowsill paint from the kitchen on County Road 8, the Coronet print shop will run the 1994 American Interiors deck in late September, and Donna Halverson will pick up this exact card from a Builders Square wall display in Toledo on a Saturday afternoon in October. Treat the printed chip card as a serious working commercial document, treat Marlys as a legitimate working color designer with genuine craft expertise, treat each of the six color names as a small printed poem worth full peer-to-peer respect, and treat Donna's act of taking chips home and never painting anything as an entirely earnest and dignified domestic gesture that needs no irony.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1962 — peak American grade-school classroom teacher-made ditto-master worksheet era, the specific moment when public elementary school teachers across the country (Mrs. Doreen Pfaff of the fourth-grade class at Lincoln Elementary in Davenport, Iowa, the kind of teacher who arrived at school at 6:45am every morning to crank out the day's worksheets) used the Ditto Spirit Duplicator in the school's mimeograph closet to produce purple-ink-on-newsprint daily exercise sheets — a teacher would hand-letter the master sheet with a stylus on the waxy carbon-backed duplicator paper, clamp the master to the rotating drum, crank the handle thirty-two times for thirty-two students, and the kids would receive their puzzle still slightly damp and smelling sweetly of methanol solvent at 8:15am, with the purple impression slightly heavier on the left side of the page where Mrs. Pfaff had pressed harder; the entire infrastructure of pre-photocopier American elementary education running on hand-drawn purple worksheets distributed by the millions every weekday morning across the nation

format.value: A genuine, fully solvable browser-based hidden-word camouflage puzzle disguised as a single Ditto-duplicated fourth-grade Tuesday-morning worksheet from Mrs. Pfaff's classroom — the entire viewport is the worksheet, purple methanol-ink imprint on cream newsprint, slightly skewed on the page the way a hand-cranked duplicator always laid the impression a degree or two off-axis; the worksheet's header reads 'TUESDAY MORNING SEAT WORK — OCTOBER 23, 1962 — MRS. PFAFF — NAME:____________ ROW:____' with a small editable name and row blank the visitor can fill in (their name renders in purple #2 pencil), and the body of the worksheet is a dense paragraph of hand-lettered purple-ink prose (~180 words, a small story about a class field trip to the Mississippi River bluffs the previous Friday) inside which exactly EIGHT specific words have been camouflaged by Mrs. Pfaff's own playful design — each hidden word is spelled by reading the first letter of every word across a consecutive stretch of the text (the words are real fourth-grade vocabulary words: AUTUMN, MAPLE, RIVER, KESTREL, COTTONWOOD, SANDBAR, MILKWEED, GEESE) — the visitor solves by clicking words in the paragraph to underline them in purple pencil (click-drag to select a consecutive stretch, release to mark it as a candidate hidden word), each candidate registers in a small ruled answer box at the bottom of the worksheet labeled 'WORDS YOU FOUND:' which accepts up to 8 entries; valid hidden-word detections (correct consecutive runs of words whose first letters spell one of the eight) light up that underline in a brighter cleaner purple and check it off in the answer box with a small purple checkmark stamp; invalid guesses appear in the answer box temporarily with a soft pencil-eraser smudge after 4 seconds; a small TURN IN YOUR PAPER button at the bottom (styled as Mrs. Pfaff's wooden 'IN' basket on the corner of her desk) activates only after all 8 words are found, which when clicked slides the worksheet into the basket and reveals Mrs. Pfaff's hand-graded return — a tiny purple-ink 'EXCELLENT WORK' annotation in her own handwriting plus a single gold foil star sticker pressed onto the upper-right corner of the page (gold foil rendered as a metallic gradient with a tiny embossed star shape), and beneath the worksheet appears a small handwritten note in Mrs. Pfaff's hand on a folded slip explaining that the eight hidden words are the eight things she saw on the bluff walk Friday that she most wanted the children to remember; persistent across visits — the name and row blanks save, the gold star persists once earned, and the bluff-walk follow-up note remains revealable after solving

subject.value: Mrs. Doreen Pfaff's fourth-grade Tuesday-morning seat-work worksheet for October 23, 1962, hand-cranked at 6:52am that morning on the Ditto Spirit Duplicator in the mimeograph closet at Lincoln Elementary School on the corner of Brady and 17th Street in Davenport, Iowa — Mrs. Pfaff, age 47, who has been teaching fourth grade in Davenport public schools for nineteen years and who genuinely believes that a well-designed hidden-word puzzle is one of the more powerful pedagogical instruments she has at her disposal because it teaches careful reading without telling the children that's what they're doing; the specific puzzle, which she sketched out Monday evening on her kitchen table after dinner while her husband Roland listened to the Cardinals game on the radio in the den, hand-lettering the master sheet in purple ditto-stylus while building a paragraph about the class trip to the Mississippi bluffs the previous Friday and quietly hiding eight first-letter acrostics through the text (AUTUMN starting at 'Always Underfoot The Underbrush Made Noises'; MAPLE starting at 'Mr. Adler Pointed Like Edward Saw'; etc.) — eight words chosen because they were the eight specific things from the bluff walk Mrs. Pfaff most wanted these particular children to carry home in their heads — the kestrel because two boys saw it dive and didn't say anything about it but she watched their faces; the milkweed because Sherrilyn Boudreau picked one open and the seeds went everywhere; the sandbar because three of the children had never seen one and stood still on the trail for a long minute trying to understand what it was; and the puzzle as a small private gift hidden inside a routine seat-work assignment that the children will never know was a love letter

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer fourth-grade-teacher voice — Mrs. Pfaff in the worksheet's hand-lettered purple prose and in the small handwritten margin returns, addressing the visitor the way she'd address a fourth-grader sitting in Row 3 Seat 4 on a Tuesday morning with their freshly distributed worksheet still slightly damp on the desk in front of them: matter-of-fact pedagogical clarity about what the assignment requires, genuine warmth without baby-talk or condescension, peer-level respect for the visitor as a capable solver who can find eight hidden words if they read carefully, present-tense Tuesday-morning steadiness of a teacher who has been at her desk since 6:45am and is now standing at the front of the classroom watching her students settle into seat work, real specific affection for the eight things from the bluff walk and for the individual children whose reactions she remembers (Sherrilyn with the milkweed seeds, the two boys watching the kestrel), full earnest belief that a hand-cranked Ditto worksheet with a hidden-word puzzle inside it is a legitimate small gift one working teacher can hand to a roomful of fourth-graders on an October morning, and that finding all eight is worth a real gold star pressed onto the corner of the page by her thumb

palette.value: Cream newsprint worksheet stock (#f1e7cf) with subtle long-fiber texture and faint horizontal ruling barely visible (#d4c6a2 at 0.3 opacity) + characteristic Ditto Spirit Duplicator purple-violet methanol ink (#5e4a8c) for all hand-lettered text and the header rule lines, with slight per-character variation in ink density (some letters slightly heavier, some lighter, the way real ditto-master impressions came out unevenly across the page) + warm purple #2 pencil shadow (#7a6a9e) for the visitor's name-blank entries and their click-to-underline marks + a slightly darker ink-bleed purple (#3e2a6e) for the brighter cleaner underlines that register as correct hidden-word detections + faint solvent-stain irregular blotches in pale lavender (#c8b8d8 at 0.2 opacity) scattered across the page where the methanol pooled slightly during duplication + soft graphite-pencil eraser-smudge tan (#c8b896 at 0.5 opacity) for invalid guesses fading away + warm Lincoln Elementary classroom-fluorescent overhead light cast (#f5e8c4 at 0.15 opacity) across the upper-left of the worksheet suggesting the angle of the ceiling fixtures + a small wooden 'IN' basket rendered in oak-grain brown (#8a6a3a) at the lower-right corner as the TURN IN YOUR PAPER button + when earned, a metallic gold-foil star sticker (#e8b347 with #fff4c8 highlight and #a8852a shadow) pressed onto the upper-right corner — evoking the specific tactile feel of a hand-cranked spirit duplicator worksheet on October 23, 1962, the warm sweet methanol smell visible as the slight unevenness of impression across the page, and the small specific dignity of a teacher's careful pedagogical labor laid out in purple ink

mandatory_element.value: A fully working hidden-word puzzle interaction: (1) the worksheet's main paragraph contains exactly 8 first-letter acrostics camouflaged within natural-sounding 4th-grade story prose about the bluff walk (AUTUMN, MAPLE, RIVER, KESTREL, COTTONWOOD, SANDBAR, MILKWEED, GEESE — each spelled by the first letters of N consecutive words for word-length N, all 8 acrostics carefully placed so the surrounding prose reads naturally as a teacher's classroom storytelling), (2) the visitor solves by click-dragging across a consecutive run of words in the paragraph to mark them as a candidate hidden word (visible purple-pencil underline appearing beneath the selected word-run as the visitor drags, with smooth real-time selection rendering), (3) on release, the underlined run is validated: if the first letters spell one of the 8 hidden words AND the word hasn't already been found, the underline brightens to deeper-purple ink-bleed and a small purple-checkmark stamp appears beside that word's slot in the ruled answer box at the bottom (labeled 'WORDS YOU FOUND:' with 8 numbered ruled slots), with the found word's letters rendering in Mrs. Pfaff's hand-lettered purple style next to its checkmark; if invalid, the underline appears briefly as a soft pencil-pressure mark then fades with a small eraser-smudge animation over 4 seconds, (4) the 8 found-word slots persist in localStorage so a returning visitor sees their previously-found words still checked off in the answer box and the corresponding paragraph underlines still active, (5) a small editable NAME blank in the header accepts up to 24 characters and renders the visitor's input in purple #2 pencil hand (slight per-letter randomization in tilt and size), and a ROW blank accepts a single digit 1-6, both persisting in localStorage, (6) a TURN IN YOUR PAPER button at the bottom (rendered as Mrs. Pfaff's wooden 'IN' basket on the corner of her desk) activates only after all 8 words have been found across visits — when clicked, it triggers a small CSS animation of the worksheet sliding into the basket with a soft paper-shuffle sound (Web Audio API), then reveals beneath it Mrs. Pfaff's graded return: the worksheet now bears a hand-written 'EXCELLENT WORK — [visitor's name]' annotation in her own purple-ink hand and a gold-foil star sticker pressed onto the upper-right corner (the gold star rendered with metallic gradient and a tiny embossed five-point shape), (7) after the gold star is earned, a small folded slip appears beneath the worksheet with Mrs. Pfaff's handwritten note explaining that the eight hidden words are the eight specific things she most wanted the children to remember from the bluff walk on Friday — each of the 8 words gets a one-sentence specific recollection in her hand (e.g., 'KESTREL: Daniel and Marcus saw it dive and did not say anything about it but I watched their faces and I will remember those faces longer than they will'; 'MILKWEED: Sherrilyn Boudreau opened one pod and the seeds went everywhere and three children chased them and the rest of us stood and watched the seeds go'), and (8) a small subtle interaction in the lower-left corner where Mrs. Pfaff's hand-stamped duplication date appears: hovering exactly the date stamp reveals a faint additional pencil annotation in Mrs. Pfaff's hand reading 'cranked at 6:52am — Roland still asleep — coffee on'

wildcard.value: Exactly one of the 8 found-word reveal sentences in Mrs. Pfaff's handwritten follow-up note (the SANDBAR sentence specifically), when read aloud line-by-line, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about brown water, three children standing still, and a shape rising out of a river that has been moving for ten thousand years — disguised naturally within an earnest fourth-grade teacher's recollection of a moment her students did not know was important. Additionally, after the visitor has earned the gold star on three separate calendar dates (tracked in localStorage), a hidden 9th acrostic quietly activates in the paragraph — the word PFAFF spelled across five consecutive words near the very end of the story — which was not on the original puzzle key and which Mrs. Pfaff never told the children about because she did not want the puzzle to be about her. This unlock is not announced and not described in any UI text; it simply exists for visitors who kept coming back, and finding it adds a small handwritten margin note in Mrs. Pfaff's hand reading 'you found the one I left for myself'

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO horror-genre framing of the school, the empty classroom, the hand-cranked duplicator, the missing children, or Mrs. Pfaff's careful labor as creepy/uncanny/sinister/ghostly — this is a working public-school classroom in October 1962, the lights are on, the kids are at their desks, Mrs. Pfaff is at the front of the room, and the methanol smells sweet. NO 'the worksheet was found decades later in an abandoned school' framing. NO museum-archival 'remember when teachers used spirit duplicators' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the purple ditto worksheet,' NO cool ironic distance from 1960s public elementary education as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Mrs. Pfaff or Lincoln Elementary as already-vanished. NO 'kindly old schoolmarm' caricature, NO Norman-Rockwell-sentimental framing, NO Leave-It-To-Beaver pastiche. NO condescension toward fourth-grade pedagogy as unskilled labor, toward hand-cranked duplication as primitive, or toward Mrs. Pfaff's nineteen years in the Davenport schools as a small wistful career. NO 'kids these days' contrast with present-day classrooms. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the worksheet as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding. NO radio/CB/broadcast/transmission framing. This IS LIVING, PRESENT-TENSE skilled fourth-grade-teacher craft happening RIGHT NOW on Tuesday morning October 23, 1962 in Davenport, Iowa — Mrs. Pfaff is at her desk at 6:52am cranking the duplicator handle, Roland is asleep at home, the children will arrive at 8:10am, and the bluff walk was last Friday and is still vivid in her head. Treat the worksheet as a serious working pedagogical document, treat Mrs. Pfaff as a legitimate working professional teacher with nineteen years of expertise and real love for her students, treat the eight hidden words as small deliberate gifts a teacher can hide inside a routine assignment, and treat the visitor solving the puzzle as real intellectual work worthy of full peer-to-peer respect and a real gold star

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1936 — peak American Federal Art Project poster-printing era under the WPA, the specific moment when silkscreen workshops in the Federal Art Project's Poster Division on East 39th Street in Manhattan were producing two-color and three-color flat-ink posters for the National Park Service, the Department of Agriculture, and public-health campaigns at a rate of roughly 200 finished posters per day across 17 cities; the bold geometric flat-color aesthetic developed by working printmakers Anthony Velonis, Vera Bock, and Beatrice Wilcox who pulled hand-cut stencil screens over heavy poster stock with rubber-edged squeegees, registering each color pass by eye against pinhole alignment marks; the entire infrastructure of relief-era public visual communication built on flat geometric shapes, hand-cut typography, simplified iconic illustration, and limited ink palettes constrained by the cost of pigment — a visual language of bold silhouette and confident negative space

format.value: A full-viewport silent generative weaving piece — the browser window IS a continuously-building two-color flat-ink WPA-style poster that draws itself, layer by layer, in real-time and never quite finishes; pure SVG shapes assembling against the cream poster-stock ground in slow procedural choreography: a flat geometric silhouette of a fictional mountain range builds upward from the bottom edge one trapezoidal mass at a time over roughly 40 seconds, then a stylized sun-disc rises slowly behind the mountains as a solid amber circle, then a sweeping band of stylized cloud-shapes pushes across the upper third of the poster in flat brick-red ink, then a single geometric bird-silhouette flies across the composition from left to right in 6 seconds and disappears off the right edge, then the entire poster gently fades and the layering begins again with a new randomized composition (different mountain rhythm, different sun position, different cloud pattern, different bird timing); the cursor position acts as a virtual pinhole registration shift — moving the cursor causes the brick-red ink layer and the amber ink layer to drift very slightly out of register relative to the cream paper ground, simulating a hand-pulled silkscreen with imperfect registration, and the visitor can find the perfect alignment by holding the cursor at the exact center of the viewport; no text, no UI, no captions, no buttons, no controls, no instructions, no title — only the poster building itself forever in two flat inks against cream paper stock

subject.value: The continuous unfinished work of the Federal Art Project Poster Division silkscreen room at 235 East 39th Street in Manhattan in the fall of 1936 — specifically the kind of generic National Park Service promotional poster that working printmaker Beatrice Wilcox might pull off her screen at 2:40pm on a Thursday afternoon as part of the day's 47-poster run, a hand-cut two-color flat-ink composition of mountain-and-sky-and-bird that was never meant to depict any particular park but instead a generalized geometric American landscape readable from across a post office lobby — the poster itself as a working public-art object produced by named hands in a real workshop with real ink and real squeegees, the kind of bold flat-color visual communication that the WPA poster division produced by the millions across the eight years of the program; the strangeness is that the poster on the viewport is not a fixed object but a continuous making — the silkscreen pulls keep happening, the composition keeps re-registering, the bird keeps flying across, and the work never finishes because the work never finished

tone.value: No tone — there is no narrator, no voice, no text visible on the page at any point. The piece speaks only through the slow patient assembly of flat ink against cream paper. The closest analogue to a voice is the silent steady focus of Beatrice Wilcox at her screen, pulling pull after pull, registering each color pass by eye, the present-tense unhurried craft of a working printmaker at 2:40pm on a Thursday in autumn, indifferent to being observed, the visual equivalent of standing in a doorway watching skilled hands do skilled work without interrupting

palette.value: Warm cream poster stock ground (#f1e4c4) with subtle visible long-fiber paper texture and tiny faint pinhole alignment marks at the four corners (#a08868 at 0.3 opacity) + flat WPA brick-red silkscreen ink (#a8352a) for the cloud band, the sun-disc, the bird silhouette, and the upper register pass (rendered as solid flat fills with the very slight uneven edge that a hand-pulled screen produces — tiny irregularity along the contour where the squeegee pressure varied) + deep forest-green silkscreen ink (#2d5a3a) for the mountain mass and the lower register pass + warm afternoon-workshop window-light cast (#f5d89a at 0.12 opacity) falling diagonally across the upper-left of the poster suggesting the East 39th Street skylight at 2:40pm + faint dried-ink fingerprint smudge (#7a3a2e at 0.18 opacity) at the lower-right corner of the poster where Beatrice's thumb rested when she lifted the screen + subtle screen-mesh ghost texture (#a8352a at 0.08 opacity) barely visible inside the brick-red fills suggesting the silkscreen weave + when the cursor is at the exact center of the viewport, all registration marks align perfectly and the piece sits silent and clean; when the cursor drifts, the brick-red and forest-green layers shift by 1-3 pixels in opposite directions, producing the soft chromatic-edge halo of an off-register print

mandatory_element.value: A fully working generative silkscreen-poster animation rendered in pure SVG: (1) a poster composition with three flat-ink layers (forest-green mountain mass, brick-red sun-disc and cloud band, brick-red bird silhouette) that assembles in a strict procedural sequence over roughly 50 seconds: first the mountain trapezoidal masses build upward from the bottom edge one shape at a time (minimum 5 trapezoids per mountain range, each appearing with a subtle CSS opacity transition simulating a single squeegee pull), then the amber sun-disc rises from behind the mountains as a solid filled circle with smooth vertical translation, then the cloud band pushes in from the right edge as 3-5 flat stylized cloud shapes that sweep across the upper third over 8 seconds, then the bird silhouette enters from the left edge and travels in a gentle arc across the composition to the right edge over 6 seconds, then the entire poster holds for 4 seconds in finished state, then fades over 3 seconds back to bare cream paper, then a new composition begins with procedurally randomized parameters (mountain count and proportions, sun-disc size and position, cloud rhythm, bird arc curvature, bird transit speed); (2) cursor-position-driven registration offset: as the cursor moves away from the exact center of the viewport, the brick-red layer shifts by up to 3 pixels in one direction and the forest-green layer shifts by up to 3 pixels in the opposite direction, both interpolating smoothly with the cursor position, simulating a hand-pulled silkscreen with imperfect color registration — when the cursor returns to dead center, all layers align perfectly; (3) per-pull edge irregularity: each shape, when it first appears, has a subtly randomized edge contour with tiny 1-2px squeegee-pressure variations along its outline (using SVG path slight-jitter), so no two pulls produce identical shapes even when the underlying composition parameters are the same; (4) absolutely no UI, no text overlay, no captions, no titles, no buttons, no controls, no instructions, no footer credits anywhere on the page; (5) the animation runs autonomously and loops forever; (6) a persistent localStorage counter tracks the total number of poster pulls the visitor has watched across all visits and silently shifts the per-pull edge-jitter parameters very gradually as the count grows, so a visitor returning for the hundredth time sees pulls that are imperceptibly cleaner-edged than the first pulls they ever watched — the way a working printmaker's hand steadies across an afternoon of repetition; (7) the bird silhouette's transit timing varies very slightly per pull within a 5-7 second range so that no two birds cross at the same speed

wildcard.value: Exactly once every 23 full poster pulls (deterministic, tracked in localStorage across visits), the bird silhouette pauses mid-arc for approximately 2 seconds — hovering motionless against the brick-red cloud band — before resuming its transit at the same speed and exiting the right edge. The visitor is given no indication this is happening, no acknowledgment in any UI, no reward for noticing — just the quiet fact that on this particular pull, the bird stopped

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO text, captions, labels-explaining-the-piece, title overlays, instructional UI, tooltip hints, loading messages, footer credits, watermarks, or any narrating voice anywhere on the page visible to the visitor — the piece must be entirely silent and entirely visual. NO faux-period poster slogans (no 'SEE AMERICA', no 'VISIT THE NATIONAL PARKS', no WPA-text pastiche of any kind — the posters being pulled are pre-text, the silkscreen room hasn't applied the typography layer yet). NO museum-archival 'remember when the WPA made posters' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of hand-pulled silkscreen,' NO cool ironic distance from Federal Art Project labor as quaint relief-era Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing of Beatrice Wilcox or the 39th Street workshop as already-vanished. NO Depression-era poverty-porn or noble-suffering framing of WPA workers. NO horror-genre framing of the empty workshop or the bird-that-stops or the endless re-pulling as creepy/uncanny/sinister — this is skilled craft happening in a working room with the skylight open. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding. NO radio/CB/broadcast/transmission framing. NO weather-app or photo-viewer interface conventions, NO settings panels, NO playback controls, NO speed sliders, NO color-pickers. The poster builds itself, the cursor adjusts registration, the bird flies across, and the visitor watches in silence — that is the entire piece

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1987 — peak American mass-market home electronics catalog era for novelty desk gadgets, specifically the fall 1987 Sharper Image catalog moment when the back pages of mail-order glossy catalogs featured small battery-operated executive desk toys: tiny chrome cradle pendulums, miniature Newton's cradles, magnetic levitation pen sets, and the briefly-popular line of pocket-sized novelty 'fortune teller' electronic devices manufactured in Taiwan and Hong Kong for distribution through Hammacher Schlemmer, Brookstone, and the Sharper Image — pressed-aluminum housings with single LED display windows, one rubber button on top, a CR2032 coin battery in the back, and a small printed instruction card folded inside the cardstock blister-pack telling you to ask a yes-or-no question silently in your head, press the button, and read the answer in the tiny red LED window. The specific Reagan-era office-culture pleasure of a battery-operated novelty fortune device sitting on a middle manager's desk next to the Rolodex and the tape dispenser, pressed roughly forty times a week by the manager himself and occasionally by the secretary and once by the regional VP who came through on a Tuesday in October and asked the device whether the Cleveland office would hit Q4 numbers and laughed at the answer.

format.value: A fully working in-browser simulation of one specific 1987 novelty desk-toy fortune device — the FORTUNATRON 200, a small pressed-aluminum pocket-sized oracle manufactured in Taipei for the Hammacher Schlemmer fall 1987 catalog (catalog item #4419-B, $19.95, shipped in a printed blister-pack with a folded instruction card) — rendered as the actual device sitting at center-viewport on a 1987 office-desk surface (faux-walnut laminate visible at the edges, a corner of a Rolodex card and a Post-it note peeking in from the periphery). The device has: (1) a polished brushed-aluminum body roughly proportioned to fit in a closed hand, (2) a small red-on-black LED display window at the top center (eight segments wide, the kind of warm dim red LED used in 1987 calculator displays), (3) one mushroom-shaped rubber CONSULT button on top labeled in tiny silkscreened white type 'PRESS WHILE THINKING YOUR QUESTION', (4) a thin chrome bezel around the display, (5) a small printed brand wordmark FORTUNATRON 200 along the lower face in 1987-corporate-italic sans-serif, (6) a tiny printed line 'MADE IN TAIWAN' along the bottom edge, (7) a small toggle switch on the side labeled MODE I / MODE II / MODE III that selects between three oracle voices (MODE I: straightforward yes/no/maybe, MODE II: cryptic single-word, MODE III: full sentence answers that scroll across the LED). The visitor presses the CONSULT button (real click with satisfying rubber-dome depression animation and a soft mechanical click sound via Web Audio API), the LED window flickers through a 2-3 second 'consulting' animation (random segments lighting in sequence, simulating the device 'thinking'), then displays a specific answer that scrolls across the LED in warm red dot-matrix segments at roughly 4 characters per second the way real 1987 scrolling LED novelty devices did. The device runs on a small simulated battery whose charge depletes by a tiny fraction with each consultation (visible as a tiny BAT indicator that dims very slowly across hundreds of uses), and a small CHANGE BATTERY button on the back (visible by clicking-and-holding to rotate the device 180 degrees, revealing the back face with the CR2032 battery compartment and the FCC ID sticker and the 'PROPERTY OF: ___________' write-on label) lets the visitor reset the battery whenever they want.

subject.value: The specific Fortunatron 200 unit serial #FT-19847, manufactured at the Hsin-Chuang Industrial District plant of Tai-Ming Electronics Co. Ltd. in Taipei in August 1987, shipped via container freight to the Hammacher Schlemmer distribution warehouse in West Caldwell, New Jersey in late September 1987, fulfilled as part of Order #88-4419-B-22014 on October 14, 1987 to Mr. Harlan Voss of 3717 Briarwood Crescent in Shaker Heights, Ohio (regional sales manager for Cleveland Industrial Belting, age 44, married to Joyce, two teenage daughters), where it sat on the right-hand corner of his metal Steelcase desk at the Cleveland Industrial Belting office on Carnegie Avenue between a chrome pen-cup holding three Bic Round Stic pens and a small framed photograph of his daughters at Cedar Point in summer 1986. Harlan pressed the CONSULT button forty-three times across the first week he had it (asked it whether the Akron account would close by Halloween, whether Joyce was going to like the anniversary dinner reservation at the Top of the Town, whether the Browns were going to make it past the Steelers on Sunday, whether his back was going to feel better by morning if he slept on the heating pad, whether his daughter Karen should switch from clarinet to oboe in the school band), and across the next nineteen months pressed it approximately 1,847 more times, never once asking it the question he actually wanted to ask, which was whether he was going to be okay. The device is still in his desk drawer at home in 2026 — Harlan retired in 2009, Joyce passed in 2019, the battery was changed three times across the decades by Harlan himself with a small jeweler's screwdriver — and it still works, and he still occasionally presses the button while sitting at the kitchen table on Tuesday mornings, and the device still gives him answers. The visitor's session is one continuous press-session on this specific Fortunatron, sitting where Harlan would be sitting, asking the questions Harlan asks.

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer voice belonging to the Fortunatron 200 itself — not Harlan, not a narrator, not a museum docent, but the small pressed-aluminum oracle device speaking directly to the visitor through the LED window the way it has spoken to Harlan since October 1987, conversational warmth in its scrolling responses without ever being precious or twee, matter-of-fact certainty in its yes/no/maybe replies, genuine peer-level engagement with the visitor as the latest in a long line of people who have pressed its button, present-tense steadiness of a working novelty fortune device that takes its job seriously even though everyone knows the answers are pseudo-random, occasional dry warmth in MODE III sentence answers that suggests the device has been listening to Harlan for thirty-nine years and has formed some opinions, no irony about being a novelty desk toy, no embarrassment about being made in Taiwan for $4.30 wholesale, no melancholy about Harlan or Joyce or the years passing — the device is in good working order, the battery is fresh, the LED is bright, and it is ready to answer your question. Full earnest belief that a battery-operated pressed-aluminum oracle from a 1987 mail-order catalog is a legitimate small instrument for thinking through a question, and that the visitor's question — whatever it is — deserves a real considered answer.
