forbidden_register.value: melancholy soft autumn

era.value: 1908 — peak American small-town summer-evening porch firefly-watching era, the very specific moment in early-twentieth-century American childhood when the screened-in front porches of frame houses on tree-lined residential streets in places like Bloomington Indiana and Athens Ohio and Decatur Illinois became, during the brief three-week window of late June through mid-July, the staging ground for a quiet domestic ritual of watching the field across the street fill with Photinus pyralis (the common Eastern firefly) beginning at approximately 8:47pm Central Daylight Time as full dusk settled into true night and the fireflies rose from the tall June grass in slow ascending zigzags, the male beetles signaling with their species-specific J-shaped half-second flash pattern at six-second intervals while the females answered from the grass with a brief two-second response flash two seconds after the male's J, the entire courtship infrastructure of a small native beetle species coordinating its mating across an entire midwestern field while a family of five sat on the porch in wicker chairs with glass tumblers of lemonade and watched without speaking; the specific patient hobby-naturalist practice among amateur entomologists of the era — schoolteachers, country doctors, retired surveyors, the rare college-trained woman botanist — of keeping a small leather-bound flash-log notebook on the porch railing in which they recorded the evening's firefly activity in fine-tipped fountain-pen ink, noting the species observed (Photinus pyralis being most common, with occasional Photuris versicolor appearing later in the evening), the estimated flash frequency, the field temperature read from a small porch-mounted thermometer, the cloud cover, and any unusual behavioral observations, the entire practice carried on as a small private contribution to the slowly-accumulating national amateur knowledge of North American Lampyridae that was being collated by Professor Frank Alpheus McDermott of the U.S. Department of Agriculture's Bureau of Entomology in his ongoing 1907-1911 firefly census; the specific evening of Wednesday, July 8, 1908, at the porch of the Hollenbeck residence at 414 Linden Street in Bloomington Indiana, when retired surveyor Mr. Adelbert Hollenbeck (age 63, amateur naturalist since boyhood, contributor to the McDermott census since 1907) sat in his wicker chair with his flash-log notebook open and watched the field across the street fill with what he later recorded as 'an estimated 4,700 individuals in active display' — a typical evening's count for the second week of July at that latitude.

format.value: A single full-viewport silent generative piece rendered in pure SVG and JavaScript: the entire browser window is the view from Mr. Hollenbeck's porch at 414 Linden Street looking out across Linden Street into the meadow at 9:14pm on Wednesday July 8, 1908, depicted as a deep blue-black field of summer dusk with the silhouettes of two tall elm trees at the far edge against a slightly lighter horizon-band and the faint suggestion of a wooden fence at the field's near edge — and rising from the meadow are approximately 340 individual fireflies, each one a precisely-programmed Photinus pyralis male executing his species-specific J-shaped half-second courtship flash on a six-second interval, with each beetle's flash-path animated as a small warm yellow-green luminous trail that draws itself in a brief rising J-curve over exactly 0.5 seconds (bright peak at the curve's hook, fading along the trail) before going dark for the remainder of its six-second cycle, all 340 individuals out of phase with one another so the meadow continuously twinkles with new J-flashes appearing every few hundredths of a second; mixed in among the Photinus pyralis are approximately 28 Photuris versicolor females (slightly higher in the field, different flash signature — a longer single 1.2-second steady glow on an irregular schedule) who occasionally answer with a predatory mimicry flash that draws a nearby Photinus toward her position, after which the Photinus disappears from the field permanently (Photuris females eat Photinus males — a real ecological fact rendered as a small quiet event roughly every 90 seconds); each firefly's vertical position in the meadow is tied to its mating-flight altitude (Photinus rise slowly across each cycle, drifting upward 1-2 pixels per flash), so over the course of the visit the meadow's overall flash-cloud gradually rises from grass-level toward elm-canopy level; the cursor's position influences nothing — the meadow does not care that the visitor is on the porch, and the fireflies will continue their patient courtship whether anyone is watching or not.

subject.value: The specific Photinus pyralis courtship activity in the meadow across from 414 Linden Street, Bloomington Indiana, on the evening of Wednesday, July 8, 1908, between approximately 8:47pm and 10:30pm Central Daylight Time — a field temperature of 78°F at dusk dropping to 71°F by 10pm, partly cloudy with a waxing gibbous moon rising in the east, the second week of peak Lampyridae display season at this latitude, with an estimated 4,700 active Photinus pyralis males signaling with their characteristic J-flash and an estimated 280 Photuris versicolor females also active in the field, several of the Photuris employing predatory mimicry flashes to lure and consume Photinus males (a behavior first formally documented in the entomological literature by J.E. Lloyd in 1965 but observed and quietly puzzled over by amateur naturalists like Mr. Hollenbeck decades earlier — Mr. Hollenbeck has noted in his flash-log seven evenings this season the specific anomaly of certain females whose answer-flash 'rings false in some way I cannot quite name' and whose nearby males 'do not return to the air after approaching them,' a passage from his July 3 entry that he has underlined twice in fountain pen and that he is planning to include in his September contribution to Professor McDermott's census); Mr. Hollenbeck himself, age 63, in his wicker chair on the porch with the leather-bound flash-log open on his knee, a stub of #2 pencil tucked behind his ear, the lemonade glass sweating onto the wicker side-table, his wife Cordelia in the next chair quietly reading a copy of the Atlantic Monthly by the light of a small kerosene porch lamp turned low so as not to interfere with the field's natural darkness, and the field itself — the entire small ecological-romantic infrastructure of approximately five thousand beetles coordinating a species-specific courtship across one specific meadow on one specific Wednesday evening in 1908, observed but not interfered with by one specific patient retired surveyor and his wife.

tone.value: No tone — there is no narrator, no voice, no text visible on the page at any point. The piece speaks only through the silent patient rising-and-fading of small luminous J-curves across a dark meadow. The closest analogue to a voice is the silent steady attentiveness of Mr. Hollenbeck himself in his wicker chair: present-tense, unhurried, neither performing nor explaining, the visual equivalent of sitting on a porch in July 1908 and watching a field do what it does every summer evening for the three weeks that this particular species pairs off and continues itself, with no expectation that the field perform for the observer and no interpretation imposed on what the field is doing — only the quiet patient act of watching small lights rise and fade in a dark place where small lights have risen and faded every summer for ten thousand years.

palette.value: Deep summer-dusk indigo (#0a1428) as the dominant field of view with subtle vertical gradient lightening toward the horizon band (#1c2848 along the upper third where the sky still holds a faint memory of sunset) + deep elm-tree silhouette near-black (#050810) for the two trees on the far edge of the meadow + slightly warmer mid-distance grass-shadow blue-black (#0e1830) for the meadow's mid-field + faint fence-rail silhouette dark gray (#1a1f2e) for the near-edge wooden fence + warm Photinus pyralis flash yellow-green (#dceb6e) at peak brightness with a soft luminous bloom halo (#9eb45a at 0.5 opacity, 6-10px soft blur) around each active flash + slightly cooler trail-fade green (#5e7438) along the J-curve trail as each flash decays + Photuris versicolor predatory mimicry flash slightly warmer and longer-glowing yellow (#e8d878) with a wider, slower-decaying halo + soft waxing-gibbous moonlight cast (#3a4868 at 0.12 opacity) very faintly bleeding into the upper-left of the viewport from the rising eastern moon + barely-perceptible warm porch-lamp kerosene amber (#e8a73a at 0.04 opacity) bleeding in from the lower-right corner of the viewport suggesting Cordelia's reading lamp turned very low + the faintest possible suggestion of stars in the upper sky-band as tiny cool white points (#dce4f0 at 0.4 opacity) just barely visible — evoking the specific quality of late-summer dusk in southern Indiana when the indigo sky still has the last memory of daylight in its upper band, the meadow is dark enough for the fireflies to register as small bright J-curves of luminous yellow-green against the deeper blue-black, and a single low porch lamp gives just enough warmth to read by without disturbing the field's natural darkness.

mandatory_element.value: A fully working generative firefly meadow rendered in pure SVG and JavaScript with the following functional behaviors all simulated correctly and continuously: (1) exactly 340 individual Photinus pyralis male instances, each with its own independent position in the meadow (randomized initial x within the viewport width and initial y within the lower 60% of the viewport), its own independent six-second flash-cycle phase offset (randomized between 0 and 6 seconds at page load so flashes are evenly distributed throughout the cycle), and its own independent gentle horizontal drift velocity (between -0.3 and +0.3 pixels per second) plus a slow upward rise velocity (between 0.15 and 0.4 pixels per second) so the entire flash-cloud gradually rises across the visit; (2) each Photinus flash rendered as a precisely J-shaped SVG path that draws itself over 500ms with stroke-dashoffset animation — the path begins at the firefly's current position, rises 14-22 pixels vertically while curving 6-10 pixels to one side (left or right randomized per individual), forming the species-characteristic J-hook — and the path's stroke brightness peaks at the hook of the J (where Photinus pyralis biologically emits its brightest light) then fades over the remaining cycle interval to invisibility, with a soft luminous bloom halo around the active portion of the path using SVG filter feGaussianBlur; (3) exactly 28 Photuris versicolor female instances rendered at slightly higher positions in the meadow (within the middle 30% of the viewport vertically), each with a distinct flash signature: a steady 1.2-second glow at a single position (no J-curve — Photuris females flash from a stationary perch in the grass) on an irregular schedule between 8 and 14 seconds, with a slightly warmer-yellow color and a wider slower-decaying bloom halo than the Photinus males; (4) predatory mimicry behavior: roughly every 90 seconds (with random jitter ±20 seconds), one Photuris female selects the nearest Photinus male within a 120-pixel radius and emits a predatory mimicry flash that imitates the female Photinus response pattern (a 2-second steady glow 2 seconds after a nearby male's J-flash) — when this happens, the targeted Photinus male's drift velocity is overridden to move directly toward the Photuris female over the next 4-6 seconds, and upon reaching her position the Photinus male flash goes permanently dark and the individual is removed from the active population, with the Photuris female briefly flaring slightly brighter for 0.8 seconds after the consumption event; (5) the population of Photinus pyralis is continuously replenished — for every male consumed by a Photuris, a new male rises from the bottom edge of the meadow within the next 30 seconds and begins its own independent flash cycle (so the meadow's total active flash-population stays roughly at 340 across the entire visit); (6) the overall meadow rises across the visit: across each 10-minute window, the average Photinus altitude drifts upward by approximately 40 pixels as the mating flight progresses, with new replenishment males starting at grass-level — so a visitor staying for a long time sees the flash-cloud gradually rise from grass-level toward canopy-level; (7) the waxing-gibbous moon's faint cast in the upper-left of the viewport very slowly brightens by approximately 8% across each 15-minute period as the moon rises higher in the eastern sky, then resets gently at the start of a new visit; (8) the field temperature is silently simulated: a hidden value starts at 78°F at page load and drops by 0.3°F per minute toward an asymptote of 70°F, and as the temperature drops, the Photinus flash interval lengthens slightly (from 6.0 seconds at 78°F to 7.2 seconds at 70°F — a real entomological fact about firefly metabolism being temperature-dependent), so a visitor staying for an hour notices that the flash rhythm has slowed perceptibly compared to when they arrived; (9) absolutely no UI text anywhere on the page — no instructions, no tooltips, no captions, no header, no title, no footer, no controls, no buttons, no loading messages, no credits; (10) the cursor position has no effect on anything — the meadow does not respond to the visitor's presence, the fireflies will continue their courtship regardless of whether anyone is watching, and the visitor's only role is to sit on the porch and observe; (11) the elm trees, fence rail, horizon gradient, moon position, and porch-lamp amber bleed are all rendered as static SVG with no animation; the only motion in the entire piece is the fireflies themselves; (12) the piece runs autonomously forever — no reset, no loop point, no end state — the field continues its courtship for as long as the page is open, which is approximately the same way the field continued its courtship on the evening of July 8, 1908 from 8:47pm until well past midnight when Mr. Hollenbeck finally closed his flash-log notebook and went inside to sleep.

wildcard.value: Exactly once every 23 minutes (deterministic, tied to elapsed time since page load), a single firefly — one specific Photinus pyralis individual at a randomly-selected position in the meadow — produces an anomalous flash that does not match either the standard Photinus J-curve or any documented Lampyridae pattern: a brief steady half-second glow followed by three quick 0.1-second sub-flashes in rapid succession, the kind of unusual signature Mr. Hollenbeck noted in his flash-log on the evening of June 29, 1908 with the underlined annotation 'one individual whose flash I cannot place — perhaps a hybrid, perhaps an aberrant male, perhaps something not yet described — I shall write to Professor McDermott about it' (a letter Mr. Hollenbeck did mail on August 14, 1908, and which Professor McDermott did receive and file in his census correspondence but did not have time to investigate before his census project was concluded in 1911, and which remained in the McDermott archive at the U.S. Department of Agriculture until 1947 when it was destroyed in a basement-flood at the Bureau of Entomology, the specific observation never followed up by anyone). The visitor is given no indication this is happening, no acknowledgment in any UI, no reward for noticing — just the quiet fact that one of the fireflies in the meadow tonight is doing something that no entomologist has ever quite figured out.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO text, captions, labels-explaining-the-piece, title overlays, instructional UI, tooltip hints, loading messages, footer credits, watermarks, or any narrating voice anywhere on the page visible to the visitor — the piece must be entirely silent and entirely visual. NO museum-archival 'remember when fireflies were everywhere' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost summer evenings of childhood' or 'the disappearance of Photinus pyralis from suburban America,' NO cool ironic distance from porch-sitting culture as quaint pre-television Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Mr. Hollenbeck or Bloomington or 414 Linden Street as already-vanished. NO 'fireflies are disappearing due to light pollution' environmentalist messaging, NO climate-grief register, NO 'remember when summers were like this' wistfulness. NO horror-genre framing of the Photuris predatory mimicry as creepy/sinister/uncanny — it is a real ecological behavior happening in a real meadow, neither tragic nor monstrous, simply how Photuris females have evolved to feed. NO 'the fireflies are signaling something else' twist, NO suggestion that the anomalous 23-minute flash is supernatural or otherworldly. NO Studio-Ghibli fairyland-aesthetic prettification, NO Disney-magic-pixie-dust framing, NO Pinterest-quote 'and the fireflies danced for us' sentimentality. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the anomalous flash, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the meadow as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of amateur entomologists as fictional small-town society, NO radio/CB/broadcast/transmission framing of the firefly courtship as signal-network metaphor (it is not a signal network metaphor — it is a real species-specific bioluminescent communication system and it should be rendered as exactly what it is). NO weather-app or photo-viewer interface conventions, NO settings panels, NO playback controls, NO speed sliders, NO color-pickers, NO 'pause the meadow' buttons, NO 'how many fireflies tonight' counters visible to the visitor. NO Walt-Whitman barbaric-yawp Americana, NO Mary-Oliver poem-quote register, NO Thoreau 'simplify simplify' philosophical handwringing. The meadow exists, the fireflies rise and fade, the Photuris occasionally consume a Photinus, the temperature slowly drops, the moon slowly rises, the field continues, and the visitor watches in silence — that is the entire piece.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1978 — peak American suburban Polish-American Catholic-parish Easter butter-lamb-and-pisanki season, the very specific moment in the week before Holy Saturday when Polish-American grandmothers across the parish belt of Cleveland's near-west side, Hamtramck Michigan, and the Greenpoint section of Brooklyn would hand-letter onto small folded cream cardstock cards the full per-item contents of the Święconka basket they were preparing to carry to St. Stanislaus or St. Hyacinth or Our Lady of Częstochowa at the noon blessing on Saturday — a basket containing the carved butter lamb in its small glass dish with whole black peppercorn eyes, the boiled-and-dyed pisanki eggs in their nest of fresh boxwood sprigs, the small loaf of placek, the ring of dry kiełbasa from Sokołowski's market on Fleet Avenue, the small dish of salt, the slice of fresh chrzan horseradish, the tiny cake of butter molded into a paschal lamb shape with the small red banner of resurrection on a toothpick, the small square of white linen cloth embroidered by the grandmother's own mother in 1924 — and beside each item on the hand-lettered card, written in fountain pen in the grandmother's careful Polish-school cursive, the small private explanation of what that specific item meant in the family it came from: not the parish-bulletin generic theological meaning but the specific family meaning, why THIS family brings THIS item in THIS basket, what grandmother or great-aunt first added it in what specific year in what specific village in Galicia or Mazovia or near Lublin before the war, who is being remembered by its presence in the basket this year; the specific Holy Saturday of April 14, 1979, at the kitchen table of Mrs. Jadwiga 'Jadzia' Pawlikowski (age 71, widow of Stanisław Pawlikowski who passed in 1973, born Jadwiga Wójcik in the village of Krzepice in 1908, emigrated to Cleveland in 1929 at age 21 with a steamer trunk and a small bundle of pisanki-dye recipes folded inside her mother's prayer book) at 4218 Marvin Avenue in the Slavic Village neighborhood, where Jadzia is hand-lettering this year's basket-contents card to tuck inside the basket alongside the items, the way she has done every Easter for fifty years

format.value: A single hand-lettered cream-cardstock folded card titled 'CO ZAWIERA NASZ KOSZYK / WHAT OUR BASKET CONTAINS — for the blessing at noon, Holy Saturday, April 14th, 1979, St. Stanislaus Parish, Cleveland' — the kind of small private bilingual contents-card a Polish-American grandmother folds in half and tucks under the embroidered linen cover of her Święconka basket so that anyone who later opens the basket (the priest, her daughter-in-law, herself remembering in November) can read what each item is and why it is there

subject.value: Jadwiga 'Jadzia' Pawlikowski's 1979 Easter Święconka basket-contents card — the full bilingual hand-lettered inventory of the eleven items in her basket this year (the butter lamb baranek wielkanocny carved by her own hand from a half-pound of Land O'Lakes pressed into the cast-iron lamb mold her sister Stefa mailed from Krzepice in 1948, the dozen pisanki dyed with onion skins and beet juice in the same enamel pot her mother used in 1916, the small loaf of placek baked Wednesday with the recipe Stanisław's mother dictated to her in 1931 the first Easter they were married, the ring of dry kiełbasa from Sokołowski's that she picked up Thursday afternoon and that Mr. Sokołowski himself wrapped for her in butcher paper because he remembers her husband, the small glass cruet of salt the parish blessed in 1962 that she has been using ever since, the slice of fresh chrzan grated this morning from a root her son Roman brought from his garden in Parma, the small square of paschal butter molded into a lamb shape with the red-and-white resurrection banner on a toothpick, the linen napkin embroidered with Pisanki-pattern stitching by her mother Anna in 1924 in Krzepice, the small bundle of fresh boxwood bukszpan from the bush by the back porch that Stanisław planted in 1955, the small printed prayer card from Father Andrzejewski's 1971 funeral Mass that she carries in every basket since, and the one new item this year — a small enameled tin medallion of Our Lady of Częstochowa that her granddaughter Małgorzata gave her at Christmas) and the specific private family reasoning beside each item explaining what it carries forward and who in the family it remembers

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working-grandmother voice belonging to Jadzia herself at the kitchen table this Friday afternoon April 13 1979, the basket lined and ready on the counter, the cardstock folded on the oilcloth in front of her, the fountain pen her husband gave her for their twenty-fifth anniversary in 1956 in her hand, the radio playing low (WJMO with the Polish-language hour Mrs. Kowalczyk hosts every Friday at three), talking to the visitor (whoever reads the card — the priest at noon tomorrow, her daughter-in-law Helena who will help carry the basket, her granddaughter Małgorzata who is finally old enough this year to walk with them, herself in November when she opens the cedar chest and finds the card folded among the linens) the way she would talk to a sister of her own age sitting across the oilcloth helping her line her basket: matter-of-fact pride in fifty years of Holy Saturday baskets carried to fifty different noon-blessing services without missing one, peer-level warmth about each item as a real specific object with a real specific history that she carries in her head, conversational ease in switching between English and Polish mid-sentence the way she actually speaks when she isn't being careful for the Americans, present-tense Friday-afternoon focus on getting the card lettered in time to slide it under the linen before evening Mass, full earnest belief that a folded contents-card tucked into a basket is one of the more honest small documents a grandmother in 1979 can write, no apology for the homemade-Catholic-folk-tradition framing as sentimental, no embarrassment about the bilingual lettering, no self-consciousness about the family-specific reasoning beside each item being more important to her than the generic parish-bulletin meaning. Real specific affection for each of the eleven items as a peer addressing each of them by what they actually are in her kitchen this afternoon, not what they symbolize in the abstract.

palette.value: Warm aged cream cardstock (#f3e6c8) as the dominant card ground with subtle long-fiber texture and very faint horizontal pencil-ruling guides Jadzia drew before lettering (#c8b48a at 0.25 opacity, barely visible) + deep fountain-pen midnight-blue ink (#1c2a5e) for the primary Polish-and-English hand-lettering with subtle per-stroke pressure variation (some letters slightly heavier where the nib caught, occasional small ink-pool at the foot of descenders, faint feathering where the cream stock absorbed extra ink) + soft Easter-paschal brick-red secondary ink (#9e3220) for the small hand-drawn paschal banner on the butter-lamb illustration, the small cross-mark Jadzia draws beside the prayer-card entry, and the title-line CO ZAWIERA NASZ KOSZYK at the top + warm onion-skin pisanki amber (#c8893a) for the small hand-illustrated pisanki egg margin sketches beside the egg entry + deep beet-juice maroon (#7a2c3a) for the second pisanki dye-color illustration + warm Land-O'-Lakes butter-lamb cream-yellow (#f4d770) for the small butter-lamb sketch + fresh boxwood bukszpan green (#5a8a3a) for the small sprig illustration beside the boxwood entry + soft graphite-pencil annotation gray (#5a4a3a) for Jadzia's small marginal corrections and the few words she lightly sketched first before inking + warm Friday-afternoon kitchen-window light cast (#fff4c8 at 0.18 opacity) falling diagonally across the upper-right of the card suggesting the south-facing window above the sink at 3:40pm + faint oilcloth-shadow at the card's lower edge (#7a6450 at 0.25 opacity) suggesting the kitchen table the card rests on + tiny ink-blot (#1c2a5e at 0.5 opacity) near the entry for the linen napkin where the pen pooled when Jadzia paused mid-sentence to remember her mother's exact embroidery stitch + the card's folded crease running vertically down the center as a soft shadow line (#a8895a at 0.2 opacity) — evoking the specific tactile feel of a hand-lettered Polish-American Easter basket-contents card prepared in a Slavic Village kitchen on a Friday afternoon in April 1979, the warmth of fountain-pen ink drying on absorbent cream cardstock, and the quiet domestic gravity of fifty years of Holy Saturday baskets carried to noon blessings

mandatory_element.value: A fully working bilingual hand-lettered Święconka basket-contents card with the following functional behaviors all simulated correctly: (1) the card renders at full viewport scale as the folded cream cardstock object opened flat across the visitor's view, with the visible vertical fold-crease running down the center, the kitchen oilcloth surface visible at the edges, the lined Easter basket itself partially visible at the lower-right corner of the viewport with a corner of the embroidered linen cover folded back ready to receive the card; (2) the title CO ZAWIERA NASZ KOSZYK / WHAT OUR BASKET CONTAINS hand-lettered at the top of the card in Jadzia's careful Polish-school cursive (web font + per-character subtle randomization in slant 0.5-2 degrees, baseline drift, letter-spacing variation, fountain-pen pressure density ±15%, occasional ink-pool at descenders) followed by the date 'Wielka Sobota / Holy Saturday — April 14th, 1979 — St. Stanislaus Parish — noon blessing' in slightly smaller hand-lettering beneath; (3) eleven numbered item-entries arranged down the card, each entry consisting of: a small hand-illustrated margin sketch of the item (butter lamb, dozen pisanki, loaf of placek, ring of kiełbasa, glass cruet of salt, slice of chrzan, small molded paschal butter-lamb with banner, embroidered linen napkin, sprig of boxwood, small printed prayer card, enameled Our Lady of Częstochowa medallion) rendered in two-color hand-drawing (midnight-blue ink outlines with brick-red, amber, maroon, butter-yellow, or boxwood-green color-fills as appropriate per item); the Polish item name in deep fountain-pen ink with a hand-drawn slash followed by the English translation; and beneath each item-name the specific private family-reasoning paragraph in Jadzia's smaller cursive (4-7 lines per item) explaining what THIS item means in THIS family, who first added it in what specific year in what specific village, what grandmother or sister or husband or godparent is being remembered by its presence, what the visitor should know about it that the parish-bulletin generic theology does not say; (4) each of the eleven item-entries is hoverable to reveal a soft warm Friday-afternoon spotlight on that entry and fades in a small tucked-in slip of cream stationery in the right margin of the card with an additional handwritten margin annotation from Jadzia containing a peer-level personal vignette that does not appear in the main entry text — minimum 11 distinct margin annotations, each its own specific private vignette (e.g., on the butter-lamb entry the margin slip reads 'Stefa mailed me the lamb mold from Krzepice in October 1948 wrapped in three layers of newspaper and a piece of her own dress. The letter inside said the mold was our mother's from before the war and Stefa had been keeping it for me since 1939. I have used it forty Easters now. It needs to be soaked in cold water for an hour before you press the butter or the lamb's tail breaks off — Stefa wrote that on the letter and I have remembered it every year'; on the kiełbasa entry 'Mr. Sokołowski's father knew Stanisław's father in the old country. When I go in on Thursday before Easter he does not let me say what I want, he wraps the ring himself in the back, ties it with the white string, and brings it out with his hands folded. He has done this since Stanisław passed. I leave the money on the counter and we do not speak about it'; on the prayer-card entry 'Father Andrzejewski baptized Roman in 1942 and married Roman and Helena in 1968 and buried Stanisław in 1973 and at his own funeral in 1971 they handed these cards out and I have kept one in every basket since because he blessed every Holy Saturday basket I ever brought to St. Stanislaus and it does not feel right to bring a basket without him in it somehow'), no two annotations recycling the same phrasing or pattern; (5) a working CLICK TO TURN OVER mechanism at the lower-right corner of the card — clicking the corner triggers a smooth CSS card-flip animation over 900ms revealing the back of the folded card, where Jadzia has hand-lettered a longer private note in her own voice (minimum 220 words) addressed 'Do tych, którzy otworzą ten koszyk / To those who open this basket' explaining in both Polish and English that she has prepared a basket every Holy Saturday for fifty years, listing the basket-blessings she remembers most (the 1932 blessing in Krzepice the spring before she left for America, the 1929 blessing at St. Stanislaus her first Easter in Cleveland when she did not yet know anyone, the 1942 blessing when Stanisław was overseas and she carried two baskets — one for herself and one for him in case he came home, the 1973 blessing the spring after Stanisław passed when Helena held her arm walking up the church steps, this year's 1979 blessing which is the first one her granddaughter Małgorzata is old enough to walk with them for) and ending with a small line in Polish only that Jadzia has chosen not to translate; (6) a working SLIDE THE CARD INTO THE BASKET button at the lower-right corner of the viewport (styled as the corner of the embroidered linen cover ready to be folded back over) which when clicked triggers a smooth CSS transform animation of the card folding closed along its center crease and sliding under the linen cover with a soft paper-on-linen rustle sound via Web Audio API, after which the basket itself becomes the central viewport element with the linen cover smoothed back over its contents and a small label 'KOSZYK GOTOWY / BASKET READY — for noon tomorrow' fading in beneath the basket in tiny fountain-pen hand; (7) the basket then opens back up to the unfolded card when clicked; (8) a small interaction along the title CO ZAWIERA NASZ KOSZYK — hovering exactly the title for 3 full seconds reveals a small additional annotation in even smaller cursive in the upper-left margin reading 'this is the fiftieth one. I started counting in 1929 when I carried my first basket up the steps of St. Stanislaus alone and not knowing the language well enough to understand most of what Father Wieczorek said over it. — J.P.'; (9) a working ADD A NOTE TO JADZIA'S BASKET field at the very bottom of the back of the card (revealed only after the visitor has flipped the card) where the visitor can type their own one-or-two-sentence note in cream-stationery style addressed to Jadzia or to Stanisław or to the basket itself — the typed note renders in a slightly different but equally careful hand (subtly more modern American cursive, different ballpoint blue) and persists in localStorage so a returning visitor sees their own note still tucked into the back of the card AND a shared persistent count at the very bottom reading 'NOTES SLID INTO THIS BASKET: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere adds a note (shared count across all visitors, starting at 47 — the imagined count of family members and parishioners and Father Andrzejewski who have ever slipped a small note into one of Jadzia's baskets across her fifty Easter years); (10) absolutely no instructional UI text visible on the page explaining how to hover the entries or how to flip the card — no tooltips, no captions, no loading messages, no 'EXPLORE JADZIA'S BASKET' header, no footer credits — the visitor figures out the page by reading the card carefully the way Father Sieracki will read it tomorrow at noon when Jadzia opens her basket for him on the side altar; (11) the card persists in localStorage across visits — which annotations have been revealed, whether the card has been flipped, the visitor's own note slid into the back — so a returning visitor finds the card exactly as they left it last time, with the basket waiting on the kitchen counter for tomorrow's blessing; (12) the page never refreshes its contents and never generates new items — it is one specific card, the one Jadzia is lettering this Friday afternoon for the basket she will carry to noon Mass tomorrow, and reading it carefully is the entire page.

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Jadzia's 11 hover-revealed margin annotations — the one tucked alongside the entry for the boxwood bukszpan sprig — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about a back porch, a bush her husband planted, and a green that comes back every spring whether anyone is watching or not — disguised naturally within an earnest grandmother's peer-level note about pruning the bush in March so the Easter sprigs will be the right length. Additionally, after a returning visitor has flipped the card and slid their own note into the back on three separate calendar dates (tracked in localStorage), a hidden twelfth item quietly appears at the very bottom of the front of the card in slightly fresher fountain-pen ink — a single small item-entry numbered '12.' with no margin illustration, reading in Polish 'Mała kartka z imionami wszystkich, którzy odeszli w tym roku' and in English beneath 'A small slip of paper with the names of everyone in the family who passed this year' — and beneath this entry Jadzia's reasoning paragraph reads: 'I add this slip every year on Holy Saturday morning. This year the slip has three names: my brother Władek in Krzepice (in February — Stefa wrote me the letter, it came two weeks late), Mrs. Helena Wojciechowska from down the block (she carried baskets to St. Stanislaus for sixty-three years and her last basket was Easter 1978 and we walked up the steps together that morning), and the small Pawlikowski cousin in Buffalo whose name I will not write here because she was three years old and her mother does not want her name written on anything yet. The slip goes in the basket with everything else and Father blesses it with everything else and afterward I burn the slip in the kitchen sink and rinse the ashes down the drain because the names have been carried to the blessing and that is what they came for. — J.P., Holy Saturday morning.' This twelfth entry is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact at the bottom of the card for visitors who kept coming back.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when Polish-Americans kept these traditions' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the Święconka basket,' NO cool ironic distance from Cleveland's Slavic Village or Hamtramck or Greenpoint Polish-American parish culture as quaint old-world Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Jadzia or St. Stanislaus or the 1979 noon blessing as already-vanished. NO 'where are they now' wistfulness about Jadzia grown old or passed, NO 'this card was found decades later in a cedar chest' framing, NO scrapbook-archival voice, NO Studs Terkel oral-history retrospect register. NO horror-genre framing of the basket, the carved butter lamb, the prayer card from Father Andrzejewski's funeral, the burned slip of paper with the names of the dead, or the empty Saturday-afternoon kitchen as creepy/uncanny/sinister/gothic-Catholic — this is a working Polish-American Catholic grandmother preparing a basket for tomorrow's noon blessing the way she has prepared one every Holy Saturday for fifty years, the kitchen radio is on, the boxwood bush is in bud, the kiełbasa is wrapped in butcher paper on the counter, and Father Sieracki is going to bless the basket at noon tomorrow alongside two hundred other baskets at the side altar of St. Stanislaus. NO 'the basket is haunted' twist, NO 'the names on the burned slip return' supernatural implication, NO Catholic-folk-magic exoticization. NO condescension toward folk-religious practice as superstitious or naive, NO smug modern superiority about pre-Vatican-II ritual culture, NO 'imagine carrying a basket of food to a priest to be blessed' framing. NO Pope-John-Paul-II political-Polish-nationalism framing (the card is dated April 1979, six months after his election — but Jadzia's basket has nothing to do with that and the page does not reference it). NO 'authentic ethnic' marketing register, NO foodie-Instagram 'discover Polish Easter traditions' tone, NO travel-magazine 'a window into old-world heritage' voice. NO condescension toward immigrant grandmothers as folksy or quaint, NO 'salt-of-the-earth' caricature, NO Babcia stereotype, NO accented-English pastiche in the Polish-English code-switching (Jadzia speaks both languages fluently and switches naturally — she does not speak broken English). NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the burned slip or the unrevealed Polish-only line on the back of the card, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the basket as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of Polish-American parish-women as fictional small-town society. NO radio/CB/broadcast/transmission/dispatcher framing of the parish network or the basket-carrying tradition as signal-relay metaphor. NO Wes-Anderson-symmetrical-frame styling, NO Pinterest-bouquet-of-pisanki aesthetic. NO bittersweet diaspora-elegy framing — Jadzia left Krzepice in 1929 and built a life in Cleveland and she does not consider herself between two countries, she considers herself a woman in Cleveland with a sister in Krzepice and a basket to prepare for tomorrow. NO instructional UI text visible on the page explaining how to use the card or how to flip it — the visitor figures it out by hovering and clicking. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'EXPLORE JADZIA PAWLIKOWSKI'S EASTER BASKET' header, NO footer credits. The page IS the cream cardstock card on the oilcloth at 3:40pm Friday April 13 1979, the visitor IS sitting across the kitchen table from Jadzia (or is Father Sieracki opening the basket tomorrow, or is Helena unfolding the linen cover Easter Sunday morning, or is Małgorzata in November opening the cedar chest and finding the card still folded among the linens), and the only voice anywhere on the page is Jadzia's voice in her fountain-pen Polish-and-English hand-lettering and her soft cream-stationery margin annotations and her longer private note on the back of the card. Treat the basket-contents card as a serious working private family document, treat Jadzia as a legitimate working grandmother with fifty years of Holy Saturday basket-preparation expertise and a real running domestic-religious craft practice, treat the eleven items in the basket as real specific objects with real specific histories that matter to her, treat the bilingual code-switching as natural and dignified fluency in two languages, treat the visitor's careful reading of each item-reasoning and each margin annotation and the back-of-card note as real participation in a real act of fifty-year Holy Saturday family witness worthy of full peer-to-peer respect, and treat the small private fact that Jadzia burns the slip of paper with the year's dead in the kitchen sink after the blessing as a real working personal practice she has decided is the right thing to do — neither pious nor furtive, simply what she does.

length.description: something to explore, multiple sections, interactive elements

era.value: 1973 — peak American small-town gas-station-courtesy-counter pocket pinball novelty era, the specific moment when truck-stop courtesy counters and bowling-alley snack-bar shelves stocked a brief proliferation of palm-sized handheld bagatelle-pinball toys manufactured in Hong Kong and distributed through American jobber catalogs at wholesale 28¢ each — pressed-tin frames roughly 4×6 inches with a domed clear-plastic playfield cover, a thumb-flicked spring-loaded ball plunger along the right edge, a printed cardstock playfield insert featuring a wraparound carnival-midway scene with numbered scoring cups punched into the cardstock at specific point values, and three or four tiny steel BBs that the player would flick one at a time up the alley and watch ricochet down through brass pins glued into the playfield until each BB came to rest in a numbered cup; a 12-year-old's prized 88¢ purchase from the rotating wire rack beside the cigarette case at the Sinclair station on Route 6 outside Sandwich, Illinois, played by hundreds of thousands of kids in the back seats of station wagons across the summer driving vacation of 1973 between Stuckey's stops; the specific tactile pleasure of the spring plunger's brass tab against a callused thumb, the dry rattle of three BBs settling against the bottom rail, and the small private satisfaction of running the score past 200 on a single round of three balls

format.value: A fully working pocket bagatelle-pinball toy filling the viewport as the actual pressed-tin frame seen from directly above through its clear domed plastic cover — the visitor flicks the brass spring plunger along the right edge by click-and-drag-down-release, the steel BB launches up the curved alley with real momentum, bounces and ricochets off the field of brass pins with genuine 2D physics (velocity, friction against the cardstock playfield, elastic collisions against pins, gravity pulling it back down), and finally settles into one of the numbered scoring cups — three BBs per round, a running score that wraps when it passes 999, and a tiny mechanical click-clack sound profile via Web Audio API for every pin contact

subject.value: The single specific pocket bagatelle toy 'CARNIVAL MIDWAY POCKET PINBALL — MODEL 88' manufactured by Wing Hing Toy Manufactory Ltd. of Kowloon Hong Kong, distributed in the United States by the Acme Novelty Jobbers wholesale catalog of Chicago Illinois in spring 1973, sold for 88¢ at the rotating wire rack beside the cigarette case at the Sinclair gas station on Route 6 outside Sandwich Illinois in late June 1973 to a 12-year-old named Linda Vacek of 814 Plumtree Lane in Cicero who was in the back seat of her family's 1968 Ford Country Squire on the way to a week at her grandparents' lake cabin in Lake Geneva Wisconsin, and the specific small private hobby Linda developed across that one week of vacation of trying to get a single round of three BBs to clear 300 points by learning the exact plunger-spring tension that would send a BB up the alley fast enough to ricochet through the upper carnival-tent scoring zone but slow enough not to bounce out — the score she finally hit on the screened-in porch at the lake cabin on Thursday afternoon July 5, 1973, while her grandfather was reading the Milwaukee Sentinel in the next chair, was 387, and she remembers it

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working pinball-engineer voice — Wayne Neyens (real Gottlieb design legend, 1947-1983) reimagined here as the Hong Kong jobber-line consulting engineer who took the gig in 1972 because his sister's husband was running the Acme Novelty import desk and asked him for a favor on the bagatelle pin-pattern layout — Wayne speaking present-tense through the small hover-revealed margin annotations on the playfield about WHY each brass pin sits where it sits, peer-level pride in pin-density math and elastic-collision physics as a serious 25-year craft, conversational warmth toward the visitor as a fellow player who has slowed down enough to learn the plunger tension, full earnest belief that an 88¢ pocket toy deserves real engineering attention because some 12-year-old is going to play it five hundred times on a screened-in porch this summer and the pin layout had better reward patience. No 'simpler times' framing. No condescension toward Hong Kong novelty manufacturing as cheap. Wayne respects the work.

palette.value: Warm aged cream-printed-cardstock playfield insert (#f3e2b8) as the dominant viewport ground with subtle long-fiber texture and tiny halftone-dot graininess (#a08868 at 0.3 opacity) + two-color offset carnival-midway illustration printed across the cardstock in deep brick-red (#a8312a) and forest-pine green (#2d5a3a) showing the wraparound midway scene (a fortune-teller tent on the lower-left, a ring-toss booth upper-left, a calliope wagon center-top, a hot-buttered-popcorn vendor upper-right, a small Ferris wheel lower-right, hand-illustrated in the flat two-color style of 1960s pulp comic backgrounds) + scoring-cup numerals in deep composing-room black (#1a1612) hand-set in chunky carnival display type + brass pin caps glinting warm pin-brass amber (#c89548) with tiny lighter specular highlights (#fde8a8) catching overhead light + pressed-tin frame border in cool muted aluminum-gray (#a8a4a0) running around the viewport edges + clear domed plastic cover rendered as a subtle vignette of faint scratches (#7a6a58 at 0.15 opacity) and a soft top-light gleam (#fffaf0 at 0.25 opacity) along the upper-left dome curve + steel BB silver (#c0c4c8) with sharp tiny highlight (#fefdf8) and tiny moving shadow (#3a2818 at 0.5 opacity) cast onto the cardstock beneath as it travels + brass plunger spring rendered in warm coiled-brass amber (#b88838) along the right edge + warm screened-porch July-afternoon-window-light cast (#fff4c8 at 0.18 opacity) falling diagonally across the upper-left of the toy suggesting the lake-cabin afternoon sun + a single faded thumbprint smudge (#7a6244 at 0.3 opacity) on the clear dome above the plunger where Linda's left thumb has rested 500 times this week

mandatory_element.value: A fully working pocket bagatelle pinball simulation with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented: (1) the toy renders at full viewport scale as the actual pressed-tin pocket pinball seen from directly above, with the printed cardstock midway playfield visible beneath a subtly-scratched clear dome and the brass spring plunger along the right edge; (2) genuine 2D rigid-body physics for each steel BB — real velocity vector, real gravity pulling toward the bottom rail, real friction coefficient against the cardstock surface (BB slows over time without pin contact), real elastic collisions against minimum 42 individually-placed brass pins distributed across the playfield in Wayne's deliberate pattern (denser at the alley entry to scatter trajectories, sparser through the mid-field to allow flow, clustered around the high-value scoring cups to make them harder to reach), real collision with the perimeter rails of the playfield with damped bounce; (3) the spring plunger is interactive: click-and-hold on the plunger and drag downward to compress the spring (visible compression animation showing the brass coils squeezing tighter and a small tension indicator beside the plunger ranging 0% to 100%), release to fire — the BB launches up the right-side alley with launch velocity proportional to compression, so the visitor can tune the firing tension; (4) minimum 11 numbered scoring cups punched into the cardstock at specific point values (10, 25, 50, 100, 200 distributed across the field, plus a single difficult 500 cup in the upper carnival-tent zone) — when a BB comes to rest in a cup (velocity below threshold for 0.4 seconds while overlapping the cup), the cup briefly lights up, the score increments by the cup's value, a small click-clack-ding sound plays via Web Audio API, and the BB is removed from play; (5) three BBs per round, displayed in a small reserve indicator at the bottom-right of the toy showing how many remain; (6) each pin contact produces a short percussive 'tink' sound (Web Audio API, randomized pitch ±15% per hit to avoid mechanical repetition); each rail bounce produces a duller 'tonk'; each cup-settle produces a satisfying 'ker-plunk' followed by the bell-ding of the score increment; (7) running score display rendered as a small printed cardstock apron at the lower-edge of the playfield with the current round's score in chunky carnival display type — the score wraps when it passes 999 (rolling odometer-style with the digits flipping over via a brief vertical CSS transform); (8) at the end of each three-BB round, a small ROUND COMPLETE plate fades in beneath the apron showing the round's final score and a small NEW BALLS button (rendered as a small dispenser slot along the bottom edge of the toy) that resets the round when clicked with a soft tin-rattle of fresh BBs loading; (9) running high-score tracking persisted in localStorage forever — a small 'BEST SCORE THIS SUMMER' plate to the left of the score apron shows the visitor's all-time high across all visits, and when a new high is set the plate briefly glows warm amber and a small handwritten note in Linda's careful 12-year-old ballpoint cursive fades in beneath the toy reading 'beat it — [date]' with the current date — accumulating across visits as a small handwritten log of personal best dates; (10) shared persistent cross-visitor leaderboard rendered as a small 'TOP SCORES — ROUTE 6 SUMMER 1973' plate that the visitor can summon by clicking the cardstock corner of the playfield — slides up showing the top 10 scores across all visitors anywhere, each with the visitor's first-name-initial-and-town if they entered one in a small SIGN MY HIGH SCORE field that appears beneath the apron when a new personal best is hit; (11) Wayne Neyens's three engineer's margin notes — hovering exactly the brass perimeter rail along the top, the brass plunger, or the cardstock midway illustration (these three zones only) reveals a soft amber spotlight and fades in Wayne's handwritten ballpoint annotation on a small slip of yellow legal-pad paper tucked alongside the toy explaining the engineering reasoning for that element (e.g., on the pin pattern the slip reads: 'I clustered four pins above the 500-cup at 2.3 inches between centers — close enough that a fast BB will scatter off the cluster and miss the cup, but a BB slowed by upper-rail friction will thread through and drop straight in. The plunger tension that does it is about 62% — try it.'; on the plunger 'I specced the spring at 4.2 lb-in/turn because that gives a 12-year-old's thumb a satisfying push without bottoming out the coils. Anything over 5 and the kid bruises her thumb after 200 rounds — Mrs. Linda Vacek's father wrote me a letter in October 1973 thanking me for getting it right.'; on the midway illustration 'I asked the Wing Hing art director to put the calliope wagon at top-center because that's where the eye lands after the plunger release — a kid wants to feel like the BB is going somewhere named, not just up a tube. — W.N., February 1973, Northbrook Illinois'); minimum three distinct annotations, each its own peer-level engineering vignette, no recycling; (12) Linda Vacek's small hand-printed name-and-town in pencil along the lower-left corner of the cardstock playfield reads 'LINDA V. — CICERO ILL. — JUNE 30 1973' as Linda's own personal claim on this specific toy, persistent always; (13) a small subtle interaction along the cardstock fortune-teller tent illustration on the lower-left of the playfield — hovering exactly the fortune-teller tent for 4 full seconds reveals a tiny additional margin annotation in Linda's ballpoint reading 'i got 387 here on a thursday — gramps was reading the sentinel — he didnt look up but he heard the dings — L.V.'; (14) absolutely no instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page explaining how to use the plunger or how scoring works — the visitor figures out the toy by trying the plunger; (15) the toy persists across visits with running high score, leaderboard entries, summer log of personal bests, and revealed annotations all preserved in localStorage; (16) the playfield never refreshes its pin layout — it is one specific Model 88 with one specific Wayne-engineered pin pattern, the same toy Linda played 500 times on her grandparents' porch in July 1973

wildcard.value: After the visitor wraps the score reel past 999 for the first time across visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden moment occurs: the printed fortune-teller-tent figure on the cardstock playfield (the small two-color illustration of a young woman in a blue headscarf with cupped hands) turns her head exactly one frame (a single subtle SVG transform of 4 degrees), the small meadowlark printed in her cupped hands briefly flutters its painted wings once via a 600ms SVG path animation, and the high-score apron updates to read the visitor's initials above the line 'LV 999 — bested at last' rendered in Wayne Neyens's handwritten ballpoint margin-note hand. Additionally, exactly one of Wayne's three engineer's-margin notes — the one on the plunger — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about cold steel, the click of a relay, and a girl who learned the nudge in a tavern on Route 6, disguised naturally within earnest engineering pride about thumb-comfort spring specifications.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when kids had pocket toys' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the 88-cent novelty,' NO cool ironic distance from 1970s gas-station-counter culture as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Linda or her grandparents' lake cabin or the Sinclair station on Route 6 as already-vanished. NO 'where is she now' wistfulness about Linda grown up. NO condescension toward Hong Kong jobber manufacturing as cheap, toward 88-cent toys as disposable, or toward bagatelle pinball as a dumber cousin of real pinball. NO horror-genre framing of the empty screened porch, the fortune-teller's head-turn, the printed meadowlark's wing-flutter, or the wrapped 999 score as creepy/uncanny/sinister/haunted — these are small warm Easter eggs hidden in a working toy by a working engineer, not ghost-story devices. NO 'the toy is haunted' twist, NO suggestion the fortune-teller knows anything supernatural. NO Stranger-Things 1970s pastiche, NO synthwave neon, NO grain-and-VHS-tracking visual gimmicks. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the toy as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of pinball-collectors as fictional small-town society, NO radio/CB/broadcast/transmission framing of the carnival-midway illustration as signal-network metaphor, NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing. NO Wes-Anderson-symmetrical-frame styling, NO Pinterest-vintage-Americana aesthetic. NO instructional UI text visible on the page explaining how to plunge or how scoring works — the visitor figures it out by trying the plunger. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'PLAY POCKET PINBALL' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS the pressed-tin pocket pinball on the screened-porch wicker side-table at the lake cabin in Lake Geneva at 3:42pm Thursday July 5 1973, the visitor IS sitting in the wicker chair beside Linda holding the toy in their own thumb-and-forefinger, and the only voices anywhere on the page are Wayne Neyens's three peer-level engineer's-margin notes in his yellow-legal-pad ballpoint hand, Linda's small cursive 'beat it' notes in the personal-best log, and the toy itself speaking through clicks and dings and the satisfying ker-plunk of a BB settling into a numbered cup. Treat the bagatelle pinball toy as a serious working piece of 1973 industrial-novelty engineering, treat Wayne Neyens as a legitimate working professional with 25 years of skilled pin-layout expertise who took the Hong Kong jobber gig seriously, treat Linda Vacek as a legitimate working 12-year-old hobbyist whose 387 on a Thursday afternoon was a real accomplishment, and treat the visitor's plunger work and slow learning of the right tension as real participation in a real Thursday-afternoon-on-the-porch act of skilled hobby play worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1987 — peak American suburban regional Sunday-paper comics-section back-page activity-corner era, the very specific moment when Knight-Ridder syndicate distributed a small four-color Sunday-paper supplement called THE PUZZLE GARDEN tucked between the Parade magazine and the TV Week listings in roughly 140 medium-market broadsheet papers across the Sunbelt and lower Midwest — Tulsa World, Akron Beacon Journal, Wichita Eagle-Beacon, Greenville News, Tallahassee Democrat, Lexington Herald-Leader — and the back page of the supplement each Sunday carried a hand-drawn maze called THE COTTONTAIL'S RETURN designed by syndicated cartoonist-puzzlist Mr. Garner Tilbury of 2814 Pin Oak Drive, Norman, Oklahoma, age 58, who had been drawing the weekly maze since the supplement launched in October 1981 and who hand-inked every maze on Bristol board at his drafting desk in the converted bedroom above the garage; the specific small American Sunday-morning ritual of a 9-year-old or 10-year-old kid spreading the comics section flat on the breakfast-room carpet after church while the rest of the family read the front section at the kitchen table, finding a freshly-sharpened Ticonderoga in the junk drawer, and tracing the maze from the cottontail at the upper-left to the carrot patch at the lower-right with the pencil-tip carefully held just above the paper without making any mark at all, because Mr. Tilbury's instructions in the small printed footer always read 'TRACE WITH YOUR EYE FIRST — PENCIL ONLY IF YOU GET STUCK,' and the very specific maze of Sunday October 18, 1987, the 314th weekly maze of Mr. Tilbury's run, which he titled in his own neat block-lettering 'A COTTONTAIL'S RETURN HOME — for the kids who already know the way'

format.value: A genuine fully-traceable in-browser cursor-maze rendered as the actual Sunday-paper back page of THE PUZZLE GARDEN supplement, the entire viewport showing one Bristol-board hand-inked maze with a small cottontail rabbit drawn in two-color flat-ink at the upper-left start gate and a hand-drawn cluster of carrots with green tops at the lower-right finish gate, the maze itself a complex organic-curve labyrinth of approximately 380 hand-inked corridor segments threading through stylized illustrations of a backyard garden (tomato cages, a wooden tool shed, a row of corn stalks, a bird bath, a clothesline with three pinned shirts, a sleeping orange tabby cat on a flagstone, a watering can tipped on its side), with NO CLICKING required — the visitor solves the maze by moving the cursor through the open corridors without ever crossing a hand-inked wall; if the cursor crosses any wall the cursor's tracked position shows a small soft pencil-smudge mark at the crossing point that fades over 3 seconds and the trail-trace momentarily breaks (the visitor must back up to where they last had a clean trace and re-attempt), and a thin pale-graphite trail draws itself behind the cursor showing the cleanly-traced path so the visitor can see their progress; reaching the carrot patch with an unbroken clean trace from start to finish triggers a small soft watercolor wash that blooms across the maze in warm honey-amber celebrating the completed return home, plus a tiny hand-lettered annotation in Mr. Tilbury's own block-lettering appearing in the lower margin

subject.value: Mr. Garner Tilbury's 314th weekly maze for the Knight-Ridder Sunday supplement THE PUZZLE GARDEN, drawn on Bristol board at his drafting desk above the garage at 2814 Pin Oak Drive, Norman, Oklahoma, on the afternoon of Tuesday, September 29, 1987, inked with a Speedball #102 crowquill nib in Higgins Black Magic ink across four hours of patient corridor-routing punctuated by a sandwich and two cups of coffee, mailed Wednesday morning in a stiff cardboard envelope to the Knight-Ridder syndicate office in Miami, photo-reproduced and distributed to 140 papers across the country, and printed in roughly 4.7 million Sunday papers on the morning of October 18, 1987 — a specific maze designed by Mr. Tilbury around a single hidden idea he had been holding for three weeks: that the maze should be solvable two ways, the obvious main-corridor solution that an attentive 9-year-old would find in eight or nine minutes, and a subtler shorter solution that runs along the very perimeter of the garden through the picket-fence shadow that he inked deliberately as a continuous corridor exactly one cursor-width wide, which only kids who had been doing his mazes for a year or two would think to look for; the specific small private practice of Mr. Tilbury after 28 years of weekly maze-drawing of building one quiet structural reward into each maze for the readers he had decided were his real audience — not the casual Sunday solvers who would draw a thick line straight through with a chewed pencil and call it good, but the patient kids who slowed down enough to read the garden as a place and not just a puzzle

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working maze-cartoonist voice belonging to Mr. Tilbury himself in the small hand-lettered footer instruction at the bottom of the maze and in the hover-revealed margin annotations along the garden elements (the tool shed, the sleeping cat, the watering can, the picket-fence shadow, the cottontail's start gate, the carrot patch finish, the three pinned shirts on the clothesline) — Mr. Tilbury at his drafting desk Tuesday afternoon September 29 1987 with the window cracked open and the maple outside just starting to turn, talking to the visitor (a 9-year-old on the breakfast-room carpet Sunday morning with a freshly-sharpened Ticonderoga held above the page) the way he'd talk to a kid at the Norman Public Library's Saturday-morning Drawing Club he ran from 1973 through 1985: matter-of-fact pride in the maze as 28 years of patient corridor-routing craft, conversational warmth about each garden element as a small specific landmark he placed for a reason, peer-level steadiness about syndicated puzzle work as serious skilled craft that pays his mortgage and his wife Helen's piano-tuning equipment and the small Cessna he and his brother Walt share at the Norman airfield, present-tense Tuesday-afternoon focus on inking the corridors at the right consistent width so the Sunday photo-reproduction won't fill them in, full earnest belief that a Sunday-paper back-page maze is a small honest gift one working cartoonist can hand to 4.7 million strangers and that the kid tracing it with a pencil held above the page is participating in a real act of patient attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect, real specific affection for the picket-fence-shadow shortcut as the small private reward he places for the kids who slow down enough to find it; no condescension toward the casual solvers who miss it (they had a good Sunday morning anyway), no apology for syndicated puzzle work as commercial labor, no irony about the maze format as childish or beneath serious craft attention
