era.value: 1971 — peak American Midwestern county-fair 4-H youth-livestock-club exhibition season, the very specific moment when 4-H clubs across the rural counties of Iowa and Nebraska and Illinois and Wisconsin culminated their summer-long youth-livestock-rearing programs at the August county-fair grounds, where 11-to-17-year-old club members from farm families across the county brought their hand-raised lambs and dairy heifers and barrows and breeding ewes and chickens and rabbits to the show ring for judging by the county Extension agent and a visiting judge from the state Extension office, and the entire infrastructure of the youth-livestock exhibition ran on a small carefully-maintained hand-printed paper document called the OFFICIAL EXHIBITORS' ROSTER, a folded 11×17 cream cardstock booklet printed at the county-seat job printer in mid-July from a manuscript hand-compiled across June and early July by the county 4-H secretary Mrs. Pearlene Gaffigan of 1817 South Walnut Street in Onawa Iowa, age 53, who had been compiling the roster every August since 1953 and who collected the exhibitor information by hand from each of the 41 county 4-H clubs via mailed-in registration cards that came back in her PO Box across May and June and that she transcribed into a leather-bound master ledger on her kitchen table in the evenings after supper; the roster booklet itself listed every exhibitor in the 1971 Monona County Fair youth livestock division by club affiliation, age, species and breed of animal, animal's registered name or barn name, animal's birth date, sire and dam, ribbons earned at prior shows, and a small column of Mrs. Gaffigan's personal notes about each exhibitor that she kept for her own use ('Becky's third year with Dorset crosses — her ewe took grand champion at the spring lamb show in Ida Grove'; 'first year for Lyle Junior — he is showing his uncle Vern's barrow and he is nervous, give him an encouraging word at the ring'; 'the Voelker twins are showing matched Holstein heifers from the same calving — they have been working on showing them since February')

format.value: A fully working browseable hand-printed exhibitors' roster booklet — the 1971 Monona County Fair Youth Livestock Division roster as a folded 11×17 cardstock booklet opened flat across the viewport, the visitor browsing the 14 ruled pages of exhibitor listings by clicking the lower corners to turn pages, with each of the 247 individual exhibitor entries hoverable to reveal Mrs. Gaffigan's hand-written personal notes about that specific 4-H kid and their animal, plus a working PIN YOUR PROGRAM panel where the visitor selects which classes they want to attend in the show ring across the four days of the fair (Wednesday Sheep, Thursday Swine, Friday Dairy, Saturday Beef) and the booklet auto-generates a small hand-pencilled personal schedule slip the visitor can tuck into the back of the roster, plus a working SIGN THE BACK COVER guestbook where the visitor adds their own short note in pencil-cursive about which class or animal they came to see, persisting in localStorage as an accumulating community page of fairgoer notes that future visitors browse alongside Mrs. Gaffigan's master ledger entries

subject.value: The 1971 Monona County Fair Youth Livestock Division Official Exhibitors' Roster, compiled across June and early July 1971 by county 4-H secretary Mrs. Pearlene Gaffigan of Onawa Iowa, printed at Holsteen Brothers Job Printers on Iowa Street in Onawa on July 16, 1971 in a run of 800 cream cardstock booklets, distributed via the county Extension office to every 4-H family in the county and stocked at the fairgrounds office across the four days of the fair August 18-21 1971; the roster as a working document that the county Extension agent Mr. Donald Kropfelder uses at the judging table, that visiting families use to find their nieces and nephews in the show ring, that the 4-H kids themselves use to look up their show times and class assignments, and that Mrs. Gaffigan uses to remember every kid across nineteen years of county fairs; the 247 individual exhibitors listed by club affiliation across the 41 active Monona County 4-H clubs (the Castana Cardinals, the Mapleton Maple Leafs, the Whiting Hustlers, the Soldier Sodbusters, the Onawa Eager Beavers, the Ute Up-and-Comers, the Moorhead Manglers, etc.) with each entry filling one ruled line containing: exhibitor's full name and age, parents' names, home address, club affiliation, species and breed, animal's barn name and registered name if applicable, animal's birthdate, sire and dam, prior-year ribbon record, and Mrs. Gaffigan's column of small personal notes; the entire infrastructure of one woman's nineteen-year working knowledge of every 4-H kid in Monona County and their animals condensed into a single hand-compiled printed booklet that she takes seriously as legitimate skilled secretarial-pedagogical craft

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working county-4-H-secretary voice belonging to Mrs. Gaffigan herself in the hover-revealed margin annotations on each exhibitor entry — Mrs. Gaffigan at her kitchen table in the evenings of June and July with the leather-bound master ledger open and the kitchen radio playing low (KMA 960 out of Shenandoah, the farm-and-home show), talking to the visitor (a fairgoer leafing through the roster on Thursday afternoon between the Holstein heifer class and the Yorkshire barrow class) the way she'd talk to a fellow Extension staffer riding along with her on the spring club-visits route: matter-of-fact pride in nineteen years of roster-compilation as serious skilled secretarial-pedagogical craft, conversational warmth in the margin notes about each kid (e.g., on Becky Tessien's entry: 'Becky is twelve this year and this is her third year with Dorset crosses — her ewe Henrietta is the daughter of the ewe Becky showed as a nine-year-old in '68 and Henrietta took grand champion at the Ida Grove spring lamb show in May, Becky has been working with her since lambing in February and she knows that ewe better than the judge will know any animal in the ring'; on Lyle Junior Brockschink's entry: 'first year exhibitor, he is twelve, he is showing his uncle Vern Brockschink's Hampshire barrow Sammy because Lyle Junior's own animal got pneumonia in May and Vern offered Sammy so Lyle Junior could still show this year — give him an encouraging word at the ring, his hands shake when he is nervous but he is a good showman when he settles'; on the Voelker twin entries (Marlene and Doris, age 14): 'matched Holstein heifers from the same calving in March, the twins have been working on showing them together since the calves were three weeks old, they brush them every morning before school, they have decided that whichever twin wins they will split the prize money'), peer-level steadiness about 4-H youth livestock as serious pedagogical-agricultural craft that teaches real working farm skills, present-tense July-evening focus on getting the roster manuscript to the print shop by July 16, real specific affection for the 247 kids in the county whose names and animals she carries in her head, full earnest belief that a hand-compiled exhibitors' roster is one of the more important documents a county fair produces and that the visitor's careful browsing of it is participation in a real act of small-community witness worthy of full peer-to-peer respect

palette.value: Warm cream Holsteen-Brothers-Job-Printers cardstock (#f1e4c4) as the dominant viewport ground with visible long-fiber paper texture and tiny halftone-stipple grain (#a89878 at 0.3 opacity) + deep composing-room ink black (#1a1612) for the printed headlines, club section dividers, column rules, and the 247 exhibitor entries set in 9-point Linotype with subtle per-character ink-density variation suggesting hand-fed press impression + warm brick-red second-color letterpress ink (#a8312a) for the booklet's masthead 'MONONA COUNTY FAIR — YOUTH LIVESTOCK DIVISION — OFFICIAL EXHIBITORS' ROSTER — AUGUST 18-21 1971', the small woodcut ornament of a 4-H clover on the front cover, the class-time dividers, and the ribbon-record column headers + cool 4-H clover green (#3a6a2e) for the four-leaf-clover club emblems beside each of the 41 club section headers + Mrs. Gaffigan's hand-written margin annotation slips rendered as small cream-stationery rectangles (#f5ebd0) tucked into the right margin of the booklet with her soft fountain-pen ink (#1c2a5e) in steady careful 1953-Iowa-secretarial cursive with subtle per-stroke pressure variation + soft graphite-pencil gray (#5a4a3a) for the visitor's pencil-cursive guestbook entries and the auto-generated personal show-schedule slips + faint coffee-cup ring shadow (#a8895a at 0.30 opacity) on one of the inner spreads where Mr. Kropfelder set his mug down at the judging table on Thursday afternoon + warm August-late-afternoon county-fair-judging-tent canvas-filtered light cast (#fde6a0 at 0.18 opacity) falling diagonally across the upper-right of the booklet suggesting the canvas-roof light at 3:40pm Thursday + tiny printed staple-shadow at the booklet's center fold (#8a6a3a at 0.4 opacity) + faint cattle-show ring sawdust-scuff (#c8a878 at 0.2 opacity) along the lower edge of one inner spread where the booklet sat momentarily on the fairgrounds ground + the cream cardstock's faint 2-color registration drift visible as 1-pixel offset between the black Linotype and the brick-red second-color pass suggesting Holsteen Brothers' hand-fed flatbed press registration was never quite perfect

mandatory_element.value: A fully working browseable roster booklet with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented: (1) the booklet renders at full viewport scale as the actual folded 11×17 cream cardstock booklet opened flat showing two facing pages of the 14-page roster interior, with the kitchen-table or judging-tent surface visible at the edges and a small subtle staple-shadow visible at the center fold; (2) page-turn mechanic where clicking the lower-right corner of the right page advances forward and the lower-left corner of the left page goes back, with smooth CSS page-curl animation showing the cardstock visibly bending and a faint paper-rustle sound via Web Audio API; (3) minimum 247 distinct exhibitor entries pre-loaded across the 14 ruled spreads, grouped under their 41 4-H club section headers (each section header introduced by a small four-leaf-clover ornament and the club's official name), each entry rendered as a single ruled-line row in Linotype-style serif type containing: exhibitor name (full first and last), age, parents' names, home address (town and route number), animal species and breed, animal's barn name, animal's birthdate, sire and dam, prior-year ribbons, and a small Mrs. Gaffigan-note marker (a tiny pencilled asterisk) indicating that hovering reveals her personal margin annotation; (4) each of the 247 entries is hoverable to reveal a soft warm canvas-light spotlight on that one row and fades in a small cream-stationery margin-slip annotation in Mrs. Gaffigan's fountain-pen cursive tucked into the right margin of the spread — minimum 247 distinct annotations, each its own specific peer-level personal vignette about that kid and their animal (no two annotations recycling phrasing or pattern), each annotation 2-5 sentences carrying the small private working knowledge Mrs. Gaffigan has accumulated about that exhibitor across her years compiling the roster; (5) a working PIN YOUR PROGRAM panel rendered as a small tear-off slip at the back of the booklet — clicking it opens an interactive schedule panel listing the four show days (Wednesday Sheep, Thursday Swine, Friday Dairy, Saturday Beef) with each day's classes broken out by hour (Wednesday 9am Market Lamb / 10:30am Breeding Ewe / 1pm Showmanship Junior / 2:30pm Showmanship Senior / etc.) and the visitor selects which classes they want to attend by clicking each class — selected classes accumulate on a small hand-pencilled personal schedule slip that renders in Mrs. Gaffigan's-pencil-cursive style and persists in localStorage; (6) a working SIGN THE BACK COVER guestbook on the inside back cover of the booklet — clicking the cover opens a small pencil-cursive input where the visitor types a short note about which class or animal they came to see (or who they are at the fair for), and the typed note renders in graphite-pencil cursive style with subtle per-stroke pressure variation and persists in localStorage AND submits to a shared persistent list so future visitors see every previous fairgoer's note accumulating on the back cover across visits; (7) the visitor's own schedule slip AND back-cover note persist forever, so a returning visitor finds their previous schedule still tucked into the back of the roster and their note still on the back cover alongside accumulating community notes; (8) a small persistent counter rendered as tiny pencilled tally marks along the lower-left of the back cover reads 'FAIRGOERS WHO SIGNED THIS YEAR: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere adds a note (shared persistent count starting at 1,847 — Mrs. Gaffigan's imagined fairgoer-signing count for the four days); (9) ten of the 247 entries have a small additional pencil-asterisk beside Mrs. Gaffigan's main annotation indicating a deeper second-tier annotation — hovering this secondary asterisk for 3 full seconds reveals an additional cream-stationery slip in even-softer fountain-pen ink with Mrs. Gaffigan's more private working knowledge about that exhibitor (e.g., on the Voelker twins' deeper note: 'their father Reuben passed in March of pancreatic cancer, the twins decided in May to dedicate this year's show to his memory but they are not telling anyone, they want the heifers to do the talking — if either twin wins, watch their faces when the ribbon is pinned'); (10) absolutely no instructional UI text visible on the page explaining how to browse the booklet or use the schedule panel or sign the cover — the visitor figures it out by clicking; (11) a small subtle interaction along the front cover's masthead — hovering exactly the brick-red 4-H clover ornament for 4 full seconds reveals a small additional annotation in Mrs. Gaffigan's hand reading 'roster no. 19 — compiled at the kitchen table across June and early July — mailed to Holsteen Brothers July 14 — picked up the print run July 17 — Glenn helped me box them into the trunk of the Buick — P.G.'; (12) the booklet does not regenerate, does not change its entries — it is one specific roster, the one Mrs. Gaffigan compiled for August 1971, and browsing it is the entire page

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Mrs. Gaffigan's 247 hover-revealed margin annotations — the one tucked alongside the entry for Becky Tessien and her ewe Henrietta — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about a barn light at 5am, a girl's hands learning to brush wool the right direction, and a ewe who knows the girl's voice — disguised naturally within Mrs. Gaffigan's earnest peer-level working-secretary note about Becky's three-year showing record. Additionally, after a returning visitor has signed the back cover on three separate calendar dates AND pinned a full four-day program at least twice across visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden 248th entry quietly appears at the very bottom of the back-cover guestbook page in slightly fresher fountain-pen ink — the entry reads in Mrs. Gaffigan's hand: 'GAFFIGAN, P. — age 53 — Onawa Iowa — Monona County 4-H Secretary since 1953 — not an exhibitor in any class — showing nothing this year — but I have been at every fair since 1953 and I have signed my own back cover every year as if I were one of the kids because I am part of this too — P.G.' This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact at the bottom of the back cover for visitors who kept coming back.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when county fairs were like this' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of 4-H youth livestock,' NO cool ironic distance from 1970s Midwestern county-fair culture as quaint Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Mrs. Gaffigan or the 41 Monona County 4-H clubs or the Onawa Iowa Extension office as already-vanished. NO 'where are they now' wistfulness about Becky Tessien grown up or about whether the Voelker twins inherited the farm, NO 'this roster was found decades later in an attic' framing, NO scrapbook-archival voice. NO horror-genre framing of the empty fairgrounds, the after-hours show ring, the Voelker twins' private dedication to their late father, or the hidden 248th entry as creepy/uncanny/sinister/gothic-rural — this is a working August county fair with the canvas tents up and the livestock barns full and the Extension office staffed and the judging tables set, Mrs. Gaffigan is at the secretary's table this Thursday afternoon checking exhibitor numbers, and the 247 kids are at the fairgrounds with their animals. NO 'the rural Midwest is dying' wistful-decline framing, NO 'family farms are disappearing' political subtext, NO Grant-Wood-American-Gothic stark-grimness framing, NO Norman-Rockwell-sentimental small-town-Americana saccharine, NO 'simpler times' framing, NO Boomer-childhood-nostalgia 'they don't make them like this anymore' voice. NO condescension toward 4-H youth livestock work as quaint farm-kid recreation, toward county-fair exhibition as small-time pageantry, toward Extension-office secretarial work as small career, toward Mrs. Gaffigan's nineteen years compiling rosters as wistful small-life pathos. NO smug urban superiority about rural agricultural pedagogy, NO 'imagine raising a lamb for the slaughter market at age twelve' framing — the 4-H kids and their families understand exactly what market livestock means and the pedagogy is honest about it. NO 'Charlotte's Web' sentimentality about animal-and-child bonds, NO Wilbur-the-pig pathos, NO 'after the fair the animals get sold' tragic-revelation framing — this is a normal August week at a normal county fair, the kids know what they signed up for in February when the lambs were born, and the work is dignified. NO Lyle-Junior-as-nervous-farm-boy caricature, NO 'simple country folk' framing of any of the 247 exhibitors or their families. NO ag-policy political subtext, NO 'these are the people who voted for Nixon' framing, NO any partisan-cultural overlay on rural Iowa in 1971. NO Mrs. Gaffigan-as-spinster-secretary framing — she is married to Glenn Gaffigan (a Monona County road-grader operator) for thirty-one years and they have three grown children and seven grandchildren and she does the 4-H secretary work because she is genuinely good at it and the county pays her a modest annual stipend that she uses for her hardback-book habit. NO Wes-Anderson-symmetrical-frame styling, NO Pinterest-vintage-farm-aesthetic mood-boarding. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the hidden 248th entry or the Voelker twins' dedication, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the roster as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding (the 41 Monona County 4-H clubs are real legitimate youth-agricultural organizations, not fictional small-town societies), NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher/transmission framing of the Extension-office communication network as signal-relay metaphor (the Extension service is the Extension service, not a signal metaphor), NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing (the four show days are contextual facts about the fair schedule, not the subject of the piece). NO Mrs. Gaffigan-as-folksy-Iowa-grandmother caricature, NO accented-English pastiche, NO 'gee-whiz' fake-Midwestern dialogue. NO Garrison-Keillor Lake-Wobegon gentle-mockery register. NO instructional UI text visible on the page. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'BROWSE THE 1971 MONONA COUNTY FAIR EXHIBITORS' ROSTER' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS the roster booklet on the judging-tent table at 3:40pm Thursday August 19 1971, the visitor IS a fairgoer leafing through it between classes, and the only voice anywhere on the page is Mrs. Gaffigan's voice in her fountain-pen margin annotations and the printed Linotype voice of the roster itself. Treat the exhibitors' roster as a serious working community document, treat Mrs. Gaffigan as a legitimate working county-Extension secretary with nineteen years of professional expertise and a real running pedagogical-agricultural craft practice, treat the 247 4-H exhibitors and their animals as real specific kids and animals doing real working agricultural pedagogy worthy of careful peer-to-peer attention, treat the four-day county fair as a real legitimate community institution worthy of full peer-to-peer respect, and treat the visitor's careful browsing of the roster and signing of the back cover as real participation in a real act of small-community witness.

length.description: something to explore, multiple sections, interactive elements

era.value: 1987 — peak American mall arcade redemption-ticket midway era, the very specific moment when shopping-mall arcades like Tilt and Aladdin's Castle and the Time-Out arcade chain stocked their carpeted floors with electromechanical and early-microprocessor redemption games (Skee-Ball lanes, Cyclone wheel-of-lights, Whac-A-Mole, Pop-A-Shot basketball, the brand-new 1986 ICE Smart Industries 'Hi-Striker' arm-test) that paid out crinkly perforated yellow paper tickets in 50-ticket strips from a chattering Deltronic Labs DL-1275 ticket dispenser mounted to the cabinet's lower bezel, and the specific late-Saturday-afternoon ritual of a 9-year-old named Jenny Vorhies of 3214 Wedgewood Lane in suburban Akron Ohio walking the carpeted aisles of the Tilt arcade in the Chapel Hill Mall on October 24, 1987, between 4:14pm and 5:38pm, her left fist clutching a thick wadded accumulating bundle of yellow tickets she had won across the previous ninety minutes (372 tickets total, the largest haul she had ever earned in a single arcade visit, accumulated mostly from a single hot streak on the rightmost Skee-Ball lane where she had hit four 50-point pockets in a row across two games), trying to decide between the two-foot plush dolphin behind the prize-counter glass (875 tickets, just out of reach), the small velvet-lined wooden jewelry box with a tiny ballerina inside (340 tickets, within her means but somehow not what she wanted), the bouquet of plastic-stem long-stem roses (220 tickets, available but feels like a settlement), or saving the 372 tickets in the small zip-pouch the prize counter would give her so she could come back next Saturday and combine them with a fresh haul toward the dolphin

format.value: A fully working tiny in-browser Skee-Ball lane simulation — the visitor sees one specific Tilt-arcade Skee-Ball cabinet from the player's standing position at the foul line, the wood-grained polished lane stretching away into the upper viewport with the five concentric scoring pockets (10/20/30/40/50 from outer ring to center) and the two corner 100-point pockets at the upper-left and upper-right, a small chrome ball-return chute at the lower-right delivering nine cream-colored hardwood balls one at a time across a single round, and the visitor rolls each ball by click-and-drag-up-release on the lane surface — drag distance controls roll velocity, drag angle controls left-right aim, release commits the throw and the ball travels up the lane with real 2D physics (rolling friction along the wood, the slight upward ramp at the top of the lane that lofts the ball into the scoring well, elastic ricochet off the brass divider hoops between pocket rings) and finally settles into one of the seven pockets with a satisfying thunk; running score accumulates across nine balls, and at end of round a Deltronic Labs DL-1275 ticket dispenser chatters out a strip of perforated yellow tickets matching the ticket-payout table (10pts=1 ticket, 20pts=2, 30pts=3, 40pts=4, 50pts=5, 100pts=12 — Jenny's actual posted payout at Tilt Chapel Hill in fall 1987), the strip tearing from the dispenser with a real perforated-paper rip animation and accumulating in Jenny's running ticket-bundle visible at the bottom-right of the viewport

subject.value: The single specific Skee-Ball lane (Lane 7 of the eight-lane row along the south wall of Tilt arcade in the Chapel Hill Mall, Akron Ohio) on the afternoon of Saturday October 24, 1987, between 4:14pm and 5:38pm, played by Jenny Vorhies (age 9, fourth-grader at Lawndale Elementary, wearing her older sister Brenda's hand-me-down purple windbreaker over a Wonder Woman t-shirt and dark wash Levi's 550 jeans and white Reebok Princess high-tops, with $4.25 in quarters in her front pocket — three dollars from her allowance saved across four weeks plus a dollar twenty-five her grandmother slipped her after church last Sunday — and a small wadded handkerchief in her back pocket her mother packed in case she needed to wipe her hands), who has been playing arcade Skee-Ball for two years and considers Lane 7 her lane because it has a slightly higher loft-ramp than the other seven lanes (a real mechanical fact about that specific Lane 7 cabinet — the lane was rebuilt by Tilt maintenance technician Mr. Vince Pellegrino in February 1987 after the original loft-ramp got cracked by a kid who threw the ball overhand, and Vince rebuilt it slightly steeper than spec because he liked the way it played) which gives Jenny's underhand rolling technique a slightly better chance at the 50-pocket if she aims true; the entire small Saturday-afternoon arcade-ritual of a 9-year-old methodically working a Skee-Ball lane she has decided is hers, tracking her ticket count in her head, deciding between the dolphin and the jewelry box, learning at age nine the specific patience of saving versus the specific satisfaction of spending

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working arcade-Skee-Ball-player voice belonging to Jenny herself in small hover-revealed margin annotations on the lane elements (the loft-ramp, the 50-pocket, the corner 100-pockets, the ball-return chute, the ticket dispenser, the prize counter visible at the far edge of the viewport), Jenny at Lane 7 this Saturday afternoon with her left hand on the ball-return rail and her right hand cradling the next ball and her ticket-bundle clutched in her left fist, talking to the visitor (someone standing at Lane 8 next to her, also playing, who has noticed Jenny's hot streak) the way she'd talk to her best friend Kristy Lemmons who isn't here today because Kristy's family is at her grandmother's in Canton: matter-of-fact pride in two years of Skee-Ball expertise as serious skilled patient craft, conversational warmth about the specific feel of Lane 7's loft-ramp as a real mechanical fact she has learned by playing this exact lane forty-seven times since February, peer-level steadiness about the dolphin-versus-jewelry-box decision as a real important choice that deserves careful thought, real specific affection for Mr. Vince Pellegrino who fixed her ticket dispenser when it jammed three weeks ago and who waved at her when she walked in this afternoon, present-tense Saturday-afternoon focus on the next roll and the running ticket count, full earnest belief that arcade Skee-Ball is one of the more important things she does with her allowance money and that the visitor next to her at Lane 8 is a real peer worthy of her quiet sharing of what she has learned about how this specific lane plays; absolutely no irony about arcade culture as childish, no smug retrospect about 1987 as quaint pre-internet Americana, no condescension toward redemption-ticket economies as silly
