mandatory_element.value: A fully working Skee-Ball lane simulation with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented: (1) the cabinet renders at full viewport scale as the actual Lane 7 of Tilt arcade Chapel Hill Mall from the player's standing position at the foul line, the polished maple lane stretching away into the upper viewport with all seven scoring pockets clearly delineated (five concentric 10/20/30/40/50 pockets plus two corner 100-pockets), the loft-ramp at the top of the lane visible as a subtle upward angle, the chrome ball-return chute at the lower-right delivering balls into a small holding cradle, and the Deltronic Labs DL-1275 ticket dispenser mounted to the lower-left bezel with its small printed payout-table card alongside (10pts=1 / 20pts=2 / 30pts=3 / 40pts=4 / 50pts=5 / 100pts=12); (2) click-and-drag-up-release rolling mechanic on the lane surface: drag distance from the foul line controls roll velocity (short drag = soft roll that may not reach the loft-ramp, long drag = fast roll that may overshoot the 50-pocket and ricochet); drag angle controls left-right aim; release commits the throw, the ball animates traveling up the lane with real 2D physics (rolling friction decelerating the ball, the loft-ramp at the top providing a controlled upward arc into the scoring well, gravity dropping the ball into the well, elastic 1-2 ricochets off brass divider hoops before the ball settles into a pocket), and a satisfying thunk sound plays via Web Audio API when the ball settles; (3) running score and ticket-count tracking across each 9-ball round, with the score displayed on a small lit-segment scoreboard mounted to the cabinet's upper bezel and the ticket dispenser chattering out a perforated strip of yellow tickets at end-of-round matching the payout table, the strip tearing from the dispenser with a real perforation-rip animation and a brief paper-rip sound; (4) accumulating ticket-bundle visible at the lower-right of the viewport as a growing wadded stack — the bundle persists in localStorage across visits forever, so a returning visitor finds their accumulated ticket-haul still in their fist; (5) a working PRIZE COUNTER panel summonable by clicking the small prize-counter glass visible at the far upper-right of the viewport — slides up showing the four available redemption prizes (plush dolphin 875 tickets / wooden jewelry box with ballerina 340 tickets / bouquet of plastic-stem long-stem roses 220 tickets / small zip-pouch FREE for saving tickets for next visit) with each prize illustrated and labeled; visitor can REDEEM by clicking any prize their ticket-bundle can afford, which subtracts the cost from the bundle and adds the prize to a persistent localStorage 'prizes Jenny has won this fall' shelf visible at the bottom edge of the viewport; (6) the zip-pouch save option lets the visitor save their current ticket-bundle without spending — the bundle persists into the next visit indefinitely, so a patient visitor can save toward the dolphin across multiple sessions; (7) minimum 5 hoverable lane elements (the loft-ramp at the top, the 50-pocket center, one of the corner 100-pockets, the ball-return chute, the ticket dispenser) each revealing Jenny's small spiral-notebook-paper margin annotation on the lane element — minimum 5 distinct annotations, each its own peer-level vignette in Jenny's voice (e.g., on the loft-ramp: 'Mr. Vince fixed this one in February when the Wagner kid cracked it — he made it steeper than spec, my mom says that's called bias but Mr. Vince says it's called knowing your craft, my balls go in the 50 about one in five rolls on this lane and one in nine on Lane 4'; on the 100-pocket: 'I have hit this one exactly four times in two years and three of those times were on Lane 7'; on the ticket dispenser: 'this is the same model as the one at Aladdin's Castle in the Summit Mall but Tilt's payout for 100 is twelve tickets and Aladdin's is only ten, that's why Kristy and I always come here'); (8) a small persistent counter at the bottom of the cabinet reads 'BALLS ROLLED ON LANE 7 THIS YEAR: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere rolls a ball (shared persistent count starting at 14,847 — Tilt's actual estimated Lane 7 ball-count for fall 1987 to-date); (9) a small subtle interaction along the cabinet's upper-right corner where a tiny piece of masking tape has been stuck with VINCE'S LANE — DO NOT REBUILD written in black Sharpie — hovering exactly the tape for 4 full seconds reveals a small additional annotation in different handwriting (Mr. Vince Pellegrino's own block-print ballpoint) reading 'rebuilt Feb 14 1987 — loft-ramp angle bumped from 14° to 16.5° — it plays better this way and Tilt corporate can argue with me about it — V.P.'; (10) absolutely no instructional UI text visible explaining how to roll or how scoring works — the visitor figures it out by clicking-and-dragging on the lane the way a 9-year-old at the Chapel Hill Mall would; (11) all visitor state persists in localStorage forever — accumulating ticket-bundle, redeemed prizes shelf, hover-revealed annotations, all-time high single-round score, total balls rolled — so a returning visitor finds their bundle and their dolphin-savings and their prize-shelf exactly where they left them

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Jenny's five hover-revealed lane-element annotations — the one on the corner 100-pocket — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about a wooden ball lifting at the top of a lane, a girl who has counted to four, and a brass hoop that knows her hand — disguised naturally within Jenny's earnest peer-level note about her four lifetime 100-pocket hits. Additionally, after a returning visitor has saved tickets across three separate calendar dates AND has accumulated at least 875 tickets at any point (tracked in localStorage), the next visit shows the prize counter has set aside the plush dolphin behind the glass with a small handwritten yellow Post-it stuck to the glass in Jenny's careful 9-year-old ballpoint cursive reading 'HELD FOR JENNY V. — until next Saturday — please do not sell to anyone else — thank you — Jenny Vorhies, 3214 Wedgewood Lane'; the dolphin remains held until the visitor redeems it, and when redeemed a small additional annotation appears at the bottom of the prize-shelf in Jenny's hand reading 'i named her Mariah, she sleeps on the pillow next to me, she came home from the mall in the Tilt bag that has the orange checkerboard pattern, my mom said 875 tickets is the most patient thing i have ever done — J.V.' This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact at the bottom of the prize-shelf for visitors who saved patiently.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when malls had arcades' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of redemption-ticket Skee-Ball,' NO cool ironic distance from 1980s mall culture as quaint pre-internet Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Tilt or the Chapel Hill Mall or Mr. Vince Pellegrino as already-vanished. NO 'where is she now' wistfulness about Jenny grown up, NO 'this Tilt arcade closed in 2003' melancholy decline narrative, NO 'malls are dying' postmodern-ruin framing. NO Stranger-Things 1980s pastiche aesthetic, NO synthwave neon, NO vaporwave mall-aesthetic ironic-mood-board styling, NO 'liminal-space dead-mall' YouTube-aesthetic horror register. NO horror-genre framing of the empty arcade lane, the ticket-dispenser chatter, the prize-counter glass, or the held-aside dolphin as creepy/uncanny/sinister/haunted — this is a working Saturday afternoon at a real working mall arcade, the lights are on, kids are at every lane, Mr. Vince is in the back office having a sandwich, and Jenny is having a good afternoon with her allowance money. NO 'the arcade was a front for something darker' twist, NO 'the dolphin is cursed' supernatural framing. NO condescension toward arcade Skee-Ball as unserious recreation, toward redemption-ticket economies as silly, toward 9-year-old Saturday-afternoon allowance-spending as small-life pathos, toward Mr. Pellegrino's lane-rebuild craft as quaint repairman work. NO 'imagine being a kid in 1987 with quarters in your pocket' smug-modern-superiority framing, NO Boomer-childhood-nostalgia 'they don't make malls like this anymore' voice. NO 'the prizes were junk' cynical-adult-looking-back framing — the dolphin is a real specific plush dolphin Jenny has been thinking about for three weeks and getting it home is going to matter to her for years. NO feminist-grievance reading of the jewelry-box-with-ballerina as gendered toy-marketing — Jenny has thought about it, she has decided the dolphin is what she actually wants, and the choice is hers. NO Mall-of-America corporate-retail political subtext, NO Reagan-era political commentary, NO 'the 80s were a time of...' historical-essay framing. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about Lane 7 or Mr. Vince's tape or the held dolphin, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the arcade as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding, NO radio/CB/broadcast/transmission framing. NO instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'PLAY TILT'S SKEE-BALL' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS Lane 7 at 4:32pm Saturday October 24, 1987, the visitor IS at Lane 8 next to Jenny, and the only voices anywhere on the page are Jenny's spiral-notebook-paper margin annotations, Mr. Vince's masking-tape note, and the cabinet itself speaking through ball-thunks and pocket-thunks and the chatter of the Deltronic Labs DL-1275 dispenser. Treat the Skee-Ball lane as a serious working piece of 1987 mall-arcade infrastructure, treat Mr. Pellegrino as a legitimate working repair technician with real mechanical-craft expertise, treat Jenny Vorhies as a legitimate working 9-year-old Skee-Ball hobbyist with two years of patient skilled practice, treat the dolphin-versus-jewelry-box decision as a real important choice worth full peer-to-peer respect, and treat the visitor's afternoon at Lane 8 next to Jenny as real participation in a real Saturday-afternoon act of arcade-craft worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1934 — peak American Dust Bowl winter-twilight prairie-Kansas-Nebraska borderland atmospheric-optics moment, the very specific weeks of late November and early December 1934 when the western High Plains were experiencing a season of catastrophic agricultural collapse and atmospheric dust suspension that produced a brief and locally remarked-upon optical phenomenon: that the setting sun, filtered through the residual high-altitude loess and topsoil particles still suspended from the Black Sunday-precursor dust storms of the previous April-May, refracted into an unusually long and saturated rose-and-ochre afterglow that persisted along the western horizon from approximately 4:47pm through 5:34pm Central Standard Time each evening, and that the particular twilight gradient produced was warmer-amber at the horizon line and cooler-violet at zenith with a narrow band of unusually saturated coral approximately 11 degrees above the horizon — a gradient amateur astronomer and retired country-schoolteacher Miss Cordelia Vossler of 414 South Cottonwood Street in Beaver City, Nebraska (age 67, taught the McCook District 14 country school from 1891 through 1929, kept a 38-year sunset-color journal in a leather-bound ledger she filled with watercolor swatches and barometer readings every clear evening of her teaching career) recorded in her ledger across November 18 through December 8 1934 with twenty-one consecutive watercolor swatch-pages of the unusual coral-and-violet gradients, each swatch hand-mixed at her kitchen window with her Winsor & Newton pan set, each swatch labeled with the exact time and the barometer reading and a small notation about the wind direction and the quality of the haze, the entire twenty-one-page sequence representing one woman's careful optical witness to a real atmospheric anomaly produced by an ecological catastrophe she could see the small evidence of in the western sky every clear evening of that particular November.

format.value: A single full-viewport silent generative piece rendered entirely in pure CSS and SVG, with no UI text and no controls anywhere: the entire browser window IS the western sky over Beaver City Nebraska at approximately 5:14pm Central Standard Time on the evening of Tuesday November 27, 1934, as seen from Miss Cordelia Vossler's kitchen window facing west across the back garden toward the cottonwood-line along the Beaver Creek bottoms — the sky a slow continuously-evolving generative gradient composed of approximately 11 horizontal color bands smoothly blending into one another via CSS linear-gradient with the warm ochre-amber horizon-band along the bottom 14% of the viewport, the narrow saturated coral band approximately 32% up from the bottom, the rose-pink band above that, the dusty-violet mid-sky band, and the cooler indigo zenith fading to near-black along the upper edge; the gradient is not static — the band positions and color values are slowly continuously interpolating along a deterministic 47-minute cycle (the actual duration of the historical anomaly window from 4:47pm to 5:34pm) so the sky visibly progresses from late-afternoon-warm through deep-twilight across the page session; suspended within the gradient are approximately 280 individual procedurally-generated dust-particle highlights rendered as tiny soft-edged SVG circles at varying altitudes, each particle catching the angled sunlight at its specific altitude — particles in the lower warm bands rendering slightly warmer-amber, particles in the coral band rendering slightly pink-edged, particles in the upper violet band rendering dimmer cooler-pearl — each particle drifting on a very slow individual horizontal velocity (between -0.2 and +0.3 pixels per second, randomized per particle), each with a barely-perceptible vertical sway (sinusoidal ±2 pixels on a 30-60 second period), and each catching a brief slightly brighter specular gleam every 12-18 seconds suggesting the particle has rotated just enough to catch the sun at a more direct angle; silhouetted along the lower 12% of the viewport is a hand-drawn-feeling SVG cottonwood-line — approximately 14 individual cottonwood-tree silhouettes in deep near-black with their characteristic open winter-bare branching, plus the suggestion of a barbed-wire fence-line and a single distant windmill at the far right; barely-perceptible in the lower-left corner of the viewport is the very faint silhouette of Miss Vossler's hand holding a small watercolor brush at the edge of a kitchen-window-sill, only visible as a soft shadow at 0.18 opacity, the brush hovering just above a small mixing-tray that catches the very edge of the viewport; the cursor position acts only as a virtual atmospheric-particle nudge — moving the cursor across the sky causes the dust-particle drift velocities within approximately 180 pixels of the cursor to gently amplify by a small amount (the cursor functions as a soft slow wind that the dust responds to with a tiny lag), but the cursor cannot stop the cycle, cannot change the gradient progression, cannot pause the sky, and cannot interact with any UI because there is no UI; over the full 47-minute deterministic cycle the gradient transitions from approximately 4:47pm-warm-amber through the peak coral saturation at the historical 5:14pm midpoint to the late-twilight deep-violet at 5:34pm, then loops smoothly back to 4:47pm for the next cycle; the silhouettes of the cottonwood-line and the windmill and Miss Vossler's hand never move; the piece runs forever, the sky continues evolving, the dust particles continue drifting, and the visitor sits at Miss Vossler's kitchen window watching one specific evening's twilight that was unusual enough that an amateur color-witness recorded twenty-one consecutive swatches of it in a leather-bound ledger.

subject.value: The single specific 47-minute western-sky twilight gradient over Beaver City Nebraska on the evening of Tuesday November 27, 1934, between 4:47pm and 5:34pm Central Standard Time, as observed from Miss Cordelia Vossler's kitchen window at 414 South Cottonwood Street — Miss Vossler herself, age 67, retired country-schoolteacher of the McCook District 14 one-room school from 1891 through 1929 where she taught grades 1 through 8 simultaneously for thirty-eight years to the children of homesteading families in the Republican River valley, never-married, living since her retirement in a small frame house on Cottonwood Street with her sister Mae Vossler (age 71, also never-married, retired postmistress of the Beaver City post office since 1932) and a yellow tabby cat named Pollux, keeper since November 1891 of a continuous evening sunset-color ledger in a leather-bound Crane's ledger book purchased at the McCook Mercantile in October 1891 with three dollars she had saved from her first month's teaching salary, which she has filled across forty-three years with approximately 4,718 individual hand-mixed watercolor swatch-pages of the western evening sky over the Republican River valley, each swatch labeled with the date, the time of observation, the barometer reading from the small Aneroid she keeps on the kitchen windowsill, the wind direction estimated from the smoke from the McConaha farmhouse chimney visible across the section, and a small handwritten note about anything unusual she observed in the sky's quality that evening; the specific twenty-one-page sequence she is in the middle of compiling across late November and early December 1934 documenting the unusually saturated coral-and-violet twilight gradients she has observed since November 18, which she has privately concluded (and noted in her ledger across multiple evenings) are produced by suspended dust from the catastrophic dust storms of April and May still drifting at high altitude across the High Plains; her quiet careful matter-of-fact understanding that the unusual sunsets she is watching this November are evidence of an agricultural and ecological catastrophe affecting hundreds of thousands of farm families across western Kansas and Oklahoma and the panhandles, including former students of hers from the McCook District 14 school whose families have lost their homesteads in the past two years and whom she still receives letters from at their new addresses in California and Oregon and Washington state, and her decision that the careful witness of the sky's beauty during this particular catastrophic season is not frivolous aestheticism but a small honest form of attention paid to a real event that is happening to the land she has lived on for forty-three years; the specific evening of November 27, 1934, when at 4:47pm she set up her watercolor pan-set at the kitchen window with a fresh ledger page and her #4 sable brush and a small dish of clean rinse-water and began mixing the first horizon-band ochre — and she has been working on the swatch for forty-seven minutes by the time the historical sky reaches its 5:34pm conclusion.

tone.value: No tone — there is no narrator, no voice, no text visible on the page at any point. The piece speaks only through the slow continuous evolution of color across the sky, the patient drift of dust particles catching angled sunlight, the silent silhouettes of the cottonwood-line and the windmill, and the barely-perceptible shadow of Miss Vossler's hand at the windowsill. The closest analogue to a voice is the silent careful attentiveness of Miss Vossler herself at the kitchen window with her watercolor pan-set and her ledger open on the sill: present-tense, unhurried, neither performing nor explaining, the visual equivalent of standing at a kitchen window in late November 1934 in a small frame house in Beaver City Nebraska beside a 67-year-old retired schoolteacher who has been mixing watercolor swatches of evening skies for forty-three years and who is genuinely absorbed in the gradient unfolding in front of her, the quality of her absorption requiring no audience and offering no narration — only the patient continuous act of attention to a real atmospheric event happening for forty-seven minutes against a real western horizon over a real Republican River valley in a season of real ecological catastrophe that she understands and is recording with the careful patient witness she has decided is the right thing to do.

palette.value: Warm low-horizon ochre-amber (#d49538) along the bottom 14% of the viewport blending upward into deeper warm honey (#c87a2a) and then into the saturated narrow coral-pink band (#e8826a at peak saturation) approximately 32% up, blending upward through dusty rose-mauve (#b86a78) into soft warm violet (#7a5a8a) in the mid-sky, then cooler grayed-violet (#5a4e74) into deep indigo-twilight (#2a2a4e) approaching the upper third, fading to near-black night-violet (#181828) along the upper 8% of the viewport + tiny suspended dust-particle highlights catching angled sunlight: warm-amber particles (#f5d89a at 0.6 opacity) in the lower ochre band, pink-edged particles (#f5b8a4 at 0.55 opacity) in the coral band, dusty-pearl particles (#c8b8c4 at 0.4 opacity) in the violet mid-sky, cool dim-pearl particles (#8a8aa4 at 0.25 opacity) in the upper indigo with brief specular gleams (#fff4c8 at 0.7 opacity) occasionally catching individual particles + cottonwood-tree silhouettes along the lower 12% in deep near-black (#1a1410) with subtle warm-edge halos (#5a3a1a at 0.3 opacity) where the warm horizon light catches the branching from behind + barbed-wire fence-line in even-deeper black (#0a0608) drawn as thin 1-pixel SVG strokes between the trees + distant windmill silhouette at the far right in near-black (#1a1410) with the tower's geometric truss and the small wind-vane visible against the coral-band saturation + Miss Vossler's hand-and-brush silhouette at the lower-left corner of the viewport in soft warm-shadow brown (#3a2418 at 0.18 opacity) barely registering against the horizon-band — the gradient transitions deterministically across the 47-minute cycle so all band positions and color values slowly continuously interpolate, with the peak coral saturation appearing at the historical 5:14pm midpoint and the deepest twilight indigo establishing itself by the 5:34pm conclusion, then looping back smoothly to the 4:47pm starting state — evoking the specific quality of an unusually saturated late-November twilight over the Republican River valley during the dust-suspended atmospheric conditions of fall 1934, the warm low light catching airborne loess particles at oblique angles, and the patient continuous evolution of a real evening sky that one woman is mixing watercolor swatches of at her kitchen window.

mandatory_element.value: A fully working generative twilight-sky piece with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented in pure CSS, SVG, and minimal JavaScript: (1) the viewport renders as a continuous full-window vertical sky gradient composed of approximately 11 horizontal color bands smoothly blending via CSS linear-gradient interpolation, with the lower cottonwood-line silhouette and distant windmill rendered as static SVG paths along the lower 12% of the viewport, and Miss Vossler's hand-and-brush silhouette barely visible in the lower-left corner; (2) a deterministic 47-minute time cycle drives the gradient evolution: a JavaScript timing variable progresses from t=0 (representing 4:47pm Central Standard Time, warm-afternoon ochre) through t=0.575 at the 27-minute mark (representing 5:14pm, peak coral saturation, the historical midpoint Miss Vossler recorded in her swatch sequence) to t=1.0 at the 47-minute mark (representing 5:34pm, deep-twilight indigo), then loops smoothly back to t=0 for the next cycle; (3) at each frame, every band's vertical position and every band's color value is computed via smooth interpolation between four keyframe states (4:47pm afternoon-warm / 5:00pm horizon-saturation-building / 5:14pm peak-coral / 5:34pm deep-twilight) using cubic-bezier easing per band so the sky evolves with the slightly non-linear quality of a real atmospheric transition rather than mechanical fade; (4) exactly 280 individual dust-particle highlights rendered as tiny soft-edged SVG circles distributed across the sky at varying altitudes, each particle with its own independent randomized horizontal drift velocity between -0.2 and +0.3 pixels per second, its own independent slow vertical sway (sinusoidal ±2 pixels on a randomized 30-60 second period), and its own independent specular-gleam cycle (a brief 0.8-second brightness boost every 12-18 seconds randomized per particle); (5) each particle's color is computed dynamically based on its current vertical position in the viewport AND the current time-cycle position — particles in the warm horizon band render warm-amber, particles in the coral band render pink-edged, particles in the violet mid-sky render dusty-pearl, particles in the upper indigo render cool dim-pearl; as the sky cycle evolves, every particle's color smoothly tracks its altitude-and-time context, so a particle that drifts upward across the viewport during the cycle transitions through warming-to-cooling color states matching the local band it currently occupies; (6) particles that drift off the left or right edge of the viewport re-spawn at the opposite edge at the same altitude, keeping the total particle count steady at 280; (7) the cursor's position acts as a virtual wind-nudge — within a 180-pixel radius of the cursor, particle horizontal drift velocities are gently amplified by a small factor (between 1.0x and 1.6x depending on cursor proximity) with smooth interpolation so cursor movement produces the soft physical feel of a slight breeze that the dust responds to with a small lag, but the cursor cannot stop, reverse, or pause the gradient cycle, and cursor-driven particle movement does not affect the gradient itself; (8) the cottonwood-line along the lower 12% renders as approximately 14 individual hand-drawn-feeling SVG tree-silhouettes with characteristic winter-bare branching patterns (deterministically generated from a fixed seed so the same tree-line renders identically every visit), the barbed-wire fence-line drawn as thin 1-pixel SVG strokes between the trees, and the single distant windmill at the far right with its geometric truss-tower and small wind-vane silhouette; (9) the cottonwood-tree silhouettes have a subtle warm-edge halo along their upper-right contours where the warm horizon-band light catches the branching from behind, with the halo color and intensity tracking the current time-cycle state (warmer-amber halo during 4:47pm-5:00pm, brief coral-pink halo at 5:14pm peak, fading to cool dim-violet halo by 5:34pm); (10) Miss Vossler's hand-and-brush silhouette at the lower-left corner renders at 0.18 opacity as a static SVG path showing approximately the suggestion of a hand holding a small watercolor brush hovering just above a small mixing-tray, with the brush tip very slightly trembling on a 4-second sinusoidal cycle suggesting Miss Vossler's slow careful breathing as she mixes a swatch; (11) absolutely no UI text anywhere on the page — no instructions, no tooltips, no captions, no header, no title, no footer, no controls, no buttons, no loading messages, no credits, no time-stamp, no date, no 'about this piece' link, no settings panel, no volume control, no pause button; (12) the piece runs autonomously and forever — no reset, no end state, no game state, no progression, no goal — the sky continues evolving through its 47-minute cycle for as long as the page is open, the dust continues drifting and catching light, and Miss Vossler's hand continues hovering at the windowsill; (13) the gradient progression is deterministic — same starting state, same peak-coral midpoint at the same elapsed time, same deep-twilight conclusion at the same elapsed time — every visit, the same November 27 1934 sky over the same Beaver City; (14) the entire piece works fully in-browser with no server, no API, no network calls, no external assets — pure CSS, SVG, and minimal vanilla JavaScript driving the time-cycle and particle-drift loops via requestAnimationFrame.

wildcard.value: Exactly once per 47-minute cycle, at exactly t=0.575 (the historical 5:14pm peak-coral midpoint of the gradient), one specific dust-particle in the saturated coral band catches a brief specular gleam that is markedly warmer and longer-duration than the others — a soft warm-amber 2.4-second gleam at 0.85 opacity instead of the standard 0.7-second brightness boost at 0.6 opacity — this is the specific particle whose unusual brightness Miss Vossler noted in her November 27 ledger entry with the small fountain-pen annotation 'one mote at 5:14 held the light longer than the others — perhaps a fragment of Beaver Creek loess raised especially high by Tuesday's wind — I have mixed the swatch to match its color as best I can, the coral is a difficult one because it is warmer than the cadmium and cooler than the alizarin and I have used both with a touch of yellow ochre to thin it.' The visitor is given no indication this is happening, no acknowledgment in any UI, no reward for noticing — just the quiet fact that on this particular frame of this particular cycle, one mote in the coral band held the light a little longer because a 67-year-old retired schoolteacher noticed it on a Tuesday evening in 1934 and mixed a watercolor swatch to match it.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO text, captions, labels-explaining-the-piece, title overlays, instructional UI, tooltip hints, loading messages, footer credits, watermarks, or any narrating voice anywhere on the page visible to the visitor — the piece must be entirely silent and entirely visual. NO horror-genre framing of the Dust Bowl, the empty Nebraska prairie, the suspended-loess atmospheric anomaly, the silhouetted cottonwood-line at twilight, or the deepening sky as creepy/uncanny/sinister/ominous/dread-inducing — this is a real beautiful evening sky over a real western Nebraska town in late November 1934, the Vossler sisters are at home, Pollux the tabby is asleep on the kitchen rug, and Miss Cordelia is mixing watercolors at the windowsill the way she has done thousands of evenings before. NO 'the dust is coming for the town' apocalyptic framing, NO Grapes-of-Wrath misery-porn register, NO Ken-Burns documentary-elegy voice, NO 'this is the last twilight before everything ends' wistfulness. NO museum-archival 'remember when the Plains had wide-open skies' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of watercolor sunset-journaling,' NO cool ironic distance from amateur naturalist hobby-craft as quaint pre-Instagram Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing of Miss Vossler or Beaver City or the Republican River valley as already-vanished. NO 'where is she now' wistfulness about Cordelia and Mae grown old or about whether the ledger survived, NO 'this watercolor journal was found in an estate sale decades later' framing, NO scrapbook-archival voice. NO Dorothea-Lange-photograph poverty-dignity framing, NO John-Steinbeck noble-suffering register, NO 'the people of the Plains endured' boosterish-PBS voice. NO Climate-grief 21st-century-environmental-anxiety overlay on the 1934 atmospheric anomaly (Miss Vossler understands what she is observing in her own terms — suspended dust from the spring storms — and the page does not retroactively rewrite her observation as climate-disaster prefiguring). NO 'spinster sisters' caricature of the Vossler household, NO mid-century-old-maid pathos — Cordelia and Mae are two retired professionals living comfortably together in a house they own outright with their savings from thirty-eight years of teaching and twenty-seven years of postmistress work, both of them genuinely content, neither of them awaiting rescue. NO condescension toward one-room country-schoolteaching as quaint frontier labor, toward small-town postmaster work as pitiable small-life, toward 43 years of watercolor sunset-journaling as eccentric hobby. NO smug modern superiority about pre-Instagram amateur color-observation. NO Wes-Anderson-symmetrical-frame styling, NO Pinterest-prairie-aesthetic mood-boarding, NO Andrew-Wyeth Christina's-World romanticization. NO night-shift dispatcher voice (the evening sky notwithstanding — this is 4:47pm to 5:34pm, late afternoon, not a midnight desk vigil), NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the unusual mote or the atmospheric anomaly, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the ledger as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of amateur sunset-watchers as fictional small-town society, NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher/switchboard/operator-shift/transmission/signal/frequency-tuner framing of the atmosphere or the light or the dust — the sky is just the sky, the dust is just dust, neither is a signal metaphor. NO weather-app or photo-viewer interface conventions, NO settings panels, NO playback controls, NO speed sliders, NO color-pickers, NO 'pause the sunset' affordance. NO Mary-Oliver pause-and-notice-the-world quote-register, NO Wendell-Berry agrarian-philosophical handwringing, NO Thoreau simplify-simplify voice. The sky evolves, the dust drifts, the cottonwoods hold still, Miss Vossler's hand hovers at the windowsill, and the visitor watches in silence — that is the entire piece. Treat the atmospheric anomaly as a real beautiful optical phenomenon worth careful observation, treat Miss Vossler as a legitimate working amateur naturalist with 43 years of patient color-witness expertise and a genuine watercolor craft practice, treat her ledger as a serious working private document, treat her understanding that the unusual sunsets are evidence of agricultural catastrophe affecting real families she knows as honest peer-level civic awareness, and treat the visitor's quiet act of watching one specific 47-minute evening's twilight progress over Beaver City Nebraska as real participation in a real act of patient atmospheric attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1978 — peak American suburban shopping-mall Orange Julius counter snack-bar era, the very specific moment when the brushed-orange-and-white tile counter at the Orange Julius stand in the food court of the Eastland Mall in Evansville Indiana sold a sweating waxed-paper-cup Original Orange drink for 65 cents through a steel cash register operated by a 16-year-old part-time counter girl named Marcia Eubank in her brown-and-orange polyester pleated visor and apron uniform between 4pm and 9pm on weekday afternoons after her shift at Central High School ended, and the specific small printed cardstock COMBO TICKET the customer received with their order — a 2.5×4 inch perforated stub printed at the regional Orange Julius commissary in Indianapolis on a four-color offset press, bearing the order number hand-stamped in purple ink, the date stamped in red, the cashier's initials handwritten in Bic blue ('M.E.'), the time of order stamped in red, the small printed combo description ('ORIGINAL JULIUS + JUMBO HOT DOG + PRETZEL = $1.85'), and a small printed marketing tear-off coupon at the bottom good for 10 cents off the customer's next visit before December 31 1978; the specific moment when a 14-year-old named Janelle Pickering of 4818 Hawthorne Drive in Evansville handed over a folded dollar bill and a quarter and three dimes at 5:47pm on Tuesday October 31 1978 (she was supposed to be home dressing for trick-or-treat with her younger brother Greg but had walked from the bus stop straight to the mall instead because her best friend Donna Stinnett had said she would meet her here at 5:30 and had not shown up) and received in return her sweating cup and her printed paper combo ticket, which she folded twice and tucked into the back pocket of her Wrangler jeans without looking at it

format.value: A single Orange Julius combo ticket from October 31 1978 — a small 2.5×4 inch perforated printed paper receipt-stub scaled up to fill the entire browser viewport edge-to-edge, viewed at slight angle from above as the physical object Janelle pulled from her back pocket at 9:14pm that evening sitting on her bedroom floor in the dark with the cup empty on the carpet beside her, the ticket the only thing she still had from a Tuesday afternoon she had been trying to figure out what to do with for the past three hours — the page IS the ticket, edge-to-edge, no chrome around it, just the printed cardstock surface with its preprinted four-color offset graphics, its hand-stamped purple order number, its red time and date stamps slightly misaligned, Marcia's blue-Bic initials in the cashier box, the small printed combo description, the perforated tear-off coupon along the bottom edge with its dashed perforation line, and a thin barely-readable handwritten note in pencil in a different hand (not Marcia's) that someone added on the back of the ticket while Janelle was waiting at the counter

subject.value: The single specific Orange Julius combo ticket numbered #3847 issued to Janelle Pickering at the Eastland Mall food court counter at 5:47pm on Tuesday October 31 1978 by counter cashier Marcia Eubank, and the specific small evening that ticket sat folded in Janelle's back pocket: Donna Stinnett never showed up at the mall (Janelle waited at the fountain in the center court from 5:30 until 5:42, then walked to Orange Julius and ordered the combo because she did not want to sit at the fountain alone any longer, ate the hot dog standing at the counter, drank the Julius walking the mall once around in a slow loop past Spencer Gifts and Waldenbooks and the Hickory Farms kiosk, caught the 6:14 bus home, walked in the back door at 6:38, told her mother she had band practice run late, went upstairs without dressing for trick-or-treat, watched her brother Greg leave with their father at 6:45 in his Wookiee costume from the back porch through her bedroom window, and sat on her bedroom floor in the dark from 7pm onward holding the empty cup and the folded ticket); the small additional fact that the ticket has a note penciled on its back in another hand — Marcia, watching Janelle wait at the fountain from her position at the Orange Julius counter and recognizing the specific quiet weight of a 14-year-old who has been stood up by her best friend on Halloween (Marcia having been stood up at the same fountain on Homecoming evening 1976 by her best friend Cyndi when they were both fourteen), wrote on the back of Janelle's ticket while ringing up the combo, in tiny careful Bic-pencil printing: 'donna isnt coming. you can sit at the counter as long as you want. theres a stool. — M.'; Janelle did not find the note on the back of the ticket until 9:14pm when she finally pulled it out of her pocket and unfolded it to look at it for the first time, sitting on her bedroom floor with the empty cup beside her, and the small private fact that finding the note made her cry for ten minutes and then made her go downstairs at 9:38pm and eat the leftover trick-or-treat candy in the kitchen with her father, who had come back without Greg because Greg was sleeping over at the Hendricks' house, and her father did not ask her why she had not gone trick-or-treating and she did not tell him, but they sat at the kitchen table and split a Mounds bar and a Reese's cup and a small bag of candy corn between them while the dishwasher ran

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working counter-cashier voice belonging to Marcia Eubank herself in the small printed-and-handwritten elements of the ticket — Marcia at the Orange Julius counter at 5:47pm Tuesday October 31 1978 between two customers with the blender machines whirring behind her and the food court PA softly playing 'Three Times A Lady' by the Commodores, looking up to see Janelle finally giving up at the fountain and walking toward the counter, and deciding without thinking about it very long that she would write something on the back of the kid's ticket because she knew exactly what Janelle was feeling and she had decided two years ago that she was going to be the kind of person who said something to fourteen-year-olds who were doing badly at the fountain: matter-of-fact warmth about counter-cashier work as serious skilled service labor that pays for her transmission rebuild fund and her records at Camelot Music, conversational steadiness about Halloween as just another shift, peer-level recognition of Janelle as a kid going through something familiar, present-tense focus on the actual work of ringing up the combo and folding the cup carrier and pulling the hot dog from the rotisserie warmer, real specific affection for the small printed combo ticket as a real working commercial document that carries the date and the time and the cashier's initials and that she has decided this afternoon will also carry one small additional honest sentence on its back; absolutely no sentimentality about being a 16-year-old counter girl, no apology for working a polyester uniform at a mall food court, no irony about the Orange Julius combo as kitsch, no nostalgia for her own Homecoming-night stranding by Cyndi (she is over it, she has decided what she learned from it, and she is choosing to act on that learning now), no condescension toward Janelle as a sad little kid (Janelle is fourteen and Marcia is sixteen and they are both having Tuesday evenings, that is all). Full earnest belief that writing one honest sentence on the back of a combo ticket to a kid who has been stood up at the fountain is a small legitimate thing a counter girl can do during her shift, and that the kid eventually finding the note hours later in her back pocket is participation in a real act of small-stranger attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

palette.value: Warm Orange Julius signature orange (#e8732a) as the dominant printed ticket-stock ground with subtle long-fiber cardstock paper texture (#c85a1e at 0.3 opacity stippling) and faint horizontal pulp-fiber striations + cream printed reverse-out white (#f5ecd4) for the printed combo description and the brand wordmark ORANGE JULIUS along the top edge in 1970s-corporate-italic display type + deep brown second-color offset ink (#3a2418) for the brand sub-wordmark 'THE DEVILISHLY GOOD DRINK' and the small printed marketing tagline and the perforated tear-off coupon's printed bordering + brick-red rubber-stamp ink (#a8312a) for the time stamp (5:47 PM), the date stamp (10-31-78), and the location code (EVV-EAS-14) with subtle stamp-pressure variation (some characters slightly heavier where the rubber pressed harder, occasional small ink-blob where the stamp pad was over-inked) + deep purple order-number rubber-stamp ink (#5e2a6e) for the order number 3847 hand-stamped in the upper-right corner + Marcia's Bic ballpoint blue (#1c3a7e) for her cashier-initials 'M.E.' in the small cashier-box on the front of the ticket with subtle per-stroke pressure variation + Marcia's #2-pencil graphite gray (#4a4540) for the small handwritten message on the back of the ticket with characteristic careful Bic-pencil printing-not-cursive, slightly heavier pressure on the underlined word 'isnt' and the period after 'M' + soft food-court fluorescent overhead light cast (#f5e8c4 at 0.15 opacity) falling diagonally across the upper-right of the ticket suggesting the angle of the food-court ceiling fixtures at 5:47pm + faint sweating-cup ring shadow (#a8895a at 0.25 opacity) in the lower-left corner of the front of the ticket where it briefly rested against Janelle's cold Julius cup before she put it in her pocket + faint Wrangler-back-pocket creasing (#7a4a2a at 0.3 opacity) running as a soft fold-shadow along the vertical center of the ticket where it was folded twice in Janelle's pocket from 5:48pm until 9:14pm + a subtle perforation-edge along the bottom of the ticket where the 10-cents-off coupon is meant to be torn off (visible as a horizontal dashed perforation line with tiny dot-punctures) + a small Orange Julius brand octopus mascot illustration in the lower-left corner of the front rendered in flat cream reverse-out on the orange ground, the mascot grinning + over very long timescales the warm overhead light cast very subtly shifts angle by 4 pixels suggesting the slow westward arc of the late-October sunset light filtering through the mall's smoked-glass roof

mandatory_element.value: A fully working physical-paper-ticket interactive experience with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented: (1) the ticket renders at full viewport scale as the actual 2.5×4 inch cardstock object scaled enormously to fill the browser window, viewed slightly from above on what reads as Janelle's bedroom carpet (faint shag-carpet texture visible at the very edges of the viewport beyond the ticket); (2) the printed front of the ticket renders deterministically with all preprinted four-color offset graphics, the brand wordmark, the combo description, the perforated coupon along the bottom, the cream octopus mascot in the corner, plus the hand-stamped order number, time, date, location code, and Marcia's blue Bic cashier-initials; (3) the perforated 10-cents-off coupon along the bottom edge of the ticket is genuinely interactive — clicking and dragging along the perforation line tears the coupon along its perforation with a soft paper-tearing sound via Web Audio API and a real SVG path-clip animation showing the tear progressing in real time along the dashed line, after which the coupon separates from the main ticket and can be dragged anywhere in the viewport, settling where the visitor releases it; the torn coupon persists in localStorage forever, so a returning visitor finds the coupon already torn off and resting where they left it; (4) a working CLICK TO TURN OVER mechanism — clicking anywhere on the front of the ticket (other than the coupon) triggers a smooth CSS card-flip animation over 900ms with a soft paper-on-cardstock rustle via Web Audio API, revealing the back of the ticket where Marcia's pencil note is written in tiny careful Bic-pencil printing reading 'donna isnt coming. you can sit at the counter as long as you want. theres a stool. — M.' rendered in hand-styled web-font + per-character subtle randomization in slant (0.5-2 degrees), baseline drift, letter-spacing variation, and Bic-pencil pressure density (±15% per character with the word 'isnt' rendering at heavier pressure where Marcia underlined it with a single graphite line); (5) the back of the ticket is otherwise mostly blank cardstock with only Marcia's note in the center and a small printed brand line at the very bottom in tiny cream type reading 'THANK YOU FOR YOUR ORDER — ORANGE JULIUS OF AMERICA INC. — 1978'; (6) clicking the back again flips the ticket forward to the front; (7) when the ticket is in the back-side-revealed state, hovering exactly the word 'donna' for 2 full seconds reveals a small additional graphite-pencil annotation fading in along the lower margin in Marcia's same printing reading 'i saw you waiting'; minimum 4 hover-revealable hidden Marcia annotations distributed across specific words on the back ('donna', 'counter', 'stool', 'M.') each its own peer-level vignette: on 'counter' reads 'you can have the orange one i mean you can have the seat — the one closest to the window'; on 'stool' reads 'the third one from the left, the others wobble'; on 'M.' reads 'my name is marcia eubank, i work tuesday thursday friday and saturday afternoons, i am usually behind the second blender — m'; each annotation persists in localStorage once revealed; (8) when the ticket is in the front-side state, three of the printed elements are also hover-revealable for 2 full seconds: hovering exactly the printed time-stamp '5:47 PM' reveals a small soft graphite annotation along the right margin in Marcia's printing reading 'i was watching the clock — donna was 17 minutes late, that is when i decided'; hovering exactly the printed order number '3847' reveals 'my 47th of the shift, halloween brings them out early'; hovering exactly Marcia's 'M.E.' cashier-initials reveals 'i sign my whole name on the back when i think the kid will keep the ticket'; (9) a working FOLD THE TICKET mechanism — clicking and holding any corner of the ticket and dragging it diagonally folds the ticket along its center crease (the genuine fold-crease the back-pocket created from 5:48pm to 9:14pm), with a smooth CSS fold-animation and a soft paper-on-paper sound; the folded state persists in localStorage; folding the ticket reveals on the front a tiny additional handwritten annotation in Marcia's same Bic-pencil printing appearing along the inner edge of the fold reading 'if you fold it twice you can read both sides at once — m'; (10) the printed cream octopus mascot in the lower-left corner of the front of the ticket is hoverable for 4 full seconds, which reveals a small additional annotation in Marcia's printing fading in along the lower-left margin reading 'his name is Julius. he has been on the cup since 1968. i drew an extra eye on this ones cup with the marker behind the register if you look — m'; (11) absolutely no instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page explaining how to flip the ticket or tear the coupon or hover the elements — the visitor figures it out by clicking and dragging the way Janelle eventually figured it out sitting on her bedroom floor at 9:14pm; (12) all visitor state persists in localStorage forever: which annotations have been revealed, whether the coupon has been torn off and where it has been placed, whether the ticket has been folded, the flipped/unflipped state — so a returning visitor finds the ticket exactly as they left it; (13) the ticket does not regenerate, does not change its order number, does not change its time stamp — it is the one specific combo ticket #3847 that Marcia handed to Janelle at 5:47pm Tuesday October 31 1978, and finding the note on the back is the entire page; (14) one small additional persistent shared cross-visitor element — at the very bottom of the back of the ticket, beneath Marcia's note, a tiny running counter rendered in small fading-cream type reads 'TICKETS KEPT BY SOMEONE: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere flips the ticket and finds Marcia's note for the first time (shared persistent count across all visitors starting at 1, representing Janelle's first finding at 9:14pm)

wildcard.value: Exactly one phrase from Marcia's pencil note on the back of the ticket — the eight-word sequence 'you can sit at the counter as long' — when read aloud at its natural stress pattern, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about a stool by a window, a girl waiting at a fountain, and a sixteen-year-old who has been there, disguised naturally within Marcia's matter-of-fact peer-level offer of a chair. Additionally, after a returning visitor has flipped the ticket, torn the coupon, folded the ticket, and revealed all four back-side and all three front-side Marcia annotations across at least three separate calendar dates (tracked in localStorage), a hidden additional element quietly appears the next time the visitor returns: a second small paper object — a folded matchbook-sized note in Janelle Pickering's own 14-year-old ballpoint cursive — appears tucked beneath the corner of the ticket on the bedroom-carpet ground, dated in the corner '11/7/78' and reading 'Marcia — I came back to the mall on Saturday and sat at the counter for a Julius. The third stool from the left really is the only one that doesnt wobble. Donna and I are not really friends anymore but it is fine. Thank you for the note. I have folded it up and put it in the small drawer in my desk where I keep the things I want to keep. — Janelle Pickering, 4818 Hawthorne Drive.' Clicking this folded note unfolds it to reveal Janelle's full message rendered in her own careful 14-year-old ballpoint cursive (different hand from Marcia's printing — slightly rounder letters, slight rightward slant, occasional small heart over the 'i' in her own name). This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the second small object on the bedroom carpet for visitors who kept coming back to look at the ticket.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when malls had Orange Julius' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the printed combo ticket,' NO cool ironic distance from 1970s mall food-court culture as quaint pre-internet Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Marcia or Janelle or the Eastland Mall or Orange Julius as already-vanished. NO 'where are they now' wistfulness about whether Marcia stayed at Orange Julius through 1979 or whether Janelle and Donna ever made up, NO 'this ticket was found decades later in a desk drawer' framing, NO scrapbook-archival voice. NO horror-genre framing of the empty fountain at 5:42pm, the empty mall at 9:38pm, the unworn Halloween costume, the unanswered question about Donna, or the secret pencil note as creepy/uncanny/sinister/dread-inducing — Marcia is at the counter doing her job, Janelle is having a hard Tuesday afternoon that she will be okay from, Donna had her own reasons that are not part of this story, and the note on the back of the ticket is warmth not surveillance. NO 'Donna never showed up because something happened to her' twist, NO suggestion that Marcia's perceptiveness is supernatural — she is sixteen and she has been at the fountain herself and she recognizes the body language. NO Stranger-Things 1970s pastiche aesthetic, NO synthwave neon, NO vaporwave mall-aesthetic ironic-mood-board styling, NO 'liminal-space dead-mall' YouTube-aesthetic horror register, NO 'the Eastland Mall closed in 2008' decline narrative overlaid on the 1978 page. NO condescension toward food-court counter work as unskilled labor, toward 16-year-old cashiers as small careers, toward Janelle's 14-year-old social-stranding as melodramatic, toward Donna's no-show as villainy (Donna had a reason — her mother needed her at home, but neither Janelle nor the page nor Marcia knows that and the page does not need to litigate it), toward Halloween-evening trick-or-treat as childish. NO 'imagine being fourteen on Halloween waiting alone at a mall fountain' Boomer-superiority or smug-modern condescension. NO feminist-grievance reading of the polyester counter-girl uniform as exploitation — Marcia chose this job, she likes the work, the income pays for her transmission fund. NO 'Marcia is a guardian angel' framing of her note — she is a sixteen-year-old who decided to write a sentence on a ticket, not a savior. NO 'Janelle's father didn't notice anything' framing of the kitchen-table candy moment — her father noticed plenty, he did not need to ask, and splitting candy at 9:38pm is the right thing they did together. NO Sylvia-Plath confessional pathos for Janelle, NO Judy-Blume-pastiche teenage-girl-coming-of-age framing, NO Stand-By-Me melancholy-childhood-tableau register. NO Wes-Anderson-symmetrical-frame styling, NO Pinterest-vintage-Americana mood-boarding, NO Saturday-Evening-Post-cover sentimentality, NO Norman-Rockwell pastiche. NO night-shift dispatcher voice (5:47pm in a food court is not a midnight desk vigil), NO lowercase sentence-fragments (Marcia's note is lowercase-printed because she is a sixteen-year-old writing quickly on the back of a ticket, NOT a stylistic register the rest of the page should adopt), NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the back-side note or the haiku or the hidden Janelle reply, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the ticket as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of mall-food-court counter-workers as fictional small-town society, NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher/switchboard/operator-shift/transmission/signal/frequency-tuner framing of the food-court PA system or the Orange Julius commissary distribution network as signal-relay metaphor — the PA is just a PA, the commissary is just a commissary, neither is a signal metaphor. NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing (Tuesday is a contextual fact about when Janelle was stood up, not the subject of the piece). NO 'fictional small-town society/club of [hobbyists]' framing. NO instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page explaining how to interact with the ticket. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'EXAMINE JANELLE'S COMBO TICKET' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS the printed cardstock combo ticket on Janelle's bedroom carpet at 9:14pm Tuesday October 31 1978, the visitor IS Janelle sitting on the floor in the dark with the empty cup beside her finally pulling the ticket out of her back pocket and unfolding it (or is Marcia at the counter at 5:47pm watching the kid walk over from the fountain, or is Janelle herself a week later on November 7 sitting at the counter on the third stool from the left writing the reply note she will tuck under the ticket in her desk drawer), and the only voices anywhere on the page are Marcia's small Bic-pencil printing on the back of the ticket, her additional hover-revealed margin annotations in the same printing, the printed 1978 Orange Julius commercial typography of the ticket itself, and (in the wildcard unlock) Janelle's own 14-year-old ballpoint cursive in the folded reply note. Treat the printed combo ticket as a serious working commercial document, treat Marcia Eubank as a legitimate working 16-year-old food-court counter cashier with two years of professional service-labor expertise and real peer-level perception about the kids who come to the fountain, treat Janelle Pickering as a legitimate 14-year-old going through a real Tuesday-evening social-stranding worthy of full peer-to-peer respect, treat the small pencil note on the back of the ticket as a small honest gesture one stranger can make to another stranger across a service counter on a working shift, and treat the visitor's careful flipping and folding and finding of the note as real participation in a real act of one-stranger-to-another small-attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.
