era.value: 1986 — peak American suburban bowling-alley birthday-party era, the very specific Saturday afternoon at Sunrise Lanes on Old Stage Road outside Marietta Georgia when nine-year-old birthday girl Tara Bickerstaff's mother Sherryl had reserved Lanes 11 and 12 from 1:30 to 3:30pm for the party of twelve fourth-grade girls from Mrs. Lutz's class at Holt Elementary, paid the $34 deposit at the front counter Wednesday evening, dropped off the Carvel ice-cream cake (Fudgie the Whale, $18.95) in the alley's walk-in freezer Friday after work, and brought a paper grocery sack of party-favor goody bags filled by hand at her kitchen table Thursday night — each goody bag a small brown lunch sack folded at the top and stapled twice and labeled in Sherryl's bubble-cursive Magic Marker with one girl's first name, containing one Bonne Bell Lip Smacker (Strawberry), one pack of Garbage Pail Kids (Series 5), one wax-paper-wrapped Tootsie Roll, one Lisa Frank rainbow-unicorn sticker sheet, three Hershey's Kisses, and one folded printed paper SCORE SHEET from Sunrise Lanes' counter rack with each girl's name pre-pencilled in the player column by Sherryl Wednesday night at the kitchen table so all the girls would have to do at the party was bowl

format.value: A page that is exactly one scorekeeper's printed pencil-and-paper bowling scoresheet from Lane 12 of Sunrise Lanes — a real two-game ten-frame manual scoresheet on the cream-pink carbonless 8.5×14 inch perforated form Sunrise Lanes ordered in boxes of 1,000 from the AMF paper-goods catalog and stocked in the wooden ball-return cubby of every lane, scaled to fill the entire browser viewport edge-to-edge as the physical object lying on the small molded-plastic scorekeeper's table at the head of Lane 12 at 2:47pm Saturday October 18, 1986, viewed from directly above as Tara's best-friend-and-appointed-scorekeeper Heather Wendling (age 9, also fourth grade at Holt, has been Tara's best friend since second grade, the only girl in the party Mrs. Lutz had taught the proper bowling-scoresheet notation to during the Cobb County 4-H Junior Bowling unit in September, takes the scorekeeper job seriously because she is good at math and because she likes Tara) is filling it in by hand with a stubby #2 pencil from the cubby. The page IS the scoresheet — the entire 12-player two-game grid filled in across the afternoon in Heather's careful nine-year-old printing, with each girl's frame-by-frame pinfall, the strike X's and spare /'s and split symbols and gutter dashes notated correctly per the AMF manual Heather studied at home Wednesday night, the running totals computed by Heather in pencil down the right side of each row, plus the small ancillary markings around the margins (Heather's name printed in the corner in slightly fancier cursive, the lane number 12 circled, the date 10/18/86, the start time 1:34, an in-progress notation noting that Lane 11's scoresheet is being kept by the alley's automatic scorer because Sherryl said one lane could go automatic and the other should be done by hand because it was educational), and the small accumulating texture of the actual afternoon happening around the scoresheet: a circular pink frosting smudge in the lower-left where Megan Tutwiler set down a piece of Fudgie the Whale at 2:14pm before bowling her seventh frame, a small grease smear from a slice of Sunrise Lanes' $4.50 small pepperoni pizza, two tiny purple Lip Smacker fingerprints along the top edge where Tara herself touched the scoresheet to point at her own row (she rolled a 78 in game one and was very proud), a small dropped Garbage Pail Kid sticker (ADAM BOMB, partially adhered) stuck to the corner of frame 6, and the soft graphite smudge along Heather's left hand's resting zone where her hand has been smearing the partially-completed columns all afternoon because she is right-handed but writes leaning forward with her left hand pressed flat to anchor the sheet. The scoresheet is fully interactive — the visitor can hover any individual girl's row to reveal a small spiral-notebook-paper margin annotation in Heather's printing about that girl (twelve distinct annotations, each its own peer-level vignette: who she is, what she's like as a bowler, what she's wearing today, whether she's having a good time, what flavor Lip Smacker she got in her goody bag and whether she traded with anyone), can click any individual completed frame to see Heather's pencilled work-out for that frame's pinfall computation rendered in tiny side-margin scratch math, can click the GAME 2 IN PROGRESS row at the bottom to advance the in-progress game frame-by-frame in real time at roughly 4 seconds per frame as the visitor watches the afternoon's remaining throws play out (each girl's throw rendered as a small animated pencil-mark appearing in her frame with Heather's notation), can click the cream-pink carbonless layer along the corner to peel back the top sheet and reveal the carbon-pink copy beneath (which the alley keeps for league records — Sherryl will take this top sheet home and tape it into Tara's birthday scrapbook), and can click the small SIGN THE BACK button (rendered as the scoresheet's perforated lower-left corner) to flip the entire sheet over and add their own pencilled signature to a growing list of party-attendees on the back where each girl has signed her name with a small drawn heart or star or curly flourish beside it. The strangeness IS that the entire page is a scoresheet — no chrome, no navigation, no header, no explanation, just the 8.5×14 carbonless form filling the viewport edge to edge with the small afternoon happening on top of it and around it and through it.

subject.value: The single specific scorekeeper's manual scoresheet for Lane 12 at Sunrise Lanes during Tara Bickerstaff's 9th birthday party on Saturday afternoon October 18, 1986, kept by hand across the 1:34pm-to-3:18pm window by Heather Wendling (age 9, fourth grade at Holt Elementary, Tara's best friend since second grade, only girl at the party who knows the AMF manual notation because she studied it Wednesday night at home with her older brother Brent who bowls in the Cobb County Junior League on Saturday mornings); the twelve fourth-grade girls at the party rolling their birthday-party games on Lane 12 across the 104 minutes between when the first ball dropped and when Sherryl Bickerstaff called everyone over to the party table at the back of the alley for cake-and-presents (Tara Bickerstaff the birthday girl, Heather Wendling the scorekeeper, Megan Tutwiler whose mom drove her and three other girls in a wood-paneled Country Squire, Kim Trotter who is the best bowler at the party because her dad bowls in the Tuesday-night men's league and has been taking her since she was four, Stacy Buchholz who has never bowled before and rolls eleven straight gutters in game one before getting her first pin in frame 6 of game two and screaming so loud the manager comes over from the front counter to see what happened, Jenny Kim who is in fourth grade at Holt but new this year because her family moved from Macon in August and who Tara invited because she felt sorry that Jenny didn't have friends yet and who turns out to be a startlingly good bowler for someone who claims she has never been to a bowling alley before (she rolls a 124 in game two), Brittany Vandermolen who is wearing her older sister Tiffany's pink Esprit sweatshirt because she wanted to look cool, Erin Halloran whose mother packed her a separate brown-bag lunch because Erin has a peanut allergy and Sherryl was not sure whether the pizza topping might have peanut oil, Crystal Boudreau who keeps losing her bowling-shoe lace bow and asking Heather to retie it, the twin sisters Amy and Lisa Trumbull who are wearing matching mint-green sweatshirts their grandmother knitted them for their joint September birthday, and Robin Eldred who is the smallest girl in fourth grade at Holt and rolls a six-pound house ball with both hands between her legs and who hits a strike in frame 4 of game two by complete accident and who Heather records the strike for in proper X notation and adds a tiny pencilled heart beside Robin's name in the margin); the entire small Saturday-afternoon ritual of twelve nine-year-old girls bowling their birthday-party two games on Lane 12 while one nine-year-old keeps the score by hand for all of them with a stubby pencil from the ball-return cubby and the careful AMF notation she practiced Wednesday night; Heather's own quiet pride in being the only girl at the party who can keep score the real way and her quiet pleasure in doing the job carefully for her best friend; the running afternoon happening around the scoresheet of cake-on-fingers and Lip Smacker chapstick passed around the bench and Garbage Pail Kids sticker trades happening on the molded-plastic seats and the alley's PA softly playing Madonna's True Blue album on rotation and the small ambient texture of Sunrise Lanes on a Saturday afternoon (the chunk-clatter of pinsetters resetting, the squeak of rented shoes on lane approaches, the occasional victorious shriek from a kid on a different lane, the soft mechanical hum of the ball-return belt under the floor); the small additional fact that Heather is going to take the carbonless pink copy of this scoresheet home with her, fold it three times, and keep it in her own bedroom desk drawer for the next forty years because it is the scoresheet from Tara's ninth birthday party and Tara is her best friend

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working scorekeeper voice belonging to Heather herself in the hand-pencilled margin annotations on each girl's row and in the small side-margin scratch math beside each frame's pinfall computation — Heather at the scorekeeper's table at the head of Lane 12 with the stubby #2 pencil in her right hand and her left hand pressed flat on the sheet to anchor it, the slice of pepperoni pizza on a paper plate beside her elbow that she has only taken two bites of because she is too busy keeping score, the cup of orange Hi-C with a bendy straw, talking to the visitor (a fellow nine-year-old who has come over to Lane 12 from a different lane and is leaning on the scorekeeper's table watching Heather work) the way she'd talk to her own older brother Brent when he is teaching her notation at the kitchen table Wednesday night: matter-of-fact pride in the AMF scorekeeping notation as serious skilled craft she learned this week and is genuinely good at, conversational warmth in the per-girl margin annotations about each fourth-grader as a real specific person with real specific stuff going on this afternoon ('Kim's dad takes her to Stars and Strikes on Tuesday nights, that's why she's so good — she's been bowling since she was four, she got a 132 last spring at the Junior League trial day which is the highest any nine-year-old at Holt has ever rolled'; 'Stacy has never bowled before in her whole life and her first ball went so far into the gutter it bounced — but then in frame 6 of game two she hit one pin and screamed so loud the front-counter man came over to check on us, I drew a star next to that frame because it counts even if it was just one'; 'Jenny moved here from Macon in August and Tara invited her because she felt bad that Jenny didn't have anyone to sit with at lunch, but it turns out Jenny is really good at bowling? She got a 124 in game two which is better than anyone except Kim, I think maybe her dad bowls too but Jenny says he doesn't, I don't know'), peer-level steadiness about scorekeeping as a real serious job that requires real attention because if you mess up one frame the running total is wrong for the rest of the game and the AMF manual says you have to start the whole row over with an eraser if that happens, present-tense Saturday-afternoon focus on the throws as they happen ('Robin is up now, she's using the six-pound green house ball, she has to roll it between her legs because the regular way is too heavy, she gets a strike here in frame 4 by complete accident and the whole party screams and Sherryl runs over from the party table to see if Robin is okay because she thought somebody got hurt'), real specific affection for Tara as her best friend and for being trusted with the scorekeeping job at Tara's party, full earnest belief that keeping score by hand for twelve fourth-grade girls at a bowling-alley birthday party is one of the more important things a nine-year-old can do on a Saturday afternoon and that the visitor leaning on the table watching her work is participation in a real act of peer-level scorekeeping-witness worthy of full peer-to-peer respect. Absolutely no irony about birthday-party bowling as childish, no smug retrospect about 1986 as quaint pre-internet Americana, no nostalgia for Lisa Frank or Garbage Pail Kids or Bonne Bell as kitsch — these are real things at the real party right now, Heather has a Strawberry Lip Smacker in her own goody bag that she has not opened yet because she is saving it for the car ride home, and the Garbage Pail Kid stickers are actively being traded on the bench behind her between frames.

palette.value: Cream-pink carbonless AMF scoresheet stock (#fce4d8) as the dominant viewport ground with subtle long-fiber paper texture and faint horizontal pulp-fiber striations (#e8b8a4 at 0.32 opacity) running across + deep printed scoresheet brick-red ink (#a82c2a) for the pre-printed AMF grid lines, the frame-numbered headers (1 through 10 across the top of each row), the column rules dividing the player column from the frame columns, the brand wordmark SUNRISE LANES — OLD STAGE ROAD — MARIETTA GA along the top, the small AMF-PAPER-CO catalog reference number along the bottom edge (FORM 712-B — 1985 REV.), and the perforated tear-line dashed border between the cream-pink top sheet and the carbon-pink copy beneath + deep printed composing-room black (#1a1612) for the small printed legend at the bottom of the sheet ('X = STRIKE / = SPARE — = MISS — F = FOUL') and the AMF-manual scorekeeping-notation key + Heather's #2-pencil graphite gray (#3e3530) for every hand-written entry — the twelve girls' first names in the player column written in her careful nine-year-old printing with slight per-letter randomization (some letters slightly larger where she pressed harder, occasional smudged correction where she erased and rewrote, the names rendered in her specific letterform with the closed-loop lowercase 'e's and the slightly-too-large lowercase 'a's that fourth-graders tend toward), every X for strike and / for spare and — for miss carefully drawn per the AMF manual, every running-total integer computed in her own pencil hand down the right side of each row (with occasional tiny side-margin scratch math visible where she had to work out a particularly tricky strike-plus-spare bonus computation), and the per-girl margin annotations in slightly smaller pencil printing tucked into the cream-pink margin to the right of each row + warm Strawberry-Bonne-Bell-Lip-Smacker waxy pink (#e88aa4 at 0.45 opacity) for the two small fingerprint smudges along the top edge where Tara touched the sheet + Fudgie-the-Whale Carvel-frosting circular pink-and-chocolate smudge (#f4a8b8 with #6a3e28 chocolate-cookie-crunch fleck) in the lower-left corner where Megan set her cake plate down + pepperoni-grease translucent amber stain (#c8893a at 0.35 opacity) along the upper-right corner where a slice rested briefly + the partially-adhered Garbage Pail Kids ADAM BOMB sticker rendered in its specific Topps Series 5 painted-illustration colors (cartoon-pink skin #f4b8a4, mushroom-cloud cream-and-orange #f8d878 and #d4623a, GPK logo brick-red #a8312a) stuck across the corner of frame 6 + Heather's name printed in slightly fancier cursive in the upper-right SCOREKEEPER box in her best handwriting (the cursive she practices in her Hello Kitty diary at home) + the lane number 12 circled in pencil twice in the lane-number box + the date 10/18/86 in the date box + the in-progress GAME 2 row at the bottom with the still-unfilled frames showing the empty grid waiting for the remaining throws + soft graphite-pencil smudge gray (#5a5048 at 0.4 opacity) along Heather's left-hand resting zone where her palm has been smearing the partially-completed columns all afternoon + warm Saturday-afternoon bowling-alley fluorescent overhead light cast (#f5e8c4 at 0.18 opacity) falling diagonally across the upper-right of the scoresheet suggesting the angle of the recessed fluorescent fixtures above Lane 12 at 2:47pm + faint ball-return-belt mechanical-vibration micro-shadow (#a89878 at 0.12 opacity) very subtly pulsing along the lower-right of the sheet at roughly 0.4Hz suggesting the belt running beneath the floor + the carbon-pink copy beneath the top sheet just barely visible along the perforated edge as a slightly deeper rose-pink (#e88a98) hinting at the second layer + the AMF-paper carbonless-form characteristic faint vertical alignment guides (#d4a89c at 0.18 opacity) running the full height of each frame column at standard 1.4-inch intervals + the alley's house-shoe-rental ink-stamp brick-red impression (#a82c2a at 0.65 opacity) along the lower-right corner reading 'SUNRISE LANES — RENTAL — 10/18' faintly + the soft cool molded-plastic scorekeeper's-table beige (#d4c8a8) visible at the very edges of the viewport beyond the scoresheet itself

mandatory_element.value: A fully working hand-pencilled scoresheet experience with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented: (1) the scoresheet renders at full viewport scale as the actual AMF Form 712-B carbonless paper object lying on the molded-plastic scorekeeper's table at the head of Lane 12, with all 12 player rows pre-filled across game 1 (complete) and game 2 (in progress, the first 5 frames completed for most girls, frame 6 in progress for several) — every frame's strike/spare/pinfall notation rendered in Heather's hand-styled pencil printing per the AMF manual with mathematically correct running totals computed accurately down each row (the score-keeping logic baked into the page as JavaScript so any visitor verification of the math against the AMF rules confirms the totals); (2) each of the 12 player rows is hoverable to reveal a soft warm graphite spotlight on that row and fade in a small spiral-notebook-paper margin annotation tucked into the cream-pink margin to the right of the row — minimum 12 distinct annotations, each its own peer-level vignette in Heather's nine-year-old printing about that specific girl (Tara, Heather herself, Megan, Kim, Stacy, Jenny, Brittany, Erin, Crystal, Amy, Lisa, Robin), each annotation 3-6 sentences carrying the small specific working knowledge Heather has about that fourth-grader (who she is, what she's like as a bowler, what she's wearing today, whether she's having a good time, what Lip Smacker flavor she got in her goody bag, who she has traded Garbage Pail Kids with, the small fact about her family or her older sibling or her favorite class at Holt that Heather knows because they have been in school together since first grade), no two annotations recycling phrasing or pattern; (3) each individual completed frame across all 12 rows in both games is clickable to reveal Heather's pencilled scratch-math work-out for that frame's pinfall computation in tiny side-margin notation — for strike frames the next-two-balls bonus computation is shown, for spare frames the next-ball bonus is shown, for split-conversions Heather's small pencilled note 'CONVERTED!' appears beside the frame; minimum 84 frames clickable across both games (some recycle simpler math, but every X and / frame shows its bonus work); (4) a working WATCH GAME 2 FINISH button rendered as a small pencilled arrow at the bottom of the in-progress game-2 row labeled 'PLAY THE REST OF THE PARTY' which when clicked advances the in-progress game frame-by-frame in real time at roughly 4 seconds per frame, with each girl's remaining throws animated as small pencil-marks appearing in her frame and Heather's running totals updating live, accompanied by soft Web Audio API sounds for each throw (the chunk-thump of a ball hitting pins, the rattle of a pinsetter resetting, occasional party-shriek and giggle background tracks at low volume, the soft squeak of rented bowling-shoe rubber on the approach), and the game-2 finishes with Robin's accidental frame-4 strike playing out and the whole party visibly reacting in the margins via small animated pencil-doodled hearts appearing around her frame and Heather's annotation in the row-12 margin updating in real-time to add 'ROBIN GOT A STRIKE!! everyone screamed!!'; (5) a working PEEL BACK THE TOP SHEET interaction along the perforated lower-left corner of the scoresheet — clicking and dragging the corner peels back the cream-pink top sheet over 1.2 seconds with a soft paper-on-paper rustle via Web Audio API revealing the deeper rose-pink carbonless copy beneath, which carries Heather's exact same notation in a slightly darker pink carbon-ink impression (showing how carbonless duplicate forms transferred the pencil pressure to the second layer); the peeled state persists in localStorage; (6) a working SIGN THE BACK button rendered as the scoresheet's perforated upper-right corner labeled 'TURN OVER TO SIGN' which when clicked flips the entire scoresheet over via a smooth CSS card-flip animation revealing the back of the AMF form, which is mostly blank cream stock with twelve hand-pencilled signatures already laid out in a rough cluster — each of the 12 girls has signed her name in her own distinct nine-year-old penmanship with a small drawn flourish beside it (Tara's name with three hearts and a star, Kim's name with a crisp underline, Stacy's name with a smiley face that has hair, Jenny's name with a small drawn flower because that's how she signs her name on her assignments at Holt, the Trumbull twins' matching mint-green-themed clovers, Robin's tiny careful name with a small drawn bowling pin beside it, etc.); the visitor adds their own signature by clicking a blank space and typing their first name, which renders in pencil-cursive style with subtle per-stroke randomization and lets them choose a small flourish from a tray (heart, star, flower, bowling-pin, smiley, lightning-bolt); the typed signature persists permanently in localStorage; (7) minimum 6 small interactive ambient objects scattered on the scorekeeper's table around the scoresheet: a partially-eaten slice of pepperoni pizza on a paper plate (clickable to take a bite — Heather's hunger meter increments and a small pencilled note in the margin reads 'finally ate the pizza — cold but ok'), Heather's cup of orange Hi-C with bendy straw (clickable to sip — the cup level visibly drops by small increments), a stubby pencil (clickable to sharpen — a small pencil-sharpener appears, the pencil-tip renews, subsequent margin-additions render slightly crisper), a small pile of three traded Garbage Pail Kids cards (BLASTED BILLY, ADAM BOMB, BARFIN' BARBARA — each hoverable to show its painted illustration full-size with Topps backing-card copy), a Bonne Bell Strawberry Lip Smacker (Heather's unopened goody-bag one she is saving — clickable to apply, the cursor briefly renders a soft pink waxy bloom that fades, and a small pencilled note in the margin reads 'put on the lip smacker — strawberry, my favorite'), and an open AMF Junior Bowling Scoring Manual (the small green-covered booklet Brent loaned her — clickable to flip through, showing real AMF scorekeeping notation diagrams across 4 pages with Heather's small underlines and her brother's older pencilled marginalia in slightly different handwriting); (8) minimum 8 small subtle interactions in the printed-form margins and the alley-environment edges: hovering exactly the SUNRISE LANES brand wordmark at the top for 3 full seconds reveals a small annotation reading 'we always come here on Tara's birthday, this is the fourth year, last year we did Lane 8 but Lane 12 is closer to the bathroom which Sherryl said matters with twelve girls'; hovering the AMF Form 712-B reference number reveals 'Brent told me they print these in Buffalo NY — Buffalo NY is where bowling alleys get their paper'; hovering the date 10/18/86 reveals 'Tara's real birthday is October 21st (Tuesday) but Sherryl said you can't have a party on a Tuesday so we are doing it today'; hovering Lane 11's automatic-scorer mention along the top reads 'Lane 11 is doing it the computer way, Sherryl said one lane could go automatic and one should be done the educational way and I volunteered'; hovering the alley's house-shoe-rental stamp reveals 'Crystal's shoe came untied four times so far, I have retied it twice and Sherryl twice'; hovering the Carvel-frosting smudge reveals 'Megan set her cake plate down here in the middle of frame 7, I will tell her about it later, the frosting is hard to wipe off without smudging the pencil'; hovering the partially-adhered ADAM BOMB sticker reveals 'Erin gave me this one because she got two of him in her pack, she said ADAM BOMB is the rarest one in Series 5 — I don't know if that is true but it was nice of her'; hovering the cream-pink carbonless border edge reveals 'the pink copy goes to the alley for the league records book, the white-pink top copy I am giving to Sherryl to put in Tara's scrapbook, that is what Sherryl said this morning'; (9) all visitor state persists in localStorage forever — which row-annotations have been hovered, whether game 2 has been played out, whether the top sheet has been peeled back, whether the back has been flipped and signed, the visitor's signature, accumulated bites of pizza and sips of Hi-C, applied Lip Smacker, traded Garbage Pail Kids, and the small Robin-strike marginalia state — so a returning visitor finds the scoresheet exactly as they left it; (10) a shared persistent cross-visitor signature count rendered on the back of the scoresheet beneath the cluster of pencil signatures reads 'GIRLS WHO SIGNED TARA'S SCORESHEET: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere signs the back (shared count starting at 12 — Tara, Heather, and the ten other party girls — and growing); (11) absolutely no instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page explaining how to hover the rows, click the frames, peel the top sheet, flip to sign, play out game 2, or interact with the table objects — no tooltips, no captions, no loading messages, no 'EXPLORE THE SCORESHEET' header banner, no footer credits — the visitor figures it out by leaning over the scorekeeper's table and watching Heather work; (12) the scoresheet does not regenerate, does not change its frame data, does not randomize the girls' performances — it is the one specific scoresheet Heather is keeping for Lane 12 at Tara Bickerstaff's ninth birthday party at Sunrise Lanes on Saturday October 18, 1986, and reading it carefully is the entire page

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Heather's 12 hover-revealed row annotations — the one tucked alongside Robin Eldred's row — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about a six-pound green house ball, two small hands between two knees, and a strike that nobody saw coming including Robin — disguised naturally within Heather's earnest peer-level scorekeeper note about why Robin is the smallest girl in fourth grade at Holt and how she bowls between her legs. Additionally, after a returning visitor has signed the back of the scoresheet on three separate calendar dates AND has played out game 2 to completion at least twice across visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden thirteenth signature quietly appears on the back of the scoresheet the next time the visitor returns — in slightly different more-careful adult handwriting (not a nine-year-old's printing, more like a grown woman's blue ballpoint cursive) in a small cluster of its own slightly apart from the twelve fourth-graders' signatures, reading 'Sherryl Bickerstaff — Tara's mom — thank you for keeping it, Heather — I taped the top sheet into Tara's scrapbook the next week, October 25 1986, and I am writing this from my kitchen table in 2026 forty years later because Tara found the scrapbook in the attic when we were cleaning out the house and she scanned the scoresheet for me — Heather you are in Athens now and Tara is going to mail you a copy.' This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact at the bottom of the back of the scoresheet for visitors who kept coming back.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when kids had birthday parties at bowling alleys' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the hand-kept bowling scoresheet,' NO cool ironic distance from 1980s suburban Marietta Georgia birthday-party culture as quaint pre-internet Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Tara or Heather or Sunrise Lanes or the twelve fourth-grade girls as already-vanished. NO Stranger-Things 1986 pastiche aesthetic, NO synthwave neon, NO vaporwave mall-aesthetic ironic mood-board styling, NO 'liminal-space dead-bowling-alley' YouTube-aesthetic horror register. NO horror-genre framing of the empty scoresheet table, the in-progress game-2 frames, Robin's accidental strike, or the hidden 40-years-later Sherryl signature as creepy/uncanny/sinister/haunted — this is a working Saturday afternoon at a working suburban Georgia bowling alley, Lane 12 is full of nine-year-olds, the pinsetters are clattering, Madonna is on the PA, and Heather is keeping score with full peer-level competence. NO 'something happened to one of the girls later' tragic foreshadowing, NO 'the alley closed in 2009' decline narrative overlaid on the 1986 page, NO 'Sunrise Lanes is now a Hobby Lobby' bittersweet update. NO condescension toward children's bowling birthday parties as small or trivial, toward goody bags and Garbage Pail Kids and Lisa Frank stickers as kitsch, toward fourth-grade girls as a serious peer-group worth full respect, toward Heather's scorekeeping job as childish play-acting at adult work — Heather is genuinely keeping accurate AMF-manual scoresheet notation and the work matters and she is good at it. NO 'imagine being nine in 1986 with a Lip Smacker in your goody bag' smug-modern-superiority framing, NO Boomer-childhood-nostalgia 'they don't make birthday parties like this anymore' voice. NO Stand-By-Me melancholy-childhood-tableau register, NO Wonder-Years narrator-looking-back voice, NO 'best summer of her life' framing. NO feminist-grievance reading of the goody-bag contents as gendered consumer culture — these are nine-year-old girls who genuinely like Lip Smackers and Garbage Pail Kids and Lisa Frank stickers and there is no agenda. NO 'the Carvel cake is going to be replaced by the obesity epidemic' moralizing, NO health-anxiety overlay on the pepperoni pizza and ice-cream cake. NO Wes-Anderson-symmetrical-frame styling, NO Pinterest-vintage-1980s aesthetic mood-boarding, NO Norman-Rockwell-Saturday-Evening-Post sentimentality. NO Jenny-Kim-as-tragic-newcomer framing — Jenny moved here in August, she is doing fine, Tara invited her, and she rolled a 124. NO Robin-as-pitiable-smallest-girl framing — Robin is the smallest girl in fourth grade and she rolled a strike by accident and the whole party cheered for her and that is the entire story. NO night-shift dispatcher voice (Saturday afternoon at 2:47pm in a bowling alley is not a midnight desk vigil), NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the scoresheet or the hidden Sherryl signature or the encoded haiku, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the scoresheet as relic (it is a working paper document being used right now), NO observatory/society/club scaffolding, NO radio/CB/broadcast/dispatcher/transmission framing of the alley's PA or the pinsetter mechanism or the ball-return belt as signal-network metaphor (the PA is just a PA, the belt is just a belt). NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing (Saturday is a contextual fact about when the party is held, not the subject of the piece). NO instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'KEEP SCORE FOR TARA'S BIRTHDAY PARTY' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS the AMF Form 712-B scoresheet lying on the molded-plastic scorekeeper's table at the head of Lane 12 at Sunrise Lanes at 2:47pm Saturday October 18, 1986, the visitor IS another nine-year-old who has come over from a different lane and is leaning on the table watching Heather work (or is Heather herself with the pencil in her hand, or is Sherryl coming back from the front counter to check on the cake situation, or is one of the twelve girls reading the scoresheet between her own throws), and the only voices anywhere on the page are Heather's small graphite-pencil printing in the margin annotations and the scratch-math computations, the printed AMF/Sunrise Lanes commercial typography of the form itself, the twelve girls' own signature flourishes on the back, and (in the wildcard unlock) Sherryl's adult ballpoint cursive 40 years later. Treat the AMF scoresheet as a serious working community document, treat Heather Wendling as a legitimate working nine-year-old scorekeeper with real AMF-manual notation expertise and full peer-level competence, treat the twelve fourth-grade girls as a real specific peer group of real specific kids doing real specific birthday-party bowling worth full peer-to-peer respect, treat Sunrise Lanes on Old Stage Road as a real legitimate suburban-Georgia recreational institution worth dignified attention, and treat the visitor's careful hovering and clicking and signing as real participation in a real Saturday-afternoon act of peer-level birthday-party witness.

era.value: 1992 — peak American suburban family-room sliding-glass-door winter-window backyard-bird-feeder hobby-watching era, the specific decade-mid moment when middle-class families across the upper Midwest hung a single black-tube Droll Yankees A-6 sunflower-seed feeder on a shepherd's-hook pole approximately fourteen feet from the sliding-glass door of the family room, kept the National Audubon Society's Eastern Birds field guide (1989 Knopf paperback, $18.95) on the small bookshelf beside the door alongside a small spiral-bound notebook the woman of the house kept as her own running winter-feeder log of every species that came to the feeder that winter, the specific Saturday morning of December 12 1992 at the home of Mrs. Patricia 'Patty' Voznesensky of 4218 Birchwood Drive in Brookfield Wisconsin (age 41, part-time medical-records clerk at the Waukesha Memorial Hospital records department three mornings a week, married fourteen years to Greg Voznesensky who is a quality-control engineer at the Pewaukee Allen-Bradley plant, mother of two boys Kyle 11 and Eric 8 who are upstairs playing Sega Genesis NHL '93), Patty at the sliding-glass door at 8:42am with her second cup of coffee in a cream-and-rust 'WORLD'S BEST MOM' mug Eric made her at Brookfield Pottery Painting Place last May, the feeder visible through the door with three black-capped chickadees and two dark-eyed juncos and one tufted titmouse and (just arriving) a single male northern cardinal landing on the perch in the fresh six inches of overnight snow, Patty's spiral notebook open on the small oak side-table beside her wing-chair, the Audubon field guide open beside it to the cardinal page, her Bic pen ready to log the time and species and notes.

format.value: A fully working tiny browser bird-identification toy: a single black-tube sunflower-seed feeder hangs in the middle of the viewport against a snowy backyard scene (a small white-pine in the background, a sliding-glass-door wood-deck railing at the very bottom edge of the viewport, the soft snowfall continuing at 2-3 pixels per second of gentle drifting flakes), and across each visit-session the visitor watches small pixel-art-styled songbirds fly into the viewport from the edges, land on the feeder perch, eat for a few seconds, and fly off — six species in the species pool (black-capped chickadee, dark-eyed junco, tufted titmouse, white-breasted nuthatch, downy woodpecker, northern cardinal), each with its own accurate flight pattern (the chickadee's bouncy undulating flight, the junco's straight-line dart, the woodpecker's deep wave-arc, the cardinal's heavy fluttery glide), each with its own landing posture and feeding behavior at the feeder, each rendered in 24×18-pixel pixel-art bodies with species-accurate field marks (the chickadee's black cap and bib, the junco's slate-gray hood and white belly, the titmouse's crest, the nuthatch's upside-down feeder grip, the woodpecker's white spots, the cardinal's crest and black mask). The visitor identifies each bird that lands by clicking on the bird while it's at the feeder, which opens a small floating field-guide panel showing six species silhouette buttons — clicking the correct silhouette logs that bird into Patty's spiral-notebook entry for the day (the notebook visible in the lower-right corner of the viewport, rendered as a small ruled spiral page that fills with hand-written entries in Patty's blue Bic cursive as the visitor identifies birds correctly). Incorrect ID's bounce gently with a soft Patty annotation ('that's not quite right — look at the cap'). The piece is the working morning at the sliding-glass door.

subject.value: The specific Saturday-morning bird-feeder watch at Patty Voznesensky's sliding-glass door on December 12 1992 between 8:42am and 10:14am, the first heavy-snow morning of the winter, the feeder freshly refilled the previous evening (Greg topped it off Friday after work with a fresh five-pound bag of Wild Birds Unlimited black-oil sunflower seed he picked up at the Brookfield Square Mall location on his lunch break), Patty's intention this morning to log every species that comes to the feeder across her two-hour coffee window because she is hoping to break her single-morning species record (eight species, set last February 15 1992 when a brown creeper and a red-bellied woodpecker both showed up on the same morning); the running infrastructure of one woman's three-winter-long backyard-feeder hobby log kept in her spiral notebook on the oak side-table, the small private skill of distinguishing chickadees from titmice at thirty feet through a sliding-glass door in falling snow, the patient watching that takes a full Saturday morning to accumulate eight species worth of data; Patty's own quiet weekly Saturday-morning ritual of sitting in the wing-chair with the field guide open and the notebook ready and the coffee getting cold and the boys upstairs and Greg at the hardware store buying weather-stripping, and the genuine working pleasure of identifying a bird correctly on her own without having to ask Greg or look it up later.

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working backyard-birder voice belonging to Patty herself in small hover-revealed margin annotations on each species and small spiral-notebook entries that appear as the visitor logs birds — Patty at the sliding-glass door this Saturday morning with the WORLD'S BEST MOM mug in her left hand and the Bic in her right and the Audubon guide open on the side-table, talking to the visitor (a fellow backyard-feeder watcher who has dropped by for coffee — maybe her sister-in-law Linda who lives two blocks over and birds her own feeder, maybe a neighbor who has been thinking about putting up a feeder this winter) the way she'd talk over a kitchen counter: matter-of-fact pride in three winters of patient backyard-feeder identification as serious skilled amateur-ornithology craft, conversational warmth about each species as a specific small individual she has been watching for weeks (the cardinal pair has been visiting since November 7, the chickadee flock has been the same six individuals all autumn, the tufted titmouse is brand new this week and Patty is pleased), peer-level steadiness about the eight-species-record goal as a real meaningful Saturday-morning project worth taking seriously, present-tense focus on each bird as it lands and feeds and flies off, real specific affection for the Audubon field guide as a working reference she has come to know by heart, full earnest belief that two hours at the sliding-glass door with a notebook and coffee is one of the more satisfying ways she spends her Saturday mornings. Absolutely no irony about backyard birding as a hobby for old people, no apology for spending a Saturday morning watching a feeder, no smug-modern detachment about pre-internet bird-identification work.

palette.value: Soft winter-morning overcast sky pale-gray (#dde4ec) as the dominant viewport background with subtle gradient lightening toward the lower third (#e8eef4) suggesting horizon glow + falling snow rendered as small soft-edged white flakes (#fefefe with halo at 0.7 opacity) drifting downward at varied velocities + the white-pine background tree in deep winter-green (#2a3e2c) with subtle snow-laden branch tips in soft cream (#f6f1e2) + the wood-deck railing at the bottom edge in warm weathered cedar (#9e7e58) with snow-cap on the top rail (#fefdf8) + the Droll Yankees A-6 feeder tube in matte charcoal-black (#2a2a2e) with chrome perches (#a8acb0) and a small clear acrylic seed-window showing the level of black-oil sunflower seeds (#3a2418) inside + species-accurate pixel-art bird body colors: chickadee black-cap (#1a1612) and white cheek (#f4f0e4) and buff flank (#c89878); junco slate-gray hood (#4a4a52) and white belly (#f0ecdc) and pink bill (#e8b8a0); tufted titmouse soft gray crest (#7a7a82) and white belly (#f0ecdc) and rust flank-patch (#a86840); white-breasted nuthatch slate-back (#4a5a6e) and white face (#f0ecdc) and black cap (#1a1612); downy woodpecker white-and-black checkered wings (#1a1612 and #f4f0e4) and red nape-spot on males (#a8312a); northern cardinal male brick-red (#a8312a) with black face-mask (#1a1612) and orange-red bill (#e8623a) + Patty's spiral notebook page cream (#f8f0d8) with faint pale-blue horizontal ruling (#9eb4c8 at 0.5 opacity) and pink left-margin rule (#d89aa4 at 0.6 opacity) + Patty's blue Bic ballpoint (#1c3a7e) for her cursive log entries with subtle per-stroke pressure variation + the Audubon field guide cover deep forest-green (#2d5a3a) with cream type (#f4e8c4) + warm WORLD'S BEST MOM mug cream (#f4ecd0) with rust glaze (#a86840) and Eric's wobbly seven-year-old hand-painted heart (#a8312a) + soft sliding-glass-door window-frame aluminum trim (#a8acb0) visible at the very edges of the viewport + warm window-light reflection (#fdf6e0 at 0.10 opacity) very faintly suggesting the family-room interior lighting reflecting back on the glass

mandatory_element.value: A fully working bird-watching and identification game with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented: (1) the viewport renders as the view through Patty's sliding-glass door of the snowy backyard with the Droll Yankees feeder centered and falling snow continuously animated; (2) a continuous procedurally-driven visit-schedule: every 18-32 seconds a new bird flies into the viewport from a random edge with its species-accurate flight pattern (chickadee bouncy undulation via sinusoidal y-displacement, junco straight dart, woodpecker deep wave-arc, cardinal heavy fluttery glide, nuthatch quick zip, titmouse direct line with brief perch-pauses), lands on the feeder perch or hangs from the tube with its species-correct posture (nuthatch upside-down, woodpecker clinging, others perched upright), feeds for 6-14 seconds with small head-pecking animation, then flies off in another direction; (3) up to 3 birds can be at the feeder simultaneously, with smaller birds occasionally fleeing when a larger bird (cardinal, woodpecker) arrives — species-realistic dominance hierarchy in feeder behavior; (4) clicking any bird while it is at the feeder pauses time briefly and opens a floating field-guide ID panel showing six species silhouette buttons in the style of Audubon guide thumbnails — clicking the correct silhouette logs the species into Patty's spiral notebook in the lower-right with a fresh entry in blue Bic cursive ('8:47 — black-capped chickadee — first of morning'), and incorrect clicks bounce gently with Patty's soft margin annotation appearing briefly ('that's a junco — look at the slate hood'); (5) Patty's spiral notebook in the lower-right accumulates entries across the visit-session: time-of-arrival, species name, and a small note Patty adds ('first of morning' / 'flock of six now' / 'new species this winter!' / 'female, no red'), the entries rendering in real-time as the visitor identifies each bird; (6) the species count for today displays at the top of the notebook page incrementing each time a new species is correctly identified, with the goal '8 SPECIES — LAST FEBRUARY'S RECORD' visible as a small pencilled note at the top of the page; (7) reaching 8 distinct species in a session triggers a small celebration moment: Patty's handwritten note appears at the bottom of the notebook reading 'beat it!! 9:47am — eight species before ten o'clock — Greg is going to hear about this when he gets home from Ace Hardware', and a 9th and rarer species pool unlocks (brown creeper, red-bellied woodpecker, hairy woodpecker, American goldfinch) appearing more rarely across the rest of the session; (8) hovering any of the six species silhouettes in the field-guide panel for 2 seconds reveals Patty's margin annotation about that species — minimum 6 distinct annotations, each its own peer-level vignette ('the cardinal pair has been coming since November 7, I think they're nesting in the spruce two yards over — Greg saw the female working on something in there in October'; 'the tufted titmouse is brand new this winter, I have never had one at the feeder before — Linda told me at the Hanukkah party she had been getting one and I was jealous, and now here we are'); (9) all visitor progress persists in localStorage forever: today's species count, today's notebook entries, lifetime species seen across visits, lifetime sessions of 8+ species (a small running 'big mornings' counter at the bottom of the notebook), and a small cross-visit field-guide page that fills in colored thumbnails for every species the visitor has ever correctly identified; (10) absolutely no instructional UI text visible explaining how to play — the visitor figures it out by clicking on birds; (11) a shared cross-visitor persistent counter at the very bottom of the notebook reads 'BIRDS LOGGED AT PATTY'S FEEDER THIS WINTER: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere correctly identifies a bird (shared persistent count starting at 1,847 — Patty's running winter tally to date).

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Patty's six hover-revealed species annotations — the one tucked alongside the tufted titmouse silhouette — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about a small crest catching morning light, a brand-new species at an old feeder, and a December morning a woman has been waiting three winters for — disguised naturally within Patty's earnest peer-level note about her sister-in-law Linda's titmouse-envy. Additionally, after a returning visitor has reached 8 species on three separate calendar dates AND has correctly identified at least one rare 9th-species bird across visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden tenth species quietly enters the visit-pool the next time the visitor returns — a single pine siskin that appears once per visit-session with no fanfare, landing on the feeder briefly and feeding for 4 seconds before flying off. The pine siskin is not in the field-guide panel by default — it requires the visitor to correctly identify it from memory by clicking the bird and then typing the species name in a small fade-in text field that appears beneath the silhouette panel. Correct identification adds the pine siskin to Patty's lifetime field-guide page with a small handwritten note in Patty's cursive: 'pine siskin — first one ever at the feeder — Saturday December 19 1992 — Linda is going to be jealous all over again — P.V.' This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact at the bottom of the field-guide page for visitors who kept coming back.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when families watched bird feeders' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of backyard birding,' NO cool ironic distance from 1990s suburban Wisconsin family-room culture as quaint pre-internet Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Patty or the Voznesensky family or 4218 Birchwood Drive as already-vanished. NO 'where are they now' wistfulness about Patty grown older or the boys grown up or the cardinal pair gone. NO horror-genre framing of the empty backyard, the falling snow, the sliding-glass door, or the watching itself as creepy/uncanny/sinister/liminal — this is a warm Saturday morning at home, the family room is heated, the coffee is fresh, the boys are happy upstairs playing NHL '93, and the feeder is doing what feeders do on snowy mornings in Wisconsin. NO climate-grief overlay about declining bird populations (this is 1992, the populations Patty is watching are healthy, and the page does not retroactively reframe). NO 'this hobby is for old people' condescension toward backyard birding, NO smug modern superiority about pre-Merlin-app bird identification ('imagine flipping through a paper field guide'). NO Mad-Men-pastiche period kitsch, NO Stranger-Things 1990s aesthetic, NO synthwave neon, NO ironic-90s vaporwave mood-boarding. NO feminist-grievance reading of Patty's part-time medical-records work and homemaker hours as oppression — Patty chose this arrangement, the work suits her, and the Saturday morning is hers. NO Greg-the-husband-as-absent-or-bad framing — Greg is at Ace Hardware buying weather-stripping because the family-room door has been drafty and he wants to fix it this weekend, and he topped off the feeder Friday evening because he thought of Patty's Saturday morning. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the pine siskin or the encoded haiku, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the notebook as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of backyard birders as fictional small-town society, NO radio/CB/broadcast/transmission framing of the feeder visits as a signal-network. NO day-of-the-week subject framing (Saturday is contextual not the subject). NO 'fictional small-town society/club' framing. NO instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'IDENTIFY THE BIRDS AT PATTY'S FEEDER' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS the view through the sliding-glass door at 8:42am Saturday December 12 1992, the visitor IS sitting in the wing-chair beside Patty (or is Patty herself with the Bic in her hand), and the only voices anywhere on the page are Patty's blue Bic cursive notebook entries and her soft hover-revealed species annotations. Treat the Droll Yankees A-6 feeder as serious working backyard wildlife infrastructure, treat Patty as a legitimate working amateur ornithologist with three winters of patient identification expertise and a real running hobby-craft practice, treat the eight-species record as a real meaningful Saturday-morning project worth peer-to-peer respect, treat the six species in the visit-pool as real specific individual birds with real specific feeder-behavior patterns, and treat the visitor's careful click-to-identify work as real participation in a real Saturday-morning act of patient backyard-birding craft worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1978 — peak American suburban Sunday-morning kitchen-table jigsaw-puzzle-club era, the very specific moment when middle-class families across the upper Midwest kept a half-completed Springbok 500-piece circular puzzle (the legendary 1965-introduced Springbok 'Round' line, manufactured at the Springbok Editions plant in Kansas City Missouri, 20.25 inches in diameter, packaged in a square gold-foil-stamped art-paper box with a small printed insert reading 'A SPRINGBOK ROUND — THE PUZZLE WITH NO STRAIGHT EDGES') laid out on a folding card table set up in the corner of the dining room from Thanksgiving through Easter, and the very specific Sunday morning of February 12, 1978, at 4814 Pinegrove Terrace in Bloomfield Hills, Michigan, when forty-three-year-old hospital lab technician Mrs. Carolyn Vandevoort sat at the card table at 10:14am with a cup of Sanka in a mustard-yellow Pyrex mug, the latest Springbok ('Carnival Glass — A Mosaic of American Pressed Glass, 1978') two-thirds complete in front of her, a small index card beside the puzzle on which she had written in her own neat cursive a single logic-puzzle she had clipped from the back page of last month's Games magazine (the September-October 1977 issue, premiere debut of the Sam Loyd retrospective column edited by Will Shortz then 25 years old and just hired at Penny Press) and that she had been working on across three Sunday mornings without solving — three of her four neighborhood friends had stopped by for Carnival Glass jigsaw progress and a slice of crumb cake at various points across those three Sundays and each of them had also tried the logic puzzle and none of them had cracked it either, and Carolyn had decided this Sunday she was going to figure it out before Phil came back from buying the Sunday Free Press

format.value: A genuine fully solvable in-browser logic-grid puzzle disguised as the actual index card Mrs. Vandevoort has written out beside her Carnival Glass jigsaw on the corner of the folding card table — the entire viewport is the green felt card-table surface viewed from slightly above with the half-completed Springbok Round puzzle visible at the upper-left edge (only the outer rim showing, the rest of the puzzle off-frame) and a cream 4×6 ruled index card centered in the middle of the viewport with Carolyn's hand-pencilled logic-puzzle written on it in her careful blue Bic ballpoint cursive, plus a smaller second card to the right of the first with the empty 3-trait deduction grid pre-ruled in pencil and waiting to be filled in. The puzzle is a real solvable 3×3×3 logic grid with exactly one valid solution: three women (Carolyn, her friend Marlene Pruss who lives two doors down, and her friend Donna Spangler from the hospital lab) have each brought a different baked good (lemon bars, crumb cake, pecan ring) to a different recent Sunday's jigsaw session (January 22, January 29, February 5), and four cross-referencing clues hand-written in Carolyn's cursive below the grid lock in exactly one assignment per axis. The visitor solves by clicking any cell of the grid to cycle it through three states (blank → checkmark → X → blank) in Carolyn's same pencil hand, with each placement rendering in soft graphite-pencil style with slight per-stroke randomization. The clues are genuinely deductive — no guessing required, all three assignments derivable from the four clues by patient elimination. A REVEAL button rendered as Carolyn's pencil-pressed margin doodle (a small star with three rays) in the lower-right corner of the second card is initially dimmed and brightens only after all three diagonal main-axis cells are correctly committed, at which point clicking it folds back the corner of the index card to reveal Carolyn's own pencilled working-solution on the back of the card (with her crossed-out wrong attempts visible from the previous three Sundays) plus a small additional pencilled note in the lower margin reading 'figured it out 11:08am — Phil came in with the paper at 11:14, I had a head start on him for once.'

subject.value: The single specific logic-grid puzzle Carolyn Vandevoort clipped from the September-October 1977 Games magazine back page (puzzle number 17 in the 'Sunday Mornings' series of light logic-grid puzzles by Penny Press contributor Mr. Edgar Halverson of Norwalk Connecticut, a retired actuarial consultant who composed the series across 1976-1979 for Games magazine at the rate of one puzzle every two months for a flat fee of $35 per puzzle, working from a small writing table by the window of his upstairs sewing room which his late wife Margaret had used as her quilting room until her passing in March 1974), and the specific small private Sunday-morning ritual across late January and early February 1978 of three middle-aged women in Bloomfield Hills working a 500-piece Springbok Round at Carolyn's dining-room corner while taking turns at a clipped logic-grid puzzle that none of them could quite crack — Carolyn (43, hospital lab tech at William Beaumont Hospital, mother of two, married twenty-one years to Phil who is district manager for a paper-supply distributor and who is currently at the 7-Eleven on Telegraph Road buying the Sunday paper and a quart of orange juice), Marlene Pruss (47, school librarian at Bloomfield Junior High, married to Hal who is at home doing his taxes), and Donna Spangler (38, fellow lab tech at Beaumont, never-married, lives alone in the duplex on West Maple); the entire small infrastructure of three women's three-Sunday running attempt at one tiny printed logic-puzzle as a real form of skilled peer-to-peer recreational craft worth taking seriously, alongside a 500-piece jigsaw they have been working since the day after Christmas; Carolyn's quiet pride this particular Sunday morning at having decided she was going to actually solve the thing before Phil came home with the paper, the small fact that she is good at logic puzzles in general and has solved the previous four in the 'Sunday Mornings' series across the past eight months, the small fact that this is the first one in the series that has resisted her across multiple sittings, and her quiet patient working-pleasure at the puzzle finally cracking open around 10:47am when she realizes the lemon bars clue and the January 29 clue interlock in a way she had missed the previous two Sundays because she had been reading the second clue too literally.

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working amateur-logician voice belonging to Carolyn herself in the hand-pencilled clue text and in the small hover-revealed margin annotations on each clue and on the grid axes — Carolyn at the card table this Sunday morning with the Pyrex mug of Sanka cooling at her right elbow and the Bic ballpoint in her left hand (she is left-handed) and the Carnival Glass jigsaw at her shoulder waiting for the puzzle to be solved before she goes back to it, talking to the visitor (a fellow Sunday-morning logic-puzzle solver who has sat down across the card table — maybe Marlene having stopped by again this morning, maybe Donna who said she might come over, maybe a daughter home from college who has wandered in for a cup of coffee) the way she'd talk to a peer at her own kitchen table: matter-of-fact pride in eight months of skilled logic-grid expertise as serious recreational craft, conversational warmth about each clue as a small specific piece of deductive engineering Mr. Halverson built carefully, peer-level steadiness about the puzzle as a real meaningful Sunday-morning project worth taking seriously across three weeks of attempts, real specific affection for the Springbok Round on the card table beside them and for the Games magazine subscription Phil renews for her every December as one of her two Christmas presents, present-tense Sunday-morning focus on getting the grid solved before 11:14am when Phil walks in with the orange juice. Absolutely no irony about middle-aged-women's Sunday-morning jigsaw-and-puzzle culture as quaint or unserious, no apology for spending three Sundays on a single clipped logic-grid, no smug retrospect about Games magazine or Springbok puzzles as kitsch.

palette.value: Card-table felt green (#2a5a3a) as the dominant viewport ground with subtle pile-direction sheen variation (#1c4628 at 0.35 opacity diagonal striations) and faint accumulated cat-hair lint specks (#a89878 at 0.3 opacity, scattered) + cream ruled index-card stock (#f4e8c4) for the central card with faint pale-blue horizontal ruling lines (#9eb4c8 at 0.5 opacity) running across and a thin pink left-margin rule (#d89aa4 at 0.6 opacity) + Carolyn's left-handed blue Bic ballpoint (#1c3a7e) for the clue text and grid-axis labels with subtle leftward-leaning slant and per-stroke pressure variation + soft graphite-pencil gray (#4a4540) for the visitor's grid-cell entries with slight per-stroke randomization + warm Pyrex-mug mustard-yellow (#c89538) visible at the upper-right edge of the viewport with the faint coffee-ring shadow (#7a5634 at 0.4 opacity) on the felt where the mug has been sitting + Springbok puzzle outer rim visible at upper-left in carnival-glass iridescent colors (deep amethyst #5e2c5a, gold-amber #c89538, peacock-blue #2c5a6e, rose-pink #c8628a) with subtle gold-foil sheen highlights along assembled-piece edges + Carolyn's small pencilled margin doodle star (#4a4540) in the lower-right corner of the second card + warm Sunday-morning February light cast (#fff4c8 at 0.18 opacity) falling diagonally across the upper-right of the viewport suggesting the south-facing dining-room window at 10:14am + faint mug-ring shadow on the felt + tiny ballpoint-blot (#1c3a7e at 0.6 opacity) where Carolyn paused mid-clue to think + soft pencil-eraser smudge tan (#c8b896 at 0.5 opacity) along the back of the second card where she has been working through wrong attempts.

mandatory_element.value: A fully working solvable 3-trait logic-grid puzzle with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented: (1) the viewport renders as the card-table scene with the central cream index card holding Carolyn's hand-pencilled clue text and the secondary card to its right holding the empty 3×3×3 deduction grid (women × baked goods × Sundays) pre-ruled in pencil; (2) the four cross-referencing clues are hand-written in Carolyn's blue Bic ballpoint cursive beneath the grid in the cream index card, each clue numbered 1-4, each clue genuinely deductive (the puzzle has exactly one valid solution derivable from the four clues by patient elimination — the clue-set and solution baked into the page as JavaScript constants, no server, no API, no network calls); (3) each cell of the deduction grid is clickable to cycle through three states (blank → checkmark → X → blank) in Carolyn's soft graphite-pencil style with subtle per-stroke randomization; (4) real-time logic validation: the page silently checks whether the current grid state contradicts any of the four clues and if so subtly highlights the conflicting clue with a soft warm-amber spotlight (no explicit error message — just a quiet visual cue that an attentive solver will notice); (5) each of the four clues is hoverable to reveal a soft warm-spotlight on that clue and fades in a small hand-pencilled margin annotation in Carolyn's cursive on a tucked-in slip-of-index-card tucked into the right margin of the cream card — minimum 4 distinct annotations, each its own peer-level vignette in Carolyn's voice about how she has been thinking about that specific clue across the past three Sundays (e.g., on clue 2: 'Marlene tried this clue first three weeks ago and decided it meant the pecan ring belonged to the middle Sunday — but I think the wording means something different, the word AFTER is doing real work here'); (6) the REVEAL button rendered as Carolyn's small pencil-pressed star doodle in the lower-right corner of the second card is initially visually dimmed (faded to roughly 40% opacity) and brightens to full graphite the moment all three correct main-axis assignments are committed in the grid; clicking the brightened star triggers a smooth CSS card-corner-fold animation over 900ms revealing Carolyn's working-solution on the back of the index card (written in pencil with her crossed-out wrong attempts from previous Sundays visible above the final correct solution), plus her small additional pencilled note at the bottom reading 'figured it out 11:08am — Phil came in with the paper at 11:14, I had a head start on him for once'; (7) hovering each of the three women's names along the row axis reveals a small Carolyn-annotation on a slip about that friend (Marlene, Donna, Carolyn herself) — each 2-4 sentences as peer-level vignette; (8) hovering each of the three Sundays along the column axis reveals a small annotation about what else happened that Sunday at the card table; (9) hovering each of the three baked goods reveals a small annotation about who actually makes that recipe well and where the recipe came from; (10) all visitor progress persists in localStorage forever: which cells have been committed in each state, which clue and axis annotations have been hovered, whether the REVEAL has been triggered; (11) a small persistent counter rendered as a tiny pencilled tally along the lower-left of the felt reads 'SUNDAYS SOMEONE SAT WITH THIS PUZZLE: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere solves the grid (shared persistent count across all visitors starting at 3 — Carolyn's three previous Sundays before this one); (12) absolutely no instructional UI text visible explaining how to use the grid or what the puzzle is — no tooltips, no captions, no 'CLICK THE CELLS TO SOLVE' guidance, no header, no footer credits — the visitor figures it out by reading the clues the way Carolyn has been reading them; (13) the puzzle does not regenerate, does not change its clues or its solution — it is one specific Sam-Loyd-retrospective-adjacent Edgar Halverson 'Sunday Mornings' puzzle from the September-October 1977 Games magazine, and solving it carefully is the entire page.

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Carolyn's four hover-revealed clue annotations — the one tucked alongside clue 3 about the January 29 Sunday — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about Sanka growing cold, three women bent over a card table, and a small printed grid that wouldn't give itself up until the third Sunday — disguised naturally within Carolyn's earnest peer-level note about how Marlene first misread the clue. Additionally, after a returning visitor has solved the grid on three separate calendar dates (tracked in localStorage), a hidden fifth clue quietly appears on the cream index card the next time the visitor returns, written in slightly fresher blue Bic ballpoint in Carolyn's same cursive and numbered 'CLUE 5 (added Sunday Feb 19 — for whoever sits here next)' — the clue reads: 'You probably already know which Sunday you came on. The grid will tell you which baked good you would have brought.' This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact at the bottom of the index card for visitors who kept coming back, and solving the extended grid using this fifth clue (which is non-deductive in the traditional sense but adds a fourth row to the women axis representing the visitor themselves) auto-fills one additional pencilled entry on the back of the index card identifying which baked good the visitor would have brought, based on the cell-cycling patterns the visitor used during their solving (visitors who used long careful think-pauses get the pecan ring, fast deciders get the lemon bars, mid-pace get the crumb cake — Carolyn's own taxonomy of solver-personalities baked into the page).

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when families did jigsaw puzzles on card tables' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the Sunday-morning logic-grid,' NO cool ironic distance from 1970s suburban Bloomfield Hills culture as quaint pre-internet Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Carolyn or Phil or Marlene or Donna or the Vandevoort family or 4814 Pinegrove Terrace as already-vanished. NO 'where are they now' wistfulness about Carolyn grown older or the friendships changed, NO 'this index card was found decades later' framing, NO scrapbook-archival voice. NO horror-genre framing of the empty card table, the half-completed jigsaw, the unsolved puzzle across three Sundays, or the hidden fifth clue as creepy/uncanny/sinister/liminal — this is a warm Sunday morning at home, the Sanka is fresh, Phil is at the 7-Eleven, the dining-room radiator is ticking quietly, and the puzzle is one Carolyn is actively having a good time with. NO 'something happened to one of the women later' tragic foreshadowing, NO 'the friendship faded' melancholy framing, NO 'this was the last winter they all sat together' wistfulness. NO condescension toward middle-aged women's Sunday-morning recreation as small or trivial, toward jigsaw puzzles and logic grids as childish, toward Sanka instant coffee or Pyrex mugs or mustard-yellow Hutzler bowls as kitsch, toward Springbok Editions as quaint. NO 'imagine being a forty-three-year-old hospital lab tech in 1978 with a clipped logic-puzzle' smug-modern-superiority framing, NO Boomer-housewife-pathos voice, NO Stepford-Wives suburban-malaise undertone. NO feminist-grievance reading of Carolyn's domestic arrangement as oppression — Carolyn has a real career at Beaumont, she chose the Sunday-morning ritual, the puzzle is hers, and Phil respects her recreational craft because he is married to a woman who is good at things. NO Mad-Men-pastiche period kitsch, NO Stranger-Things 70s aesthetic, NO synthwave neon, NO vaporwave. NO night-shift dispatcher voice (10:14am Sunday morning is not a midnight desk vigil), NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the unsolved puzzle or the hidden fifth clue or the encoded haiku, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the index card as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of jigsaw-and-puzzle-club women as fictional small-town society (the three friends are real, they are not a society of anything), NO radio/CB/broadcast/transmission/dispatcher/switchboard framing of the friendship network as signal-relay metaphor. NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing (Sunday is a contextual fact about when the puzzle-working happens, not the subject of the piece). NO instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page. The page IS the card table at 10:14am Sunday February 12 1978, the visitor IS sitting across from Carolyn with their own cup of coffee, and the only voice anywhere on the page is Carolyn's voice in her blue Bic ballpoint cursive on the index card and her soft graphite-pencil grid-work and her hover-revealed margin annotations. Treat the clipped logic-grid puzzle as a serious working piece of skilled deductive craft, treat Edgar Halverson as a legitimate working puzzle-constructor with real expertise, treat Carolyn Vandevoort as a legitimate working amateur logician with eight months of skilled solving practice and a real running peer-to-peer Sunday-morning recreational community with Marlene and Donna, treat the visitor's careful clue-reading and cell-cycling as real participation in a real Sunday-morning act of patient deductive attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.
