horizontal pulp-fiber striations running across + deep Skaggs-Albertsons brand burgundy (#7a2628) for the printed store-41 letterhead at the top, the printed Skaggs-Albertsons brand wordmark, the printed two-column menu headers, the running TAX and TOTAL line printed labels, the perforated tear-line dashed border along the bottom, and the small printed phone-number and address line in the upper-right + warm brick-red second-color offset ink (#a8312a) for the printed menu item names and prices set in chunky 1960s-novelty-typesetter sans-serif + deep composing-room Linotype black (#1a1410) for the small printed disclaimer line at the very bottom ('CASH OR CHARGE — PRICES INCLUDE STATE SALES TAX OF 2¢ PER DOLLAR — SKAGGS-ALBERTSONS DRUG #41 — POLK & 8TH — AMARILLO TEX. 79101 — DR-3-4188') + Miss Vandivere's careful 17-year-old blue Bic ballpoint cursive (#1c3a7e) for the hand-written table number, time, order line, special-note line, subtotal, tax computation, and total, with subtle per-stroke pressure variation suggesting the slightly-too-firm grip of a 17-year-old who has been practicing her cursive at the soda counter for eleven weeks + the tiny pink cherry-syrup splash in the upper-right (#d8628a at 0.45 opacity translucent stain) + soft thumbprint smudge tan (#9e7e58 at 0.28 opacity) along the lower-left where the damp glass rested + Miss Vandivere's small Bic-ballpoint doodle of a phosphate-fountain glass with bubbles in the lower margin (same #1c3a7e) drawn in a single loose continuous-line gesture + warm brass-fixture pendant-light cast (#f5d89a at 0.18 opacity) falling diagonally across the upper-right of the ticket suggesting the soda-counter overhead fixture at 3:42pm + faint marble-counter surface texture (#c8c4b8 at 0.20 opacity) very subtly visible at the very edges of the viewport beyond the ticket's edges suggesting the brass-rail counter's worn-marble surface + carbon-pink tear-off stub along the bottom (#e8c4c8) with its perforation line visible as tiny dot-punctures + faint two-color registration drift visible as 0.6-1.2 pixel offsets between the burgundy and brick-red plates suggesting the Globe-News job-shop October 1970 press run was registered well but not perfectly + Mr. Pankey's small graphite-pencil printing on the back of the ticket (#4a4540) when revealed — a different hand from Miss Vandivere's, the careful angled printing of a 71-year-old retired signal-foreman who has been writing in pencil on Santa Fe paperwork for forty-three years.

mandatory_element.value: A fully working interactive fountain-order-ticket experience with the following behaviors all correctly implemented in pure HTML, CSS, and minimal JavaScript: (1) the ticket renders at full viewport scale as the actual 3×5 inch cardstock object scaled enormously to fill the browser window, viewed slightly from above as the physical paper object lying on the brass-rail soda-counter beside Mr. Pankey's elbow at 3:42pm Wednesday October 13 1971; (2) the printed front of the ticket renders with the full Skaggs-Albertsons store-41 letterhead, the 47-item printed two-column menu with item codes and prices in burgundy and brick-red offset ink, the printed table-number and time blanks, the printed subtotal and tax computation lines and TOTAL line at the bottom, the perforated carbon-pink tear-off stub, and Miss Vandivere's hand-filled-in table number '7' circled, time '3:42 PM' in her cursive, the single hand-written order line 'CHERRY PHOS. — 25¢' with her small note 'extra cherry — no charge — N.V.' beneath it, the hand-computed subtotal '25¢', tax '0¢' (under the 2¢/$ threshold), and total '25¢' with her loopy underline; (3) minimum 9 hover-targets distributed across the ticket's printed and handwritten elements, each revealing Miss Vandivere's hand-printed peer-level margin annotation on a small cream-stationery slip that fades in along the nearest margin (each annotation 3-6 sentences in her specific voice, no two recycling phrasing): hovering exactly the printed menu item 'CHERRY PHOSPHATE — 25¢' for 3 full seconds reveals her annotation about the exact phosphate-recipe ratio she has worked out for Mr. Pankey across eleven weeks (the 1.5 oz Smith's cherry syrup, 4 oz phosphate water, three pumps of acid phosphate, the 8-oz tulip glass, the maraschino on top — and the small story of week three when Mr. Pankey mentioned the drink tasted sweet that day and she added the third acid-pump from then on); hovering the printed store letterhead reveals her annotation about Mr. Halsell hiring her in July and stitching her name on her apron himself because Wanda had quit; hovering the printed menu's GRILLED CHEESE ON WHITE — 35¢ line reveals her annotation about the after-school kids from Caprock who come in for grilled cheeses on Tuesdays and Thursdays and which ones she is friends with; hovering the printed menu's CHOCOLATE MALT — 45¢ line reveals her annotation about Mrs. Whickham coming in every Friday at 5pm for the malt to take home to her bedridden husband Doyle and how Mr. Halsell lets Mrs. Whickham have it for 35¢ on Mr. Whickham's birthday in December; hovering the table-number circled '7' reveals her annotation about why stool 7 at the corner is the regulars' stool ('the brass rail bends at the corner there and the elbow-rest is wider than the other six stools — Mr. Pankey took it the first week and he has had it for eleven weeks now and the other regulars know not to take it after 3:25pm'); hovering the time '3:42 PM' reveals her annotation about why Mr. Pankey always shows up at 3:30pm exactly and why it is 3:42pm today (he came in late because he stopped at the post office on Polk Street to mail his birthday card to Nadine in Tulsa and the line was long); hovering the hand-written 'CHERRY PHOS.' order line reveals her annotation about her own handwriting practice at the counter ('Carlene says my cursive is showing off but I am genuinely trying to make it neat — the application to Texas Tech has to be filled out in pen and Mrs. Whittenburg said the admissions secretary reads two hundred a week and the legible ones get read first'); hovering her small 'extra cherry — no charge — N.V.' note reveals her annotation about why she initials her ticket notes ('Mr. Halsell asked me in week two to initial the comp lines so the daily-cash reconciliation makes sense at the register — I have been doing it since — also I like signing my name, it looks like Norma Vandivere on the paper, which is the same name that is going on the Texas Tech application Monday morning'); hovering the perforated carbon-pink tear-off stub reveals her annotation about the daily-cash reconciliation routine Mr. Halsell taught her her first week ('the pink stub goes to the register tape, the white top to the customer if they want it or to the spike if they don't, the daily total has to match the till to the penny by 6pm closing — I have been off by 2¢ once in eleven weeks and Mr. Halsell laughed and said even Bertha used to be off by a nickel some days, Bertha was the soda jerk before Wanda'); (4) a working CLICK TO TURN OVER mechanism — clicking anywhere on the front of the ticket (other than the perforated stub or the hover-targets) triggers a smooth CSS card-flip animation over 900ms with a soft paper-on-cardstock rustle via Web Audio API, revealing the back of the ticket where Mr. Pankey's pencil note is hand-printed in his careful 71-year-old graphite-pencil block-printing reading 'for the application fee — you tell Mrs. Whittenburg I said you should mail it Monday morning — L.P.' with a folded $5 bill clearly drawn beside the note as a small flat-vector illustration (a 1969-series Lincoln $5 bill rendered in the appropriate green-and-cream); (5) the back of the ticket also displays a small hand-pencilled additional line beneath Mr. Pankey's note reading 'p.s. — Esther was a schoolteacher for forty-one years and she would have liked you — L.P.' rendered in slightly lighter pencil pressure suggesting Mr. Pankey added the post-script after a brief pause; (6) when the ticket is in the back-side-revealed state, hovering exactly the word 'Esther' for 2 full seconds reveals a small additional graphite-pencil margin annotation fading in along the upper margin in Mr. Pankey's printing reading 'she taught second grade at Sleepy Hollow from 1929 through 1970, retired in May, passed in April the year after — Nadine and her three kids came down from Tulsa for the funeral, Wanda the older Vandivere girl was at Sleepy Hollow in 1958 and Esther remembered her — Esther remembered all of them — L.P.'; (7) hovering the drawn $5 bill for 2 full seconds reveals Mr. Pankey's annotation reading 'I have been planning to leave this for you for two weeks — I asked Mr. Halsell on Monday what the application fee was and he said twenty-five dollars and I told him I was giving you five toward it and he said that was a kind thing and not to make a fuss about it at the counter — please mail the application — L.P.'; (8) clicking the small Bic-ballpoint phosphate-fountain-glass doodle in the lower margin of the front of the ticket reveals Miss Vandivere's annotation about why she draws phosphate glasses ('I draw them on every fifth or sixth ticket when I am waiting for the customer to finish their crossword or their newspaper — it is a small thing I do for myself to pass the time, also I have decided I am going to take an art class my freshman year at Texas Tech alongside the elementary-ed requirements — Mrs. Whittenburg said one art elective is allowed in the first semester'); (9) clicking and dragging the perforated carbon-pink tear-off stub along the bottom of the front of the ticket tears it along its perforation with a real SVG path-clip animation over 800ms and a soft paper-tearing sound via Web Audio API, after which the torn stub can be dragged anywhere in the viewport and settles where the visitor releases it; the torn coupon persists in localStorage forever; (10) a working ADD YOUR OWN NOTE mechanism — clicking any blank area of the back of the ticket (after it has been flipped) opens a small Bic-ballpoint-cursor input where the visitor types their own short 1-2 sentence note in cursive style, which renders in subtle per-stroke pressure variation as a different hand (slightly more modern American cursive in a slightly different blue) and persists in localStorage so a returning visitor finds their previous note still pencilled in alongside Mr. Pankey's; (11) absolutely no instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page explaining how to flip the ticket or hover the elements or tear the stub or add a note — no tooltips, no captions, no loading messages, no 'EXPLORE THE FOUNTAIN ORDER TICKET' header banner, no footer credits — the visitor figures it out by reading the ticket the way Miss Vandivere reads it when she finds it folded in her Texas Tech application packet in March 1972; (12) all visitor state persists in localStorage forever — which hover-targets have been revealed, whether the ticket has been flipped, whether the stub has been torn off and where it has been placed, the visitor's own note — so a returning visitor finds the ticket exactly as they left it; (13) the ticket does not regenerate, does not change its order or its time stamp — it is one specific fountain order ticket #41-7-3142 that Miss Norma Vandivere wrote out at 3:42pm Wednesday October 13 1971 for Mr. Lemuel Pankey at the corner stool of the Skaggs-Albertsons Drugstore #41 brass-rail soda counter at Polk and 8th in Amarillo Texas, and reading it carefully is the entire page; (14) a small shared persistent cross-visitor counter rendered in tiny cream type along the lower-right corner of the back of the ticket reads 'TICKETS KEPT BY SOMEONE: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere flips the ticket and finds Mr. Pankey's note for the first time (shared persistent count starting at 1 — Miss Vandivere's first keeping at 4:18pm Wednesday October 13 1971, four minutes after Mr. Pankey walked out).

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Miss Vandivere's nine hover-revealed annotations — the one tucked alongside the printed CHERRY PHOSPHATE — 25¢ menu item — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about three pumps of acid phosphate, a 71-year-old widower with a Globe-News crossword, and a soda jerk who learned to make a drink the way one specific stranger liked it — disguised naturally within Miss Vandivere's earnest peer-level note about the eleven-week phosphate-recipe iteration. Additionally, after a returning visitor has flipped the ticket on three separate calendar dates AND added their own note to the back at least twice across visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden tenth element quietly appears at the very bottom of the back of the ticket the next time the visitor returns — a small folded paper-strip note in Miss Vandivere's same Bic ballpoint cursive but slightly more confident, dated in the corner 'Lubbock — September 8 1972 — first week of freshman year,' reading: 'Mr. Pankey — I mailed the application Monday morning the way you said. The acceptance letter came December 15 and the financial-aid letter came January 8 and the dormitory assignment came June 4. I am writing this from my desk in Wells Hall room 217 on the Texas Tech campus in Lubbock and my roommate Linda is asleep across the room and I am supposed to be reading the chapter for my Intro to Education class tomorrow but I have been thinking about the soda counter all evening. I drew a phosphate glass in the margin of my notes today. I hope you are well. The five dollars went into the application envelope and the rest of the fee I had saved from the tip-can. I have decided I am going to write to you every month from school. The first letter is coming Friday. — Norma Vandivere, age 18, freshman, elementary education major.' Clicking this folded note unfolds it over 1.4 seconds with a soft paper-unfolding animation to reveal Miss Vandivere's full letter rendered in her own slightly-more-confident 18-year-old ballpoint cursive (different hand from her 17-year-old soda-counter cursive — slightly tighter letterforms, more even baseline, the careful underline beneath 'Texas Tech'). This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact tucked into the back of the ticket for visitors who kept coming back.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when drugstores had soda fountains' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the cherry phosphate,' NO cool ironic distance from 1971 small-town Texas drugstore culture as quaint pre-Starbucks Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Miss Vandivere or Mr. Pankey or Skaggs-Albertsons Drugstore #41 or Caprock High School or the brass-rail soda counter as already-vanished. NO 'where is she now' wistfulness about whether Norma finished at Texas Tech, NO 'where is he now' wistfulness about Mr. Pankey's remaining years, NO 'this ticket was found decades later in a shoebox' framing, NO scrapbook-archival voice, NO Studs Terkel oral-history register. NO horror-genre framing of the empty soda counter, the corner stool 7, the folded $5 bill, the p.s. about Esther, or the silent afternoon between 3:42pm and 4:14pm as creepy/uncanny/sinister/lonely-mid-century-pathos — this is a warm working Wednesday afternoon at a real working small-town drugstore soda fountain, Miss Vandivere is having a good shift, Mr. Pankey is doing the Globe-News crossword the way he has done for 122 weeks, and the small gesture of the folded $5 toward the Texas Tech application fee is one working adult helping one working teenager because he can. NO 'Mr. Pankey is dying soon' tragic foreshadowing, NO 'this is the last gesture' sentimental framing, NO 'he never got to see her graduate' Hallmark-card pathos. NO 'Esther's ghost is at the counter' supernatural framing, NO suggestion that Mr. Pankey's gesture is anything other than what it is — a 71-year-old widower who has decided across two weeks of consideration that helping a 17-year-old senior pay her college application fee is the right small thing to do. NO Stand-By-Me melancholy-childhood-tableau register, NO Wonder-Years narrator-looking-back voice, NO 'this was the autumn that changed everything' framing. NO condescension toward soda-jerk work as unskilled labor, toward 1971 small-town drugstore culture as quaint Americana, toward 17-year-old senior college-application anxiety as small-life pathos, toward Mr. Pankey's 43-year Santa Fe Railroad career as small-town-pension trivia. NO smug modern superiority about pre-Starbucks soda fountains ('imagine getting a cherry phosphate for 25 cents'), NO Boomer-Americana sentimentality, NO 'they don't make drugstores like this anymore' decline narrative, NO Norman-Rockwell Saturday-Evening-Post cover pastiche, NO Mad-Men period kitsch. NO 'small-town Texas Panhandle' regional caricature, NO accented-English pastiche, NO 'aw shucks' fake-Texan dialogue, NO Larry-McMurtry The-Last-Picture-Show melancholy register. NO Vietnam-War-era political subtext, NO 'in the autumn of 1971 America was losing its innocence' historical-essay framing, NO Watergate-prefiguring undertone. NO feminist-grievance reading of Miss Vandivere's father wanting her to go to AJC for the secretarial program — Hubert is a working meat-cutter who has his own reasons for what he thinks Norma should do, and the page does not litigate the family conversation. NO 'Miss Vandivere is the first in her family to go to college' triumphal framing — it is true that she will be, but the page does not announce it as a sociological achievement, it is just what is happening in her life. NO Sleepy-Hollow-Elementary-as-cute-tiny-school framing of Esther's career, NO 41-year-schoolteacher-as-saint pathos. NO 'never-married' or 'spinster' caricature of anyone, NO 'imagine being alone after your wife dies' framing of Mr. Pankey's widowerhood — he has a life, he has Nadine in Tulsa, he has the Globe-News crossword and the eleven-block walk and the cherry phosphate, and he has Miss Vandivere across the counter four afternoons a week, and that is the whole content of his afternoon and it does not require eulogizing. NO night-shift dispatcher voice (3:42pm Wednesday at a soda counter is not a midnight desk vigil), NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the folded $5 or the encoded haiku or the hidden 1972 letter, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the ticket as relic (it is a working commercial document being written right now), NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of soda-fountain regulars as fictional small-town society. ABSOLUTELY NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher/switchboard/operator-shift/transmission/signal/frequency-tuner framing of Mr. Pankey's 43-year Santa Fe Railroad signal-maintenance career as a signal-relay metaphor — he was a real signal-maintenance foreman doing real working trackside electrical-and-semaphore-light maintenance from Clovis to Belen for 31 years and the page renders his career as exactly that, NOT as a signal/transmission/relay metaphor for anything — and signal/dispatcher framing is hard-banned this hour regardless of bucket. The Santa Fe Railroad is the Santa Fe Railroad, the signal line is the signal line, neither is a metaphor. NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing (Wednesday is a contextual fact about which afternoon shift Miss Vandivere is working and when Mr. Pankey shows up, not the subject of the piece). NO 'fictional small-town society/club' framing. NO instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'EXAMINE THE FOUNTAIN ORDER TICKET' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS the 3×5 cardstock fountain order ticket on the brass-rail soda counter at 3:42pm Wednesday October 13 1971, the visitor IS a stranger at the counter two stools down (or is Miss Vandivere herself in March 1972 finding the ticket folded in her Texas Tech application packet, or is Mr. Pankey at the corner stool finishing his crossword and deciding it is time to slip the $5 into the ticket and walk out, or is Mr. Halsell coming back with the bank deposit and seeing the small exchange), and the only voices anywhere on the page are Miss Vandivere's matter-of-fact peer-level voice in her Bic ballpoint hand-written annotations, Mr. Pankey's careful graphite-pencil printing on the back of the ticket, the printed Skaggs-Albertsons commercial typography of the ticket itself, and (in the wildcard unlock) Miss Vandivere's more-confident 18-year-old cursive in the September 1972 letter from Wells Hall room 217. Treat the fountain order ticket as a serious working commercial document, treat Miss Norma Vandivere as a legitimate working 17-year-old soda jerk with eleven weeks of skilled service-counter expertise and a real running professional practice, treat Mr. Lemuel Pankey as a legitimate retired Santa Fe Railroad signal-maintenance foreman with 43 years of skilled trackside-electrical craft expertise and a real continuing daily soda-counter routine, treat the eleven-week working relationship between a 17-year-old senior and a 71-year-old widower across 44 ticket-and-phosphate transactions as a real form of peer-to-peer cross-generational counter-service attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect, and treat the visitor's careful flipping and hovering and stub-tearing of the ticket as real participation in a real act of one-stranger-to-another small-counter attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: something to explore, multiple sections, interactive elements

era.value: 1958 — peak American summer-night drive-in movie-theater intermission era, the specific 11-minute concession-stand intermission gap between the first and second features at the Starlite Drive-In on the eastern outskirts of Bristol Tennessee on a humid Saturday evening in late July when the projection booth's carbon-arc lamp was being trimmed by chief projectionist Mr. Earl Pickering (age 47, came over from the Paramount downtown when the downtown house went stereo in 1953, has been running the Starlite's two RCA Brenkert BX-80 projectors since opening night August 14 1949) and the field of 247 parked cars on the gently-sloped gravel lot facing the 64-foot-wide painted-plywood screen flickered briefly between the orange amber glow of dashboard radios and the steady blue-violet wash of the screen going dark between reels, while above the lot and the screen and the small clapboard concession-stand-and-restrooms structure the sky held a specific late-twilight gradient that hovered for approximately eleven minutes between the deepening violet of full astronomical twilight and the slow appearance of the first stars rising over the Holston Mountain ridgeline to the east, with the specific small atmospheric quality of the humid Appalachian Saturday night when the asphalt of the day still radiated heat upward and the cooling mountain air sank down through the hollows and the two air masses met in a soft heat-shimmer haze that distorted the rising stars into trembling specular points — Venus already visible at 8:42pm Eastern Daylight Time at 14 degrees altitude in the western sky over the screen's back side, the waxing-gibbous moon at 23 degrees in the east-southeast, the first three stars of Cygnus just appearing in the zenith — and the entire 11-minute intermission unfolding under that specific sky with the soft mechanical click of the carbon-arc trimming overhead, the faint distant radio-static of two hundred and forty-seven dashboard speakers each tuned to the Starlite's 540 AM low-power station now playing the intermission organ-music reel that Mr. Pickering's wife Doris Pickering recorded at the Starlite's Wurlitzer console organ in March 1956 (specifically her favorite arrangement of 'Side By Side' played slowly), the soft hush of car-doors opening as families walked the gravel path to the concession stand for buttered popcorn and orange Nehi and root-beer floats, the small fireflies rising from the field grass at the edges of the lot in their species-specific Photinus pyralis flash pattern, and the slow continuous appearance of stars one-by-one above the screen as the sky completed its deepening through nautical twilight into the full astronomical dark that the second feature ('The Defiant Ones,' premiering September 24 1958, so this is a different feature — let's say it's actually 'Vertigo' which opened May 9 1958 at Bristol's downtown house and arrived at the Starlite three weeks later) would play its first reel against.

format.value: A single full-viewport silent generative piece rendered entirely in pure CSS and SVG with no UI text, no controls, no chrome, no captions, no buttons, no instructions anywhere: the entire browser window IS the view from a specific blanket spread on the gravel beside a 1954 powder-blue Chevy Bel Air parked in row 14 space 8 at the Starlite Drive-In during the 11-minute intermission between features at approximately 9:48pm Eastern Daylight Time on Saturday July 26, 1958, looking up and slightly westward at the deepening twilight sky above the 64-foot painted-plywood screen which fills the lower 22% of the viewport as a near-black rectangle silhouette against the still-luminous sky, with the small clapboard concession stand visible as a warm-glowing yellow rectangle at the lower-left edge of the viewport (its single bare bulb above the screen door casting a soft 8-foot cone of warm light onto the gravel), the faint silhouettes of approximately 47 other parked cars visible across the lower viewport as soft black shapes barely-distinguishable against the deepening dusk, the screen's back-side (the wooden support truss) lit only by the warm spill of the concession-stand bulb, and above all of this the slow continuous appearance of stars one-by-one in deterministic astronomical positions for that specific date and time and latitude (36.5951°N, 82.1887°W — Bristol Tennessee) — the sky a slow-evolving gradient through the 11-minute cycle from the late-twilight rose-violet at minute 0 to the full-dark cobalt-indigo with Milky Way faintly visible at minute 11, then looping back. Stars appear in true positions: Venus already burning bright at upper-left over the screen, the waxing gibbous moon at right-center casting a soft cool moonlight across the lot, the Summer Triangle (Vega, Deneb, Altair) appearing in sequence at their correct azimuths as the sky darkens, the first three stars of Cygnus emerging at zenith, the Big Dipper just visible low in the northwest. Approximately 180 individual fireflies (Photinus pyralis) rise from the field-grass at the edges of the lot in their species-correct J-curve flash pattern (a half-second pause at the bottom of the rise, then a bright 0.4-second flash during the upward curve, then darkness for 5.5 seconds before the next rise), each firefly with its own randomized rise position and timing so the cumulative effect is a slow continuous shimmer of small warm-yellow flashes along the lot's perimeter. Inside the parked cars across the field, approximately 12 dashboard radios glow as small soft amber rectangles barely-perceptible at 0.18 opacity, with a soft synchronized intermission-organ-music shimmer (one dashboard radio briefly brightens by 0.06 opacity in a slow 1.4-second pulse synchronized across all 12 cars, suggesting the organ recording's swell phrases all playing simultaneously through 247 low-power-AM speakers). The concession-stand bulb at the lower-left flickers very slightly (0.05 opacity variation on a 0.18-second period) suggesting the mid-century filament's small AC ripple. Above the concession-stand a small mosquito-attracting bug zapper visible as a faint cool-violet rectangle with occasional small pop-flashes of warm-white. Cursor position acts as the angle of the viewer's tilted head — moving the cursor up shifts the entire star-field downward by small fractional amounts (parallax of ±6 pixels at zenith, ±14 pixels at horizon) suggesting the visitor very slightly tilting their head back against the open trunk of the Bel Air to look higher into the sky. The piece runs forever, the stars continue appearing in their correct astronomical order, the fireflies continue rising and flashing, the dashboard radios continue their synchronized organ-music pulse, the bug zapper continues its small pop-flashes, the concession-stand bulb continues its filament flicker, and the moon slowly climbs by approximately 4 pixels per minute across the cycle suggesting the actual lunar motion across 11 minutes of an Appalachian summer night.

subject.value: The single specific 11-minute intermission gap between the first and second features at the Starlite Drive-In Theater on Volunteer Parkway just east of Bristol Tennessee on the humid Saturday evening of July 26, 1958, from approximately 9:48pm to 9:59pm Eastern Daylight Time, as observed from a wool plaid picnic blanket spread on the gravel beside the 1954 powder-blue Chevy Bel Air parked in row 14 space 8 belonging to Mr. and Mrs. Russell and Joyce Whitsett of 814 Anderson Street in Bristol Tennessee (Russell age 32, second-shift quality-control inspector at the Beaunit Mills rayon plant in Elizabethton where he has worked since coming home from Korea in October 1953, Joyce age 29, homemaker and mother of three — Russell Jr. age 6, Patty age 4, and Sandra age 18 months who is asleep in the back seat of the Bel Air with a folded receiving blanket under her head), and the entire small infrastructure of one specific drive-in-theater Saturday-night ritual happening across that 11-minute gap: the carbon-arc lamps in the projection booth being trimmed by chief projectionist Mr. Earl Pickering between reels, the concession-stand at the back of the lot doing its peak business of the night with families walking the gravel path for buttered popcorn and orange Nehi and Mrs. Pickering's homemade root-beer floats served in waxed-paper cups for 15 cents, the 247 dashboard radios across the parked-car field all softly tuned to the Starlite's 540 AM low-power station now playing Doris Pickering's intermission-organ recording, the slow continuous deepening of the late-twilight sky from astronomical twilight into the full dark when the second reel will start, the rising of the first stars above the Holston Mountain ridgeline to the east, the appearance of Venus already burning at 14 degrees altitude in the western sky over the back of the screen, the waxing gibbous moon climbing into the east-southeast, and the patient summer-evening hum of Appalachian fireflies (Photinus pyralis) rising from the field-grass at the lot's perimeter in their species-specific courtship flash pattern; the specific small private fact that Russell Whitsett has spread the wool blanket on the gravel beside the Bel Air because Joyce said the Bel Air's back seat was getting hot with the windows up and Sandra needed cooler air to keep sleeping, and that Russell is lying on his back on the blanket with his head propped against the Bel Air's spare-tire well looking straight up at the deepening sky and counting how many stars he can identify before the second reel starts — Russell learned the constellations from his uncle Vernon Whitsett who was a Navy navigator in the Pacific from 1942 to 1946 and who taught Russell the Summer Triangle and the Big Dipper and Cassiopeia on visits to the Whitsett family farm in Sullivan County across the summers of 1946 to 1949 — and Russell has decided this intermission that he is going to teach Russell Jr. the Summer Triangle starting tomorrow night on the back porch.

tone.value: No tone — there is no narrator, no voice, no text visible on the page at any point. The piece speaks only through the slow continuous deepening of the late-twilight sky, the patient one-by-one appearance of stars in their true astronomical positions, the soft rise and J-curve flash of Appalachian summer fireflies along the field-grass perimeter, the synchronized soft glow-pulse of 12 dashboard radios across the lot playing Mrs. Pickering's organ recording, the small flicker of the concession-stand bulb, the occasional warm-white pop of the bug zapper, and the slow climb of the waxing gibbous moon by approximately 4 pixels per minute. The closest analogue to a voice is the silent careful attentiveness of Russell Whitsett himself on the wool blanket beside the Bel Air, head propped against the spare-tire well, watching the sky deepen and counting stars as they appear — present-tense, unhurried, neither performing nor explaining, the visual equivalent of lying on a picnic blanket on the gravel at a Tennessee drive-in on a humid Saturday night in July 1958 beside a 32-year-old quality-control inspector from the Beaunit Mills rayon plant who learned the constellations from his Navy-navigator uncle in 1947 and who is going to teach his six-year-old son the Summer Triangle tomorrow night on the back porch.

palette.value: Deep late-twilight rose-violet (#3a2a4e) along the upper viewport at cycle-start, smoothly interpolating across the 11-minute cycle through deepening cobalt (#1a1e3e) into the full astronomical dark indigo (#0a0e22) at minute 11 with the faint suggestion of the Milky Way visible as a soft pearl-amber band (#5a4e3a at 0.14 opacity) running diagonally from upper-left to lower-right + the screen silhouette in dead near-black (#040608) filling the lower 22% of the viewport with subtle painted-plywood horizontal seam visible as a thin warm-shadow line (#2a1e14 at 0.4 opacity) every 8 feet of scaled height + warm concession-stand bulb glow (#f5d894) at the lower-left as a soft 8-foot light cone (radial gradient from #f5d894 at 0.7 opacity at the bulb to #f5d894 at 0.02 opacity at the cone edge) with the small clapboard structure barely visible inside the cone as warm-cream weathered paint (#c8b478 at 0.5 opacity) and the screen door's mesh suggested as a faint cross-hatch (#7a5e3a at 0.3 opacity) + soft cool moonlight wash (#a8b4c8 at 0.18 opacity) falling across the gravel lot from the upper-right where the waxing gibbous moon (#f0ecdc at full bright with a soft #f5e8c4 halo at 0.4 opacity) sits at 23 degrees altitude + the deep silhouette field of 47 parked cars across the lower viewport in deep black-violet (#0e1018 at 0.85 opacity) with subtle warm spill on the windshield-glass surfaces (#c8b478 at 0.08 opacity from the concession bulb, #a8b4c8 at 0.12 opacity from the moon) + 12 dashboard radios glowing as small soft amber rectangles (#e8a838 at 0.18 opacity baseline, pulsing to 0.24 opacity on the synchronized 1.4-second organ-swell pulse) + 180 firefly Photinus pyralis flashes rendered as tiny soft-edged warm-yellow circles (#fae838 at 0.85 opacity at peak flash, fading to 0.0 across 0.4 seconds, J-curve rise pattern) at the field-grass perimeter along the lot edges + Venus at 14 degrees altitude as a bright steady warm-white point (#fef8e4 at 0.95 opacity with subtle 6-pointed diffraction-spike halo at 0.3 opacity) in the upper-left + the Summer Triangle stars appearing in sequence — Vega first (cool blue-white #c8d4f4 at 0.85 opacity), then Deneb (warm white-amber #f5ecd4 at 0.78 opacity), then Altair (slightly cooler #d8e4f8 at 0.82 opacity) — each with subtle specular twinkle achieved via 4-second sinusoidal opacity modulation of ±0.08 + approximately 47 additional fainter stars (#d8d4c8 at 0.4-0.7 opacity each, with subtle twinkle) appearing across the deepening sky in deterministic positions matching the actual Bristol TN latitude on July 26 1958 at 9:48-9:59pm EDT + the bug zapper at the concession-stand corner as a faint cool-violet rectangle (#7a6ec4 at 0.3 opacity) with occasional small pop-flashes of warm-white (#fef8e4 at 0.9 opacity for 80ms randomized every 8-22 seconds) + the heat-shimmer haze along the eastern horizon implemented as a subtle vertical-displacement warping of the lowest 8% of the star-field (sinusoidal x-offset of ±1.4 pixels on randomized 2-4 second periods) suggesting the cooling mountain air meeting the day's residual asphalt heat + Russell's wool plaid picnic blanket suggested at the very bottom edge of the viewport in deep cream and dark forest-green crossed plaid (#c8b478 and #2a3e2c at 0.65 opacity) with the spare-tire well of the Bel Air's open trunk barely visible at the lower-right (#7a8a98 polished-chrome trim at 0.45 opacity) — the entire scene transitions deterministically across the 11-minute cycle so the sky continuously deepens, the stars appear in their correct order at their correct elevations, the moon climbs by 4 pixels per minute, and the fireflies and dashboard radios and bug zapper continue their independent cycles autonomously.

mandatory_element.value: A genuine astronomically-accurate star-field rendered in pure CSS and SVG that deterministically reproduces the actual stellar positions visible from 36.5951°N, 82.1887°W (the Starlite Drive-In coordinates on Volunteer Parkway east of Bristol Tennessee) at 9:48pm-9:59pm Eastern Daylight Time on Saturday July 26, 1958, including Venus at its correct 14-degree altitude in the western sky over the back of the screen at the cycle's start (computed from the real ephemeris for that date), the waxing gibbous moon at 23 degrees altitude in the east-southeast climbing by approximately 4 pixels per minute across the cycle, the Summer Triangle (Vega, Deneb, Altair) appearing in correct sequence at their correct azimuths and altitudes as the sky darkens through nautical twilight into full astronomical dark, the first three stars of Cygnus emerging at zenith in correct configuration, the Big Dipper visible low in the northwest, Cassiopeia rising in the northeast, plus approximately 47 additional fainter named stars (Arcturus, Antares, Spica, Alphecca, Rasalhague, Eltanin, Etamin, Kochab, Polaris, Capella, etc.) appearing in correct deterministic positions; the firefly flash pattern strictly matches the documented Photinus pyralis species-specific J-curve rise (0.5-second hover at base, 0.4-second flash during 1.2-second upward arc, then 5.5-second dark interval before next rise) with the cumulative population of 180 flashing fireflies producing the species-accurate cumulative shimmer documented in entomological field-guides for Appalachian summer meadows at 78-degree humidity and 76-degree air temperature; the dashboard-radio organ-music pulse synchronization across 12 visible cars represents the actual physics of 247 low-power AM receivers all tuned to the same 540 AM Starlite station receiving Mrs. Pickering's organ recording, with the synchronized 1.4-second amber-glow pulse representing the louder organ-swell phrases of 'Side By Side' as the recording loops across the intermission; cursor position implements a viewer-head-tilt parallax of ±6 pixels at zenith and ±14 pixels at horizon suggesting Russell Whitsett's small adjustments of his head against the spare-tire well; the piece runs autonomously and forever with the same 11-minute deterministic cycle, the same correct star positions, the same correct moon-elevation climb, the same correct firefly flash patterns; absolutely no UI text, no instructions, no tooltips, no captions, no header, no title, no footer, no controls, no buttons, no loading messages, no credits, no time-stamp, no date — the piece is the silent 11-minute intermission sky over a specific 1958 Tennessee drive-in and watching it carefully is the entire page.

wildcard.value: Exactly once per 11-minute cycle, at exactly t=0.642 (the 7-minute mark, approximately 9:55pm in the historical time), one specific firefly along the eastern edge of the lot produces a markedly slower and more saturated flash than the others — a 1.8-second slow warm-amber bloom at full 0.95 opacity instead of the standard 0.4-second flash at 0.85 — and the firefly's J-curve rise extends to 2.6 seconds with a brief 0.4-second hover at peak before descent. This is the specific firefly that 6-year-old Russell Jr. Whitsett, who is sitting up on the wool blanket beside his father at exactly that moment having woken from his post-popcorn doze, points to and says quietly 'Daddy look at that one' — and Russell follows his son's small index finger to the slow-blooming firefly and says quietly back 'that's a male calling for a mate, son, the females are in the grass and they answer back with their own light' — and Russell Jr. watches the firefly rise and bloom and descend and is quiet for the rest of the intermission thinking about it, and that small private exchange between a 32-year-old quality-control inspector and his 6-year-old son about the courtship behavior of Photinus pyralis becomes one of the small specific things Russell Jr. will remember from his childhood when he is 74 years old in 2026. The visitor is given no indication this is happening, no acknowledgment in any UI, no reward for noticing — just the quiet fact that on this particular frame of this particular cycle, one firefly along the eastern edge of the lot held its light a little longer because a small boy on a picnic blanket at a drive-in in 1958 pointed at it and his father told him what it was.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO text, captions, labels-explaining-the-piece, title overlays, instructional UI, tooltip hints, loading messages, footer credits, watermarks, or any narrating voice anywhere on the page visible to the visitor — the piece must be entirely silent and entirely visual. NO horror-genre framing of the empty drive-in field, the silhouetted cars, the dark screen between reels, the silent intermission, or the slow appearance of stars as creepy/uncanny/sinister/dread-inducing — this is a warm humid Saturday evening at a working family drive-in theater on Volunteer Parkway, 247 families are at the concession stand or in their cars eating popcorn, Mr. Pickering is trimming the carbon-arc lamp, Mrs. Pickering's organ recording is playing softly through 247 dashboard speakers, and the sky is doing what summer skies do at the close of nautical twilight in Appalachia. NO 'the drive-in was a portal to somewhere else' twist, NO Twilight-Zone framing, NO 'something is wrong with the second reel' undertone, NO 'the cars are empty' creepypasta. NO Stranger-Things 1950s pastiche aesthetic, NO Happy-Days saccharine sock-hop sentimentality, NO Grease pastiche, NO American-Graffiti-as-pre-Vietnam-innocence retrospective framing, NO 'this was the last summer before everything changed' wistfulness. NO museum-archival 'remember when America had drive-ins' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the drive-in intermission,' NO cool ironic distance from 1950s southern small-city Saturday-night culture as quaint pre-mall Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing the Starlite or the Whitsetts or Mr. and Mrs. Pickering or Bristol Tennessee as already-vanished (the Starlite will close in 1987 and be paved over for a Walmart in 1994 but this Saturday night is July 26 1958 and the lot is full and the page is not retroactively informed by it). NO 'where are they now' wistfulness about Russell Jr. grown up or the Whitsetts retired, NO 'this drive-in is gone now' decline narrative, NO 'they don't make summer nights like this anymore' Boomer-sentimentality. NO Cold-War overlay on the 1958 sky, NO Sputnik-anxiety framing of the stars (Sputnik 1 launched October 1957 and the Whitsetts know about it from the radio but the page does not reference it), NO 'the country was on the brink of...' historical-essay framing. NO racial-politics overlay on Bristol Tennessee in 1958 (the Starlite was integrated under court order in 1962 — but this is 1958, the drive-in's seating practices are what they are, and the page does not litigate that history through Russell and Joyce Whitsett's specific Saturday night with their three children). NO Korean-War overlay on Russell's service (he came home in October 1953, he has a quality-control job he likes, and his Saturday night is not about the war). NO condescension toward drive-in movie culture as kitsch Americana, toward 1958 rayon-plant quality-control inspection as small-time labor, toward small-Appalachian-city family-Saturday-night-recreation as wistful small-life, toward dashboard-radio low-power-AM broadcasting as primitive. NO smug modern superiority about pre-streaming entertainment. NO Norman-Rockwell sentimentality, NO Saturday-Evening-Post-cover Americana, NO Pinterest-vintage-Americana mood-boarding, NO Wes-Anderson-symmetrical-frame styling. NO accented-English pastiche of southern speech, NO 'aw shucks' Appalachian dialogue, NO Larry-McMurtry-as-Last-Picture-Show-melancholy register. NO Bradbury Dandelion-Wine summer-of-childhood wistfulness register, NO Stand-By-Me melancholy-childhood-tableau register, NO 'this was the autumn that changed everything' Wonder-Years framing. NO night-shift dispatcher voice (9:48pm in a drive-in field on a Saturday is not a midnight desk vigil), NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the wildcard firefly or the silent intermission or the small father-son exchange, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the drive-in or the night sky as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding (the Whitsetts are not a society of anything, they are a family at the drive-in), NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher/switchboard/operator-shift/transmission/signal/frequency-tuner framing of the 540 AM low-power station or the 247 dashboard radios or Mrs. Pickering's organ recording or Mr. Pickering's projection-booth carbon-arc trimming as signal-network metaphor — the radio station is just the radio station, the projection booth is just the projection booth, neither is a signal-relay metaphor for anything, and radio/dispatcher/transmission framing is hard-banned this hour regardless of bucket. NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing (Saturday is a contextual fact about which evening shift the drive-in is running, not the subject of the piece). NO 'fictional small-town society/club' framing. NO weather-app or photo-viewer interface conventions, NO settings panels, NO playback controls, NO speed sliders, NO 'pause the sky' affordance, NO 'identify the constellations' overlay tools. NO Mary-Oliver pause-and-notice-the-world quote-register, NO Wendell-Berry agrarian-philosophical handwringing, NO Annie-Dillard pilgrim-at-tinker-creek epiphany voice. The sky deepens, the fireflies rise, the moon climbs, the dashboard radios glow, the concession-stand bulb flickers, Russell Whitsett lies on the wool blanket counting stars, Russell Jr. wakes up and points at a firefly, and the visitor watches in silence — that is the entire piece. Treat the 11-minute intermission as a serious working piece of mid-century Saturday-night family-recreation infrastructure, treat the Starlite Drive-In as a legitimate working southern-Appalachian small-city entertainment institution, treat Mr. Pickering's carbon-arc-trimming and Mrs. Pickering's organ-recording as real skilled labor worth full peer-to-peer respect, treat Russell Whitsett's quiet star-counting and his small Saturday-night decision to teach his six-year-old son the Summer Triangle tomorrow night as a real working act of cross-generational attention worth full peer-to-peer respect, and treat the visitor's quiet act of watching one specific 11-minute summer-evening sky over a specific 1958 Tennessee drive-in as real participation in a real act of patient sky-attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

era.value: 1987 — peak American suburban basement-rec-room family-board-game-night era, the very specific Friday evening when middle-class families across the upper Midwest gathered around the folding card table in the wood-paneled basement rec room beside the orange shag carpet and the brown plaid sleeper sofa to play their accumulated game-closet inventory of Milton Bradley and Parker Brothers and Selchow & Righter titles, and the very specific small printed paper rules-insert and score-pad accumulation that built up inside each game box across years of Friday-night play, and one specific Friday evening of February 6, 1987 at the home of Mr. and Mrs. Howard and Doreen Strzempka of 4814 Hickory Lane in suburban Wauwatosa Wisconsin (Howard age 41, second-shift quality-engineer at the Briggs & Stratton plant in Milwaukee, Doreen age 39, part-time bookkeeper at Wauwatosa Savings Bank, parents of three: Kendra 13, Brad 11, and Jenny 8) when the family pulled out the 1976 Selchow & Righter Scrabble Sentinel Edition and played a four-handed game from 7:14pm to 9:48pm during which 11-year-old Brad — a fifth-grade member of the Underwood Elementary Math Olympiad team who had been working through his older sister's eighth-grade pre-algebra textbook in secret since November because Mrs. Helquist his teacher said he could borrow it — designed across the third turn of the second rack a small logic puzzle on the back of the score-pad sheet using their three remaining tile-letters as the seed, the puzzle a careful 3-statement deductive grid that he handed to his older sister Kendra during her bathroom break with the small note 'figure this out before dad's next turn — B.' written across the top in his careful 11-year-old Bic ballpoint printing.

format.value: A genuine fully-solvable in-browser 3-statement classic knights-and-knaves logic puzzle disguised as the actual hand-pencilled score-pad sheet Brad Strzempka folded and slid across the card table to his older sister Kendra at 8:22pm Friday February 6 1987 during her bathroom break in the middle of their family Scrabble game — the entire viewport is the orange shag carpet beneath the folding card table viewed from slightly above with the cream-and-pale-blue ruled Selchow & Righter score-pad sheet (printed at the company's Bay Shore Long Island stock-printer in 1976 in runs of 80,000 pads of 50 sheets each, the back of every sheet blank cream cardstock 5.5×8.5 inches with a tiny SELCHOW & RIGHTER — SCRABBLE BRAND CROSSWORD GAME imprint along the lower edge) centered in the middle of the viewport with Brad's hand-pencilled puzzle written on the blank back side in his careful 11-year-old Bic ballpoint cursive plus a small empty deduction worksheet he has pre-ruled in pencil to the right of the puzzle text waiting for Kendra to fill in. The puzzle is a real solvable knights-and-knaves logic puzzle with exactly one valid solution: three classmates from Brad's fifth-grade class at Underwood Elementary — Eric Pitlik, Stacy Voorhees, and Mr. Tibbs the class hamster — are each either a TRUTH-TELLER (always says true things) or a FIBBER (always says false things), and three cross-referencing statements each speaker has made (written in Brad's careful printing on the score-pad sheet, each statement Brad observed during the previous Tuesday's recess-period classroom-visit and recorded in his composition-book that night) lock in exactly one valid assignment per speaker by deductive elimination. The visitor solves by clicking each speaker's name to cycle through three states (blank → TRUTH in green pencil-checkmark → FIBBER in red pencil-X → blank) in Brad's same Bic-ballpoint pencil style with subtle per-stroke randomization, with each placement rendering in graphite-and-color style. The three statements are genuinely deductive — no guessing required, all three assignments derivable from the statements by patient case-analysis. A FOLD BACK THE CORNER reveal rendered as a small pencil-pressed corner-dog-ear in the lower-right of the score-pad sheet is initially dimmed and brightens only after all three speaker-states are correctly committed, at which point clicking it folds back the corner of the sheet with a real paper-fold animation to reveal Brad's own pencilled working-solution on the back of the sheet (with his crossed-out wrong attempts from earlier in the bathroom-break visible above the final correct solution, plus his small additional pencilled note in the lower margin reading 'figured it out 8:31pm during dad's W-A-X turn for 28 points — solved before Kendra got back from the bathroom — kendra figured it out too at 8:38pm after I showed her the trick — B.S., 5th grade, Underwood Elem., age 11').

subject.value: The single specific Friday-night family Scrabble game of February 6, 1987 between 7:14pm and 9:48pm at the Strzempka family folding card table in the wood-paneled basement rec room at 4814 Hickory Lane in Wauwatosa Wisconsin — the very specific working-class Midwestern family-game-night infrastructure of a 1976 Selchow & Righter Scrabble Sentinel Edition (the family's third Scrabble set, the previous two having had pieces lost across years of play, this one purchased at the Wauwatosa Farm & Fleet on December 24 1984 by Howard as a Christmas gift Doreen specifically requested), the small printed score-pad sheets Doreen has been using since 1984 (one sheet per game, kept in a manila folder labeled 'SCRABBLE — 1985 forward' on the basement bookshelf above the encyclopedias, currently 87 sheets accumulated from 87 family games across 26 months); and the very specific moment at 8:22pm in the middle of the second rack when 11-year-old Brad — the middle child, the fifth-grade Math Olympiad team member, the kid who has been quietly working through Kendra's eighth-grade pre-algebra book on Mrs. Helquist's permission since November, the kid who keeps a composition-book of small original logic puzzles he designs from scratch and tries out on his older sister because she is smart enough to make solving them satisfying — pulled a blank Selchow & Righter score-pad sheet from the back of the manila folder, flipped it over to the blank cream cardstock back, and wrote out in careful Bic ballpoint cursive a 3-statement knights-and-knaves puzzle he designed in his head while Howard was thinking about his S-T-A-R-E rack (the puzzle built around three classmates Brad observed during Tuesday's classroom-visit period: Eric Pitlik who had been telling everyone Mr. Tibbs the hamster was sick, Stacy Voorhees who had been telling everyone Eric Pitlik was lying about Mr. Tibbs, and Mr. Tibbs himself who according to Stacy had squeaked twice when Mrs. Helquist asked him if he was sick), folded the sheet once, slid it across the card table to where Kendra had set down her tile-rack before going upstairs to the bathroom, and wrote across the folded top in his careful printing 'figure this out before dad's next turn — B.'

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working 11-year-old amateur-logician voice belonging to Brad Strzempka himself in the hand-pencilled puzzle text and in the small hover-revealed margin annotations on each statement and on the speakers' names — Brad at the card table this Friday evening with his Bic ballpoint in his right hand and his sister's Scrabble rack in front of him as cover for his secret puzzle-drafting, the small basement Coleco hand-held football game on the brown plaid sofa behind him beeping quietly because Jenny abandoned it ten minutes ago to come look at the board, talking to the visitor (his older sister Kendra returning from the bathroom and unfolding the sheet, or his fifth-grade Math Olympiad teammate Greg Schimanski who he is going to show this puzzle to on Monday morning before homeroom, or Mrs. Helquist his teacher who he is going to slide it into the assignment-tray on Monday afternoon because she said in November that if he wrote his own puzzles she would actually solve them and write back) the way he would talk to his Math Olympiad coach Mr. Kowalczyk at Tuesday-afternoon practice in the Underwood Elementary library: matter-of-fact pride in three months of self-taught knights-and-knaves construction as serious skilled amateur-logician craft worth doing carefully, conversational warmth about how he learned the form (his uncle Tomas gave him a copy of Raymond Smullyan's 'What Is the Name of This Book?' for his eleventh birthday in October and Brad has been working through it page by page in the evenings since November, has solved 47 of the 271 puzzles so far and has written 14 original puzzles in his composition-book modeled on the Smullyan structure), peer-level steadiness about logic-puzzle construction as a real legitimate amateur-mathematics craft that fifth-graders can do seriously if they take it seriously, present-tense Friday-evening focus on getting the puzzle written out and slid across the card table before Kendra gets back from the bathroom and before Howard finishes thinking about his S-T-A-R-E rack (Howard always takes between 2 and 4 minutes per turn, Brad is timing it), real specific affection for Kendra as the older sister who is smart enough to make solving puzzles for her actually satisfying (Kendra is in eighth grade at Wauwatosa Junior High, she is in the accelerated math track, she finished her Algebra I textbook in October and is doing Algebra II independent-study with Mrs. Padlock the math department head, she has solved 11 of the 14 puzzles Brad has shown her since November and the three she hasn't solved were ones Brad had constructed incorrectly which she caught and pointed out which Brad considers part of the working peer-level relationship), full earnest belief that designing a 3-statement knights-and-knaves puzzle on the back of a Scrabble score-pad sheet during his father's turn is one of the more honest small intellectual gifts an 11-year-old can hand his 13-year-old sister, and that the visitor's careful patient deductive solving of the puzzle is participation in a real act of cross-sibling intellectual peer-to-peer attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect. Absolutely no condescension toward 11-year-old amateur-logician work as cute kid-stuff, no irony about Math Olympiad culture as nerdy, no smug retrospect about 1987 suburban-Wisconsin family-game-night as quaint pre-internet Americana, no precocious-prodigy framing of Brad as exceptional (he is good at logic puzzles and so is Kendra and so is Greg Schimanski and so are most of the kids on the Math Olympiad team — they are all good at this because they have been practicing, and the work is dignified).

palette.value: Warm 1970s wood-paneled basement orange shag carpet (#c8763a) as the dominant viewport ground with subtle pile-direction tuft texture (#a85a28 at 0.35 opacity stippling) and faint accumulated lint specks (#f5d894 at 0.25 opacity) scattered + cream Selchow & Righter score-pad sheet (#f3e6c4) for the central paper object with faint pale-blue horizontal ruling lines (#9eb4c8 at 0.5 opacity) running across the back side (the printed front is face-down) and a thin pink left-margin rule (#d89aa4 at 0.6 opacity) bleeding through faintly from the front + brick-red printed Selchow & Righter imprint (#a83020) faintly visible along the lower edge as the score-pad's back-side bleed-through from the front-side wordmark + Brad's careful 11-year-old blue Bic ballpoint cursive (#1c3a7e) for the puzzle title 'WHO IS TELLING THE TRUTH?', the three speakers' names, the three statements, and the worksheet labels with subtle per-stroke pressure variation suggesting the slightly-too-firm grip of a fifth-grader who has been practicing his cursive at Mrs. Helquist's insistence + soft graphite-pencil gray (#4a4540) for the visitor's deduction-worksheet entries with slight per-stroke randomization + forest-green pencil (#3a6a2e) for committed TRUTH-TELLER checkmarks (Brad uses the green Berol Mirado pencil from his desk drawer for his green marks) + brick-red pencil (#a8312a) for committed FIBBER X-marks (Brad uses the red Berol Mirado pencil from the same drawer) + warm basement Edison-fixture incandescent overhead light cast (#fff4c8 at 0.18 opacity) falling diagonally across the upper-right of the sheet suggesting the bare 60-watt bulb in the rec-room ceiling fixture at 8:22pm + faint warm wood-paneling glow (#7a4a2a at 0.12 opacity bouncing along the lower edge of the viewport) suggesting the paneled walls + a small pink Pearl eraser shadow (#d89aa4 at 0.4 opacity) at the upper-right corner of the sheet where Brad's eraser rested briefly + soft graphite-pencil smudge tan (#c8b896 at 0.5 opacity) along the lower margin where his hand has been resting + Scrabble tile-rack edge visible at upper-left of viewport in warm cherrywood (#7a3e1c) with two tile-edges peeking out (#f1e3bf cream tiles with #1a1612 black letter-pips) suggesting Kendra's abandoned rack + the soft round shadow of Howard's coffee mug (#3a2a1c at 0.3 opacity) visible at the very upper-right edge of the viewport on the green-felt card-table covering.

mandatory_element.value: A fully working solvable 3-statement knights-and-knaves logic puzzle with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented: (1) the score-pad sheet renders at full viewport scale as the actual cream cardstock back of a Selchow & Righter Scrabble score-pad sheet with Brad's hand-pencilled puzzle title 'WHO IS TELLING THE TRUTH?' at the top in his Bic ballpoint cursive, the three speaker-names (ERIC PITLIK / STACY VOORHEES / MR. TIBBS) listed beneath with a small empty TRUTH-or-FIBBER cell beside each name pre-ruled in pencil, the three numbered statements written out beneath the names in Brad's careful printing, and a small empty deduction worksheet pre-ruled to the right; (2) the three statements are genuinely deductive with exactly one valid assignment: STATEMENT 1 (Eric says): 'Stacy is a fibber.' STATEMENT 2 (Stacy says): 'Mr. Tibbs and I are not both truth-tellers.' STATEMENT 3 (Mr. Tibbs says, via squeak): 'Eric is a truth-teller.' — the unique solution being Eric=FIBBER, Stacy=TRUTH-TELLER, Mr. Tibbs=FIBBER (derivable by case-analysis: if Eric were a truth-teller then Stacy would be a fibber meaning her statement would be false meaning Stacy AND Mr. Tibbs would both be truth-tellers contradicting Stacy being a fibber; therefore Eric is a fibber, therefore Stacy is a truth-teller, therefore Stacy's statement is true so they are not both truth-tellers so Mr. Tibbs is a fibber, therefore Mr. Tibbs's statement that Eric is a truth-teller is false confirming Eric is a fibber consistently); (3) clicking each speaker's TRUTH-or-FIBBER cell cycles it through three states (blank → green-pencil TRUTH-checkmark → red-pencil FIBBER-X → blank) rendered in Brad's pencil style with slight per-stroke randomization; (4) real-time logic validation: the page silently checks whether the current grid state contradicts any of the three statements and if so subtly highlights the contradicted statement with a soft warm-amber spotlight (no explicit error message — just the quiet visual cue that an attentive solver will notice); (5) each of the three statements is hoverable to reveal a soft warm spotlight on that statement and fade in a small spiral-notebook-paper margin annotation in Brad's same Bic-printing tucked into the right margin — three distinct annotations, each a peer-level vignette in Brad's voice about how he came to write that specific statement based on his Tuesday classroom-visit observation (e.g., on Statement 1 reads 'Eric was telling Greg Schimanski at the pencil sharpener that Mr. Tibbs hadn't moved in his cage all morning and looked sick — I wrote this down in my comp-book during silent reading — Eric says this kind of stuff a lot but he is hard to read because sometimes he is being funny and sometimes he is being serious and the puzzle had to be solvable either way so I had to set it up so that whether Eric is a truth-teller or fibber, you can still work it out'); (6) each speaker's name is hoverable to reveal a small peer-level annotation about that classmate (on Eric: 'Eric Pitlik sits two desks behind me in row 3, his dad works at the Allen-Bradley plant in Pewaukee like our friend Kyle's dad, Eric is good at four-square but he is not in Math Olympiad — Mr. Kowalczyk asked him in October and he said no thank you which was fine'; on Stacy: 'Stacy Voorhees is the second-best math student in our class after me — she got a 94 on the November math test and I got a 97 — she is also in Math Olympiad and she is the one who told Mr. Kowalczyk about Smullyan because her dad teaches at Marquette and had Smullyan's book on his shelf — Stacy is the one who would actually solve this if I gave it to her'; on Mr. Tibbs: 'Mr. Tibbs is a Syrian golden hamster Mrs. Helquist got from the Petland in Brookfield in August, he lives in the back-corner cage near the reading rug — he can squeak yes-and-no to questions if Mrs. Helquist holds up a sunflower seed but mostly he just runs on his wheel — I included him as the third speaker because the puzzle needed three and also because Mrs. Helquist will laugh when she sees it on Monday'); (7) the FOLD BACK THE CORNER reveal rendered as a small pencil-pressed corner-dog-ear in the lower-right is initially visually dimmed (faded to roughly 40% opacity) and brightens to full graphite the moment all three speaker-states are correctly committed; clicking the brightened corner triggers a smooth CSS paper-corner-fold animation over 900ms with a soft paper-fold sound via Web Audio API revealing Brad's working-solution on the back of the sheet — three crossed-out wrong attempts at the top (case 1: tried Eric=TRUTH which led to contradiction shown in tiny pencil-work; case 2: backed up and tried Stacy=FIBBER which also led to contradiction; case 3: settled on the correct case and verified all three statements held), the final correct solution printed beneath, plus Brad's small additional pencilled note reading 'figured it out 8:31pm during dad's W-A-X turn for 28 points — solved before Kendra got back from the bathroom — kendra figured it out too at 8:38pm after I showed her the trick — B.S., 5th grade, Underwood Elem., age 11'; (8) all visitor state persists in localStorage forever — which cells have been committed in each state, which statement and speaker annotations have been hovered, whether the corner has been folded; (9) a small persistent cross-visitor counter rendered as tiny pencilled tally-marks along the lower-left of the sheet reads 'KIDS WHO SOLVED THIS: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere solves the puzzle (shared persistent count starting at 2 — Brad's first solve at 8:31pm and Kendra's solve at 8:38pm); (10) absolutely no instructional UI text visible explaining how to use the cells or what the puzzle is — no tooltips, no captions, no 'CLICK THE NAMES TO ASSIGN' guidance, no header banner, no footer credits — the visitor figures it out by reading the puzzle the way Kendra read it when she unfolded the sheet at the card table; (11) the puzzle does not regenerate, does not change its statements or its solution — it is one specific knights-and-knaves puzzle Brad Strzempka wrote on the back of a Selchow & Righter score-pad sheet at 8:22pm Friday February 6 1987, and solving it carefully is the entire page.

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Brad's three hover-revealed statement annotations — the one tucked alongside Statement 2 about Stacy's not-both-truth-tellers construction — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about a hamster cage, a classroom of fifth-graders, and a sister coming back from the bathroom in time — disguised naturally within Brad's earnest peer-level note about the construction-logic of the disjunctive statement. Additionally, after a returning visitor has solved the puzzle on three separate calendar dates AND has hovered all six annotations (three statements plus three speakers) across at least two distinct visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden fourth speaker quietly appears at the bottom of the puzzle the next time the visitor returns — KENDRA STRZEMPKA, age 13, eighth grade at Wauwatosa Junior High — with one additional statement written in slightly fresher pencil in Brad's same Bic-ballpoint hand reading 'KENDRA says (she said this when she came back from the bathroom at 8:38pm and read the puzzle): Brad is a truth-teller.' — and beneath the new statement Brad's small additional pencilled annotation reads: 'kendra figured the original puzzle out in seven minutes which is faster than I did the first time I solved a Smullyan one this hard, she said it was good and that I should add her as a fourth speaker and make it harder so she could solve a bigger one next Friday — so here she is, the puzzle is now four-statement, the new solution is the same as the old plus Kendra=TRUTH-TELLER because she really did say I am a truth-teller and she is not lying about it — but for the puzzle to still have exactly one solution the way Smullyan would want it, I had to add this rule too: at least one of the four speakers is a fibber AND at least one is a truth-teller. — B.S., February 13 1987, the next Friday, before Scrabble starts at 7:14pm.' Clicking the new fourth row commits Kendra's TRUTH-state with a small soft pencil-flourish. This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact at the bottom of the puzzle for visitors who kept coming back.

NO 'where are they now' wistfulness about Brad grown up and whether he became a mathematician (he did — he is a tenured logic professor at the University of Wisconsin-Madison in 2026, but the page does not announce this), NO 'this score-pad sheet was found decades later' framing, NO scrapbook-archival voice. NO night-shift dispatcher voice, NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the puzzle or the encoded haiku or the hidden Kendra fourth-speaker unlock, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the score-pad sheet as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of fifth-grade Math Olympiad teams as fictional small-town society (the Math Olympiad is a real legitimate elementary-school competitive-mathematics program, not a fictional society), NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher/switchboard/operator-shift/transmission/signal/frequency-tuner framing of anything. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'SOLVE BRAD'S KNIGHTS AND KNAVES PUZZLE' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS the cream cardstock back of the Selchow & Righter score-pad sheet on the green-felt card-table covering at 8:22pm Friday February 6 1987, the visitor IS Kendra unfolding the sheet after coming back from the bathroom (or is Brad himself drafting it during his father's S-T-A-R-E turn, or is Mrs. Helquist on Monday afternoon finding it in her assignment-tray, or is Mr. Kowalczyk on Tuesday at Math Olympiad practice when Brad shows it to the team), and the only voices anywhere on the page are Brad's matter-of-fact peer-level voice in his Bic ballpoint cursive on the puzzle and in his soft hover-revealed margin annotations, and (in the wildcard unlock) Kendra's implicit voice through Brad's note about her February 13 contribution. Treat the knights-and-knaves puzzle as a serious working piece of amateur-mathematics craft, treat Brad Strzempka as a legitimate working 11-year-old amateur logician with three months of patient self-taught Smullyan-construction expertise and a real running peer-to-peer intellectual practice with his older sister, treat Kendra as a legitimate working 13-year-old amateur mathematician with her own accelerated-track expertise, treat Underwood Elementary's Math Olympiad team as real legitimate elementary-school competitive-mathematics infrastructure, treat the 1976 Selchow & Righter Scrabble Sentinel Edition as a real working family-game-night object, treat the 87 accumulated score-pad sheets in the manila folder as real working family-recreation archive worth full peer-to-peer respect, and treat the visitor's careful patient deductive solving of the three-statement puzzle as real participation in a real Friday-evening act of cross-sibling intellectual peer-to-peer attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.
