era.value: 1973 — peak American suburban kitchen-window late-summer Japanese-beetle-on-the-rosebush gardener's-thumb-and-forefinger era, the very specific moment when middle-class housewives across the upper Midwest kept a small empty Sanka coffee jar (the 8-ounce brown glass instant-coffee jar with the orange-and-cream foil-laminate label peeled off in the dishwasher) half-filled with kerosene from the metal can Don keeps in the garage on the shepherd's-hook beside the back door, and every morning between 6:42am and 7:18am before the kids came down for breakfast the woman of the house walked the brick path to the rose bed Don planted along the south fence in spring 1969 (four Mister Lincoln hybrid teas, two Tropicana, one Peace, the small handwritten white plastic stake-tags faded but legible) and picked the metallic-green-and-copper Japanese beetles off the chewed leaves and the half-eaten Tropicana blossoms one by one with her thumb-and-forefinger and dropped each beetle into the Sanka jar where it floated for a quarter-second on the kerosene meniscus before sinking; the very specific Tuesday morning of August 14, 1973 at 4218 Larchmont Drive in Brookfield Wisconsin when Mrs. Margaret 'Marge' Pulkownik (age 38, dental hygienist three mornings a week at Dr. Halverson's practice on Bluemound Road, married fifteen years to Stan Pulkownik who is a tool-and-die maker at the Allis-Chalmers plant in West Allis, mother of three: Gregory 13, Eileen 10, Patrick 6) stood at 6:54am in her quilted housecoat with the Sanka jar in her left hand and a small spiral-bound steno pad in her apron pocket (the daily beetle-count log she has been keeping since June 19 when the first beetle appeared on the Mister Lincoln nearest the gate) and counted the morning's beetles (yesterday 47, the day before 52, the day before that 61 — the count is trending down, she has decided the kerosene method is working) and entered today's count in the steno pad in her round confident Bic-blue cursive: 'Aug 14 — 38 — first morning under 40 — the Tropicana on the corner has new bud growth — M.P.'

format.value: A page that IS a single 4×6 inch ruled steno-pad page from Marge Pulkownik's beetle-count log, scaled enormously to fill the entire browser viewport edge-to-edge as the physical object lying open on the Formica kitchen counter beside the Sanka jar at 7:02am Tuesday August 14 1973 — and the page IS the page, no chrome, no navigation, no header — just the pale-green ruled steno-pad surface filling the viewport with the printed pale-blue horizontal rules and the thin red center divider running floor-to-ceiling down the middle of the spread, plus Marge's hand-written daily entries in round Bic-blue cursive accumulating across the eight weeks since June 19 — every single morning's beetle-count entered in her own hand with the date, the count, the rose-variety status, and a small private margin note about the morning (weather, what she heard on the kitchen radio, what Patrick said at breakfast, what bird was in the maple), the entries scrolling deterministically with the visitor's mouse-wheel up the page from August 14 (top) back through August 1, July, all the way back to the first entry on June 19 ('First beetle — Mister Lincoln by gate — 1 — I will get the kerosene jar from the garage tonight — M.P.'). The page is interactive — hovering any individual day's count for 3 seconds reveals Marge's fuller margin annotation on a small pale-yellow stationery slip that fades in along the right margin (minimum 56 distinct annotations across the eight weeks, each a peer-level vignette in her matter-of-fact morning voice); clicking exactly the Sanka jar visible at the lower-right edge of the viewport beside the steno pad opens a small floating jar-cross-section view showing the day's drowned beetles floating on the kerosene meniscus with the count rendered as a small handwritten tally-mark cluster on the jar's foil-label residue; clicking the small Bic-pen visible resting in the spine of the spiral binding lets the visitor add their own day's count in their own cursive on a new line at the top, persisting in localStorage forever; the entire experience is the steno-pad page lying on the kitchen counter at 7:02am Tuesday August 14 1973 in the warm slanting east-window summer light, and reading Marge's count log carefully is the entire page.

subject.value: The single specific 57-day continuous daily Japanese-beetle-count log Mrs. Marge Pulkownik has been keeping in her 4×6 spiral-bound steno pad on the kitchen counter at 4218 Larchmont Drive in Brookfield Wisconsin from June 19 through August 14, 1973 — Marge herself (age 38, dental hygienist three mornings a week at Dr. Halverson's practice on Bluemound Road since the boys started school, married fifteen years to Stan Pulkownik who works the second-shift tool-and-die at the Allis-Chalmers plant, mother of Gregory and Eileen and Patrick, grew up on a working dairy farm outside Stevens Point and knows how to do a thing every morning whether she feels like it or not), her seven rose-bushes along the south fence (the four Mister Lincoln hybrid teas Don planted on May 12 1969 the spring they moved in, the two Tropicana she added Mother's Day 1971, the one Peace she splurged on at Stein's Garden Center in April 1972), the small empty Sanka 8-ounce brown-glass jar Don rinsed out for her on June 19 and the half-pint of kerosene from the garage shepherd's-hook, the specific morning practice of standing at the rose bed between 6:42am and 7:18am every weekday and Saturday (Sunday she lets the beetles have it because Sunday is for Mass) picking each beetle off with thumb-and-forefinger and dropping it into the jar and then walking back to the kitchen and entering the count in the steno pad with the small private margin note about whatever happened that morning, and the patient seven-and-a-half-week emerging picture of one specific suburban Wisconsin rose bed in one specific Japanese-beetle infestation summer attended to by one specific gardener with full peer-level skilled rosarian attention; the small fact that the count has trended down across the past eleven days from 61 to 38 and that Marge has decided the kerosene method is working and that the Tropicana on the corner has put out fresh bud growth she did not expect this late in the season, and that she is going to keep the log going through the end of August even though the beetles will be mostly done by Labor Day, because she has decided across seven weeks that the log is now its own thing worth finishing

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working amateur-rosarian voice belonging to Marge Pulkownik herself in the daily entries and in the small hover-revealed margin annotations — Marge at the Formica kitchen counter this Tuesday morning with the Sanka jar at her right elbow and the kerosene-meniscus settling around the morning's 38 beetles and the steno pad open in front of her and the Bic blue in her right hand and the percolator finishing its cycle behind her and WTMJ 620 Milwaukee playing low on the kitchen radio (the morning farm-and-home program Mr. Bob Heiss has hosted since 1958, currently doing the soybean futures), talking to the visitor (her sister-in-law Eunice Pulkownik who lives in Wauwatosa and has her own rose bed and her own beetle problem, or her mother Helena Stankiewicz back in Stevens Point who taught her how to keep a daily log when she was nine years old on the farm, or Mrs. Doris Hellweg next door who has been asking about the kerosene method, or Marge herself in February 1974 when she opens the steno pad on the kitchen counter to remind herself the beetles will pass) the way she'd talk over the counter at Dr. Halverson's during a slow Tuesday afternoon: matter-of-fact pride in seven and a half weeks of skilled daily rosarian attention as serious working amateur-horticulture craft, conversational warmth about each rose-bush as a real specific individual with a real specific history and a real specific current condition this August (the Mister Lincoln by the gate is the strongest of the four and has tolerated the beetle pressure best, the Tropicana on the corner surprised her by putting out new buds last Friday, the Peace took the worst of the early-July damage and she has been giving it extra fish-emulsion to compensate), peer-level steadiness about the kerosene method versus the Sevin-dust method versus the milky-spore method as a real working agricultural-extension debate she has read up on in the Brookfield Public Library's copy of the Wisconsin Garden Journal and decided about for herself based on what works in her own rose bed, real specific affection for the morning ritual itself as a small working solitary half-hour she has come to value across the summer (the east window light at 6:54am, the quiet before the kids come down, the smell of the kerosene which she does not mind, the patient one-by-one picking), full earnest belief that a 57-day continuous daily beetle-count log is one of the more honest small horticultural documents a suburban Wisconsin housewife can produce and that the visitor's careful reading of the eight weeks of entries is participation in a real act of skilled amateur-rosarian peer-level attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect. Absolutely no irony about suburban-rose-gardening as quaint mid-century housewife labor, no condescension toward the daily beetle-count as obsessive small-life, no Mad-Men-pastiche period kitsch, no Stepford-Wives suburban-malaise undertone, no feminist-grievance reading of the morning ritual as constraint (Marge chose this rose bed and she chose the log and she is genuinely good at it).

mandatory_element.value: A fully working browseable 57-day beetle-count log with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented in pure HTML, CSS, and minimal JavaScript: (1) the steno-pad page renders at full viewport scale as the actual 4×6 spiral-bound steno-pad spread scaled enormously to fill the browser window, with all 57 daily entries from June 19 through August 14 pre-laid-out in Marge's round Bic-blue cursive on the pale-green ruled cardstock, each entry occupying 2-3 ruled lines and containing the date in her cursive at the left, the beetle-count integer in slightly heavier ballpoint pressure, the rose-bush status note, and the small private morning margin note; (2) mouse-wheel and trackpad-scroll up/down moves the entire steno pad vertically through the viewport revealing entries above and below the current view with smooth physical-feeling momentum scrolling — the visitor scrolls UP to go FORWARD in time (so August 14 is at the top, June 19 is at the bottom, mimicking how a real steno-pad fills downward and you flip up to read newer entries), with a soft paper-rustle sound via Web Audio API on each scroll-acceleration; (3) each of the 57 daily entries is hoverable to reveal Marge's fuller margin annotation on a small pale-yellow stationery slip that fades in along the right margin of the steno pad — minimum 57 distinct annotations, each its own specific peer-level morning vignette in Marge's voice (3-7 sentences each, no two recycling phrasing or pattern), each annotation carrying the small specific working detail of that morning's beetle-picking that has accumulated in Marge's head across the summer (June 22: 'first morning Patrick came down early and watched me through the kitchen window — he is six and he wanted to know if the beetles were dead when they hit the kerosene and I said yes pretty fast and he was satisfied with that — M.P.'; July 4: 'the boys were at Eunice's overnight so I took the Sanka jar out at 6:18am — count was 73, a Wednesday-after-the-holiday peak, the Mister Lincoln by the gate had three chewed buds — Stan got up at 7am to start the brats for the Halloran picnic at noon — M.P.'; July 29: 'Sunday — Mass at 9 — I let the beetles have the bushes today the way I always do, the count entry is just SUNDAY in pencil — Father Mikulski's homily was about the woman at the well, I have been thinking about it — M.P.'; August 8: 'count is 44 and trending down, the Tropicana on the corner has put out new bud growth which surprised me, I am going to bring Doris next door a Sanka jar of her own tomorrow — M.P.'); (4) each rose-bush mentioned in an entry is its own hoverable secondary target — hovering any 'Mister Lincoln by gate' or 'Tropicana on corner' or 'Peace' reveals a small additional margin annotation about that specific bush's current condition and the small history Marge has with it (the four Mister Lincolns each have their own annotation, the two Tropicanas each have their own, the one Peace has its own — minimum 7 distinct bush-annotations); (5) hovering any of the small WTMJ-radio program references in the morning notes for 3 seconds reveals a small annotation about what Mr. Bob Heiss was saying on the kitchen radio that specific morning (the soybean futures, the Brewers losing to Detroit, the small advisory about the Japanese-beetle pressure across the southern Wisconsin rose-growing region that Mr. Heiss read on July 12 from a UW-Madison Extension Service press release that Marge cut out of the Milwaukee Journal Green Sheet and taped to the inside back cover of the steno pad); (6) clicking the small Bic-pen visible resting in the spiral binding lets the visitor add their own day's count to a new line at the top of the steno pad — a small ballpoint-cursor input appears, the visitor types the date and the count and a short morning margin note, the entry renders in their own slightly-different blue-Bic cursive with subtle per-stroke randomization, and persists permanently in localStorage so a returning visitor finds their previous entries still in the log alongside Marge's; (7) clicking the Sanka jar visible at the lower-right edge of the viewport opens a small floating cross-section view of the jar with the day's drowned beetles floating on the pale-yellow kerosene meniscus, the count rendered as a small handwritten tally-mark cluster on the jar's foil-label residue (the foil-label has been peeled but residue remains, Marge has been writing the daily tally on the residue in pencil); clicking the jar again closes the cross-section view; (8) a working FLIP TO THE INSIDE BACK COVER button rendered as a small cream paper-tab along the lower-right corner of the steno pad — clicking it triggers a smooth CSS page-flip animation over 1.4 seconds with a soft paper-on-paper sound revealing the inside back cover of the steno pad which carries the taped-in Milwaukee Journal Green Sheet clipping from July 12 1973 about the regional Japanese-beetle pressure (the small 2-column article rendered in period-correct newspaper Linotype with Marge's blue-Bic underlines on three specific sentences and a small margin note in her cursive reading 'this matches what I have been counting — the average household rose bed in southeastern Wisconsin saw peak beetle pressure July 1-15 with counts in the 50-80 range — my June 19-August 14 log will confirm this for our yard — M.P., July 12'), plus three small additional ephemera Marge has tucked into the back of the pad (a hand-pencilled rose-bush layout diagram showing the south-fence bed in plan view with the seven bushes labeled and the two Tropicana corner positions marked with small drawn beetle-icons, a clipped 1972 Brookfield Public Library xerox of an Iowa State Extension Service bulletin on Japanese-beetle life-cycle with Marge's marginalia, and a folded handwritten letter from her mother Helena Stankiewicz dated July 30 1973 in Polish-English code-switching cursive responding to Marge's letter about the kerosene-jar method); hovering any of these three ephemera reveals a small Marge-annotation about how it came into the pad; (9) a soft persistent counter rendered as tiny Bic-pencilled tally-marks along the lower-left corner of the steno pad reads 'TOTAL BEETLES PICKED THIS SUMMER: [N]' running-summing all 57 days' counts (a real specific number derived from the actual entries in the log, which sums to 2,847 as of August 14 — the page computes this from the actual daily-count data baked into the JavaScript); (10) a small persistent shared cross-visitor counter rendered beside the first one reads 'ROSARIANS WHO READ MARGE'S LOG: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere scrolls past June 19 (the bottom of the log) for the first time (shared persistent count starting at 4 — Marge's husband Stan flipped through it once on July 8, her sister-in-law Eunice read it on July 22, her mother Helena read it via letter-excerpts Marge mailed her August 1, and Mrs. Doris Hellweg next door has read it twice); (11) absolutely no instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page explaining how to scroll the pad or hover the entries or click the jar or flip to the back cover — no tooltips, no captions, no loading messages, no 'BROWSE MARGE'S BEETLE LOG' header banner, no footer credits — the visitor figures it out by reading the entries the way Eunice did when she stopped by on July 22; (12) all visitor state persists in localStorage forever — which annotations have been hovered, whether the back cover has been flipped, the visitor's own appended entries, the current scroll position — so a returning visitor finds the steno pad exactly as they left it; (13) the log does not regenerate, does not change its counts or its margin notes — it is one specific 57-day continuous daily beetle-count log Mrs. Marge Pulkownik has been keeping at her kitchen counter at 4218 Larchmont Drive in Brookfield Wisconsin from June 19 through August 14 1973, and reading it carefully is the entire page.

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Marge's 57 daily margin annotations — the entry for July 22 1973, a Sunday — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about a kitchen window at sunrise, a brown glass jar on a counter, and a woman who has decided across forty-eight summers that doing one thing every morning is enough — disguised naturally within Marge's earnest peer-level Sunday morning entry about Father Mikulski's homily and the woman at the well. Additionally, after a returning visitor has scrolled the full log on three separate calendar dates AND has added their own daily count to the top of the pad at least four times across visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden 58th entry quietly appears at the very top of the log the next time the visitor returns — dated 'Aug 15 — Wednesday' in slightly fresher Bic-blue cursive, reading: 'Count was 31 this morning, lowest since July 3 — I think the kerosene method really has done what I wanted it to do, the rose bed will recover through September if the rain holds — Doris came over at 7:14am with her own Sanka jar and we walked our rose beds together and I showed her the steno pad and she said she was going to start her own log tomorrow morning and we agreed to compare counts on Sundays after Mass — I have been keeping this log alone for 57 days and starting tomorrow there will be two of us — that is what I wanted to write down today — M.P., August 15 1973, 7:18am.' This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact at the top of the steno pad for visitors who kept coming back.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when housewives kept logs like this' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the daily beetle-count,' NO cool ironic distance from 1970s suburban Wisconsin housewife rose-gardening as quaint pre-Roundup Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Marge or 4218 Larchmont Drive or the rose bed or Brookfield as already-vanished. NO 'where is she now' wistfulness about Marge grown older or whether the rose bed survived the 1976 drought, NO 'this steno pad was found decades later in a kitchen drawer' framing, NO scrapbook-archival voice. NO horror-genre framing of the kerosene jar, the drowned beetles, the empty kitchen at 7:02am, or the hidden 58th entry as creepy/uncanny/sinister/dread-inducing — this is a warm Tuesday August morning in a working Brookfield kitchen, the percolator is going, the radio is on, Stan is still asleep upstairs from his second shift, the kids are still asleep, and the beetle-picking and the count-logging are a normal dignified part of Marge's morning. NO 'the kerosene is environmentally devastating' moralizing — Marge chose her method based on what she read at the library and the kerosene jar is a real working 1973 rose-gardening practice that the page renders as exactly that, not as a chemical-anxiety parable for the visitor to scold from a 2026 vantage. NO Stranger-Things 1970s pastiche aesthetic, NO synthwave neon, NO vaporwave kitchen-aesthetic ironic mood-board styling, NO 'liminal-space empty-suburban-kitchen' YouTube-aesthetic horror register. NO condescension toward dental-hygienist work as small-time labor, toward Bluemound Road as quaint suburban infrastructure, toward suburban rose-gardening as kitsch housewife pastime, toward steno-pad daily-logging as obsessive small-life ritual. NO 'imagine being a 38-year-old housewife in 1973 keeping a beetle log' smug-modern-superiority framing, NO Boomer-housewife pathos voice, NO Stepford-Wives suburban-malaise undertone, NO Mad-Men-pastiche period kitsch, NO Revolutionary-Road quiet-desperation register. NO feminist-grievance reading of Marge's domestic arrangement as oppression — Marge has a real dental-hygienist career three mornings a week that she chose, the rose bed is hers, the log is hers, Stan respects her gardening because he is married to a woman who is good at things, and the marriage is a normal working partnership. NO Stan-as-absent-or-domineering framing — Stan rinsed out the Sanka jar for her on June 19 and asks after the rose bed when he comes off his second shift at 11:14pm and that is how he is. NO Polish-American grandmother caricature for Helena Stankiewicz back in Stevens Point, NO accented-English pastiche, NO 'old-country' folk-wisdom framing of the daily-log practice Helena taught Marge at age nine (Helena taught her on the working dairy farm because daily logs are how working farms run, and the page renders this as exactly that). NO Norman-Rockwell sentimentality, NO Saturday-Evening-Post-cover Americana, NO Pinterest-vintage-1970s aesthetic mood-boarding, NO 'they don't make rose gardens like this anymore' Boomer-sentimentality, NO 'simpler times' framing. NO Vietnam-War political subtext, NO Watergate undertone, NO 'in the summer of 1973 America was...' historical-essay framing — it is the summer of 1973 in Marge's kitchen and the beetles are on the roses and that is the whole subject. NO 'Patrick the youngest is going to grow up to be a tragic figure' framing — Patrick is six and he is going to start first grade in September and that is the whole content of his small role in the page. NO Norman-Maclean River-Runs-Through-It pastoral-elegiac voice, NO Wendell-Berry agrarian-philosophical handwringing about suburban yards as ecologically diminished. NO night-shift dispatcher voice (7:02am Tuesday in a Brookfield kitchen is not a midnight desk vigil), NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the beetle-count or the hidden 58th entry or the encoded haiku, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the steno pad as relic (it is a working document being filled in right now), NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of suburban-rosarians as fictional small-town society, NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher/switchboard/operator-shift/transmission/signal/frequency-tuner framing of the kitchen radio or WTMJ 620 Milwaukee or Mr. Bob Heiss's farm-and-home program or the UW-Madison Extension Service press release as signal-relay metaphor — the radio is just the radio, the Extension Service is just the Extension Service, and signal/dispatcher/transmission framing is hard-banned this hour regardless of bucket. NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing (Tuesday is a contextual fact about which morning the log is being read on, not the subject of the piece). NO 'fictional small-town society/club of [hobbyists]' framing. NO instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'READ MARGE'S BEETLE LOG' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS the steno-pad page on the Formica counter at 7:02am Tuesday August 14 1973, the visitor IS Eunice Pulkownik who has driven over from Wauwatosa for coffee (or is Mrs. Doris Hellweg next door who has been thinking about her own beetle problem, or is Marge herself in February 1974 reminding herself that the beetles will pass, or is Helena Stankiewicz back in Stevens Point reading the entries Marge has copied into her August 1 letter home), and the only voices anywhere on the page are Marge's round confident Bic-blue cursive in the daily entries and her warm cream-stationery hover-revealed margin annotations, the printed Milwaukee Journal Green Sheet Linotype in the back-cover clipping, Helena's Polish-English cursive in the back-cover letter, and (in the wildcard unlock) Marge's slightly-fresher Bic-blue cursive in the August 15 entry. Treat the daily beetle-count log as a serious working amateur-horticultural document, treat Mrs. Marge Pulkownik as a legitimate working amateur rosarian with seven and a half weeks of patient skilled morning-attention craft and a real running peer-to-peer rose-bed practice with her sister-in-law and her neighbor, treat the seven rose-bushes along the south fence as real specific individuals with real specific histories worth careful peer-level attention, treat the kerosene-jar method and the Sanka-jar repurposing as real working 1973 rose-gardening practice, treat Helena Stankiewicz's letter and the dairy-farm daily-log pedagogy as real working multi-generational craft-transmission, and treat the visitor's careful scrolling through 57 days of entries as real participation in a real act of patient amateur-horticultural attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: something to explore, multiple sections, interactive elements

era.value: 1991 — peak American shopping-mall pet-shop window kitten-and-puppy-watching era, the specific Tuesday-after-school moment when the PETLAND franchise at the Lakeforest Mall in Gaithersburg Maryland kept its wide plate-glass storefront window stocked with a rotating cast of small mammals in tiered Plexiglas display cubes positioned at exactly 4-foot-6 child-eye height, viewable from the mall corridor without entering the shop, and the specific January-through-March 1991 fluorescent-overhead-lit shop-display ritual when after-school kids walking back from the Sears arcade to the food-court pretzel counter would press their palms flat against the cool glass for two or three minutes to watch one specific kitten or rabbit or chinchilla without ever buying anything, and the specific late-Wednesday-afternoon arrival of one specific gray-and-white domestic-shorthair kitten — kitten cube #4 in the left-hand display, ear-tag 91-K-0184 — placed in the window at 11:14am Wednesday February 27 1991 by assistant manager Mr. Doug Henneman from the morning delivery van out of the regional supplier in Frederick, watched for forty-one minutes between 3:47pm and 4:28pm by 8-year-old Erin Tarczewski of 6814 Yardley Court in Montgomery Village who had walked over from Strawberry Knoll Elementary with her older brother Kyle and his friend Greg Mosely and who had stopped at the window while the boys went on to the arcade because the kitten was sitting upright with its tail wrapped exactly around its front paws and looking directly at her through the glass and she had decided in the first eleven seconds that the kitten knew her — and the small private fact that Erin returned to the same window every weekday afternoon for the next nine days to check on the same kitten and that on the tenth day, Friday March 8 1991, the cube was empty because the kitten had been adopted at 11:48am that morning by a family from Damascus Maryland and Mr. Henneman had restocked the cube with a younger calico that Erin did not know.

format.value: A fully working tiny in-browser kitten-watching window where the entire viewport IS the pet-shop window glass at 3:47pm Wednesday February 27 1991 viewed from the mall-corridor side as Erin Tarczewski sees it, with the gray-and-white kitten in cube #4 sitting upright in the center of the viewport behind the plate-glass at child-eye height — the visitor's cursor leaves a small soft warm condensation-fingerprint smudge on the glass wherever the cursor rests for more than 1.2 seconds (the smudges accumulate and persist in localStorage forever, slowly fading along their edges across visits the way real fingerprint-smudges on cold mall-window glass do), the kitten responds to the cursor's position by turning its head and following it across the cube with species-accurate slow tracking movement and occasional slow blink, clicking exactly the kitten triggers a soft purr-vibration via Web Audio API and the kitten stretches and re-settles in a new position, clicking exactly the small plastic mouse toy in the corner of the cube triggers the kitten to pounce on it with a brief 0.8-second pounce-animation and a soft thunk before returning to upright tail-wrapped-around-paws posture, and the entire piece is the patient forty-one-minute window-watching session of one 8-year-old pressing her palm to the glass deciding privately that this is her kitten.

subject.value: The single specific 41-minute kitten-window-watching session of 8-year-old Erin Tarczewski at the Lakeforest Mall PETLAND franchise storefront window between 3:47pm and 4:28pm on Wednesday February 27 1991, the specific gray-and-white domestic-shorthair kitten in cube #4 ear-tag 91-K-0184 (male, eleven weeks old, born December 15 1990 at the regional supplier farm outside Frederick Maryland, weighs 1.8 pounds, has a small white triangular blaze on his forehead and four white socks and a single small black spot on his left hind foot, sits upright with his tail wrapped exactly around his front paws when he is paying attention to something, has been in the window since Mr. Henneman placed him there at 11:14am this morning and will be adopted at 11:48am Friday March 8 by the Vellinga family from Damascus), and the small private working relationship Erin builds with the kitten across the forty-one minutes — Erin pressing her right palm flat against the cool glass, the kitten approaching the glass from inside the cube to investigate the warm shape, Erin deciding at minute 3 that she will name him Mister Mittens (after the white socks), deciding at minute 12 that she will tell her mother tonight at dinner, deciding at minute 27 that her mother will say no because of Kyle's allergy, deciding at minute 34 that she will come back tomorrow anyway, and watching the kitten settle into a slow afternoon nap at minute 38 with one small white paw covering his nose, and Erin's quiet patient understanding at 4:28pm when Kyle calls her name from the food-court entrance that she has to leave but that the kitten will be here tomorrow.

tone.value: Warm, focused, peer-to-peer working 8-year-old kitten-watcher voice belonging to Erin Tarczewski herself in the small hover-revealed margin annotations on the window-glass elements and on the kitten's specific behaviors — Erin standing in the mall corridor at 3:47pm Wednesday February 27 1991 in her purple puffer jacket with the small embroidered E on the chest pocket and her pink-and-purple LA Gear high-tops with the white double-velcro straps and her Strawberry Knoll Elementary third-grade homework folder tucked under her left arm and her right palm pressed flat against the cool plate glass, talking to the visitor (Kyle when he eventually comes back from the arcade to get her, or her best friend Stephanie Pruzinsky who she will tell about the kitten at school tomorrow morning at the bus-stop, or her mother tonight at dinner who she has decided in advance is going to say no but who she wants to tell anyway, or Erin herself in March 8 when she finds the cube empty and the calico in it instead) the way she would talk to Stephanie at the bus-stop: matter-of-fact warmth and certainty about the kitten as a real specific individual she has just met and decided is hers, conversational focus on the kitten's specific behaviors as meaningful evidence (the way his tail is wrapped exactly is the way her grandmother's cat Tippy used to sit when he was paying attention, the slow blink he just did means he likes her because her grandmother explained that to her last summer in Erie, the small head-tilt when she moved her palm an inch to the left was him watching the warm shape and not just looking at the glass), peer-level steadiness about kitten-watching as a real serious thing she is doing carefully, present-tense Wednesday-afternoon focus on the window and the kitten with no distraction by the rest of the mall, real specific affection for the kitten as Mister Mittens (the name is decided, it is not going to change), full earnest belief that pressing her palm against the glass for forty-one minutes and watching one specific kitten do specific things is one of the more important things she has done this winter and that the visitor standing beside her at the window is a real peer worthy of her quiet sharing of what she has noticed. Absolutely no irony about 8-year-old kitten-window-watching as cute kid-stuff, no condescension toward mall pet-shop culture as suspect commerce, no smug retrospect about 1991 as quaint pre-internet Americana, no sentimentality about the kitten being adopted by someone else nine days from now (Erin does not yet know that and she would not need the visitor to soften it for her if she did — she is paying attention to this Wednesday afternoon and this kitten in this cube and that is the whole content of the forty-one minutes).

palette.value: Cool plate-glass storefront window blue-gray (#b8c4cc) as the dominant viewport ground with subtle vertical glass-pane reflection streaks (#d4dce4 at 0.32 opacity) running floor-to-ceiling and faint mall-corridor fluorescent overhead reflection (#f5e8c4 at 0.15 opacity) along the upper edge + warm interior pet-shop fluorescent-cube glow (#fde8a8 at 0.6 opacity) inside the Plexiglas display cube backlighting the kitten + the kitten Mister Mittens rendered in pixel-art-style 56×42 pixel body with species-accurate domestic-shorthair coloring (soft warm gray #8a8a90 main coat, cream-white #f4ecdc belly and four socks and forehead-blaze, deep slate-charcoal #2a2a2e tabby tip-of-tail and ear-tips, warm amber-gold #c89538 eyes with subtle vertical-slit pupils that dilate when he tracks the cursor, small pink nose #e8a8a4, single tiny black hind-paw spot #1a1612) + cream cedar-shavings cube-floor bedding (#e8d8a8) with subtle scattered-shaving texture + small bright-red plastic mouse toy in the corner of the cube (#c8312a with #f0f0f0 cream-string-tail) + small cream ceramic water-bowl (#f4ecd4) with subtle blue water-line (#a8c4d4) + Erin's warm fingerprint condensation-smudges rendered as soft warm-tan radial gradients (#a89878 at 0.45 opacity center fading to 0 at 24-pixel radius) accumulating wherever the cursor rests + warm purple puffer-jacket sleeve visible at the lower-right edge of the viewport (#5e3a7e) with subtle quilted-pattern stitching + small printed PETLAND store-policy card taped to the inside of the cube in cream cardstock (#f3e6c4) with brick-red printed type (#a83020) reading 91-K-0184 / GRAY-WHITE DSH / MALE / $89.99 + warm mall-corridor late-afternoon fluorescent light cast (#f5e8c4 at 0.16 opacity) falling across the viewport from the upper edge + faint mall-corridor smoked-glass background reflection (#5a6878 at 0.18 opacity) very subtly visible bleeding into the window glass from behind the visitor's position + warm cube-corner cardboard-name-tag (#c8a878) with hand-written black Sharpie 'MITTENS' lettering (#1a1612) that Mr. Henneman wrote at 11:14am this morning + soft kitten-breath visible at the cube's interior glass surface as tiny intermittent warm-cream fog-spots (#f4ecdc at 0.4 opacity) appearing and fading on a 4-second cycle.

mandatory_element.value: A fully working interactive kitten-watching window with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented in pure HTML, CSS, and minimal JavaScript: (1) the storefront window renders at full viewport scale as the cool plate-glass surface with the Plexiglas display cube centered in the middle of the viewport at child-eye height and the kitten Mister Mittens positioned upright inside the cube with his tail wrapped around his front paws; (2) the kitten's head tracks the cursor's horizontal position across the viewport with smooth species-accurate slow head-turning animation (cursor at left = head turned slightly left, cursor at right = head turned slightly right, cursor moving fast = the kitten's head follows with a small 0.3-second lag suggesting feline attention-cost), and the kitten's eyes track within the head's range with a tighter lag; (3) the kitten performs autonomous idle behaviors on a deterministic schedule that runs forever — slow blink every 8-14 seconds (the kitten's eyes close for 0.4 seconds and reopen, the cream-white inner eyelids briefly visible), small ear-flick every 22-38 seconds, slow tail-tip twitch every 18-31 seconds, full head-tilt-to-investigate-sound every 41-67 seconds with a 1.4-second hold, and a full settle-and-stretch cycle every 3-4 minutes where the kitten stands up, arches his back into a small stretch with his front paws extended forward and his tail straight up, walks two steps to a new position in the cube, and re-settles into upright tail-wrapped-around-paws posture; (4) the cursor leaves a soft warm condensation-fingerprint smudge on the glass wherever it rests for more than 1.2 continuous seconds — the smudge appears as a soft 32-pixel-radius warm-tan radial gradient, fades along its edges over the next 90 seconds back to invisibility, and the smudges accumulate so a visitor who has lingered at multiple positions sees their fingerprint-history across the glass; smudges persist in localStorage forever, fading slightly each visit and being added to with each return visit, so the glass of a returning visitor carries the accumulated palm-prints of all their previous afternoons at the window; (5) clicking exactly the kitten triggers a soft purr-vibration sound via Web Audio API (a low 25Hz fundamental tone with a gentle 0.6Hz amplitude modulation matching real cat purring) that plays for 4.8 seconds while the kitten's body subtly rocks with the purr at 4 pixels of amplitude, and during the purr the kitten's eyes half-close in contentment; clicking the kitten while he is already purring extends the purr by 2 seconds; (6) clicking exactly the small bright-red plastic mouse toy in the corner of the cube triggers the kitten to pounce on it — a 0.8-second pounce-animation with the kitten leaping from his upright position to the mouse with all four paws extended, landing on the mouse with a soft thunk sound via Web Audio API, batting the mouse 2-3 times with his right front paw, then returning to his upright tail-wrapped-around-paws posture beside the mouse for 12 seconds before slowly walking back to the center of the cube; (7) clicking exactly the cream ceramic water-bowl triggers the kitten to walk to the bowl and take three slow lap-drinks with a small pink tongue visible (each lap a 0.4-second tongue-extension-and-retraction with a tiny water-ripple at the bowl's surface), then walk back to the center of the cube and re-settle; (8) hovering exactly the kitten for 4 continuous seconds reveals Erin's small ballpoint-cursive margin annotation on a small pale-pink stationery slip that fades in along the right margin of the viewport in her careful 8-year-old handwriting reading 'his name is Mister Mittens because of the four white socks, I decided in the first eleven seconds — he is sitting up like Tippy used to sit at Grandma Patty's house in Erie, his tail is wrapped exactly the way Tippy used to wrap his — I am going to tell mom at dinner tonight, she is going to say no because of Kyle's allergy but I am going to ask anyway — Erin T., third grade Strawberry Knoll, 3:47pm Wednesday'; (9) minimum 7 hover-targets distributed across the cube and the kitten's specific features for 3-second hovers each revealing a distinct peer-level Erin-annotation on a small pale-pink stationery slip — hovering exactly the small white triangular blaze on Mister Mittens's forehead reveals 'the white triangle on his head is like the white star Grandma Patty's old cat Whiskers had, Whiskers passed when I was five and I cried but Grandma said cats come back as different cats and I am pretty sure Mister Mittens is one of them — E.T.'; hovering exactly the small black spot on his left hind paw reveals 'the small black spot on the back paw is the only one he has, I have looked at him for twelve minutes now and I am sure — the spot is the size of a peanut — E.T.'; hovering exactly the bright-red plastic mouse toy reveals 'he is not interested in the mouse right now because he is paying attention to me, that is why he has not pounced on it even though it is right there — when he is alone in the cube tonight after the store closes he will play with it, I have decided this — E.T.'; hovering exactly the cream water-bowl reveals 'he has not drunk from the bowl since I have been here, I am going to wait and see if he drinks before Kyle comes to get me — E.T.'; hovering exactly the cream cardstock PETLAND store-policy card reveals 'the card says eighty-nine dollars and ninety-nine cents which is more than the eighty-four dollars I have saved in my pink piggy bank from my birthday and Christmas, I would need six more dollars and I could earn that doing the dishes for two weeks at fifty cents a load Mom said she would pay me but only if I asked first which I have not — E.T.'; hovering exactly the cube-corner cardboard name-tag MITTENS reveals 'the tag already says Mittens, the store man wrote it this morning before I got here — that is how I knew his name was already what I named him, the man knew too — E.T.'; hovering exactly the cedar-shavings cube-floor reveals 'the bedding is fresh, it is not dusty or stuck-together which means he just got here today, my friend Stephanie's mom said pet-shop bedding gets messy by day two — so I am the first person from outside who knows him — E.T.'; (10) all visitor state persists in localStorage forever: which hover-targets have been revealed, accumulated fingerprint-smudge positions and ages, cumulative time the visitor has spent at the window across all visits (rendered as a small running counter in Erin's pencil cursive at the very bottom of the viewport reading 'I HAVE WATCHED MISTER MITTENS FOR [N] MINUTES'), number of purrs triggered (rendered as a small running tally reading 'TIMES HE HAS PURRED FOR ME: [N]'), and the visitor's accumulated palm-print position across the glass; (11) a small shared persistent cross-visitor counter rendered in tiny pencil-cursive along the lower-right corner of the viewport reads 'KIDS WHO HAVE PRESSED THEIR PALM TO THE GLASS: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere leaves a fingerprint-smudge for the first time (shared persistent count starting at 1 — Erin's first palm-print at 3:47pm February 27 1991); (12) the kitten does not regenerate, does not change his coat pattern, does not switch to a different kitten — he is one specific gray-and-white domestic-shorthair kitten 91-K-0184 named Mister Mittens by Erin and by Mr. Henneman simultaneously, and watching him through the glass carefully is the entire page; (13) absolutely no instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page explaining how to track the kitten or click the toys or leave fingerprints or hover the targets — no tooltips, no captions, no loading messages, no 'WATCH THE KITTEN' header banner, no footer credits — the visitor figures it out by pressing their palm to the glass the way Erin did at 3:47pm.

wildcard.value: Exactly one of Erin's seven hover-revealed annotations — the one tucked alongside the small black spot on Mister Mittens's left hind paw — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about twelve patient minutes, one warm palm against cold glass, and the small peanut-sized fact that decides a friendship — disguised naturally within Erin's earnest peer-level note about the spot. Additionally, after a returning visitor has accumulated more than 90 cumulative minutes at the window across at least four separate calendar dates AND has triggered at least 12 purrs across visits (tracked in localStorage), a hidden additional element quietly appears the next time the visitor returns — at 3:48pm Wednesday February 27 1991 viewport-time on the new visit, a small folded note appears tucked into the cube-corner beside Mister Mittens's water-bowl, written in Mr. Doug Henneman's careful adult ballpoint cursive on a small piece of PETLAND inventory-tag paper, reading 'kid — I see you at the window every afternoon. I am not supposed to do this but Mr. Vellinga from Damascus is coming Friday morning to pick up the kitten and I have decided I am going to tell him the kitten is already adopted. If your mom and dad come in Saturday morning before noon I will hold him for you. The price tag says eighty-nine ninety-nine but he is going to be sixty dollars cash to you, the difference is on me. Bring a carrier. — D. Henneman, assistant manager, PETLAND Lakeforest, written Wednesday Feb 27 at 4:18pm while you were still at the window.' Clicking the folded note unfolds it over 1.4 seconds with a soft paper-unfolding animation. This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact tucked into the corner of the cube for visitors who kept coming back to press their palm to the glass.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO horror-genre framing of the empty mall, the lit cube, the kitten staring through glass, the cumulative palm-prints, or the hidden Mr. Henneman note as creepy/uncanny/sinister/dread-inducing — this is a warm Wednesday afternoon at a working 1991 suburban Maryland mall, the fluorescent overheads are on, kids are everywhere walking back from the Sears arcade to the food court, Mr. Henneman is in the back stocking the dog-food shelf, and Erin is having a normal kitten-window-watching session of the kind 8-year-olds genuinely did in 1991. NO 'the kitten is something other than a kitten' twist, NO 'the mall is liminal-space' YouTube-aesthetic register, NO 'PETLAND was acquired by [X] in [Y] and the Lakeforest Mall closed in 2017' decline narrative overlaid on the 1991 page, NO Stranger-Things 1990s pastiche aesthetic, NO synthwave neon, NO vaporwave mall-aesthetic ironic mood-board styling, NO 'dead-mall' liminal-space horror register. NO museum-archival 'remember when malls had pet shops' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost world of the storefront kitten window,' NO cool ironic distance from early-90s suburban Maryland mall culture as quaint pre-internet Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Erin or PETLAND or Lakeforest Mall or Strawberry Knoll Elementary as already-vanished. NO 'where is she now' wistfulness about Erin grown up, NO 'where is Mister Mittens now' melancholy (he was adopted by the Vellingas, he lived to be 17, he had a good cat-life, the page does not need to litigate that), NO scrapbook-archival voice. NO animal-welfare-political overlay on the pet-shop business model — the page is not arguing about commercial pet sales versus shelter adoption, it is the story of one 8-year-old at one window on one Wednesday afternoon in 1991 and Erin is not aware of that political conversation and the page does not need to be either. NO 'the kitten will probably be euthanized if not adopted' tragic foreshadowing — Mister Mittens is going to be adopted Friday morning by a real family from Damascus, and the wildcard reveals that Mr. Henneman has decided to redirect him to Erin's family instead, and the page is not interested in worst-case scenarios. NO condescension toward 8-year-old kitten-window-watching as cute kid-stuff, toward pet-shop counter-work as small-time labor, toward Lakeforest Mall as suburban kitsch, toward LA Gear high-tops or pink-and-purple velcro shoes as 90s-childhood-aesthetic-mood-board ephemera. NO smug modern superiority about pre-internet kid recreation, NO Boomer-childhood-nostalgia voice (Erin is Gen Y, not Boomer, and the page is in her present tense not anyone's retrospective). NO Mom-as-mean-figure framing for Erin's mother's anticipated no — Cheryl Tarczewski has real reasons (Kyle's documented cat-allergy diagnosis from Dr. Vorhauer in October 1989, the family's already-committed budget for Kyle's allergy medication, Cheryl's own bookkeeping at the Gaithersburg branch of the Suburban Bank that funds the household), and the no is not villainy. NO Kyle-as-bad-older-brother framing — Kyle is 11, he genuinely likes his sister, he is going to come back from the arcade at 4:28pm and call her name from the food-court entrance because that is what they agreed on and he is keeping his word, and the page is not setting up sibling-conflict drama. NO Mr. Henneman-as-creepy-stranger framing — he is 34, married to Janet Henneman who is a 4th-grade teacher at Goshen Elementary, has been at PETLAND Lakeforest for six years, knows Erin by sight from her previous afternoon visits, and his note in the wildcard is the small honest gesture of an adult who has been watching her watch the kitten and has decided to help, NOT a grooming scenario or anything sinister. NO night-shift dispatcher voice (Wednesday 3:47pm in a mall corridor is not a midnight desk vigil), NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the kitten or the note or the haiku, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the pet-shop window as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of mall-window kitten-watchers as fictional small-town society, NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher/switchboard/operator-shift/transmission/signal/frequency-tuner framing of the mall PA or the regional pet-supplier delivery network as signal-relay metaphor — the PA is just a PA, the supplier is just a supplier, and signal/dispatcher framing is hard-banned this hour regardless of bucket. NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing (Wednesday is a contextual fact about which afternoon Erin walked over from school, not the subject of the piece). NO 'fictional small-town society/club' framing. NO instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'WATCH THE KITTEN AT THE PETLAND WINDOW' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS the storefront window glass at 3:47pm Wednesday February 27 1991, the visitor IS Erin pressing her right palm against the cool glass (or is Kyle when he comes to get her at 4:28pm, or is Stephanie at the bus-stop tomorrow morning when Erin tells her about the kitten), and the only voices anywhere on the page are Erin's careful 8-year-old ballpoint cursive in her hover-revealed margin annotations, the small printed PETLAND store-policy commercial typography, the cardboard name-tag in Mr. Henneman's marker, and (in the wildcard unlock) Mr. Henneman's adult ballpoint cursive on the inventory-tag note. Treat the storefront kitten window as a serious working piece of 1991 retail infrastructure that genuinely mattered to suburban kids who walked past it every afternoon, treat Erin Tarczewski as a legitimate working 8-year-old kitten-watcher with patient peer-level observational expertise that her grandmother taught her, treat Mister Mittens as a real specific kitten with a real specific personality being met by a real specific child at a real specific window, treat the forty-one-minute window-watching session as a real form of cross-species peer-level attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect, and treat the visitor's careful palm-pressing and head-tracking and toy-clicking and hover-target-revealing as real participation in a real Wednesday-afternoon act of patient kitten-watching craft worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1978 — peak American small-town hardware-store paint-counter color-card-rack era, the very specific Tuesday-afternoon moment when independent hardware stores across the upper South kept a tall freestanding wire rack beside the Sherwin-Williams tinting machine stocked with hundreds of small printed paper paint-chip cards from the Pittsburgh Paints PITTSBURGH COLORS '78 spring catalog distributed to subscribing dealers in March 1978, each chip card a 2.25×4.5 inch four-color offset-printed cream cardstock rectangle bearing the color's hand-mixed swatch in a 1.5×2 inch flat-ink rectangle centered above the color's printed name and four-digit catalog number and a small printed paragraph of suggested-room-use copy written by the in-house Pittsburgh Paints copywriter Mrs. Lurleen Bagshaw of Allegheny County who had been writing the chip-card copy since 1961 from her small home office on Mt. Lebanon Avenue, the rack restocked every Wednesday morning by the regional Pittsburgh Paints sales representative Mr. Doyle Vickerstaff who drove the Tri-State territory in a wood-paneled 1974 Ford Country Squire with a printed-cardboard chip-card display kit in the cargo area; the specific Tuesday afternoon of April 11, 1978 at 3:14pm at Brockman's True Value Hardware on East Main Street in Murfreesboro Tennessee when 34-year-old Mrs. Caroline Sneed of 818 Wedgewood Drive stood at the chip-card rack with her 6-year-old son Wesley holding her left hand and a small grocery-bag of Brockman's-deli pimento-cheese sandwiches for supper in her right hand and considered the 47 cream-and-yellow chip cards in the WARM NEUTRALS section trying to decide which one to pick for the small front parlor she and her husband Don had been meaning to repaint since they moved into the Wedgewood house in October 1976, and the specific chip card she pulled from the rack at 3:23pm being card number 4218-Y titled BUTTERMILK MORNING which Mrs. Bagshaw had written the copy for at her writing desk on a Wednesday morning in August 1977

format.value: A page that IS a single 2.25×4.5 inch Pittsburgh Paints chip card scaled enormously to fill the entire browser viewport edge-to-edge as the physical paper rectangle Caroline Sneed pulled from the rack at 3:23pm Tuesday April 11 1978 and held up against the cream-painted wall of Brockman's Hardware to see how the swatch would read in afternoon light — no chrome, no navigation, no header, just the cream cardstock surface filling the viewport with the centered flat-ink swatch rectangle of BUTTERMILK MORNING (a soft warm pale yellow-cream), the printed color name in chunky 1970s display type, the four-digit catalog number 4218-Y, Mrs. Bagshaw's printed suggested-use paragraph beneath the swatch in small Linotype serif, the small Pittsburgh Paints brand cluster along the lower edge, the printed BROCKMAN'S TRUE VALUE rubber-stamp impression in faded brick-red along the lower-right corner where Mr. Earl Brockman stamps every chip card before stocking the rack, plus the small accumulated texture of the afternoon (a tiny grease-thumb smudge along the upper-left where Caroline held it after touching the deli-bag waxed-paper, a soft pencil-mark in the lower margin where she lightly checked the catalog number to remember it, a tiny pale-blue Bic dot in the corner where Wesley pressed his pen down accidentally while she was reading the copy). Hovering the swatch rectangle for 3 seconds reveals Mrs. Bagshaw's hand-written margin annotation on a small cream stationery slip about how she chose the name BUTTERMILK MORNING after watching her own kitchen window light the morning of August 9 1977; minimum 7 hover-targets across the printed elements each revealing a distinct peer-level annotation (the catalog number, the suggested-room-use paragraph, the Pittsburgh brand cluster, the BROCKMAN'S stamp, the small printed AVAILABLE IN SEMI-GLOSS AND EGGSHELL line, the pricing-per-quart line, the small printed COVERAGE 400 SQ FT line); clicking the swatch rectangle scales it up to fill 80% of the viewport so the visitor can see the flat-ink color at the same scale Caroline saw it when she held it against the parlor wall the next morning at 8:14am; clicking the BROCKMAN'S stamp reveals Mr. Earl Brockman's annotation about the 11-year history of stamping every chip card by hand; clicking the small printed catalog number flips the card to its back side where Caroline has hand-pencilled at 3:24pm 'Parlor — south wall — Don to look Saturday — semi-gloss — 2 quarts — C.S.'; an accumulating localStorage-backed counter at the very bottom reads 'CHIP CARDS PULLED FROM THE BROCKMAN'S RACK THIS SPRING: [N]' starting at 1,847 (Mr. Brockman's actual stocking-and-restocking count for the March-April 1978 rotation).

subject.value: The single specific Pittsburgh Paints chip card number 4218-Y titled BUTTERMILK MORNING from the PITTSBURGH COLORS '78 spring catalog, pulled at 3:23pm Tuesday April 11 1978 from the freestanding wire chip-card rack at Brockman's True Value Hardware on East Main Street in Murfreesboro Tennessee by Mrs. Caroline Sneed (age 34, part-time bookkeeper at Dr. Whitsitt's veterinary practice on the Shelbyville Road two mornings a week, married eleven years to Don Sneed who is a heating-and-cooling installer for Murfreesboro Climate Control, mother of Wesley who is 6 and starting first grade at McFadden Elementary in August, the Sneeds moved into the small 1947 Cape-Cod-style house at 818 Wedgewood Drive in October 1976 and have been meaning to repaint the front parlor since the closing-day walkthrough when Caroline noticed the previous owner had painted the south wall a strange grayish-green that catches the afternoon light wrong); Mrs. Lurleen Bagshaw of Mt. Lebanon Pennsylvania herself, age 58, in-house Pittsburgh Paints copywriter since 1961, who wrote the BUTTERMILK MORNING suggested-use paragraph at her writing desk on the morning of Wednesday August 17 1977 between 9:14am and 10:42am while watching the warm late-summer sun come through her own kitchen window above the sink and thinking specifically about a young couple in a small house somewhere in the Tri-State territory trying to figure out what color to paint a parlor they had not yet decided was theirs; Mr. Earl Brockman of Brockman's True Value Hardware, age 61, third-generation hardware dealer (his grandfather Reuben Brockman opened the store in 1898, his father Harlan ran it from 1932 to 1969, Earl took over the year his father retired), who has been stocking the Pittsburgh Paints chip-card rack every Wednesday morning since 1967 and stamping every single chip card on the front lower-right corner with the small brick-red BROCKMAN'S TRUE VALUE rubber stamp his father had made at the Murfreesboro Sign Shop in 1953 because Earl had decided in 1967 that the chip cards traveled with the customer and the small brand impression was a quiet form of honest hometown commercial presence worth doing carefully; Mr. Doyle Vickerstaff the regional sales representative, age 47, who has driven the Tri-State Pittsburgh Paints territory in his wood-paneled Country Squire since 1968 stocking 84 independent hardware-store accounts across middle Tennessee and northern Alabama and southwestern Kentucky and who restocked the Brockman's chip-card rack on Wednesday April 5 1978 with the fresh spring catalog cards including a full sleeve of 40 BUTTERMILK MORNING cards (Brockman's standard reorder quantity); and the very specific 3:23pm Tuesday-afternoon moment when Caroline pulled card 4218-Y from the rack and held it up against the cream-painted wall of Brockman's beside the tinting machine to see how the warm yellow-cream would read in the slanting afternoon light through the storefront window, and decided in the first eleven seconds that this was the one she would bring home to show Don on Saturday morning, and pencilled the small note on the back of the card at 3:24pm before tucking it into the side pocket of her purse beside her checkbook

Brockman's quiet third-generation hardware-dealer voice in the annotation tucked beside the rubber-stamp impression — Caroline at the chip-card rack at 3:23pm Tuesday April 11 1978 in her pale-blue cotton spring blouse and her gray pleated A-line skirt and her cream Eastland loafers with the small brass buckle, holding Wesley's left hand and the pimento-cheese sandwich bag and the chip card all at once, talking to the visitor (Don when she shows him the card at the kitchen table Saturday morning, or her sister-in-law Sue Anne Sneed who lives two blocks over and has been helping her think about the parlor since February, or Caroline herself in October 1978 when the parlor is finally repainted and she finds the card tucked into the bookkeeping ledger from work) the way she'd talk to Sue Anne over coffee at the Wedgewood kitchen counter: matter-of-fact warmth about eighteen months of patient parlor-color consideration as serious working homeowner-craft, conversational steadiness about
