mandatory_element.value: A fully working interactive paint-chip-card experience with the following behaviors all correctly implemented in pure HTML, CSS, and minimal JavaScript: (1) the chip card renders at full viewport scale as the actual 2.25×4.5 inch cardstock object scaled enormously to fill the browser window with all printed elements (Pittsburgh Paints brand cluster, WARM NEUTRALS section header, the centered flat-ink BUTTERMILK MORNING swatch rectangle, the color name in chunky display type, the catalog number 4218-Y, Mrs. Bagshaw's printed suggested-use paragraph beneath the swatch, the small printed AVAILABLE IN SEMI-GLOSS AND EGGSHELL line, the COVERAGE 400 SQ FT line, the pricing-per-quart line $4.49) plus the hand-stamped brick-red BROCKMAN'S TRUE VALUE circular impression in the lower-right corner and the small accumulated texture of Caroline's afternoon (the grease-thumb smudge, the pencil-mark catalog-number checkmark, Wesley's pale-blue Bic dot); (2) minimum 7 hover-targets across the printed and handwritten elements each revealing a distinct peer-level cream-stationery-slip margin annotation in the relevant person's voice (Caroline, Mrs. Bagshaw, or Mr. Brockman) on a 3-second hover — hovering the BUTTERMILK MORNING swatch rectangle reveals Mrs. Bagshaw's annotation about choosing the name on the morning of August 9 1977 watching her own kitchen window light ('I had been writing chip-copy for sixteen years by that August and the name had not come to me, I had drafted four names that week — Morning Cream, Kitchen Light, Pale Honey, Soft Custard — and none of them were the right name for a Tri-State homeowner standing at a hardware-store rack on a Tuesday afternoon. The buttermilk came to me at 9:42am when I poured the half-and-half into my coffee and watched the cream fold into the dark and the morning light catch it on the way down. Buttermilk Morning. I wrote it down on the back of an envelope and Hubert came into the kitchen and I read it to him and he said that is the one, Lurleen. — L.B.'); hovering the catalog number 4218-Y reveals Mrs. Bagshaw's annotation about the Pittsburgh Paints numbering system; hovering the suggested-use paragraph reveals her annotation about the seventeen-year practice of writing for one specific imagined Tri-State homeowner; hovering the AVAILABLE IN SEMI-GLOSS AND EGGSHELL line reveals her annotation about the eggshell-versus-semi-gloss debate she has had with the marketing department twice a year since 1965; hovering the COVERAGE 400 SQ FT line reveals her annotation about the test-painting practice at the Pittsburgh Paints Allegheny test kitchen; hovering the BROCKMAN'S rubber-stamp impression reveals Mr. Brockman's annotation about the 11-year history of stamping every chip card by hand ('My father had this stamp made at the Murfreesboro Sign Shop in 1953 when he was running the store and we had just signed the Pittsburgh Paints account. The Pittsburgh rep at the time, Mr. Halverson, asked my father why he was stamping the chip cards and my father said because the chip card goes home with the customer and the customer keeps it in a drawer for two years and pulls it out in 1980 and the stamp is the only way they remember where they got it. Mr. Halverson did not require it but he did not stop us. I have been stamping them since 1967 when I took over. We go through one stamp pad every fourteen months. — E.B., third-generation, since 1967'); hovering the Pittsburgh Paints brand cluster reveals Mr. Doyle Vickerstaff the regional sales rep's annotation about his Wednesday-restocking route across the 84 Tri-State accounts; hovering the small grease-thumb smudge reveals Caroline's annotation about stopping at the Brockman's deli counter for the pimento-cheese sandwiches before the chip-card rack; hovering Wesley's pale-blue Bic dot reveals Caroline's annotation about Wesley wanting to help pick the color and asking if he could draw on the card and her saying yes just one dot in the corner; hovering the pencilled catalog-number checkmark reveals Caroline's annotation about her own working homeowner-bookkeeping practice; (3) clicking the swatch rectangle scales it up to fill 80% of the viewport for 4 seconds with a smooth CSS scale animation suggesting Caroline holding the card up against the cream wall of Brockman's to see the color at room-scale; clicking again returns to normal scale; (4) clicking the small catalog-number cluster flips the entire chip card over with a smooth CSS card-flip animation over 900ms revealing the back side with Caroline's hand-pencilled note in her careful blue Bic ballpoint cursive reading 'Parlor — south wall — Don to look Saturday — semi-gloss — 2 quarts — C.S., 3:24pm Tues Apr 11 1978' rendered in hand-styled web-font with per-character subtle slant and pressure variation; (5) on the back side, hovering Caroline's note for 3 seconds reveals her longer annotation about the south wall and the eleven previous chip cards she has brought home from Brockman's since November and why this is the one; (6) a working CLIP IT TO YOUR PURSE button rendered as a small dashed-line scissors mark along the upper-right corner of the back of the card — clicking it triggers a small CSS slide-into-purse animation and the card persists into the visitor's localStorage-backed personal chip-card collection visible as a small accumulating pile at the lower-right of the viewport across visits; (7) all visitor state persists in localStorage forever — which hover-targets have been revealed, whether the card has been flipped, whether the swatch has been scaled up, whether the card has been clipped to the purse — so a returning visitor finds the card exactly as they left it; (8) a small persistent cross-visitor counter rendered in tiny cream type along the very bottom of the front of the chip card reads 'CHIP CARDS PULLED FROM THE BROCKMAN'S RACK THIS SPRING: [N]' incrementing every time any visitor anywhere pulls the card for the first time (shared persistent count starting at 1,847 — Mr. Brockman's actual stocking-and-restocking count for the March-April 1978 rotation through 3:23pm Tuesday April 11 when Caroline pulled hers); (9) absolutely no instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page explaining how to hover, flip, scale, or clip — no tooltips, no captions, no loading messages, no 'EXPLORE THE CHIP CARD' header banner, no footer credits — the visitor figures it out by looking at the card the way Caroline did at the rack; (10) the chip card does not regenerate, does not change its color or its catalog number — it is one specific Pittsburgh Paints chip card 4218-Y BUTTERMILK MORNING from the PITTSBURGH COLORS '78 spring catalog, pulled at 3:23pm Tuesday April 11 1978 by Caroline Sneed at Brockman's True Value Hardware in Murfreesboro Tennessee, and reading it carefully is the entire page.

wildcard.value: Exactly one of the seven hover-revealed annotations — Mrs. Bagshaw's annotation tucked alongside the BUTTERMILK MORNING swatch rectangle about the morning of August 9 1977 — when read aloud line-by-line at its natural sentence breaks, encodes a perfect haiku (5-7-5 syllable structure) about cream folding into coffee at the kitchen window, a copywriter who has been waiting sixteen years for the right two words to arrive, and the small private morning a name finally came — disguised naturally within Mrs. Bagshaw's earnest peer-level note about the August 1977 naming moment. Additionally, after a returning visitor has flipped the card and revealed all seven hover-annotations and clipped the card to their purse on three separate calendar dates (tracked in localStorage), a hidden eighth element quietly appears the next time the visitor returns — a small additional folded paper-slip tucked alongside the chip card in the visitor's purse-collection, dated in the corner 'October 18 1978 — six months later — for whoever kept this card,' written in Caroline Sneed's same blue Bic cursive but slightly more confident, reading: 'Don and I painted the parlor the second weekend of May. Two quarts of Buttermilk Morning semi-gloss from Brockman's, Don rolled the walls and I cut in the trim on Saturday afternoon while Wesley played in the yard with the Bagley girls next door. We were finished by Sunday at 4pm. The south wall reads exactly the way I hoped it would in the afternoon light — Mrs. Bagshaw was right about what buttermilk does to a morning. I have kept this chip card in the front of my bookkeeping ledger at Dr. Whitsitt's office for six months and I look at it sometimes between accounts. I am writing this slip in October to tuck into the card because the parlor turned out and somebody who picks a chip card from a hardware-store rack deserves to know that sometimes the color is the right one. — Caroline Sneed, 818 Wedgewood Drive, Murfreesboro Tennessee, October 18 1978.' This unlock is not announced anywhere, no UI text refers to it, no counter celebrates it — it simply becomes the small private fact tucked alongside the chip card for visitors who kept coming back.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO museum-archival 'remember when hardware stores had chip-card racks' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of independent hardware-store paint-counter culture,' NO cool ironic distance from 1970s small-town Tennessee hardware-store culture as quaint pre-Home-Depot Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Brockman's or Pittsburgh Paints or Mrs. Bagshaw or Caroline Sneed or 818 Wedgewood Drive as already-vanished. NO 'where are they now' wistfulness about whether Brockman's survived the 1989 Home Depot opening in Nashville (it did, it is still there in 2026 under Earl's grandson Lee Brockman), NO 'this chip card was found decades later in a kitchen drawer' framing, NO scrapbook-archival voice. NO horror-genre framing of the empty hardware store at 3:23pm, the cream-painted wall, the chip-card rack, the rubber-stamp impression, or the hidden October 1978 follow-up slip as creepy/uncanny/sinister/dread-inducing — this is a warm Tuesday spring afternoon at a working independent hardware store on East Main Street, the sun is coming through the storefront window, Mr. Brockman is at the register reading the Murfreesboro Daily News-Journal, Wesley is patient because Caroline promised him a Coke from the deli cooler when she was done, and Caroline is genuinely having a good moment picking a paint color. NO 'the parlor is haunted by previous owners' framing, NO 'Caroline never actually painted the parlor' twist, NO 'the chip card is the only thing left' decline narrative. NO Stranger-Things 1970s pastiche aesthetic, NO synthwave neon, NO vaporwave hardware-store-aesthetic ironic mood-board styling. NO condescension toward 1978 small-town Tennessee homeowner culture as quaint, toward independent hardware-store paint-counter craft as small-time labor, toward Mrs. Bagshaw's seventeen-year career as in-house copywriter as wistful small-life, toward Mr. Brockman's stamp-every-card practice as fussy, toward Caroline's eighteen months of color consideration as obsessive small-life. NO smug modern superiority about pre-Sherwin-Williams-color-app paint-selection ('imagine deciding a wall color from a 1.5×2 inch swatch'), NO Boomer-Americana sentimentality. NO Mad-Men-pastiche period kitsch, NO Stepford-Wives suburban-malaise undertone, NO feminist-grievance reading of Caroline's part-time bookkeeping and parlor-painting domestic arrangement as oppression — Caroline chose the part-time arrangement when Wesley started kindergarten last fall, she likes the Whitsitt veterinary-practice work, Don respects her color decisions because he is married to a woman who is good at things, and the marriage is a normal working partnership. NO Don-as-absent-or-domineering framing — Don is at a heating-installation job on Old Hickory Boulevard this Tuesday afternoon and will look at the chip card Saturday morning at the kitchen table over coffee and will say the color is fine if Caroline likes it. NO Wesley-as-restless-or-bratty framing — Wesley is six and he is patient because his mother promised him a Coke. NO Stand-By-Me melancholy-childhood-tableau register, NO Wonder-Years narrator-looking-back voice. NO Norman-Rockwell sentimentality, NO Saturday-Evening-Post-cover Americana, NO Pinterest-vintage-1970s aesthetic mood-boarding. NO Watergate political subtext, NO 'in the spring of 1978 America was...' historical-essay framing — it is spring 1978 in Murfreesboro and Caroline is picking a paint color and that is the whole subject. NO 'Tennessee folksy' caricature, NO accented-English pastiche, NO 'aw shucks' fake-Southern dialogue. NO 'Mrs. Bagshaw is a sad lonely housewife with a writing job' framing — Mrs. Bagshaw has a successful 17-year career, a husband Hubert who is a Pittsburgh attorney, two grown children, and she is genuinely good at writing chip-card copy. NO night-shift dispatcher voice (3:23pm Tuesday at a hardware store is not a midnight desk vigil), NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the chip card or the encoded haiku or the hidden October follow-up slip, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the chip card as relic (it is a working commercial document being read right now), NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of paint-chip-card collectors as fictional small-town society, NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher/switchboard/operator-shift/transmission/signal/frequency-tuner framing of the Pittsburgh Paints Tri-State distribution network or Mr. Vickerstaff's Country Squire route as signal-relay metaphor — the distribution network is just a distribution network, neither it nor the chip-card rack is a signal metaphor. NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing (Tuesday is a contextual fact about which afternoon Caroline stopped at Brockman's, not the subject of the piece). NO 'fictional small-town society/club' framing. NO instructional UI text visible anywhere on the page. NO captions, NO tooltips, NO loading messages, NO 'EXAMINE THE BUTTERMILK MORNING CHIP CARD' header banner, NO footer credits. The page IS the 2.25×4.5 inch chip card Caroline pulled from the rack at 3:23pm Tuesday April 11 1978, the visitor IS Caroline herself holding it up against the cream wall of Brockman's (or is Don at the kitchen table Saturday morning, or is Mrs. Bagshaw at her writing desk in Mt. Lebanon in August 1977 imagining the homeowner who would eventually pull her card, or is Mr. Brockman at the rack on Wednesday morning stamping the fresh sleeve), and the only voices anywhere on the page are Caroline's careful blue Bic cursive in the back-side note and her small hover-revealed annotations, Mrs. Bagshaw's peer-level copywriter annotations in her in-house writing-desk voice, Mr. Brockman's quiet third-generation hardware-dealer annotation, Mr. Vickerstaff's regional-sales-rep annotation, and the printed Pittsburgh Paints commercial typography of the chip card itself. Treat the chip card as a serious working commercial document, treat Mrs. Bagshaw as a legitimate working professional copywriter with seventeen years of skilled commercial-writing craft, treat Mr. Brockman as a legitimate third-generation hardware-dealer with eleven years of patient stamp-every-card practice, treat Caroline Sneed as a legitimate working amateur homeowner with eighteen months of careful color consideration, treat the BUTTERMILK MORNING swatch as a real specific flat-ink color worth careful peer-level attention, and treat the visitor's careful hovering and flipping and clipping of the card as real participation in a real Tuesday-afternoon act of patient color-selection craft worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1968 — peak American suburban Saturday-morning kitchen-linoleum scuffed-wax era, the specific Saturday-morning moment when middle-class housewives across the upper Midwest mopped the kitchen linoleum with Johnson Wax's Glo-Coat liquid-floor-wax product applied with a rectangular cellulose-sponge mop from a yellow plastic bucket filled at the deep-set Kohler kitchen sink, the very specific 7:14am Saturday-morning moment when Mrs. Roberta 'Bobbie' Stiefelmeier (age 36, homemaker, married thirteen years to Don who is at the Allis-Chalmers Tractor Division plant in West Allis doing a Saturday overtime shift, mother of three: Kevin 11, Joanne 9, Mark 5) at 4118 Edgewood Drive in Greendale Wisconsin had just finished mopping the small kitchen linoleum (a cream-and-pale-mint speckled-pattern Armstrong Excelon vinyl-asbestos tile installed October 1962 by Don himself across three weekends with a borrowed Sears tile-cutter) and was watching the Glo-Coat wax dry across the floor in patient slow horizontal sweep-marks where the sponge mop had laid down the second coat, the wax catching the early east-window October light at a precise low angle that produced the very specific small rainbow-iridescent oil-slick sheen across the wet-wax surface, and Bobbie standing at the kitchen door with her second cup of Folger's percolator coffee in a cream-and-gold-band Anchor Hocking mug watching the iridescent sheen slowly settle and dull into the final hard satin finish across the 47 minutes it took the wax to fully cure

format.value: A single full-viewport silent generative piece rendered entirely in pure CSS and SVG with no UI text and no controls anywhere: the entire browser window IS the cream-and-pale-mint speckled Armstrong Excelon vinyl-asbestos kitchen linoleum at 7:14am on Saturday October 19 1968, viewed from directly above as Bobbie sees it standing at the kitchen door with her coffee mug — the 9×11 foot floor scaled to fill the viewport with each individual 9-inch tile clearly visible in its grid pattern, every tile bearing its own slightly different cream-mint speckle pattern, the floor freshly mopped with Johnson Wax Glo-Coat in slow horizontal sponge-mop sweep-marks running left-to-right across the tiles, and the wet-wax surface catching the rising east-window October light at a precise low angle to produce continuously evolving rainbow-iridescent oil-slick sheens across the floor. The piece is a deterministic 47-minute generative cycle in which the wax very slowly cures across the floor — the iridescent sheen begins at peak saturation at minute 0 (representing 7:14am, the moment Bobbie set down the bucket and stepped back), evolves continuously across the cycle as the wax dries and the oil-slick colors slowly shift and dull into their final hard satin finish at minute 47 (representing 8:01am, when the floor is fully cured), then loops smoothly back to minute 0. The iridescent sheen is rendered as overlapping translucent CSS conic-gradient bands flowing across the linoleum in the patterns of real curing-floor-wax surface tension — broad warm-coral and butter-yellow and pearl-cream and dusty sage-green and cool dusty-violet bands at 0.18-0.32 opacity blending into each other with subtle internal swirl, each band slowly drifting at 0.4-0.8 pixels per second along the sweep-mark direction, and each band's saturation and edge-definition very slowly diminishing across the cycle as the wax cures. The sponge-mop sweep-marks themselves are visible as parallel slightly-darker stripes spaced 11 inches apart running floor-to-ceiling, each stripe carrying the faint ridged texture of where the cellulose sponge laid down its second coat with subtle pool-pattern variation along the seam between sweeps. Suspended within the wax surface are approximately 340 tiny procedurally-generated micro-bubbles (the small bubbles a cellulose mop leaves in wet wax) rendered as soft-edged 2-4 pixel SVG circles each catching the iridescent light at its own specific angle — bubbles in warm-band regions render warmer-coral, bubbles in cool-band regions render cooler-violet, each bubble drifting very slowly with the band it sits in, each bubble slowly contracting in radius across the cycle as the wax cures around it and the bubble shrinks toward eventual invisibility by minute 47. Cast across the floor from the upper-right edge of the viewport (the east kitchen window) is the warm October sunrise light at 0.22 opacity wash with subtle warm-cream undertone, the light catching the iridescent sheen at the specific 14-degree angle that produces the peak oil-slick refraction. Visible at the lower-right edge of the viewport is the very edge of Bobbie's yellow plastic bucket (#f0c838) with the cellulose-sponge mop-head resting in it (#c8b478 with darker damp interior) and the small handle of the wooden mop visible angling up out of frame. Visible at the upper-left edge of the viewport is the very edge of the Kohler kitchen sink's chrome leg (#a8acb0) with a small pool of clear water gathered at the floor seam where the bucket was filled. The cursor's position acts as a virtual reflected-light angle — moving the cursor across the floor causes the iridescent band positions within approximately 200 pixels of the cursor to gently shift their conic-gradient angle by ±8 degrees suggesting the visitor very slightly tilting their head to catch the sheen at a slightly different angle, but the cursor cannot stop the cycle, cannot reverse the curing, cannot summon back the wet-wax peak iridescence, and cannot interact with any UI because there is no UI. The sweep-marks never move, the bucket and sink-leg never move, the floor pattern never changes; the iridescent bands continuously evolve, the bubbles slowly contract, the saturation slowly dulls; the piece runs forever, the wax keeps curing across each 47-minute cycle, and the visitor stands at the kitchen door beside Bobbie Stiefelmeier with the coffee mug watching the rainbow leave the floor.

subject.value: The single specific 47-minute kitchen-linoleum Glo-Coat curing window from 7:14am to 8:01am on Saturday October 19, 1968, at 4118 Edgewood Drive in Greendale Wisconsin, as Mrs. Roberta 'Bobbie' Stiefelmeier (age 36, homemaker since Kevin was born in 1957, married thirteen years to Don Stiefelmeier the second-shift quality-engineer at the Allis-Chalmers Tractor Division plant in West Allis, mother of Kevin and Joanne and Mark, grew up the oldest of seven on a small Sheboygan County dairy farm and learned at age nine from her mother Helma how to wax a kitchen floor on Saturday mornings before the men came in from milking) watches the second coat of Johnson Wax Glo-Coat dry across the cream-and-pale-mint Armstrong Excelon speckled vinyl-asbestos linoleum Don installed in October 1962 — the very specific small Saturday-morning ritual Bobbie has done every Saturday morning at 6:30am since the linoleum went in six years ago, the slow careful mop-down with the cellulose sponge that produces the iridescent oil-slick sheen across the wet wax that catches the east-window October light at the precise low sunrise angle, and the patient 47-minute curing window during which Bobbie sits at the kitchen door with her second cup of Folger's percolator coffee in the cream-and-gold-band Anchor Hocking mug watching the rainbow slowly leave the floor as the wax hardens into its final hard satin finish; the entire continuous six-year ritual of one specific working-class Wisconsin homemaker keeping one specific kitchen floor in honest weekly Saturday-morning condition as a real form of skilled domestic-maintenance craft worth doing carefully, and Bobbie's quiet patient understanding across those six years that the rainbow-iridescent sheen at minute 0 of the curing cycle is the small private weekly reward she gives herself for doing the work — a small specific beautiful thing visible only to her, only on Saturday mornings, only for 47 minutes, that no one else in the family is awake to see and that she has decided across six years she does not need to share with anyone.

tone.value: No tone — there is no narrator, no voice, no text visible on the page at any point. The piece speaks only through the slow continuous curing of floor-wax across linoleum, the patient drift of iridescent oil-slick bands, the slow contraction of micro-bubbles, and the silent angled sunrise light cast across the kitchen floor. The closest analogue to a voice is the silent careful attentiveness of Bobbie Stiefelmeier herself standing at the kitchen door with her coffee mug, watching the rainbow leave the floor across 47 minutes the way she has watched it every Saturday morning for six years: present-tense, unhurried, neither performing nor explaining, the visual equivalent of standing at a kitchen door in October 1968 in a Greendale Wisconsin split-level beside a 36-year-old homemaker who learned to wax floors from her mother on a Sheboygan County dairy farm in 1941 and who has been doing the small private Saturday-morning ritual since the linoleum went in six years ago, the quality of her absorption requiring no audience and offering no narration — only the patient continuous act of attention to a real domestic-maintenance moment happening for 47 minutes in a real working kitchen on a real October Saturday.

mandatory_element.value: A fully working generative kitchen-linoleum curing piece with the following functional behaviors all correctly implemented in pure CSS, SVG, and minimal JavaScript: (1) the viewport renders the full 9×11 foot Armstrong Excelon floor scaled to fill the browser window with the regular 9-inch tile grid visible across the entire surface, every individual tile bearing its own deterministically-generated cream-mint speckle pattern that does not change across visits; (2) a deterministic 47-minute time cycle drives all scene evolution via a JavaScript timing variable that progresses from t=0 (representing 7:14am, the moment Bobbie set down the bucket) through t=1.0 (representing 8:01am, full cure) and then loops smoothly back to t=0; (3) at each frame, every iridescent band's CSS conic-gradient angle, position, and opacity is computed via smooth interpolation between four keyframe states (7:14am peak wet-iridescence / 7:28am early-curing-shift / 7:46am mid-cure-dull / 8:01am final-satin-finish) so the bands continuously evolve with the non-linear quality of real wax surface-tension settling rather than mechanical fade; (4) exactly 340 micro-bubble highlights are rendered as soft-edged SVG circles distributed across the floor with their initial positions seeded from a fixed random seed so the same bubble field renders identically every visit, each bubble with its own independent horizontal drift velocity (between 0.3 and 0.8 pixels per second matching its local band's drift), each bubble with its own initial radius (between 2 and 4 pixels) that slowly contracts across the cycle to 0 at minute 47, and each bubble with its own independent specular-gleam cycle (a brief 0.4-second brightness boost every 18-30 seconds randomized per bubble); (5) each bubble's color is computed dynamically based on its current position relative to the overlapping iridescent bands — bubbles sitting under the warm-coral band render warm-coral with +0.15 opacity boost, bubbles under the dusty-violet band render dusty-violet, bubbles in the overlap zones render the blended color; (6) the sponge-mop sweep-marks render as 12 parallel CSS gradient stripes running floor-to-ceiling at fixed positions matching where the cellulose sponge laid down its second coat, each stripe with its own subtle internal ridged-pool texture that does not animate; (7) the east-window sunrise light cast is a CSS linear-gradient overlay across the upper-right quadrant of the viewport at 14-degree angle, with its opacity slowly increasing from 0.18 at t=0 to 0.26 at t=1.0 suggesting the climbing October sun, and with its position very slowly rotating by 3 pixels across the cycle; (8) the cursor's position implements a virtual reflected-light-angle nudge — within a 200-pixel radius of the cursor, the conic-gradient angles of iridescent bands are gently shifted by ±8 degrees with smooth radial falloff so cursor movement produces the soft physical feel of the visitor tilting their head slightly to catch the sheen at a different angle, but the cursor CANNOT stop, reverse, pause, or accelerate the 47-minute cycle, CANNOT bring back peak iridescence after it has cured, CANNOT make bubbles re-grow, and CANNOT interact with any UI because there is no UI; (9) the bucket, mop, sink-leg, water-pool, and Bobbie's barely-visible stocking-foot at the doorway are all rendered as static SVG paths that never move across the cycle; (10) absolutely no UI text anywhere on the page — no instructions, no tooltips, no captions, no header, no title, no footer, no controls, no buttons, no loading messages, no credits, no time-stamp, no date, no 'about this piece' link, no settings panel, no volume control, no pause button, no 'iridescence fading' announcement; (11) the piece runs autonomously and forever — no reset, no end state, no game state, no progression, no goal — the wax continues curing across the 47-minute cycle, the bubbles continue contracting, the bands continue dimming, the sunrise light continues climbing for as long as the page is open; (12) the cycle is deterministic — same starting state, same peak iridescence at the same elapsed time, same final satin finish at the same elapsed time — every visit, the same October 19 1968 morning on the same Greendale Wisconsin kitchen floor; (13) the entire piece works fully in-browser with no server, no API, no network calls, no external assets — pure CSS, SVG, and minimal vanilla JavaScript driving the time-cycle, band-drift, bubble-contraction, and sunrise-light loops via requestAnimationFrame.

wildcard.value: Exactly once per 47-minute cycle, at exactly t=0.064 (the 3-minute mark, approximately 7:17am in the historical time), one specific micro-bubble in the warm-coral band near the center of the floor produces a markedly slower and more saturated specular gleam than the others — a 2.4-second slow warm-coral bloom at full 0.95 opacity instead of the standard 0.4-second gleam at 0.7 opacity — and the bubble's radius briefly expands by 1.4 pixels during the bloom before slowly contracting back. This is the specific bubble that Bobbie standing at the kitchen door notices at 7:17am as the prettiest single point of light on the floor this Saturday morning — she does not photograph it, she does not mention it to anyone, she just notices it across the 2.4 seconds it holds its color and she takes a small slow sip of her coffee and she watches it settle. The visitor is given no indication this is happening, no acknowledgment in any UI, no reward for noticing — just the quiet fact that on this particular frame of this particular cycle, one bubble in the warm-coral band held its light a little longer because a 36-year-old homemaker standing in a kitchen door in Greendale Wisconsin in October 1968 noticed it once and decided privately it was the prettiest small thing she would see all week.

forbidden_register.value: Absolutely NO text, captions, labels-explaining-the-piece, title overlays, instructional UI, tooltip hints, loading messages, footer credits, watermarks, or any narrating voice anywhere on the page visible to the visitor — the piece must be entirely silent and entirely visual. NO horror-genre framing of the empty kitchen, the curing wax, the iridescent sheen, the bubbles, or the silent Saturday morning as creepy/uncanny/sinister/dread-inducing — this is a normal warm Saturday morning at a normal working-class Wisconsin split-level, Bobbie is at the kitchen door with her coffee, the kids are asleep upstairs, Don is at the Allis-Chalmers plant, and the floor is curing exactly the way it has cured every Saturday morning for six years. NO 'the floor is reflecting something it shouldn't' twist, NO 'something is wrong with the bubbles' creepypasta framing, NO 'the rainbow is a portal' Twilight-Zone register, NO David-Lynch domestic-uncanny atmosphere, NO It-Follows suburban-dread aesthetic. NO museum-archival 'remember when housewives waxed floors' nostalgic detachment, NO soft melancholy about 'the lost art of the Saturday-morning floor-wax,' NO cool ironic distance from late-1960s suburban Wisconsin housewife domestic-maintenance culture as quaint pre-Swiffer Americana, NO retrospective elegy framing Bobbie or 4118 Edgewood Drive or Greendale Wisconsin or Armstrong Excelon vinyl-asbestos tile or Johnson Wax Glo-Coat as already-vanished. NO 'where is she now' wistfulness about Bobbie grown older or the linoleum eventually torn out, NO 'this kitchen was photographed in 2019 just before the house was sold' framing, NO scrapbook-archival voice. NO health-anxiety overlay on the vinyl-asbestos tile (Bobbie does not know what asbestos is in 1968 and the page does not retroactively reframe her Saturday morning as carcinogenic exposure), NO environmental-anxiety overlay on the Glo-Coat solvents, NO 'imagine the chemicals she was breathing' smug-modern moralizing. NO Mad-Men-pastiche period kitsch, NO January-Jones-suburban-housewife-as-tragic framing, NO Stepford-Wives suburban-malaise undertone, NO Revolutionary-Road quiet-desperation register, NO Cheever-style suburban-anomie voice. NO feminist-grievance reading of Bobbie's Saturday-morning floor-wax ritual as oppression or trapped domesticity — Bobbie chose to be a homemaker when Kevin was born in 1957, she likes the work, the Saturday-morning ritual is hers, and Don respects her domestic craft because he is married to a woman who is good at things. NO Don-as-absent-or-domineering framing — Don is at a real Saturday overtime shift at Allis-Chalmers because the family needs the overtime money for Kevin's upcoming bracing-and-orthodontia appointment, and he topped off the percolator at 6:14am before he left because he knew Bobbie would want fresh coffee. NO 'imagine being a 36-year-old housewife in 1968 watching wax dry' smug-modern-superiority framing, NO Boomer-housewife pathos voice, NO 'simpler times' Boomer-nostalgia framing, NO 'they don't make housewives like this anymore' decline narrative. NO Vietnam-War political subtext, NO 1968-as-year-America-came-apart historical-essay framing, NO Nixon-election political overlay, NO 'Bobbie's son Kevin will be drafted in eight years' tragic foreshadowing — it is October 1968 in a Greendale kitchen and Bobbie is watching her floor dry and that is the whole subject of the piece. NO Norman-Rockwell sentimentality, NO Saturday-Evening-Post-cover Americana, NO Pinterest-vintage-1960s aesthetic mood-boarding, NO Wes-Anderson-symmetrical-frame styling, NO Andrew-Wyeth Helga-series romanticization. NO 'small-town Wisconsin folksy' caricature, NO accented-English pastiche, NO 'ya hey there' fake-Wisconsin dialogue, NO Garrison-Keillor Lake-Wobegon gentle-mockery register. NO night-shift dispatcher voice (7:14am Saturday morning is not a midnight desk vigil), NO lowercase sentence-fragments, NO hyperminimal museum-card prose, NO SCP-cryptic register about the curing wax or the wildcard bubble or the small private moment of noticing, NO archival cabinet/drawer/vault framing of the linoleum or the wax-can or the percolator as relic, NO observatory/society/club scaffolding of suburban-floor-waxers as fictional small-town society, NO radio/CB/ham-radio/broadcast/dispatcher/switchboard/operator-shift/transmission/signal/frequency-tuner framing of the kitchen radio (which is not even on — Bobbie wanted quiet this morning) or the Allis-Chalmers shift-whistle or the Johnson Wax regional-distribution-network as signal-relay metaphor — the wax is just wax, the floor is just a floor, neither is a signal metaphor, and signal/dispatcher/transmission framing is hard-banned this hour regardless of bucket. NO 'day-of-the-week' subject framing (Saturday is a contextual fact about when Bobbie waxes the floor, not the subject of the piece). NO 'fictional small-town society/club of [hobbyists]' framing. NO weather-app or photo-viewer interface conventions, NO settings panels, NO playback controls, NO speed sliders, NO color-pickers, NO 'pause the curing' affordance, NO 'identify the bubble' overlay tools. NO Mary-Oliver pause-and-notice-the-world quote-register, NO Wendell-Berry agrarian-philosophical handwringing, NO Annie-Dillard pilgrim-at-tinker-creek epiphany voice. The wax cures, the bands drift, the bubbles slowly contract, the sunrise light slowly climbs, Bobbie stands at the kitchen door with her coffee, and the visitor watches in silence — that is the entire piece. Treat Johnson Wax Glo-Coat as a serious working piece of mid-century domestic-maintenance product engineering, treat the Armstrong Excelon vinyl-asbestos tile as legitimate working 1962 flooring craft installed by Don himself across three weekends with skilled patient labor, treat Bobbie Stiefelmeier as a legitimate working homemaker with six years of patient skilled floor-maintenance expertise and a real running weekly domestic-craft practice she learned from her mother Helma on a Sheboygan County dairy farm in 1941, treat her quiet noticing of the prettiest bubble in the warm-coral band at 7:17am as a real small private weekly aesthetic experience worth full peer-to-peer respect, and treat the visitor's quiet act of standing at the kitchen door watching one specific 47-minute Saturday-morning floor-wax cure across a specific Greendale Wisconsin kitchen in October 1968 as real participation in a real act of patient domestic-craft attention worthy of full peer-to-peer respect.

length.description: a thing you read, light interactivity, one or two sections

era.value: 1994 — peak American suburban Sunday-newspaper-color-comics-section weekly puzzle-page era, the very specific Sunday morning when middle-class families across the upper Midwest spread out the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel's color-comics-and-puzzles section across the breakfast table beside the coffee carafe and the box of Eggo waffles, and when the back page of the Family Weekly insert printed by the syndicated Parade Magazine job-shop in Toledo carried a weekly Logic Maze feature designed by Mr. Wilfred 'Wif' Krzanowski (age 52, syndicated puzzle constructor since 1979, working out of his home office on Larchmont Avenue in Toledo Ohio with a drafting table by the south window and a cup of Maxwell House at his elbow, who composed the Logic Maze each week across three or four drafts on Monday mornings for the following Sunday's syndication run to 184 newspapers), and one specific Sunday morning of October 23, 1994 at 9:48am at the home of the Tessmann family of 4218 Maplewood Drive in Brookfield Wisconsin when 12-year-old Andrea Tessmann (sixth grade at Pilgrim Park Middle School, member of the math team, has been solving Wif Krzanowski's Logic Maze every Sunday morning since the start of fifth grade because her grandfather Walter Tessmann who lives in Sheboygan started clipping them for her in October 1992 and mailing them in a manila envelope every other Wednesday) sat at the kitchen table with the Family Weekly folded open to page 23 and her Bic blue pen ready

format.value: A genuine fully-solvable in-browser hidden-word find puzzle disguised as the actual Family Weekly back-page Logic Maze column from October 23, 1994 — the entire viewport renders as the printed newsprint page scaled enormously to fill the browser window edge-to-edge, with the syndicated Wif Krzanowski LOGIC MAZE column header at the top in chunky newspaper display type, a dense centered paragraph of 247 words of running prose written by Mr. Krzanowski as a small first-person essay-style word-puzzle about his Monday-morning drafting practice in Toledo (the paragraph reads as a single continuous piece of warm peer-level newspaper-puzzle-column copy about how he constructs his weekly Logic Mazes at the drafting table by the south window with the Maxwell House at his elbow, the small specific working details of his Monday-morning ritual, his quiet pride in 15 years of syndicated puzzle-column craft, and his patient hope that the Sunday-morning solver in Wisconsin or Iowa or Ohio is enjoying the maze), within which exactly ONE specific 8-letter word is camouflaged by running across word-and-punctuation boundaries (e.g., the letters spelling the hidden word appear consecutively as the end of one printed word and the start of the next), with no visual indication of which letters belong to the hidden word — the visitor must read the prose carefully and find the word — plus a small printed instruction box beneath the paragraph reading 'A common 8-letter word is hidden in the paragraph above, crossing word-boundaries. Can you find it before next Sunday's column?', plus a small Andrea-Tessmann-style hand-pencilled work-area along the right margin where the visitor's attempted underlines accumulate as they click letter-spans in the paragraph to test candidates, plus a small CHECK MY ANSWER button styled as Andrea's pencilled checkmark-doodle that becomes active only after the visitor has clicked a contiguous 8-letter span and which on click softly highlights the span in warm-coral if correct (the genuine hidden word) and softly nudges the span back to graphite-pencil-gray if not — the puzzle has exactly one valid solution, the hidden word is genuinely a common English 8-letter word, and finding it requires patient careful reading of the prose for letter-runs that cross spaces

subject.value: The single specific Sunday-morning hidden-word puzzle from the October 23, 1994 Family Weekly back-page LOGIC MAZE column by Mr. Wilfred 'Wif' Krzanowski of Toledo Ohio, designed by Wif
